title: Interactive Exploration of Large-scale Streamlines of Vector Fields via a Curve Segment Neighborhood Graph
summary: Streamlines have been widely used to represent and analyze various steady vector fields. To sufficiently represent important features in complex vector fields (like flow), a large number of streamlines are required. Due to the lack of a rigorous definition of features or patterns in streamlines, user interaction and exploration are required to achieve effective interpretation. Existing approaches based on clustering or pattern search, while valuable for specific analysis tasks, often face challenges in supporting interactive and level-of-detail exploration of large-scale curve-based data, particularly when real-time parameter adjustment and iterative refinement are needed. To address this, we design and implement an interactive web-based system. Our system utilizes a Curve Segment Neighborhood Graph (CSNG) to encode the neighboring relationships between curve segments. CSNG enables us to adapt a fast community detection algorithm to identify coherent flow structures and spatial groupings in the streamlines interactively. CSNG also supports a multi-level exploration through an enhanced force-directed layout. Furthermore, our system integrates an adjacency matrix representation to reveal detailed inter-relations among segments. To achieve real-time performance within a web browser, our system employs matrix compression for memory-efficient CSNG storage and parallel processing. We have applied our system to analyze and interpret complex patterns in several streamline datasets. Our experiments show that we achieve real-time performance on datasets with hundreds of thousands of segments.

AI.tldr: A web-based interactive system using Curve Segment Neighborhood Graph (CSNG) to analyze large streamline datasets in real time, featuring community-detection-based structure identification, enhanced multi-level force-directed layout, adjacency matrix relations, and memory-efficient, parallelized in-browser processing for hundreds of thousands of segments.
AI.motivation: To enable interactive, scalable exploration of complex vector fields where a large number of streamlines is needed, addressing the lack of rigorous feature definitions and the limitations of clustering/pattern-search approaches for real-time, multi-level exploration.
AI.method: Introduce CSNG to encode neighbor relations among curve segments; adapt a fast community detection algorithm to identify coherent flow structures; employ an enhanced force-directed layout for multi-level exploration; use an adjacency matrix to reveal detailed inter-relations among segments; implement matrix compression for memory-efficient storage; leverage parallel processing for real-time in-browser performance.
AI.result: Demonstrates real-time performance on datasets with hundreds of thousands of segments and applies the system to several streamline datasets.
AI.conclusion: CSNG-based interactive browser system enables scalable, interactive analysis of large streamline fields, combining structural discovery via community detection, detailed inter-relations via adjacency matrices, and efficient in-browser performance through compression and parallelism.

title: On the Doubling Dimension and the Perimeter of Geodesically Convex Sets in Fat Polygons
summary: Many algorithmic problems can be solved (almost) as efficiently in metric spaces of bounded doubling dimension as in Euclidean space. Unfortunately, the metric space defined by points in a simple polygon equipped with the geodesic distance does not necessarily have bounded doubling dimension. We therefore study the doubling dimension of fat polygons, for two well-known fatness definitions. We prove that locally-fat simple polygons do not always have bounded doubling dimension, while any $(α,β)$-covered polygon does have bounded doubling dimension (even if it has holes). We also study the perimeter of geodesically convex sets in $(α,β)$-covered polygons (possibly with holes), and show that this perimeter is at most a constant times the Euclidean diameter of the set. Using these two results, we obtain new results for several problems on $(α,β)$-covered polygons, including an algorithm that computes the closest pair of a set of $m$ points in an $(α,β)$-covered polygon with $n$ vertices that runs in $O(n + m\log{n})$ expected time.

AI.tldr: Bounding doubling dimension for fat polygons hinges on the fatness notion: locally-fat polygons can have unbounded doubling dimension, while (α,β)-covered polygons have bounded doubling dimension even with holes. This yields a perimeter bound for geodesically convex sets and enables efficient closest-pair computation in such polygons (O(n + m log n) expected).
AI.motivation: Bridge between metric-space algorithm design (bounded doubling dimension) and geodesic domains defined by polygons. Identify polygon classes where doubling dimension is bounded to enable efficient algorithms, despite holes.
AI.method: Theoretically analyze doubling dimension under two fatness definitions for polygons: locally-fat vs (α,β)-covered. Prove unboundedness for locally-fat simple polygons; prove bounded doubling dimension for (α,β)-covered polygons (even with holes). Derive a perimeter bound for geodesically convex sets in (α,β)-covered polygons. Use these results to design an algorithm for closest-pair in an (α,β)-covered polygon with n vertices and m points, achieving O(n + m log n) expected time.
AI.result: 1) Locally-fat simple polygons do not guarantee bounded doubling dimension. 2) Any (α,β)-covered polygon has bounded doubling dimension, even with holes. 3) The perimeter of geodesically convex sets in (α,β)-covered polygons is at most a constant factor of the Euclidean diameter. 4) Closest-pair algorithm for m points in an (α,β)-covered polygon with n vertices runs in O(n + m log n) expected time.
AI.conclusion: (α,β)-covered polygons form a robust class for geodesic metric algorithms: they ensure bounded doubling dimension and modest perimeter growth, enabling efficient problems such as nearest/farthest neighbor and closest-pair computations, in contrast to the inadequacy of local fatness alone.

title: Online Algorithms for Geometric Independent Set
summary:
In the classical online model, the maximum independent set problem admits an $Ω(n)$ lower bound on the competitive ratio even for interval graphs, motivating the study of the problem under additional assumptions. We first study the problem on graphs with a bounded independent kissing number $ζ$, defined as the size of the largest induced star in the graph minus one. We show that a simple greedy algorithm, requiring no geometric representation, achieves a competitive ratio of $ζ$. Moreover, this bound is optimal for deterministic online algorithms and asymptotically optimal for randomized ones. This extends previous results from specific geometric graph families to more general graph classes.
  Since this bound rules out further improvements through randomization alone, we investigate the power of randomization with access to geometric representation. When the geometric representation of the objects is known, we present randomized online algorithms with improved guarantees. For unit ball graphs in $\mathbb{R}^3$, we present an algorithm whose expected competitive ratio is strictly smaller than the deterministic lower bound implied by the independent kissing number. For $α$-fat objects and for axis-aligned hyper-rectangles in $\mathbb{R}^d$ with bounded diameters, we obtain algorithms with expected competitive ratios that depend polylogarithmically on the ratio between the maximum and minimum object diameters. In both cases, the randomized lower bound implied by the independent kissing number grows polynomially with the ratio between the maximum and minimum object diameters, implying substantial performance guarantees for our algorithms.

AI.tldr: Greedy online MIS achieves a competitive ratio equal to the bounded independent kissing number ζ on graphs with that property; this bound is optimal for deterministic algorithms and asymptotically tight for randomization. If geometry is available, randomization yields improved guarantees: sub-ζ ratios for unit-ball graphs in R^3, and polylogarithmic dependence on diameter ratio for α-fat objects and axis-aligned hyper-rectangles with bounded diameters; randomized lower bounds grow polynomially with diameter ratio, showing substantial gains from geometric information.
AI.motivation: To overcome Ω(n) competitive bounds for MIS in the online model by exploiting structural properties (bounded independent kissing number) and by leveraging geometric information when available.
AI.method: 1) Greedy online MIS on ζ-bounded graphs; prove ζ-competitive and optimality for deterministic, asymptotic optimality for randomized. 2) With known geometry, design randomized online algorithms for unit-ball graphs in R^3, α-fat objects, and axis-aligned hyper-rectangles with bounded diameters; derive competitive ratios dependent on diameter ratios; establish lower bounds that scale polynomially with diameter ratio.
AI.result: 1) A simple greedy achieves competitive ratio ζ; optimal for deterministic, asymptotically optimal for randomized. 2) With geometry, improved randomized algorithms: unit-ball graphs in R^3 have expected ratio below deterministic lower bound implied by ζ; α-fat objects and axis-aligned hyper-rectangles achieve polylog dependencies on max/min diameter, with randomized lower bounds that grow polynomially in diameter ratio.
AI.conclusion: Randomization plus available geometric representation can significantly outperform ζ-based limits, yielding nontrivial improvements for several natural geometric graph families. The results extend prior geometric-specific findings to broader graph classes and quantify how diameter ratios govern randomized performance.

title: Orthogonal Strip Partitioning of Polygons: Lattice-Theoretic Algorithms and Lower Bounds
summary:
We study a variant of a polygon partition problem, introduced by Chung, Iwama, Liao, and Ahn [ISAAC'25]. Given orthogonal unit vectors $\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}\in \mathbb{R}^2$ and a polygon $P$ with $n$ vertices, we partition $P$ into connected pieces by cuts parallel to $\mathbf{v}$ such that each resulting subpolygon has width at most one in direction $\mathbf{u}$. We consider the value version, which asks for the minimum number of strips, and the reporting version, which outputs a compact encoding of the cuts in an optimal strip partition.
  We give efficient algorithms and lower bounds for both versions on three classes of polygons of increasing generality: convex, simple, and self-overlapping. For convex polygons, we solve the value version in $O(\log n)$ time and the reporting version in $O\!\left(h \log\left(1 + \frac{n}{h}\right)\right)$ time, where $h$ is the width of $P$ in direction $\mathbf{u}$. We prove matching lower bounds in the decision-tree model, showing that the reporting algorithm is input-sensitive optimal with respect to $h$. For simple polygons, we present $O(n \log n)$-time, $O(n)$-space algorithms for both versions and prove an $Ω(n)$ lower bound. For self-overlapping polygons, we extend the approach for simple polygons to obtain $O(n \log n)$-time, $O(n)$-space algorithms for both versions, and we prove a matching $Ω(n \log n)$ lower bound in the algebraic computation-tree model via a reduction from the $δ$-closeness problem.
  Our approach relies on a lattice-theoretic formulation of the problem. We represent strip partitions as antichains of intervals in the Clarke--Cormack--Burkowski lattice, originally developed for minimal-interval semantics in information retrieval. Within this lattice framework, we design a dynamic programming algorithm that uses the lattice operations of meet and join.

AI.tldr: Efficient algorithms and tight lower bounds for a variant of a polygon partition problem into width-bounded strips, across convex, simple, and self-overlapping polygons, via a Clarke–Cormack–Burkowski lattice formulation and dynamic programming.
AI.motivation: Extend and analyze the value and reporting versions of the Chung–Iwama–Liao–Ahn polygon-partition problem. Develop efficient algorithms and tight lower bounds for increasingly general polygon classes, using a lattice-theoretic framework.
AI.method: Model strip partitions as antichains of intervals in the Clarke–Cormack–Burkowski lattice. Use lattice operations (meet and join) within a dynamic programming scheme to compute optimal partitions. Provide class-specific algorithms (convex, simple, self-overlapping) and establish lower bounds (decision-tree for convex, algebraic computation-tree for self-overlapping).
AI.result: Convex: value version in O(log n); reporting in O(h log(1+n/h)); matching lower bounds in decision-tree model. Simple: O(n log n) time and O(n) space for both versions, with Ω(n) lower bound. Self-overlapping: O(n log n) time and O(n) space for both versions, with Ω(n log n) lower bound (via reduction from δ-closeness).
AI.conclusion: The lattice-based DP provides input-sensitive optimal reporting and near-linear algorithms across polygon classes, linking geometry problems with lattice-theoretic methods and yielding tight lower bounds.

title: CART: Context-Aware Terrain Adaptation using Temporal Sequence Selection for Legged Robots
summary: Animals in nature combine multiple modalities, such as sight and feel, to perceive terrain and develop an understanding of how to walk on uneven terrain in a stable manner. Similarly, legged robots need to develop their ability to stably walk on complex terrains by developing an understanding of the relationship between vision and proprioception. Most current terrain adaptation methods are susceptible to failure on complex, off-road terrain as they rely on prior experience, particularly observations from a vision sensor. This experience-based learning often creates a Visual-Texture Paradox between what has been seen and how it actually feels. In this work, we introduce CART, a high-level controller built on a context-aware terrain adaptation approach that integrates proprioception and exteroception from onboard sensing to achieve a robust understanding of terrain. We evaluate our method on multiple terrains using an ANYmal-C robot on the IsaacSim simulator and a Boston Dynamics SPOT robot for our real-world experiments. To evaluate the learned contextual terrain properties, we adapt vibrational stability on the base of the robot as a metric. We compare CART with various state-of-the-art baselines equipped with multimodal sensing in both simulation and the real world. CART achieves an average success rate improvement of 5% over all baselines in simulation and improves the overall stability up to 45% and 24% in the real world without increasing the time taken by the robot to accomplish locomotion tasks.

AI.tldr: CART is a context-aware high-level controller that fuses proprioception and exteroception for terrain adaptation to improve stability and success on uneven terrain, validated on ANYmal-C in simulation and SPOT in the real world, showing modest simulation gains and substantial stability improvements without extra time.
AI.motivation: Vision-based learning can fail on complex off-road terrain due to misalignment between observed texture and tactile feedback (Visual-Texture Paradox); integrating proprioception and exteroception aims to create a robust, context-aware understanding of terrain.
AI.method: Introduce CART, a high-level controller that integrates onboard proprioception and exteroception to achieve context-aware terrain adaptation. Evaluation on multiple terrains using ANYmal-C in IsaacSim and Boston Dynamics SPOT in real world. Stability-based vibrational metric used to assess learned terrain properties. Comparisons against state-of-the-art multimodal baselines in simulation and real-world tests.
AI.result: CART achieves an average 5% improvement in success rate over baselines in simulation. Real-world tests show stability improvements up to 45% and 24% in different conditions, without increasing locomotion time.
AI.conclusion: Context-aware fusion of proprioceptive and exteroceptive signals enables more robust terrain understanding and stable locomotion for legged robots. CART demonstrates tangible gains in both simulated and real-world environments and offers a path toward reducing reliance on vision-only experiences for off-road locomotion.

title: QualiaNet: An Experience-Before-Inference Network
summary: Human 3D vision involves two distinct stages: an Experience Module, where stereo depth is extracted relative to fixation, and an Inference Module, where this experience is interpreted to estimate 3D scene properties. Paradoxically, although our experience of stereo vision does not provide us with distance information, it does affect our inferences about visual scale. We propose the Inference Module exploits a natural scene statistic: near scenes produce vivid disparity gradients, while far scenes appear comparatively flat. QualiaNet implements this two-stage architecture computationally: disparity maps simulating human stereo experience are passed to a CNN trained to estimate distance. The network can recover distance from disparity gradients alone, validating this approach.

AI.tldr: Two-stage model where an Experience Module yields stereo disparities tied to fixation, and an Inference Module uses disparity gradients to infer distance; QualiaNet demonstrates that distance can be recovered from disparity-gradients alone.
AI.motivation: Explain how human stereo experience shapes inference of visual scale, and operationalize a two-stage architecture that links low-level stereo cues to high-level 3D scene properties.
AI.method: Create disparity maps that mimic human stereo experience (relative to fixation) and train a CNN to estimate distance from these disparities; evaluate whether distance can be inferred from disparity-gradients alone.
AI.result: The CNN successfully estimates distance from disparity-gradient information, validating the two-stage architecture and the role of natural scene statistics in scale inference.
AI.conclusion: Perceptual experience in the first stage encodes cues (disparity gradients) that the second-stage inference can read to estimate scene distance, supporting a two-stage explanation of visual scale perception.

title: The Devil Is in Gradient Entanglement: Energy-Aware Gradient Coordinator for Robust Generalized Category Discovery
summary: Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) leverages labeled data to categorize unlabeled samples from known or unknown classes. Most previous methods jointly optimize supervised and unsupervised objectives and achieve promising results. However, inherent optimization interference still limits their ability to improve further. Through quantitative analysis, we identify a key issue, i.e., gradient entanglement, which 1) distorts supervised gradients and weakens discrimination among known classes, and 2) induces representation-subspace overlap between known and novel classes, reducing the separability of novel categories. To address this issue, we propose the Energy-Aware Gradient Coordinator (EAGC), a plug-and-play gradient-level module that explicitly regulates the optimization process. EAGC comprises two components: Anchor-based Gradient Alignment (AGA) and Energy-aware Elastic Projection (EEP). AGA introduces a reference model to anchor the gradient directions of labeled samples, preserving the discriminative structure of known classes against the interference of unlabeled gradients. EEP softly projects unlabeled gradients onto the complement of the known-class subspace and derives an energy-based coefficient to adaptively scale the projection for each unlabeled sample according to its degree of alignment with the known subspace, thereby reducing subspace overlap without suppressing unlabeled samples that likely belong to known classes. Experiments show that EAGC consistently boosts existing methods and establishes new state-of-the-art results. Code is available at https://haiyangzheng.github.io/EAGC.

AI.tldr: A gradient-level module EAGC reduces gradient entanglement in Generalized Category Discovery by anchoring supervised gradients and adaptively projecting unlabeled gradients away from the known-class subspace, yielding state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Generalized Category Discovery (GCD) jointly optimizes supervised and unsupervised objectives, causing gradient entanglement that distorts known-class discrimination and creates subspace overlap between known and novel classes. This bottleneck limits performance; a gradient-centric solution is needed to disentangle updates.
AI.method: Energy-Aware Gradient Coordinator (EAGC) is a plug-and-play module with two components: Anchor-based Gradient Alignment (AGA) introduces a reference model to anchor the gradient directions of labeled samples, preserving the discriminative structure of known classes; Energy-aware Elastic Projection (EEP) softly projects unlabeled gradients onto the complement of the known-class subspace and uses an energy-based coefficient to scale the projection according to each sample's alignment with the known subspace, reducing subspace overlap while not suppressing unlabeled samples likely belonging to known classes.
AI.result: Experiments show that EAGC consistently boosts existing GCD methods and achieves new state-of-the-art results. The authors provide code at the cited URL.
AI.conclusion: EAGC offers a lightweight, gradient-level intervention that mitigates gradient entanglement, preserves discriminative structure for known classes, and improves separability for novel categories, with broad compatibility across GCD frameworks.

title: NuHF Claw: A Risk Constrained Cognitive Agent Framework for Human Centered Procedure Support in Digital Nuclear Control Rooms
summary: The rapid digitization of nuclear power plant main control rooms has fundamentally reshaped operator interaction patterns, introducing complex soft-control behaviors and elevated cognitive risks that are not adequately addressed by existing human reliability analysis approaches. Although recent advances in large language models and autonomous agents offer new opportunities for intelligent decision support, their deployment in safety critical environments remains constrained by risks of hallucinated reasoning and weakened human authority. This study proposes NuHF Claw, a persistent cognitive-risk agent framework that enables risk governed human centered autonomy for digital nuclear operations. The core methodological innovation lies in the introduction of a risk constrained agent runtime, which tightly couples cognitive state inference with probabilistic safety assessment to regulate autonomous system behavior in real time. By integrating cognitively grounded workload and situational awareness estimation with dynamic human error probability prediction, the framework transforms conventional offline reliability analysis into a proactive intervention mechanism embedded directly within operational workflows. Experimental validation on a high-fidelity digital control room simulator demonstrates that NuHF Claw can anticipate interface induced cognitive degradation, dynamically constrain unsafe autonomous recommendations, and provide risk-aware navigational guidance while preserving human decision authority. The results highlight a fundamental shift from automation-driven operation toward cognition-aware autonomy, offering a principled pathway for the safe integration of intelligent agents into next-generation nuclear control environments.

AI.tldr: A risk-constrained agent framework (NuHF Claw) enables cognition-aware autonomy in digital nuclear control by coupling cognitive-state inference with probabilistic safety assessment to guide autonomous decisions while preserving human authority; validated in a high-fidelity simulator.
AI.motivation: Digitalization of nuclear power plant main control rooms introduces soft-control patterns and cognitive risks not captured by traditional HRA; risks of hallucinated reasoning and erosion of human authority from autonomous agents hinder safe deployment.
AI.method: Introduce a risk-constrained agent runtime that tightly couples cognitive state inference with probabilistic safety assessment to regulate autonomous behavior in real time. Integrate cognitively grounded workload and situational awareness estimation with dynamic human error probability prediction, turning offline reliability analysis into proactive, workflow-embedded intervention.
AI.result: Experimental validation in a high-fidelity digital control room (DCR) simulator shows NuHF Claw anticipates interface-induced cognitive degradation, dynamically constrains unsafe autonomous recommendations, and provides risk-aware navigational guidance while preserving human decision authority.
AI.conclusion: Represents a shift from automation-driven operation to cognition-aware autonomy, offering a principled pathway for safe integration of intelligent agents into next-generation nuclear control environments.

title: RoSLAC: Robust Simultaneous Localization and Calibration of Multiple Magnetometers
summary: Localization of autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) in enclosed or semi-enclosed environments such as offices, hotels, hospitals, indoor parking facilities, and underground spaces where GPS signals are weak or unavailable remains a major obstacle to the deployment of fully autonomous systems. Infrastructure-based localization approaches, such as QR codes and RFID, are constrained by high installation and maintenance costs as well as limited flexibility, while onboard sensor-based methods, including LiDAR- and vision-based solutions, are affected by ambiguous geometric features and frequent occlusions caused by dynamic obstacles such as pedestrians. Ambient magnetic field (AMF)-based localization has therefore attracted growing interest in recent years because it does not rely on external infrastructure or geometric features, making it well-suited for AMR applications such as service robots and security robots. However, magnetometer measurements are often corrupted by distortions caused by ferromagnetic materials present on the sensor platform, which bias the AMF and degrade localization reliability. As a result, accurate magnetometer calibration to estimate distortion parameters becomes essential. Conventional calibration methods that rely on rotating the magnetometer are impractical for large and heavy platforms. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a robust simultaneous localization and calibration (RoSLAC) approach based on alternating optimization, which iteratively and efficiently estimates both the platform pose and magnetometer calibration parameters. Extensive evaluations conducted in high-fidelity simulation and real-world environments demonstrate that the proposed RoSLAC method achieves high localization accuracy while maintaining low computational cost compared with state-of-the-art magnetometer calibration techniques.

AI.tldr: RoSLAC is a robust alternating-optimization framework for simultaneous localization and magnetometer calibration, enabling accurate AMR localization in GPS-denied environments without magnetometer rotation and with low computational cost.
AI.motivation: AMF-based localization is attractive due to no external infrastructure, but magnetometer distortions from ferromagnetic materials bias measurements. Conventional calibration requiring magnetometer rotation is impractical for large/heavy platforms, necessitating online, efficient calibration integrated with localization.
AI.method: An alternating-optimization approach that jointly estimates the platform pose and magnetometer calibration parameters. The method avoids physical rotation of the magnetometer and iteratively updates pose and distortion parameters to refine localization.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations in high-fidelity simulation and real-world environments show high localization accuracy with lower computational cost compared with state-of-the-art magnetometer calibration techniques.
AI.conclusion: RoSLAC provides a practical, efficient solution for simultaneous localization and magnetometer calibration in AMR deployments, improving robustness to magnetic distortions without intrusive hardware or manual calibration.

title: HY-World 2.0: A Multi-Modal World Model for Reconstructing, Generating, and Simulating 3D Worlds
summary: We introduce HY-World 2.0, a multi-modal world model framework that advances our prior project HY-World 1.0. HY-World 2.0 accommodates diverse input modalities, including text prompts, single-view images, multi-view images, and videos, and produces 3D world representations. With text or single-view image inputs, the model performs world generation, synthesizing high-fidelity, navigable 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) scenes. This is achieved through a four-stage method: a) Panorama Generation with HY-Pano 2.0, b) Trajectory Planning with WorldNav, c) World Expansion with WorldStereo 2.0, and d) World Composition with WorldMirror 2.0. Specifically, we introduce key innovations to enhance panorama fidelity, enable 3D scene understanding and planning, and upgrade WorldStereo, our keyframe-based view generation model with consistent memory. We also upgrade WorldMirror, a feed-forward model for universal 3D prediction, by refining model architecture and learning strategy, enabling world reconstruction from multi-view images or videos. Also, we introduce WorldLens, a high-performance 3DGS rendering platform featuring a flexible engine-agnostic architecture, automatic IBL lighting, efficient collision detection, and training-rendering co-design, enabling interactive exploration of 3D worlds with character support. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HY-World 2.0 achieves state-of-the-art performance on several benchmarks among open-source approaches, delivering results comparable to the closed-source model Marble. We release all model weights, code, and technical details to facilitate reproducibility and support further research on 3D world models.

AI.tldr: HY-World 2.0 extends multi-modal 3D world modeling to handle text, images, and videos, producing 3D Gaussian Splatting scenes via a four-stage pipeline: panorama generation, trajectory planning, world expansion, and world composition, with new modules HY-Pano 2.0, WorldNav, WorldStereo 2.0, WorldMirror 2.0, and WorldLens; claims state-of-the-art among open-source approaches and competitive with Marble; released open-source weights and code.
AI.motivation: To enable flexible, end-to-end creation and exploration of high-fidelity navigable 3D worlds from diverse input modalities, addressing limitations of prior HY-World 1.0 in fidelity, planning, and multi-view consistency; to deliver an extensible, architecture-agnostic rendering platform with co-design of training and rendering.
AI.method: A four-stage pipeline: (a) Panorama Generation with HY-Pano 2.0 to improve panorama fidelity, (b) Trajectory Planning with WorldNav for 3D scene understanding and navigation planning, (c) World Expansion with WorldStereo 2.0 to enhance keyframe-based view generation with memory, and (d) World Composition with WorldMirror 2.0 for universal 3D prediction. Introduces WorldLens as a high-performance rendering platform with engine-agnostic design, automatic image-based lighting, and efficient collision detection. Upgrades to model architectures and learning strategies enable reconstruction from multi-view images or videos, and a sentiment of co-design between training and rendering processes.
AI.result: Demonstrations report state-of-the-art performance among open-source 3D world-model approaches, with results comparable to the closed-source Marble model across several benchmarks; comprehensive ablations and qualitative analyses; extensive experiments validate fidelity, consistency, and interactive exploration capabilities; release of model weights and code for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: HY-World 2.0 advances multi-modal 3D world modeling by integrating enhanced panorama generation, planning, memory-enabled view synthesis, and universal 3D prediction within a flexible rendering platform, achieving strong open-source performance and enabling reproducible research and broader applicability in interactive 3D environments.

title: MixAtlas: Uncertainty-aware Data Mixture Optimization for Multimodal LLM Midtraining
summary: Domain reweighting can improve sample efficiency and downstream generalization, but data-mixture optimization for multimodal midtraining remains largely unexplored. Current multimodal training recipes tune mixtures along a single dimension, typically data format or task type. We introduce MixAtlas, a method that produces benchmark-targeted data recipes that can be inspected, adapted, and transferred to new corpora. MixAtlas decomposes the training corpus along two axes: image concepts (10 visual-domain clusters discovered via CLIP embeddings) and task supervision (5 objective types including captioning, OCR, grounding, detection, and VQA). Using small proxy models (Qwen2-0.5B) paired with a Gaussian-process surrogate and GP-UCB acquisition, MixAtlas searches the resulting mixture space with the same proxy budget as regression-based baselines but finds better-performing mixtures. We evaluate on 10 benchmarks spanning visual understanding, document reasoning, and multimodal reasoning. On Qwen2-7B, optimized mixtures improve average performance by 8.5%-17.6% over the strongest baseline; on Qwen2.5-7B, gains are 1.0%-3.3%. Both settings reach baseline-equivalent training loss in up to 2 times fewer steps. Recipes discovered on 0.5B proxies transfer to 7B-scale training across Qwen model families.

AI.tldr: MixAtlas introduces a two-axis data-reweighting strategy for multimodal midtraining by decomposing the corpus into image concepts (10 CLIP-based clusters) and task supervision types (5 objectives). It uses small proxies (Qwen 0.5B) with a Gaussian-process surrogate and GP-UCB to search the mixture space, achieving superior mixtures that transfer to larger models, reducing training steps to reach baseline loss and improving performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Data-mixture optimization for multimodal midtraining is underexplored, and existing work typically tunes mixtures along a single dimension. There is a need for interpretable, benchmark-targeted data recipes that can be inspected, transferred across corpora and model scales, and that improve sample efficiency.
AI.method: Decompose training data into two axes: image concepts (10 CLIP-derived visual clusters) and task supervision types (5 objectives: captioning, OCR, grounding, detection, VQA). Use small proxy models (Qwen2-0.5B) with a Gaussian-process surrogate and GP-UCB to optimize data mixtures under a fixed proxy budget, then transfer discovered recipes to larger models.
AI.result: Optimized mixtures yield 8.5%–17.6% average gains over the strongest baseline on Qwen2-7B and 1.0%–3.3% on Qwen2.5-7B. Training loss reaches baseline-equivalent levels in up to 2x fewer steps. Recipes discovered on 0.5B proxies transfer to 7B-scale training across Qwen model families.
AI.conclusion: MixAtlas provides benchmark-targeted, transferable data recipes for multimodal midtraining, demonstrating strong cross-model transferability and significant sample efficiency gains via a two-axis data-mipel optimization guided by a GP-UCB proxy search.

title: Simulating Human Cognition: Heartbeat-Driven Autonomous Thinking Activity Scheduling for LLM-based AI systems
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in reasoning and tool use, yet they often suffer from rigid, reactive control flows that limit their adaptability and efficiency. Most existing frameworks rely on fixed pipelines or failure-triggered reflection, causing agents to act impulsively or correct errors only after they occur. In this paper, we introduce Heartbeat-Driven Autonomous Thinking Activity Scheduling, a mechanism that enables proactive, adaptive, and continuous self-regulation. Mirroring the natural rhythm of human cognition, our system employs a periodic ``heartbeat'' mechanism to orchestrate a dynamic repertoire of cognitive modules (e.g., Planner, Critic, Recaller, Dreamer). Unlike traditional approaches that rely on hard-coded symbolic rules or immediate reactive triggers, our scheduler learns to determine when to engage specific thinking activities -- such as recalling memories, summarizing experiences, or strategic planning -- based on temporal patterns and historical context. This functional approach allows cognitive modules to be dynamically added or removed without structural reengineering. Meanwhile, we propose a meta-learning strategy for continual policy adaptation, where the scheduler optimizes its cognitive strategy over time using historical interaction logs. Evaluation results demonstrate that our approach effectively learns to schedule cognitive activities based on historical data and can autonomously integrate new thinking modules.

AI.tldr: Heartbeat-driven scheduler enables proactive, adaptive self-regulation in LLM agents by dynamically managing cognitive modules.
AI.motivation: Conventional LLM agent control flows are rigid and reactive, relying on fixed pipelines or post-hoc reflection, which hinders adaptability and efficiency; there is a need for proactive, continuous self-regulation and modular extensibility.
AI.method: Introduce a periodic heartbeat to orchestrate a dynamic set of cognitive modules (Planner, Critic, Recaller, Dreamer). The scheduler learns to engage specific thinking activities based on temporal patterns and historical context, allowing modules to be added or removed without reengineering. A meta-learning strategy optimizes the scheduler’s cognitive policy over time using historical interaction logs.
AI.result: Evaluation shows the scheduler learns to schedule cognitive activities from historical data and can autonomously integrate new thinking modules.
AI.conclusion: Heartbeat-driven scheduling enables proactive, adaptive cognitive control with dynamic modularity and continual policy adaptation for LLM agents.

title: SpaceMind: A Modular and Self-Evolving Embodied Vision-Language Agent Framework for Autonomous On-orbit Servicing
comment: 23 pages, 6 figures, 7 tables. Code available at https://github.com/wuaodi/SpaceMind
summary: Autonomous on-orbit servicing demands embodied agents that perceive through visual sensors, reason about 3D spatial situations, and execute multi-phase tasks over extended horizons. We present SpaceMind, a modular and self-evolving vision-language model (VLM) agent framework that decomposes knowledge, tools, and reasoning into three independently extensible dimensions: skill modules with dynamic routing, Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools with configurable profiles, and injectable reasoning-mode skills. An MCP-Redis interface layer enables the same codebase to operate across simulation and physical hardware without modification, and a Skill Self-Evolution mechanism distills operational experience into persistent skill files without model fine-tuning. We validate SpaceMind through 192 closed-loop runs across five satellites, three task types, and two environments, a UE5 simulation and a physical laboratory, deliberately including degraded conditions to stress-test robustness. Under nominal conditions all modes achieve 90--100% navigation success; under degradation, the Prospective mode uniquely succeeds in search-and-approach tasks where other modes fail. A self-evolution study shows that the agent recovers from failure in four of six groups from a single failed episode, including complete failure to 100% success and inspection scores improving from 12 to 59 out of 100. Real-world validation confirms zero-code-modification transfer to a physical robot with 100% rendezvous success. Code: https://github.com/wuaodi/SpaceMind

AI.tldr: SpaceMind introduces a modular, self-evolving VLM agent for autonomous on-orbit servicing, achieving cross-domain transfer with robust performance and minimal fine-tuning; demonstrated across simulation and real hardware with self-improvement.
AI.motivation: To create an extensible, robust agent architecture capable of long-horizon reasoning, multi-task operations, and seamless sim-to-real transfer in the harsh space environment.
AI.method: Three orthogonal dimensions: skill modules with dynamic routing; Model Context Protocol (MCP) tools with configurable profiles; injectable reasoning-mode skills. An MCP-Redis bridge enables the same codebase to run on both simulation and physical hardware. A Skill Self-Evolution mechanism distills operational experience into persistent skill files without model fine-tuning. Evaluation involved 192 closed-loop runs across five satellites, three task types, and two environments (UE5 simulation and a physical lab), including degraded conditions.
AI.result: Nominal conditions yield 90–100% navigation success across modes. Under degradation, the Prospective mode uniquely succeeds in search-and-approach tasks where others fail. Self-evolution experiments show recovery in four of six groups from a single failed episode; inspection scores rise from 12 to 59/100. Real-world validation achieves 100% rendezvous with zero-code modification.
AI.conclusion: SpaceMind demonstrates modular extensibility, robust sim-to-real transfer, and effective self-improvement, with strong performance under degraded conditions; code is available at the stated GitHub URL.

title: Geometrically Consistent Multi-View Scene Generation from Freehand Sketches
summary:
We tackle a new problem: generating geometrically consistent multi-view scenes from a single freehand sketch. Freehand sketches are the most geometrically impoverished input one could offer a multi-view generator. They convey scene intent through abstract strokes while introducing spatial distortions that actively conflict with any consistent 3D interpretation. No prior method attempts this; existing multi-view approaches require photographs or text, while sketch-to-3D methods need multiple views or costly per-scene optimisation.
  We address three compounding challenges; absent training data, the need for geometric reasoning from distorted 2D input, and cross-view consistency, through three mutually reinforcing contributions: (i) a curated dataset of $\sim$9k sketch-to-multiview samples, constructed via an automated generation and filtering pipeline; (ii) Parallel Camera-Aware Attention Adapters (CA3) that inject geometric inductive biases into the video transformer; and (iii) a Sparse Correspondence Supervision Loss (CSL) derived from Structure-from-Motion reconstructions.
  Our framework synthesizes all views in a single denoising process without requiring reference images, iterative refinement, or per-scene optimization. Our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art two-stage baselines, improving realism (FID) by over 60% and geometric consistency (Corr-Acc) by 23%, while providing up to a 3.7$\times$ inference speedup.

AI.tldr: Generates geometrically consistent multi-view scenes from a single freehand sketch using a synthetic dataset, CA3 attention adapters, and a CSL loss, enabling a single-pass denoising-based synthesis without per-scene optimization and achieving major gains in realism, geometry, and speed.
AI.motivation: Freehand sketches convey scene intent but are geometrically impoverished and distorted, making 3D interpretation hard. No prior method handles single-sketch to multi-view generation; training data is scarce; cross-view consistency is challenging.
AI.method: Three contributions: (i) a ~9k, automatically generated and filtered sketch-to-multiview dataset; (ii) Parallel Camera-Aware Attention Adapters (CA3) that inject geometric inductive biases into a video transformer; (iii) a Sparse Correspondence Supervision Loss (CSL) derived from Structure-from-Motion reconstructions. The framework synthesizes all views in one denoising pass without reference images, iterative refinement, or per-scene optimization.
AI.result: Outperforms two-stage baselines, with realism (FID) improving by >60% and geometric consistency (Corr-Acc) by ~23%, and inference speedup up to 3.7x.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-aware, data-efficient single-pass framework can produce multi-view scenes from freehand sketches with strong realism and cross-view coherence, thanks to a synthetic dataset, CA3 inductive biases, and CSL supervision.

title: Portfolio Optimization Proxies under Label Scarcity and Regime Shifts via Bayesian and Deterministic Students under Semi-Supervised Sandwich Training
comment: 18 pages of main text. 10 pages of appendices. 35 references. Around 13 figures
summary: This paper proposes a machine learning assisted portfolio optimization framework designed for low data environments and regime uncertainty. We construct a teacher student learning pipeline in which a Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR) optimizer generates supervisory labels, and neural models (Bayesian and deterministic) are trained using both real and synthetically augmented data. The synthetic data is generated using a factor based model with t copula residuals, enabling training beyond the limited real sample of 104 labeled observations. We evaluate four student models under a structured experimental framework comprising (i) controlled synthetic experiments (3 x 5 seed grid), (ii) in-distribution real market evaluation (C2A) and (iii) cross-universe generalization (D2A). In real-market settings, models are deployed using a rolling evaluation protocol where a frozen pretrained model is periodically fine tuned on recent observations and reset to its base state, ensuring stability while allowing limited adaptation. Results show that student models can match or outperform the CVaR teacher in several settings, while achieving improved robustness under regime shifts and reduced turnover. These findings suggest that hybrid optimization learning approaches can enhance portfolio construction in data constrained environments

AI.tldr: A machine learning assisted portfolio optimization framework for low-data, regime-uncertain environments using a teacher-student CVaR setup with synthetically augmented data; student models trained on real and synthetic data; improved robustness and reduced turnover under regime shifts.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity and regime uncertainty in portfolio optimization by leveraging hybrid optimization-learning and synthetic data augmentation to train robust models beyond the limited real sample.
AI.method: A teacher-student pipeline where a CVaR optimizer generates supervisory labels for training neural models (Bayesian and deterministic). Synthetic data is produced via a factor-based model with t copula residuals to augment the limited 104 labeled observations. Four student models are evaluated under (i) controlled synthetic experiments (3x5 seed grid), (ii) in-distribution real market evaluation (C2A), and (iii) cross-universe generalization (D2A). A rolling evaluation protocol at deployment freezes the pretrained model and periodically fine-tunes on recent data, then resets to the base state for stability.
AI.result: Student models match or exceed the CVaR teacher in several settings while showing improved robustness under regime shifts and reduced turnover. The findings support that hybrid optimization-learning can enhance portfolio construction in data-constrained environments.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid optimization-learning approaches provide a promising pathway for robust, data-efficient portfolio optimization in low-data and regime-changing settings; the framework's use of synthetic data and teacher-student labeling demonstrates competitive performance and stability advantages.

title: Fun-TSG: A Function-Driven Multivariate Time Series Generator with Variable-Level Anomaly Labeling
summary: Reliable evaluation of anomaly detection methods in multivariate time series remains an open challenge, largely due to the limitations of existing benchmark datasets. Current resources often lack fine-grained anomaly annotations, do not provide explicit intervariable and temporal dependencies, and offer little insight into the underlying generative mechanisms. These shortcomings hinder the development and rigorous comparison of detection models, especially those targeting interpretable and variable-specific outputs. To address this gap, we introduce Fun-TSG, a fully customizable time series generator designed to support high-quality evaluation of anomaly detection systems. Our tool enables both fully automated generation, based on randomly sampled dependency structures and anomaly types, and manual generation through user-defined equations and anomaly configurations. In both cases, it provides full transparency over the data generation process, including access to ground-truth anomaly labels at the variable and timestamp levels. Fun-TSG supports the creation of diverse, interpretable, and reproducible benchmarking scenarios, enabling fine-grained performance analysis for both classical and modern anomaly detection models.

AI.tldr: A customizable time-series generator (Fun-TSG) for anomaly detection benchmarking, enabling automated and manual data generation with ground-truth labels at the variable and timestamp level to support fine-grained evaluation.
AI.motivation: Benchmark datasets for multivariate time series anomaly detection suffer from coarse annotations, missing inter-variable and temporal dependencies, and opaque generative mechanisms, hindering fair comparison and interpretability.
AI.method: Two-generation modes: automated generation using randomly sampled dependency structures and anomaly types, and manual generation via user-defined equations and anomaly configurations; full transparency of data generation with ground-truth labels at variable and timestamp levels; supports diverse, interpretable, and reproducible benchmarking for both classical and modern anomaly detection models.
AI.result: Conceptual framework introduced; the abstract does not report empirical results or experiments.
AI.conclusion: Fun-TSG has the potential to standardize benchmarking and improve interpretability and reproducibility in anomaly detection for multivariate time series if implemented.

title: BIEVR-LIO: Robust LiDAR-Inertial Odometry through Bump-Image-Enhanced Voxel Maps
summary: Reliable odometry is essential for mobile robots as they increasingly enter more challenging environments, which often contain little information to constrain point cloud registration, resulting in degraded LiDAR-Inertial Odometry (LIO) accuracy or even divergence. To address this, we present BIEVR-LIO, a novel approach designed specifically to exploit subtle variations in the available geometry for improved robustness. We propose a high-resolution map representation that stores surfaces as compact voxel-wise oriented height images. This representation can directly be used for registration without the calculation of intermediate geometric primitives while still supporting efficient updates. Since informative geometry is often sparsely distributed in the environment, we further propose a map-informed point sampling strategy to focus registration on geometrically informative regions, improving robustness in uninformative environments while reducing computational cost compared to global high-resolution sampling. Experiments across multiple sensors, platforms, and environments demonstrates state-of-the-art performance in well-constrained scenes and substantial improvements in challenging scenarios where baseline methods diverge. Additionally, we demonstrate that the fine-grained geometry captured by BIEVR-LIO can be used for downstream tasks such as elevation mapping for robot locomotion.

AI.tldr: BIEVR-LIO introduces a high-resolution voxel-based oriented height image map to represent surfaces for LiDAR-Inertial Odometry, coupled with a map-informed point sampling strategy to focus registration on informative geometry, improving robustness in uninformative environments and enabling downstream tasks like elevation mapping.
AI.motivation: Odometry in challenging, information-poor environments suffers from unreliable point-cloud constraints. A robust representation and selective registration can improve accuracy and prevent divergence without excessive computation.
AI.method: Propose BIEVR-LIO with a compact voxel-wise oriented height image map for direct, primitive-free registration; implement a map-informed sampling strategy to concentrate computations on geometrically informative regions; allow efficient updates of the high-resolution map.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance in well-constrained scenes and substantial improvements in challenging, divergence-prone scenarios; demonstrates usefulness of the dense geometry representation for downstream tasks such as elevation mapping.
AI.conclusion: A geometry-aware, high-resolution map and targeted sampling yield robustness and accuracy in LIO across diverse environments, with potential benefits for additional robotics tasks that rely on detailed surface geometry.

title: Towards Verified and Targeted Explanations through Formal Methods
comment: Paper has been accepted at JAIR
summary: As deep neural networks are deployed in safety-critical domains such as autonomous driving and medical diagnosis, stakeholders need explanations that are interpretable but also trustworthy with formal guarantees. Existing XAI methods fall short: heuristic attribution techniques (e.g., LIME, Integrated Gradients) highlight influential features but offer no mathematical guarantees about decision boundaries, while formal methods verify robustness yet remain untargeted, analyzing the nearest boundary regardless of whether it represents a critical risk. In safety-critical systems, not all misclassifications carry equal consequences; confusing a "Stop" sign for a "60 kph" sign is far more dangerous than confusing it with a "No Passing" sign. We introduce ViTaX (Verified and Targeted Explanations), a formal XAI framework that generates targeted semifactual explanations with mathematical guarantees. For a given input (class y) and a user-specified critical alternative (class t), ViTaX: (1) identifies the minimal feature subset most sensitive to the y->t transition, and (2) applies formal reachability analysis to guarantee that perturbing these features by epsilon cannot flip the classification to t. We formalize this through Targeted epsilon-Robustness, certifying whether a feature subset remains robust under perturbation toward a specific target class. ViTaX is the first method to provide formally guaranteed explanations of a model's resilience against user-identified alternatives. Evaluations on MNIST, GTSRB, EMNIST, and TaxiNet demonstrate over 30% fidelity improvement with minimal explanation cardinality.

AI.tldr: ViTaX provides targeted, formally guaranteed explanations by combining feature subset sensitivity analysis with reachability-based robustness against a user-specified target class.
AI.motivation: In safety-critical domains, explanations must be interpretable and mathematically trustworthy. Heuristic XAI methods offer no guarantees, while untargeted formal methods miss critical risks tied to specific misclassifications.
AI.method: Select the minimal feature subset most sensitive to the y→t transition; apply formal reachability analysis to prove that perturbing these features by epsilon cannot cause the model to output the target class t. Formalize this as Targeted epsilon-Robustness to certify robustness toward a user-identified alternative.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation showing over 30% fidelity improvement with minimal explanation size across MNIST, GTSRB, EMNIST, and TaxiNet datasets.
AI.conclusion: ViTaX is the first method to provide formally guaranteed, targeted explanations of a model’s resilience against user-identified alternative classes, demonstrating promise for safety-critical applications with concise explanations.

title: Interpretable and Explainable Surrogate Modeling for Simulations: A State-of-the-Art Survey and Perspectives on Explainable AI for Decision-Making
comment: Accepted for publication in Archives of Computational Methods in Engineering, 2026, ID d9d36aab-3723-4a70-b2ce-166435179528
summary: The simulation of complex systems increasingly relies on sophisticated but fundamentally opaque computational black-box simulators. Surrogate models play a central role in reducing the computational cost of complex systems simulations across a wide range of scientific and engineering domains. Notwithstanding, they inevitably inherit and often exacerbate this black-box nature, obscuring how input variables drive physical responses. Conversely, Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) offers powerful tools to unpack these models. Yet, XAI methods struggle with engineering-specific constraints, such as highly correlated inputs, dynamical systems, and rigorous reliability requirements. Consequently, surrogate modeling and XAI have largely evolved as distinct fields of research, despite their strong complementarity. To reconnect these approaches, this state-of-the-art survey provides a structured perspective that maps existing XAI techniques onto the various stages of surrogate modeling workflows for design and exploration. To ground this synthesis, we draw upon illustrative applications across both equation-based simulations and agent-based modeling. We survey a broad spectrum of techniques, highlighting their strengths for revealing interactions and supporting human comprehension. Finally, we identify pressing open challenges, including the explainability of dynamical systems and the handling of mixed-variable systems, and propose a research agenda to make explainability a core, embedded element of simulation-driven workflows from model construction through decision-making. By transforming opaque emulators into explainable tools, this agenda empowers practitioners to move beyond accelerating simulations to extracting actionable insights from complex system behaviors.

AI.tldr: A survey bridging surrogate modeling and XAI to make black-box simulators explainable across equation-based and agent-based models; maps XAI techniques to surrogate workflows, outlines challenges, and proposes an integrated research agenda.
AI.motivation: Surrogate models reduce computational cost but remain opaque; XAI can reveal input–output mechanisms, yet engineering constraints complicate applying XAI; integrating both enhances understanding and decision-making in simulations.
AI.method: Literature survey and synthesis; develops a structured mapping from XAI methods to stages of surrogate modeling; uses illustrative applications; discusses a broad spectrum of techniques.
AI.result: A taxonomy of techniques with strengths for revealing interactions; identification of open challenges (dynamical systems explainability, mixed-variable handling); proposed research agenda to embed explainability within simulation-driven workflows.
AI.conclusion: Explainability should be core in simulation pipelines to transform emulators into actionable insights, enabling better design and decision-making in complex systems.

title: CooperDrive: Enhancing Driving Decisions Through Cooperative Perception
summary: Autonomous vehicles equipped with robust onboard perception, localization, and planning still face limitations in occlusion and non-line-of-sight (NLOS) scenarios, where delayed reactions can increase collision risk. We propose CooperDrive, a cooperative perception framework that augments situational awareness and enables earlier, safer driving decisions. CooperDrive offers two key advantages: (i) each vehicle retains its native perception, localization, and planning stack, and (ii) a lightweight object-level sharing and fusion strategy bridges perception and planning. Specifically, CooperDrive reuses detector Bird's-Eye View (BEV) features to estimate accurate vehicle poses without additional heavy encoders, thereby reconstructing BEV representations and feeding the planner with low latency. On the planning side, CooperDrive leverages the expanded object set to anticipate potential conflicts earlier and adjust speed and trajectory proactively, thereby transforming reactive behaviors into predictive and safer driving decisions. Real-world closed-loop tests at occlusion-heavy NLOS intersections demonstrate that CooperDrive increases reaction lead time, minimum time-to-collision (TTC), and stopping margin, while requiring only 90 kbps bandwidth and maintaining an average end-to-end latency of 89 ms.

AI.tldr: CooperDrive is a cooperative perception framework that preserves each vehicle’s own stack while sharing object-level information to extend awareness and enable early, proactive planning with low latency and modest bandwidth.
AI.motivation: Occlusion and non-line-of-sight (NLOS) scenarios increase collision risk due to delayed reactions; existing onboard perception alone is insufficient in these conditions.
AI.method: Reuse detector BEV features to estimate vehicle poses without heavy encoders, reconstruct BEV representations, perform lightweight object-level sharing/fusion to inform planning, and allow the planner to anticipate conflicts earlier for proactive control.
AI.result: Real-world closed-loop tests at occlusion-heavy NLOS intersections show increased reaction lead time, higher minimum time-to-collision (TTC), and larger stopping margins, with only 90 kbps bandwidth and an average end-to-end latency of 89 ms.
AI.conclusion: CooperDrive demonstrates safer, proactive driving under challenging occlusions with low bandwidth and low latency, while preserving each vehicle’s native perception-planning stack.

title: Interpretable Human Activity Recognition for Subtle Robbery Detection in Surveillance Videos
summary: Non-violent street robberies (snatch-and-run) are difficult to detect automatically because they are brief, subtle, and often indistinguishable from benign human interactions in unconstrained surveillance footage. This paper presents a hybrid, pose-driven approach for detecting snatch-and-run events that combines real-time perception with an interpretable classification stage suitable for edge deployment. The system uses a YOLO-based pose estimator to extract body keypoints for each tracked person and computes kinematic and interaction features describing hand speed, arm extension, proximity, and relative motion between an aggressor-victim pair. A Random Forest classifier is trained on these descriptors, and a temporal hysteresis filter is applied to stabilize frame-level predictions and reduce spurious alarms. We evaluate the method on a staged dataset and on a disjoint test set collected from internet videos, demonstrating promising generalization across different scenes and camera viewpoints. Finally, we implement the complete pipeline on an NVIDIA Jetson Nano and report real-time performance, supporting the feasibility of proactive, on-device robbery detection.

AI.tldr: A real-time, edge-deployable, pose-driven system for detecting snatch-and-run robberies using YOLO-based pose estimation, hand-crafted features, a Random Forest classifier, and a temporal hysteresis filter, evaluated on staged and internet-video datasets and implemented on Jetson Nano.
AI.motivation: Snatch-and-run events are brief, subtle, and easily confused with ordinary interactions in unconstrained surveillance; there is a need for an interpretable, on-device detection solution to enable proactive responses.
AI.method: Pipeline: (1) use YOLO-based pose estimator to extract body keypoints per tracked person; (2) compute kinematic and interaction features (hand speed, arm extension, proximity, relative motion between aggressor and victim); (3) train a Random Forest classifier on these descriptors; (4) apply a temporal hysteresis filter to stabilize frame-level predictions; (5) deploy on edge hardware (NVIDIA Jetson Nano) for real-time performance.
AI.result: The method shows promising generalization across different scenes and camera viewpoints, with real-time edge performance demonstrated on Jetson Nano on a disjoint test set from internet videos.
AI.conclusion: The proposed pose-driven, edge-deployable approach is feasible for proactive snatch-and-run detection and can operate in real-world surveillance settings with on-device computation.

title: Shapley Value-Guided Adaptive Ensemble Learning for Explainable Financial Fraud Detection with U.S. Regulatory Compliance Validation
comment: 28 pages. Submitted to Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence (Elsevier). IEEE-CIS dataset (590,540 transactions). Includes SGAE algorithm, SHAP stability evaluation, and OCC/SR 11-7 regulatory compliance mapping
summary: Financial crime costs U.S. institutions over $32 billion each year. Although AI tools for fraud detection have become more advanced, their use in real-world systems still faces a major obstacle: many of these models operate as black boxes that cannot provide the transparent, auditable explanations required by regulations such as OCC Bulletin 2011-12 and Federal Reserve SR 11-7. This study makes three main contributions. First, it offers a thorough evaluation of explanation quality across faithfulness (sufficiency and comprehensiveness at k=5, 10, and 15) and stability (Kendall's W across 30 bootstrap samples). XGBoost paired with TreeExplainer achieves near-perfect stability (W=0.9912), while LSTM with DeepExplainer shows weak results (W=0.4962). Second, the paper introduces the SHAP-Guided Adaptive Ensemble (SGAE), which dynamically adjusts per-transaction ensemble weights based on SHAP attribution agreement, achieving the highest AUC-ROC among all tested models (0.8837 held-out; 0.9245 cross-validation). Third, a complete three-architecture evaluation of LSTM, Transformer, and GNN-GraphSAGE on the full 590,540-transaction IEEE-CIS dataset is provided, with GNN-GraphSAGE achieving AUC-ROC 0.9248 and F1=0.6013. All results are mapped directly to OCC, SR 11-7, and BSA-AML regulatory compliance requirements.

AI.tldr: The abstract reports evaluation of explainability in fraud-detection models, introduces SHAP-Guided Adaptive Ensemble (SGAE) for per-transaction weighting, and compares LSTM, Transformer, and Graph Neural Network architectures on a large IEEE-CIS dataset, achieving strong AUC-ROC with XGBoost/TreeExplainer stability and GraphSAGE performance, with regulatory mapping to OCC and related guidelines.
AI.motivation: Regulatory demands require transparent, auditable explanations for fraud-detection models. Black-box models impede compliance; the work seeks to quantify explanation quality and enhance explainability without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: Assess explanation quality via faithfulness (sufficiency and comprehensiveness at k=5,10,15) and stability (Kendall's W across 30 bootstrap samples). Evaluate XGBoost+TreeExplainer, LSTM+DeepExplainer, SGAE-based ensemble weighting using SHAP attribution agreement. Conduct full-architecture comparison (LSTM, Transformer, GraphSAGE) on 590,540 transactions (IEEE-CIS) with held-out and cross-validation assessments.
AI.result: XGBoost+TreeExplainer shows near-perfect stability (W=0.9912); LSTM+DeepExplainer is weaker (W=0.4962). SGAE achieves highest held-out AUC-ROC (0.8837) and CV (0.9245). GraphSAGE (GNN) attains AUC-ROC 0.9248 and F1 0.6013 on the full dataset. All results aligned with OCC SR 11-7 and BSA-AML requirements.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that explainability-aware fraud-detection can meet regulatory standards while delivering high predictive performance, with SGAE providing adaptive, attribution-guided ensemble weighting and GraphSAGE offering state-of-the-art performance on a large-scale dataset.

title: Formalizing Kantian Ethics: Formula of the Universal Law Logic (FULL)
summary: The field of machine ethics aims to build Artificial Moral Agents (AMAs) to better understand morality and make AI agents safer. To do so, many approaches encode human moral intuition as a set of axioms on actions e.g., do not harm, you must help others. However, this introduces (at least) two limitations for future AMAs. First, it does not consider the agent's purposes in performing the action. Second, it assumes that we humans can enumerate our moral intuition. This paper explores formalizing a moral procedure that alleviates these two limitations. We specifically consider Kantian ethics and present a multi-sorted quantified modal logic we call the Formula of the Universal Law Logic (FULL). The FULL formalizes Kant's first formulation of the categorical imperative, the Formula of the Universal Law (FUL), and concepts such as causality and agency. We demonstrate on three cases from Kantian ethics that the FULL can reason to evaluate agents' actions for certain purposes without built-in moral intuition, given that it has sufficient (non-normative) background knowledge. Therefore, the FULL is a contribution towards more robust and autonomous AMAs, and a more formal understanding of Kantian ethics.

AI.tldr: Introduces a Kantian logic (FULL) to evaluate actions for purposes using background knowledge, without built-in moral intuitions, via a formalized Formula of the Universal Law Logic.
AI.motivation: Addresses two limitations of axiom-based morality: (1) ignoring the agent's purposes, and (2) assuming humans can enumerate all moral intuitions. A formal Kantian framework with background knowledge could yield more robust, autonomous AI morality.
AI.method: Develop a multi-sorted quantified modal logic (FULL) that formalizes Kant's Formula of the Universal Law (FUL), including causality and agency. Validate with three Kantian ethics case studies to show reasoning about actions for given purposes using non-normative background knowledge.
AI.result: Shows that FULL can reason about agents' actions for specific purposes without built-in moral intuition, given sufficient background knowledge. Represents a step toward robust autonomous AMAs and a formal understanding of Kantian ethics.
AI.conclusion: FULL contributes to more robust AMAs and formal Kantian ethics understanding, enabling purpose-aware moral evaluation. Future work should address grounds for background knowledge, interpretability, and handling of conflicting duties or ambiguities.

title: A Nonasymptotic Theory of Gain-Dependent Error Dynamics in Behavior Cloning
summary: Behavior cloning (BC) policies on position-controlled robots inherit the closed-loop response of the underlying PD controller, yet the effect of controller gains on BC failure lacks a nonasymptotic theory. We show that independent sub-Gaussian action errors propagate through the gain-dependent closed-loop dynamics to yield sub-Gaussian position errors whose proxy matrix $X_\infty(K)$ governs the failure tail. The probability of horizon-$T$ task failure factorizes into a gain-dependent amplification index $Γ_T(K)$ and the validation loss plus a generalization slack, so training loss alone cannot predict closed-loop performance. Under shape-preserving upper-bound structural assumptions the proxy admits the scalar bound $X_\infty(K)\preceqΨ(K)\bar X$ with $Ψ(K)$ decomposed into label difficulty, injection strength, and contraction, ranking the four canonical regimes with compliant-overdamped (CO) tightest, stiff-underdamped (SU) loosest, and the stiff-overdamped versus compliant-underdamped ordering system-dependent. For the canonical scalar second-order PD system the closed-form continuous-time stationary variance $X_\infty^{\mathrm{c}}(α,β)=σ^2α/(2β)$ is strictly monotone in stiffness and damping over the entire stable orthant, covering both underdamped and overdamped regimes, and the exact zero-order-hold (ZOH) discretization inherits this monotonicity. The analysis provides the first nonasymptotic explanation of the empirical finding that compliant, overdamped controllers improve BC success rates.

AI.tldr: Nonasymptotic theory links PD gains to BC failure via a gain-dependent proxy X∞(K); failure probability factors into an amplification index and validation loss; provides structural bounds and regime rankings; for second-order PD, the continuous-time variance X∞^c(α,β)=σ²α/(2β) is monotone in stiffness and damping, and discretization preserves this; explains why compliant, overdamped controllers improve BC success rates.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of nonasymptotic theory connecting PD controller gains to behavior cloning failure on position-controlled robots, specifically how gain-dependent closed-loop dynamics affect tail probabilities.
AI.method: Model action errors as independent sub-Gaussians; derive a gain-dependent proxy X∞(K) for closed-loop position errors; show horizon-T failure probability factorizes into Γ_T(K) and a validation loss with generalization slack; propose a shape-preserving upper-bound X∞(K) ≼ Ψ(K) X̄ with Ψ(K) decomposed into label difficulty, injection strength, and contraction; analyze canonical regimes and discretization; derive closed-form variance for a second-order PD system.
AI.result: Failure probability decomposes into a gain-dependent amplification and data-driven error; a scalar bound ranks the four regimes (CO tightest, SU loosest); monotone variance in the canonical PD system across both underdamped and overdamped regimes; ZOH discretization preserves monotonicity; provides nonasymptotic explanation for better BC success with compliant overdamped controllers.
AI.conclusion: The work delivers the first nonasymptotic explanation for how compliant, overdamped controllers improve BC performance and offers a structured bound framework to guide gain selection for reducing closed-loop BC failures.

title: SatBLIP: Context Understanding and Feature Identification from Satellite Imagery with Vision-Language Learning
summary: Rural environmental risks are shaped by place-based conditions (e.g., housing quality, road access, land-surface patterns), yet standard vulnerability indices are coarse and provide limited insight into risk contexts. We propose SatBLIP, a satellite-specific vision-language framework for rural context understanding and feature identification that predicts county-level Social Vulnerability Index (SVI). SatBLIP addresses limitations of prior remote sensing pipelines-handcrafted features, manual virtual audits, and natural-image-trained VLMs-by coupling contrastive image-text alignment with bootstrapped captioning tailored to satellite semantics. We use GPT-4o to generate structured descriptions of satellite tiles (roof type/condition, house size, yard attributes, greenery, and road context), then fine-tune a satellite-adapted BLIP model to generate captions for unseen images. Captions are encoded with CLIP and fused with LLM-derived embeddings via attention for SVI estimation under spatial aggregation. Using SHAP, we identify salient attributes (e.g., roof form/condition, street width, vegetation, cars/open space) that consistently drive robust predictions, enabling interpretable mapping of rural risk environments.

AI.tldr: SatBLIP is a satellite-aware vision-language framework that combines GPT-4o-generated structured satellite descriptions with a satellite-tuned BLIP model and CLIP/LLM fusion to predict county-level SVI, with SHAP-based interpretation of rural risk factors.
AI.motivation: Address coarse, coarse-grained vulnerability indices and shortcomings of prior remote-sensing pipelines (handcrafted features, manual audits, and VLMs trained on natural images) by enabling context-aware, interpretable rural risk mapping from satellite data.
AI.method: GPT-4o generates structured tile descriptions (roof type/condition, house size, yard attributes, greenery, road context). A satellite-adapted BLIP model is fine-tuned to produce captions for unseen images. Captions are encoded with CLIP and fused with embeddings from a large language model via attention. SVI is estimated with spatial aggregation. SHAP identifies salient features driving predictions.
AI.result: Identified salient rural-risk attributes (roof form/condition, street width, vegetation, cars/open space) that consistently drive robust SVI predictions; enables interpretable mapping of rural risk environments.
AI.conclusion: Combines satellite semantics with vision-language modeling to yield interpretable, geographically-resolved vulnerability estimates, addressing prior limitations and revealing key contextual drivers.

title: Explainable Graph Neural Networks for Interbank Contagion Surveillance: A Regulatory-Aligned Framework for the U.S. Banking Sector
comment: 28 pages, submitted to Research in International Business and Finance (RIBAF)
summary: The Spatial-Temporal Graph Attention Network (ST-GAT) framework was created to serve as an explainable GNN-based solution for detecting bank distress early warning signs and for conducting macro-prudential surveillance of the interbank system in the United States. The ST-GAT framework models 8,103 FDIC insured institutions across 58 quarterly snapshots (2010Q1-2024Q2). Bilateral exposures were reconstructed from publicly available FDIC Call Reports using maximum entropy estimation to produce a dynamic directed weighted graph. The framework achieves the highest AUPRC among all GNN architectures (0.939 +/- 0.010), trailing only XGBoost (0.944). Ablation analysis confirms the BiLSTM temporal component contributes +0.020 AUPRC; temporal attention weights exhibit a monotonically decreasing pattern consistent with long-run structural vulnerability weighting. Permutation importance identifies ROA (0.309) and NPL Ratio (0.252) as dominant predictors, consistent with post-mortem analyses of the 2023 regional banking crisis. All data are publicly available FDIC Call Reports and FRED series; all code and results are released.

AI.tldr: ST-GAT uses a dynamic directed weighted graph of 8,103 banks over 58 quarterly snapshots to predict distress. It achieves top AUPRC among GNNs (0.939 ± 0.010), just below XGBoost (0.944); BiLSTM temporal component adds ~0.020 AUPRC; ROA and NPL ratio are dominant predictors; data and code are publicly released.
AI.motivation: Address the need for an explainable, graph-based early warning framework for bank distress and macro-prudential surveillance, leveraging dynamic interbank exposures inferred from regulatory data.
AI.method: Spatial-Temporal Graph Attention Network (ST-GAT) applied to a dynamic directed weighted graph constructed from bilateral exposures via maximum entropy estimation, spanning 8,103 institutions and 58 quarterly snapshots. Includes a BiLSTM temporal component, temporal attention, ablation analyses, and permutation importance.
AI.result: AUPRC of 0.939 ± 0.010 for ST-GAT among GNNs, slightly below XGBoost at 0.944. Ablation shows BiLSTM contributes +0.020 AUPRC. Temporal attention weights decline monotonically, consistent with long-run vulnerability weighting. ROA (0.309) and NPL Ratio (0.252) are top predictors. All data are publicly available FDIC Call Reports and FRED series; code and results released.
