AI.conclusion: ST-GAT provides competitive, interpretable performance for bank distress early warning and macro-prudential surveillance, with transparent predictors and reproducible results that align with post-mortem analyses of the 2023 regional banking crisis.

title: GFT: From Imitation to Reward Fine-Tuning with Unbiased Group Advantages and Dynamic Coefficient Rectification
summary: Large language models are typically post-trained using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL), yet effectively unifying efficient knowledge injection with robust generalization remains challenging. In this work, we provide a training-dynamics analysis showing that SFT can be interpreted as a special case of policy gradient optimization with an extremely sparse implicit reward and unstable inverse-probability weighting, which together lead to single-path dependency, entropy collapse, and gradient explosion. Motivated by this diagnosis, we propose Group Fine-Tuning (GFT), a unified post-training framework that addresses these intrinsic limitations through two mechanisms: Group Advantage Learning, which constructs diverse response groups and derives normalized contrastive supervision to alleviate reward sparsity, and Dynamic Coefficient Rectification, which adaptively bounds inverse-probability weights to stabilize optimization while preserving efficient knowledge injection. Experiments demonstrate that GFT consistently surpasses SFT-based methods and yields policies that integrate more smoothly with subsequent RL training.

AI.tldr: SFT is reframed as degenerate policy-gradient with sparse reward and unstable importance weights; Group Fine-Tuning (GFT) uses Group Advantage Learning and Dynamic Coefficient Rectification to stabilize optimization and diversify knowledge injection, improving RL integration.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between efficient knowledge injection and robust generalization in language models by diagnosing SFT's training dynamics and proposing a unified post-training framework.
AI.method: Propose Group Fine-Tuning (GFT) with two components: (1) Group Advantage Learning to create diverse response groups and provide normalized contrastive supervision to mitigate reward sparsity; (2) Dynamic Coefficient Rectification to adaptively bound inverse-probability weights, stabilizing optimization while preserving knowledge injection.
AI.result: Empirical evidence shows GFT consistently outperforms SFT-based methods and yields policies that integrate more smoothly with subsequent RL training.
AI.conclusion: GFT offers a unified post-training framework that mitigates intrinsic SFT limitations, enhancing knowledge injection, generalization, and downstream RL compatibility.

title: CT-VIR: Continuous-Time Visual-Inertial-Ranging Fusion for Indoor Localization with Sparse Anchors
summary: Visual-inertial odometry (VIO) is widely used for mobile robot localization, but its long-term accuracy degrades without global constraints. Incorporating ranging sensors such as ultra-wideband (UWB) can mitigate drift; however, high-accuracy ranging usually requires well-deployed anchors, which is difficult to ensure in narrow or low-power environments. Moreover, most existing visual-inertial-ranging (VIR) fusion methods rely on discrete time-based filtering or optimization, making it difficult to balance positioning accuracy, trajectory consistency, and fusion efficiency under asynchronous multi-sensor sampling. To address these issues, we propose a spline-based continuous-time state estimation method for VIR fusion localization. In the preprocessing stage, VIO motion priors and UWB ranging measurements are used to construct virtual anchors and reject outliers, thereby alleviating geometric degeneration and improving range reliability. In the estimation stage, the pose trajectory is parameterized in continuous time using a B-spline, while inertial, visual, and ranging constraints are formulated as factors in a sliding-window graph. The spline control points, together with a small set of auxiliary parameters, are then jointly optimized to obtain a continuous-time trajectory estimate. Evaluations on public datasets and real-world experiments demonstrate the effectiveness and practical potential of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: Continuous-time visual-inertial-ranging fusion using a B-spline trajectory representation and virtual anchors to mitigate drift under asynchronous sensor sampling.
AI.motivation: Long-term drift in VIO and geometric degeneration; reliance on well-deployed UWB anchors is impractical in constrained environments; need robust fusion for asynchronous multi-sensor data.
AI.method: Preprocess with VIO priors and UWB to build virtual anchors and reject outliers. Represent pose as a B-spline in continuous time; formulate inertial, visual, and ranging constraints as factors in a sliding-window graph; jointly optimize spline control points and auxiliary parameters.
AI.result: Validated on public datasets and real-world experiments, showing effectiveness and practical potential in improving accuracy, consistency, and robustness under challenging conditions.
AI.conclusion: A practical continuous-time VIR fusion framework that reduces reliance on dense anchor deployments, handles asynchronous sensor data, and improves long-term localization performance.

title: FoodSense: A Multisensory Food Dataset and Benchmark for Predicting Taste, Smell, Texture, and Sound from Images
summary: Humans routinely infer taste, smell, texture, and even sound from food images a phenomenon well studied in cognitive science. However, prior vision language research on food has focused primarily on recognition tasks such as meal identification, ingredient detection, and nutrition estimation. Image-based prediction of multisensory experience remains largely unexplored. We introduce FoodSense, a human-annotated dataset for cross-sensory inference containing 66,842 participant-image pairs across 2,987 unique food images. Each pair includes numeric ratings (1-5) and free-text descriptors for four sensory dimensions: taste, smell, texture, and sound. To enable models to both predict and explain sensory expectations, we expand short human annotations into image-grounded reasoning traces. A large language model generates visual justifications conditioned on the image, ratings, and descriptors. Using these annotations, we train FoodSense-VL, a vision language benchmark model to produce both multisensory ratings and grounded explanations directly from food images. This work connects cognitive science findings on cross-sensory perception with modern instruction tuning for multimodal models and shows that many popular evaluation metrics are insufficient for visually sensory inference.

AI.tldr: A new cross-sensory dataset and baseline model for predicting multisensory experiences from food images. FoodSense provides 66,842 image–participant pairs over 2,987 images with 4 sensory dimensions (taste, smell, texture, sound) (1–5 ratings and free-text descriptors); it extends annotations to image-grounded reasoning traces via LLMs and introduces FoodSense-VL, a vision–language model that predicts ratings and grounded explanations, highlighting evaluation metric gaps for visually sensory inference.
AI.motivation: Bridge cognitive science findings on cross-sensory perception with modern multimodal AI, moving beyond recognition tasks in food vision to predict and explain multisensory experiences from images.
AI.method: 1) Assemble FoodSense dataset with 66,842 participant–image pairs across 2,987 food images, each with numeric ratings (1–5) and free-text descriptors for taste, smell, texture, and sound. 2) Expand short annotations into image-grounded reasoning traces by generating visual justifications with a large language model conditioned on the image, ratings, and descriptors. 3) Train FoodSense-VL, a vision–language benchmark model, to output multisensory ratings and grounded explanations directly from food images. 4) Analyze evaluation metrics and demonstrate gaps in existing metrics for visually inferred sensory data.
AI.result: A large, richly annotated dataset for cross-sensory inference is introduced (FoodSense) and a multimodal model (FoodSense-VL) is trained to predict multisensory ratings and provide grounded explanations from images; the work also shows current evaluation metrics are insufficient for evaluating visually inferred sensory content.
AI.conclusion: The study links cognitive science on cross-sensory perception with multimodal instruction tuning, providing resources for future research while highlighting the need for improved evaluation protocols tailored to visually inferred sensory experiences.

title: Graph-Based Fraud Detection with Dual-Path Graph Filtering
summary: Fraud detection on graph data can be viewed as a demanding task that requires distinguishing between different types of nodes. Because graph neural networks (GNNs) are naturally suited for processing information encoded in graph form through their message-passing operations, methods based on GNN models have increasingly attracted attention in the fraud detection domain. However, fraud graphs inherently exhibit relation camouflage, high heterophily, and class imbalance, causing most GNNs to underperform in fraud detection tasks. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Graph-Based Fraud Detection Model with Dual-Path Graph Filtering (DPF-GFD). DPF-GFD first applies a beta wavelet-based operator to the original graph to capture key structural patterns. It then constructs a similarity graph from distance-based node representations and applies an improved low-pass filter. The embeddings from the original and similarity graphs are fused through supervised representation learning to obtain node features, which are finally used by an ensemble tree model to assess the fraud risk of unlabeled nodes. Unlike existing single-graph smoothing approaches, DPF-GFD introduces a frequency-complementary dual-path filtering paradigm tailored for fraud detection, explicitly decoupling structural anomaly modeling and feature similarity modeling. This design enables more discriminative and stable node representations in highly heterophilous and imbalanced fraud graphs. Comprehensive experiments on four real-world financial fraud detection datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method.

AI.tldr: A dual-path graph filtering method (DPF-GFD) for fraud detection. It uses a beta wavelet operator on the original graph and a distance-based similarity graph with an enhanced low-pass filter; embeddings from both are fused via supervised learning and fed to an ensemble tree classifier, achieving robust performance in highly heterophilous and imbalanced fraud graphs.
AI.motivation: Fraud graphs exhibit camouflage, high heterophily, and class imbalance, which degrade GNN performance. There is a need for representations that separately capture structural anomalies and feature similarity to improve fraud detection.
AI.method: 1) Apply a beta wavelet-based operator to the original graph to extract structural patterns. 2) Build a similarity graph from distance-based node representations and apply an enhanced low-pass filter. 3) Fuse embeddings from the two graphs through supervised representation learning. 4) Use an ensemble tree model to estimate fraud risk of unlabeled nodes.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four real-world financial fraud datasets shows the proposed method improves detection performance, yielding more discriminative and stable node representations under high heterophily and class imbalance.
AI.conclusion: A frequency-complementary dual-path filtering paradigm decouples structural anomaly modeling from feature similarity modeling, enabling robust fraud detection on heterogeneous and imbalanced graphs; the approach outperforms single-graph smoothing baselines.

title: Seeing Through Experts Eyes A Foundational Vision Language Model Trained on Radiologists Gaze and Reasoning
summary: Large scale vision language models have shown promise in automating chest Xray interpretation, yet their clinical utility remains limited by a gap between model outputs and radiologist reasoning. Most systems optimize for semantic information without emulating how experts visually examine medical images, often overlooking critical findings or diverging from established diagnostic workflows. Radiologists follow structured protocols (e.g., the ABCDEF approach) that ensure all clinically relevant regions are systematically examined, reducing missed findings and supporting reliable diagnostic reasoning. We introduce GazeX, a vision language model that leverages radiologists' eye tracking data as a behavioral prior to model expert diagnostic reasoning. By incorporating gaze trajectories and fixation patterns into pretraining, GazeX learns to follow the spatial and temporal structure of radiologist attention and integrates observations in a clinically meaningful sequence. Using a curated dataset of over 30,000 gaze key frames from five radiologists, we demonstrate that GazeX produces more accurate, interpretable, and expert consistent outputs across radiology report generation, disease grounding, and visual question answering, utilizing 231,835 radiographic studies, 780,014 question answer pairs, and 1,162 image sentence pairs with bounding boxes. Unlike autonomous reporting systems, GazeX produces verifiable evidence artifacts, including inspection trajectories and finding linked localized regions, enabling efficient human verification and safe human AI collaboration. Learning through expert eyes provides a practical route toward more trustworthy, explainable, and diagnostically robust AI systems for radiology and beyond.

AI.tldr: GazeX leverages radiologist eye-tracking data as a behavioral prior to guide a vision-language model in chest X-ray interpretation, improving accuracy, interpretability, and expert-consistency across report generation, disease grounding, and visual question answering, with verifiable evidence artifacts.
AI.motivation: To close the gap between AI outputs and radiologist reasoning by encoding expert visual inspection processes and structured protocols (e.g., ABCDEF), thereby reducing missed findings and increasing trust and safety in radiology AI.
AI.method: Pretrain a vision-language model with radiologist gaze trajectories and fixation patterns as a behavioral prior. The approach uses a curated dataset of ~30,000 gaze key frames from five radiologists and integrates spatial-temporal gaze information into model reasoning. Evaluations span radiology report generation, disease grounding, and visual question answering, leveraging 231,835 radiographic studies, 780,014 QA pairs, and 1,162 image-sentence pairs with bounding boxes. Outputs include inspection trajectories and localized finding links to support verification.
AI.result: GazeX yields more accurate, interpretable, and expert-consistent outputs across tasks, and generates verifiable evidence artifacts (inspection trajectories and linked localization) that enable efficient human verification and safe human-AI collaboration.
AI.conclusion: Learning from expert gaze provides a practical route toward more trustworthy, explainable, and diagnostically robust AI systems for radiology and beyond.

title: Model-Based Reinforcement Learning Exploits Passive Body Dynamics for High-Performance Biped Robot Locomotion
summary: Embodiment is a significant keyword in recent machine learning fields. This study focused on the passive nature of the body of a biped robot to generate walking and running locomotion using model-based deep reinforcement learning. We constructed two models in a simulator, one with passive elements (e.g., springs) and the other, which is similar to general humanoids, without passive elements. The training of the model with passive elements was highly affected by the attractor of the system. This lead that although the trajectories quickly converged to limit cycles, it took a long time to obtain large rewards. However, thanks to the attractor-driven learning, the acquired locomotion was robust and energy-efficient. The results revealed that robots with passive elements could efficiently acquire high-performance locomotion by utilizing stable limit cycles generated through dynamic interaction between the body and ground. This study demonstrates the importance of implementing passive properties in the body for future embodied AI.

AI.tldr: Passive body elements in a biped robot, trained with model-based DRL, yield robust, energy-efficient locomotion via attractor-driven limit cycles, though achieving high rewards may require longer training.
AI.motivation: To understand how passive body dynamics influence learning and locomotion performance in embodied AI.
AI.method: Simulator-based study comparing two humanoid-like models—one with passive elements (springs) and one without—trained using model-based deep reinforcement learning, evaluating convergence, rewards, robustness, and energy efficiency.
AI.result: The passive-model harnessed attractor dynamics to achieve robust, energy-efficient locomotion through stable limit cycles. Trajectories quickly converged, but attaining large rewards took more training time; the non-passive model did not exhibit the same attractor-driven benefits.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating passive properties into the body can enhance locomotion learning and efficiency, highlighting the importance of passive body design for embodied AI.

title: Zero-Ablation Overstates Register Content Dependence in DINO Vision Transformers
comment: 12 pages, 10 figures, to be published in CVPR 2026 HOW Vision Interpretability Workshop Proceedings
summary: Zero-ablation -- replacing token activations with zero vectors -- is widely used to probe token function in vision transformers. Register zeroing in DINOv2+registers and DINOv3 produces large drops (up to $-36.6$\,pp classification, $-30.9$\,pp segmentation), suggesting registers are functionally indispensable. However, three replacement controls -- mean-substitution, noise-substitution, and cross-image register-shuffling -- preserve performance across classification, correspondence, and segmentation, remaining within ${\sim}1$\,pp of the unmodified baseline. Per-patch cosine similarity shows these replacements genuinely perturb internal representations, while zeroing causes disproportionately large perturbations, consistent with why it alone degrades tasks. We conclude that zero-ablation overstates dependence on exact register content. In the frozen-feature evaluations we test, performance depends on plausible register-like activations rather than on exact image-specific values. Registers nevertheless buffer dense features from \texttt{[CLS]} dependence and are associated with compressed patch geometry. These findings, including the replacement-control results, replicate at ViT-B scale.

AI.tldr: Zero-ablation overestimates dependence on exact register content in vision transformers; replacement controls preserve performance, implying registers buffer features rather than storing exact information; findings replicate at ViT-B.
AI.motivation: Clarify what zero-ablation reveals about register function in vision transformers and provide robust probes using replacement controls; assess across tasks and in frozen-feature regime; evaluate scale to ViT-B.
AI.method: Apply zero-ablation to registers in DINOv2+ and DINOv3; compare with three replacement controls (mean-substitution, noise-substitution, cross-image register-shuffling); measure task performance across classification, segmentation, and correspondence; analyze per-patch cosine similarity; test frozen-feature evaluations; replicate at ViT-B scale.
AI.result: Zero-ablation yields large performance drops; replacement controls keep performance within ~1 percentage point; per-patch cosine similarity confirms perturbations; zeroing causes disproportionate perturbation; registers buffer [CLS]-dependence and relate to patch geometry; results hold at ViT-B scale.
AI.conclusion: Zero-ablation is an overly aggressive probe of register importance; plausible register-like activations suffice for performance; use replacement controls for robust interpretation; findings extend to larger ViT scales.

title: TOPCELL: Topology Optimization of Standard Cell via LLMs
comment: Accepted to the 63rd ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC 2026). 7 pages, 4 figures
summary: Transistor topology optimization is a critical step in standard cell design, directly dictating diffusion sharing efficiency and downstream routability. However, identifying optimal topologies remains a persistent bottleneck, as conventional exhaustive search methods become computationally intractable with increasing circuit complexity in advanced nodes. This paper introduces TOPCELL, a novel and scalable framework that reformulates high-dimensional topology exploration as a generative task using Large Language Models (LLMs). We employ Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to fine-tune the model, aligning its topology optimization strategy with logical (circuit) and spatial (layout) constraints. Experimental results within an industrial flow targeting an advanced 2nm technology node demonstrate that TOPCELL significantly outperforms foundation models in discovering routable, physically-aware topologies. When integrated into a state-of-the-art (SOTA) automation flow for a 7nm library generation task, TOPCELL exhibits robust zero-shot generalization and matches the layout quality of exhaustive solvers while achieving an 85.91x speedup.

AI.tldr: A scalable, LLM-based framework (TOPCELL) for transistor topology optimization that treats design exploration as a generative task, achieving fast, routable, physics-aware topologies via GRPO fine-tuning, outperforming baselines and showing strong zero-shot generalization with major speedup.
AI.motivation: Exhaustive search for transistor topology is computationally intractable at advanced nodes; need scalable methods to find routable, physically consistent topologies within industrial flows.
AI.method: TOPCELL formulates topology search as a generative task using LLMs; fine-tunes with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to enforce circuit-level and layout constraints; demonstrates with 2nm industrial flow and 7nm library generation, focusing on routability and layout compatibility.
AI.result: Outperforms foundation models in discovering routable, physically-aware topologies; integrates into a SOTA automation flow, shows zero-shot generalization, matches exhaustive solvers in layout quality, and achieves 85.91x speedup.
AI.conclusion: TOPCELL provides a scalable, effective approach for transistor topology optimization using LLMs that preserves layout quality while drastically reducing search time, with strong potential for industrial adoption.

title: Mistake gating leads to energy and memory efficient continual learning
summary: Synaptic plasticity is metabolically expensive, yet animals continuously update their internal models without exhausting energy reserves. However, when artificial neural networks are trained, the network parameters are typically updated on every sample that is presented, even if the sample was classified correctly. Inspired by the human negativity bias and error-related negativity, we propose 'memorized mistake-gated learning' -- a biologically plausible plasticity rule where synaptic updates are strictly gated by current and past classification errors. This reduces the number of updates the network needs to make by $50\%\sim80\%$. Mistake gating is particularly well suited in two cases: 1) For incremental learning where new knowledge is acquired on a background of pre-existing knowledge, 2) For online learning scenarios when data needs to be stored for later replay, as mistake-gating reduces storage buffer requirements. The algorithm can be implemented in a few lines of code, adds no hyper-parameters, and comes at negligible computational overhead. Learning on mistakes is an energy efficient and biologically relevant modification to commonly used learning rules that is well suited for continual learning.

AI.tldr: Mistake-gated learning: updates occur only on current/past errors, halving–80% of updates, enabling energy-efficient continual learning with no extra hyperparameters.
AI.motivation: Reduce the metabolic cost of synaptic plasticity and leverage error-monitoring signals to gate learning, improving efficiency in incremental and online continual learning.
AI.method: Introduce memorized mistake-gated plasticity: synaptic updates are strictly gated by current and past classification errors; simple implementation, no added hyperparameters, negligible overhead.
AI.result: Update frequency reduced by ~50–80%; reduced storage buffer requirements; effective for incremental and online continual learning; energy-efficient.
AI.conclusion: Learning on mistakes is biologically plausible, computationally efficient, and well-suited for continual learning; easy to implement with minimal overhead.

title: Crowdsourcing of Real-world Image Annotation via Visual Properties
summary: Recent advances in data-centric artificial intelligence highlight inherent limitations in object recognition datasets. One of the primary issues stems from the semantic gap problem, which results in complex many-to-many mappings between visual data and linguistic descriptions. This bias adversely affects performance in computer vision tasks. This paper proposes an image annotation methodology that integrates knowledge representation, natural language processing, and computer vision techniques, aiming to reduce annotator subjectivity by applying visual property constraints. We introduce an interactive crowdsourcing framework that dynamically asks questions based on a predefined object category hierarchy and annotator feedback, guiding image annotation by visual properties. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of this methodology, and annotator feedback is discussed to optimize the crowdsourcing setup.

AI.tldr: An interactive crowdsourcing-based image annotation framework that reduces annotator subjectivity by leveraging knowledge representation, NLP, and visual property constraints within a category hierarchy, with dynamic question-asking guided by annotator feedback.
AI.motivation: To address semantic gap-induced many-to-many mappings between visual data and linguistic descriptions, which biases object recognition datasets and degrades CV performance.
AI.method: Integrate knowledge representation, natural language processing, and computer vision to impose visual-property constraints; deploy an interactive crowdsourcing framework that adaptively asks questions based on a predefined object category hierarchy and annotator feedback to guide annotation.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate the framework's effectiveness and provide insights from annotator feedback to optimize the crowdsourcing setup.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach offers a pathway to tighter, more objective image annotations by combining structured knowledge, language processing, and guided crowdsourcing, potentially improving downstream CV tasks.

title: Optimistic Policy Learning under Pessimistic Adversaries with Regret and Violation Guarantees
summary:
Real-world decision-making systems operate in environments where state transitions depend not only on the agent's actions, but also on \textbf{exogenous factors outside its control}--competing agents, environmental disturbances, or strategic adversaries--formally, $s_{h+1} = f(s_h, a_h, \bar{a}_h)+ω_h$ where $\bar{a}_h$ is the adversary/external action, $a_h$ is the agent's action, and $ω_h$ is an additive noise. Ignoring such factors can yield policies that are optimal in isolation but \textbf{fail catastrophically in deployment}, particularly when safety constraints must be satisfied.
  Standard Constrained MDP formulations assume the agent is the sole driver of state evolution, an assumption that breaks down in safety-critical settings. Existing robust RL approaches address this via distributional robustness over transition kernels, but do not explicitly model the \textbf{strategic interaction} between agent and exogenous factor, and rely on strong assumptions about divergence from a known nominal model.
  We model the exogenous factor as an \textbf{adversarial policy} $\barπ$ that co-determines state transitions, and ask how an agent can remain both optimal and safe against such an adversary. \emph{To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to study safety-constrained RL under explicit adversarial dynamics}. We propose \textbf{Robust Hallucinated Constrained Upper-Confidence RL} (\texttt{RHC-UCRL}), a model-based algorithm that maintains optimism over both agent and adversary policies, explicitly separating epistemic from aleatoric uncertainty. \texttt{RHC-UCRL} achieves sub-linear regret and constraint violation guarantees.

AI.tldr: First work on safety-constrained RL with explicit adversarial dynamics; introduces Robust Hallucinated Constrained UCRL (RHC-UCRL), a model-based algorithm that optimistically plans over both the agent and a strategic adversary; achieves sublinear regret and constraint-violation guarantees.
AI.motivation: Real-world safety-critical decision-making occurs in environments where exogenous factors (e.g., competing agents, disturbances, adversaries) influence state transitions. Standard constrained RL assumes the agent solely drives dynamics, which can yield unsafe policies when deployed.
AI.method: Propose a model-based, optimistic RL algorithm (RHC-UCRL) that maintains confidence sets over both the agent’s policy and an adversarial policy. The approach explicitly separates epistemic (model) and aleatoric (inherent) uncertainty, and treats the exogenous factor as an adversarial policy that co-determines transitions.
AI.result: Algorithm achieves sub-linear regret and sub-linear constraint-violation guarantees. It is the first work to study safety-constrained RL under explicit adversarial dynamics and provides theoretical performance guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a novel framework and algorithm (RHC-UCRL) for safety-constrained RL with explicit adversaries. Claims novelty, provides sublinear guarantees, and suggests a new direction for robust, safe RL in adversarial environments.

title: Credo: Declarative Control of LLM Pipelines via Beliefs and Policies
summary: Agentic AI systems are becoming commonplace in domains that require long-lived, stateful decision-making in continuously evolving conditions. As such, correctness depends not only on the output of individual model calls, but also on how to best adapt when incorporating new evidence or revising prior conclusions. However, existing frameworks rely on imperative control loops, ephemeral memory, and prompt-embedded logic, making agent behavior opaque, brittle, and difficult to verify. This paper introduces Credo, which represents semantic state as beliefs and regulates behavior using declarative policies defined over these beliefs. This design supports adaptive, auditable, and composable execution through a database-backed semantic control plane. We showcase these concepts in a decision-control scenario, where beliefs and policies declaratively guide critical execution choices (e.g., model selection, retrieval, corrective re-execution), enabling dynamic behavior without requiring any changes to the underlying pipeline code.

AI.tldr: Credo proposes a semantic-control-plane for agentic AI that uses beliefs and declarative policies to govern behavior, enabling adaptive, auditable control without modifying pipeline code.
AI.motivation: Current agentic AI systems rely on imperative control loops, ephemeral memory, and prompt-embedded logic, which makes behavior opaque, brittle, and hard to verify. A declarative, database-backed control plane aims to improve transparency, adaptability, and verifiability.
AI.method: Represent semantic state as beliefs and regulate behavior with declarative policies over those beliefs. Implement a database-backed semantic control plane that interacts with existing pipelines, allowing dynamic behavior without changing underlying code.
AI.result: Demonstrated in a decision-control scenario where beliefs and policies guide critical execution choices (e.g., model selection, retrieval, corrective re-execution), enabling dynamic behavior without pipeline code changes.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables adaptive, auditable, and composable execution, improving verifiability and flexibility of agentic AI systems by decoupling decision logic from pipeline implementation.

title: DigiForest: Digital Analytics and Robotics for Sustainable Forestry
summary: Covering one third of Earth's land surface, forests are vital to global biodiversity, climate regulation, and human well-being. In Europe, forests and woodlands reach approximately 40% of land area, and the forestry sector is central to achieving the EU's climate neutrality and biodiversity goals; these emphasize sustainable forest management, increased use of long-lived wood products, and resilient forest ecosystems. To meet these goals and properly address their inherent challenges, current practices require further innovation. This chapter introduces DigiForest, a novel, large-scale precision forestry approach leveraging digital technologies and autonomous robotics. DigiForest is structured around four main components: (1) autonomous, heterogeneous mobile robots (aerial, legged, and marsupial) for tree-level data collection; (2) automated extraction of tree traits to build forest inventories; (3) a Decision Support System (DSS) for forecasting forest growth and supporting decision-making; and (4) low-impact selective logging using purpose-built autonomous harvesters. These technologies have been extensively validated in real-world conditions in several locations, including forests in Finland, the UK, and Switzerland.

AI.tldr: A novel, large-scale precision forestry framework (DigiForest) integrating autonomous robots for tree-level data collection, automated tree-trait extraction, a growth-forecasting decision support system, and autonomous harvesters, validated in Nordic and UK forests.
AI.motivation: To align forestry practices with EU climate neutrality and biodiversity targets by innovating sustainable forest management through digital technologies and robotics, enabling accurate inventories, growth forecasting, and low-impact harvesting.
AI.method: Four components: (1) autonomous heterogeneous mobile robots (aerial, legged, marsupial) for tree-level data collection; (2) automated extraction of tree traits to build forest inventories; (3) a decision support system for forecasting forest growth and supporting decision-making; and (4) low-impact selective logging using purpose-built autonomous harvesters. Real-world validation conducted in Finland, the UK, and Switzerland.
AI.result: The approach has been extensively validated under real-world conditions across multiple locations (Finland, UK, Switzerland), demonstrating feasibility and practical applicability of the DigiForest platform.
AI.conclusion: DigiForest represents a scalable, integrated precision forestry solution that enhances monitoring, growth forecasting, and low-impact harvesting, supporting sustainable forest management and alignment with climate and biodiversity objectives.

title: Co-distilled attention guided masked image modeling with noisy teacher for self-supervised learning on medical images
summary: Masked image modeling (MIM) is a highly effective self-supervised learning (SSL) approach to extract useful feature representations from unannotated data. Predominantly used random masking methods make SSL less effective for medical images due to the contextual similarity of neighboring patches, leading to information leakage and SSL simplification. Hierarchical shifted window (Swin) transformer, a highly effective approach for medical images cannot use advanced masking methods as it lacks a global [CLS] token. Hence, we introduced an attention guided masking mechanism for Swin within a co-distillation learning framework to selectively mask semantically co-occurring and discriminative patches, to reduce information leakage and increase the difficulty of SSL pretraining. However, attention guided masking inevitably reduces the diversity of attention heads, which negatively impacts downstream task performance. To address this, we for the first time, integrate a noisy teacher into the co-distillation framework (termed DAGMaN) that performs attentive masking while preserving high attention head diversity. We demonstrate the capability of DAGMaN on multiple tasks including full- and few-shot lung nodule classification, immunotherapy outcome prediction, tumor segmentation, and unsupervised organs clustering.

AI.tldr: DAGMaN introduces an attention-guided masking scheme for Swin-based self-supervised learning in medical imaging, augmented by a noisy teacher in a co-distillation framework to preserve attention head diversity and reduce information leakage, enabling robust pretraining across multiple medical tasks.
AI.motivation: Random masking in masked image modeling causes information leakage in medical images due to high patch similarity; Swin transformers lack a global CLS token, hindering advanced masking; there is a need to selectively mask semantically meaningful patches while preserving attention diversity, enabling more challenging SSL for medical data.
AI.method: Proposes a hierarchical Swin transformer with attention-guided masking within a co-distillation framework (DAGMaN). It selects semantically co-occurring and discriminative patches to mask, reducing information leakage. To counter reduced attention head diversity from masking, a noisy teacher is integrated into the co-distillation pipeline to maintain high attention head diversity.
AI.result: Demonstrated across multiple medical-imaging tasks, including full- and few-shot lung nodule classification, immunotherapy outcome prediction, tumor segmentation, and unsupervised organ clustering, showing capability and robustness of the approach.
AI.conclusion: DAGMaN effectively balances masking difficulty and attention head diversity, enabling robust self-supervised pretraining for medical imaging and offering broad applicability to diverse tasks.

title: Awakening Dormant Experts:Counterfactual Routing to Mitigate MoE Hallucinations
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have achieved remarkable scalability, yet they remain vulnerable to hallucinations, particularly when processing long-tail knowledge. We identify that this fragility stems from static Top-$k$ routing: routers tend to favor high-frequency patterns over rare factual associations. Consequently, ``specialist experts'' possessing critical long-tail knowledge are often assigned low gating scores and remain ``dormant'' -- under-prioritized for specific tokens despite their proven causal importance on other inputs. To address this, we propose Counterfactual Routing (CoR), a training-free inference framework designed to awaken these dormant experts. CoR integrates layer-wise perturbation analysis with the Counterfactual Expert Impact (CEI) metric to dynamically shift computational resources from syntax-dominant to knowledge-intensive layers while maintaining a constant total activation count, effectively retrieving causally decisive experts via virtual ablation. Extensive experiments on TruthfulQA, FACTOR, and TriviaQA demonstrate that CoR improves factual accuracy by 3.1\% on average without increasing the inference budget, establishing a superior Pareto frontier compared to static scaling strategies.

AI.tldr: Counterfactual Routing (CoR): a training-free, perturbation-based inference framework for sparse MoEs that awakens dormant long-tail experts, improving factual accuracy by 3.1% without extra compute, via dynamic layer-wise resource reallocation guided by Counterfactual Expert Impact (CEI).
AI.motivation: Static Top-k routing biases toward high-frequency patterns, underutilizing specialist experts and causing hallucinations on long-tail knowledge.
AI.method: Layer-wise perturbation analysis combined with the Counterfactual Expert Impact (CEI) metric to identify causally influential experts; virtual ablation to reallocate computation from syntax-dominant to knowledge-intensive layers while keeping total activation count constant; inference-time, training-free.
AI.result: Across TruthfulQA, FACTOR, and TriviaQA, CoR yields an average factual accuracy gain of 3.1% without increasing the inference budget, achieving a superior Pareto frontier relative to static routing strategies.
AI.conclusion: CoR effectively retrieves and utilizes causally decisive experts by perturbation-informed reallocation, providing a robust, budget-neutral improvement for factuality in sparse MoE models.

title: Equifinality in Mixture of Experts: Routing Topology Does Not Determine Language Modeling Quality
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures employ increasingly sophisticated routing mechanisms -- learned routers, multi-hop trajectories, token-dependent gating. We ask: does routing topology actually determine language modeling quality? We build a geometric MoE (ST-MoE) using cosine-similarity routing against learned centroids in a low-dimensional space ($d_{space} = 64$), requiring 80% fewer routing parameters than standard linear routers. Through 62 controlled experiments on WikiText-103 at 76--84M parameters trained to convergence (50K steps, 1.64B tokens), we find that routing topology does not determine asymptotic perplexity (PPL): five cosine-routing variants are statistically equivalent within a 1-PPL margin (Two One-Sided Tests [TOST], $p < 0.05$ for all 10 pairwise comparisons; 15 runs across 3 seeds, observed range 33.93--34.72). The finding extends to hash, random-fixed, and top-1 routing (single-seed; graceful 1.1--2.2 PPL degradation) and replicates on OpenWebText (0.03 PPL gap, 6 runs, 3 seeds each). A standard linear router with 5.3$\times$ more routing parameters reaches PPL 32.76, but iso-parameter cosine routing closes 67% of this gap -- the true mechanism advantage is $\sim$1.2%. The mechanistic explanation is convergent redundancy: multi-hop updates are collinear ($\cos(Δh_0, Δh_1) = 0.805$), implementing magnitude amplification rather than compositional reasoning; a single learnable scalar replicates multi-hop performance. As a practical payoff, zero-shot relative-norm halting saves 25% of MoE FLOPs at +0.12% PPL. Expert-level specialization and causal controllability -- which coexist with topology-level equifinality -- are explored in a companion paper.

AI.tldr: Routing topology does not fix language-modeling quality in sparse MoEs; cosine-based routing with far fewer routing parameters achievesalmost the same perplexity as larger, more complex routers; the observed gains arise from convergent redundancy (magnitude amplification) rather than compositional reasoning; notable compute savings achievable via gating techniques.
AI.motivation: Test whether routing topology (router type, multi-hop structure, gating) is the primary determinant of language-modeling quality in sparse MoEs, and whether simpler, parameter-efficient routing can match or approach the performance of standard linear routers.
AI.method: Geometric ST-MoE with cosine-similarity routing against learned centroids in a 64-d space, using ~80% fewer routing parameters than standard linear routers. 62 controlled experiments on WikiText-103 (~76–84M parameters, 50k steps, 1.64B tokens) across 15 runs and 3 seeds; TOST (1-PPL margin) across 10 pairwise comparisons; replication on OpenWebText. Comparisons include hash, random-fixed, and top-1 routing; plus a linear-router baseline with 5.3× more routing parameters. Analyses include delta-h vector relationships (cosine similarity) and zero-shot relative-norm halting to assess compute, with mention of a companion paper on specialization and controllability.
AI.result: Cosine-routing variants show PPL within 1 across five routing variants (TOST, p<0.05 for all 10 pairwise tests); OpenWebText replication with minimal PPL gap (0.03). A linear router with substantially more routing params reduces PPL to 32.76, while iso-parameter cosine routing closes about 67% of that gap, yielding an effective ~1.2% true advantage for topology. Mechanistically, multi-hop updates are highly aligned (cos(Δh0, Δh1)≈0.805), indicating convergent redundancy and magnitude amplification rather than compositional reasoning. A single learnable scalar reproduces multi-hop performance. Zero-shot relative-norm halting achieves ~25% MoE FLOPs savings at +0.12% PPL. A companion paper discusses expert-level specialization and causal controllability alongside topology-level equifinality.
AI.conclusion: Routing topology exhibits equifinality with respect to asymptotic language-modeling quality; improvements arise from magnitude amplification due to convergent redundancy rather than true compositional reasoning. Practically, substantial compute savings are achievable with efficient routing; results motivate exploration of specialization and controllability in MoEs, beyond routing topology alone.

title: World-Value-Action Model: Implicit Planning for Vision-Language-Action Systems
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a promising paradigm for building embodied agents that ground perception and language into action. However, most existing approaches rely on direct action prediction, lacking the ability to reason over long-horizon trajectories and evaluate their consequences, which limits performance in complex decision-making tasks. In this work, we introduce World-Value-Action (WAV) model, a unified framework that enables implicit planning in VLA systems. Rather than performing explicit trajectory optimization, WAV model learn a structured latent representation of future trajectories conditioned on visual observations and language instructions. A learned world model predicts future states, while a trajectory value function evaluates their long-horizon utility. Action generation is then formulated as inference in this latent space, where the model progressively concentrates probability mass on high-value and dynamically feasible trajectories. We provide a theoretical perspective showing that planning directly in action space suffers from an exponential decay in the probability of feasible trajectories as the horizon increases. In contrast, latent-space inference reshapes the search distribution toward feasible regions, enabling efficient long-horizon decision making. Extensive simulations and real-world experiments demonstrate that the WAV model consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving significant improvements in task success rate, generalization ability, and robustness, especially in long-horizon and compositional scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proposes WAV, a latent-space planning framework for Vision-Language-Action models that uses a world model and a trajectory-value function to implicitly plan futures in latent space, improving long-horizon decision making without explicit trajectory optimization.
AI.motivation: Direct action-prediction in VLA models struggles with long-horizon planning and evaluating consequences, limiting performance on complex tasks.
AI.method: Learn a structured latent representation of future trajectories conditioned on observations and instructions; a world model predicts future states; a trajectory value function estimates long-horizon utility; action selection via latent-space inference biased toward high-value, feasible trajectories; provides theoretical analysis contrasting action-space planning vs latent-space planning.
AI.result: Empirical results show WAV outperforms state-of-the-art, with higher task success, better generalization and robustness, especially for long-horizon and compositional tasks, across simulations and real-world experiments.
AI.conclusion: Planning in latent space via a world-value-action framework yields efficient long-horizon decision making, better grounding of perception and language into action, and strong empirical performance; provides a theoretical explanation for the benefits of latent-space planning over direct action-space planning.

title: H2VLR: Heterogeneous Hypergraph Vision-Language Reasoning for Few-Shot Anomaly Detection
summary: As a classic vision task, anomaly detection has been widely applied in industrial inspection and medical imaging. In this task, data scarcity is often a frequently-faced issue. To solve it, the few-shot anomaly detection (FSAD) scheme is attracting increasing attention. In recent years, beyond traditional visual paradigm, Vision-Language Model (VLM) has been extensively explored to boost this field. However, in currently-existing VLM-based FSAD schemes, almost all perform anomaly inference only by pairwise feature matching, ignoring structural dependencies and global consistency. To further redound to FSAD via VLM, we propose a Heterogeneous Hypergraph Vision-Language Reasoning (H2VLR) framework. It reformulates the FSAD as a high-order inference problem of visual-semantic relations, by jointly modeling visual regions and semantic concepts in a unified hypergraph. Experimental comparisons verify the effectiveness and advantages of H2VLR. It could often achieve state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance on representative industrial and medical benchmarks. Our code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A VLM-based few-shot anomaly detection method (H2VLR) using a heterogeneous hypergraph to model high-order visual-semantic relations, addressing global consistency and data scarcity; reports SOTA on industrial/medical benchmarks; code to be released.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity in few-shot anomaly detection and the limitation of pairwise feature matching in current VLM-based FSAD methods, which neglect structural dependencies and global consistency.
AI.method: Proposes Heterogeneous Hypergraph Vision-Language Reasoning (H2VLR) framework that reformulates FSAD as high-order visual-semantic inference by jointly modeling visual regions and semantic concepts within a unified hypergraph.
AI.result: Experimental results show the approach is effective and advantageous, achieving state-of-the-art performance on representative industrial and medical benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: H2VLR enhances FSAD by leveraging high-order visual-semantic reasoning via a heterogeneous hypergraph, with promising generalization to industrial/medical anomaly detection; code will be released after acceptance.

title: Metric-Aware Principal Component Analysis (MAPCA):A Unified Framework for Scale-Invariant Representation Learning
summary:
We introduce Metric-Aware Principal Component Analysis (MAPCA), a unified framework for scale-invariant representation learning based on the generalised eigenproblem max Tr(W^T Sigma W) subject to W^T M W = I, where M is a symmetric positive definite metric matrix. The choice of M determines the representation geometry. The canonical beta-family M(beta) = Sigma^beta, beta in [0,1], provides continuous spectral bias control between standard PCA (beta=0) and output whitening (beta=1), with condition number kappa(beta) = (lambda_1/lambda_p)^(1-beta) decreasing monotonically to isotropy. The diagonal metric M = D = diag(Sigma) recovers Invariant PCA (IPCA), a method rooted in Frisch (1928) diagonal regression, as a distinct member of the broader framework. We prove that scale invariance holds if and only if the metric transforms as M_tilde = CMC under rescaling C, a condition satisfied exactly by IPCA but not by the general beta-family at intermediate values.
  Beyond its classical interpretation, MAPCA provides a geometric language that unifies several self-supervised learning objectives. Barlow Twins and ZCA whitening correspond to beta=1 (output whitening); VICReg's variance term corresponds to the diagonal metric. A key finding is that W-MSE, despite being described as a whitening-based method, corresponds to M = Sigma^{-1} (beta = -1), outside the spectral compression range entirely and in the opposite spectral direction to Barlow Twins. This distinction between input and output whitening is invisible at the level of loss functions and becomes precise only within the MAPCA framework.

AI.tldr: MAPCA is a unified framework for scale-invariant representation learning built around a generalized eigenproblem max Tr(W^T Σ W) with W^T M W = I, where the metric M governs representation geometry. The canonical beta-family M(β) = Σ^β links standard PCA (β=0) to output whitening (β=1) with a monotonically improving isotropy κ(β) = (λ1/λp)^(1−β). IPCA (diagonal M = diag(Σ)) is the special case that preserves scale invariance under rescaling, a property not shared by intermediate β. The framework also reinterprets self-supervised objectives: Barlow Twins and ZCA Whitenings map to β=1, VICReg’s variance term corresponds to a diagonal metric, and W-MSE corresponds to M = Σ^−1 (β = −1), i.e., opposite spectral direction to β=1. Beyond loss-level view, MAPCA provides a geometric language linking PCA, whitening, and SSL losses.
AI.motivation: Provide a unified geometric framework for scale-invariant representation learning that connects classical dimensionality reduction (PCA, whitening) with modern self-supervised learning objectives, clarifying invariances, spectral biases, and the relationships among different methods.
AI.method: Define the optimization problem max Tr(W^T Σ W) subject to W^T M W = I, with M chosen from the beta-family M(β) = Σ^β (β ∈ [0,1]) or as a diagonal metric M = diag(Σ). Analyze scale invariance: scale invariance holds iff M tilde = C M C under rescaling C; show this holds exactly for IPCA (diagonal M) but not for intermediate β. Map SSL objectives to specific M: Barlow Twins and ZCA whitening correspond to β=1; VICReg’s variance term corresponds to the diagonal metric; W-MSE corresponds to β = −1 (M = Σ^−1), opposite to β=1. Compute spectral bias κ(β) = (λ1/λp)^(1−β).
AI.result: The MAPCA framework yields: (1) a continuous spectral-bias control across β ∈ [0,1], (2) a precise condition for scale invariance tied to the metric transform M → C M C, with IPCA satisfying it exactly, (3) a set of direct correspondences between SSL losses and metric choices (β=1 for Barlow Twins/ZCA, diagonal metric for VICReg, β=−1 for W-MSE), and (4) an explicit interpretation that input vs output whitening distinctions are only evident within MAPCA, not merely at the loss level.
AI.conclusion: MAPCA provides a compact geometric lens to analyze and relate PCA, whitening, and SSL objectives, clarifying when scale invariance holds and how different methods inhabit the same framework, including counterintuitive mappings such as W-MSE at β=−1 opposite to the β=1 whitening direction.

title: Demonstration of Pneuma-Seeker: Agentic System for Reifying and Fulfilling Information Needs on Tabular Data
summary: Data analysts working with relational data often start with vague or underspecified questions and refine them iteratively as they explore the data. To support this iterative process, we demonstrate Pneuma-Seeker, a system that reifies a user's information need as explicit, inspectable relational specifications, enabling iterative refinement of the information need, targeted data discovery, and provenance-aware execution. Through two real-world procurement use cases, we show how Pneuma-Seeker leverages LLMs as transparent, interactive analytical collaborators rather than opaque answer engines.

AI.tldr: Pneuma-Seeker namespaces iterative, transparent specification-driven data exploration for relational data, using LLMs as collaborators to enable inspectable information needs and provenance-aware execution across real-world procurement cases.
AI.motivation: Help data analysts move from vague, underspecified questions to explicit, refineable information needs, by reifying queries as inspectable relational specifications and enabling provenance tracking to support iterative discovery.
AI.method: Introduce Pneuma-Seeker which converts user information needs into explicit relational specifications; use LLMs as interactive, transparent collaborators rather than opaque answer engines; demonstrate via two procurement use cases showing data discovery, refinement, and provenance-aware execution.
AI.result: Shows that LLMs can function as transparent analytical partners in a system that makes user needs inspectable, supports iterative refinement, targeted data discovery, and provenance tracking in real-world workflows.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, inspectable information needs coupled with provenance-aware execution and LLM collaboration improve iterative data analysis workflows for relational data, enabling more transparent and controllable analytics.

title: Differentiable Object Pose Connectivity Metrics for Regrasp Sequence Optimization
summary: Regrasp planning is often required when one pick-and-place cannot transfer an object from an initial pose to a goal pose while maintaining grasp feasibility. The main challenge is to reason about shared-grasp connectivity across intermediate poses, where discrete search becomes brittle. We propose an implicit multi-step regrasp planning framework based on differentiable pose sequence connectivity metrics. We model grasp feasibility under an object pose using an Energy-Based Model (EBM) and leverage energy additivity to construct a continuous energy landscape that measures pose-pair connectivity, enabling gradient-based optimization of intermediate object poses. An adaptive iterative deepening strategy is introduced to determine the minimum number of intermediate steps automatically. Experiments show that the proposed cost formulation provides smooth and informative gradients, improving planning robustness over other alternatives. They also demonstrate generalization to unseen grasp poses and cross-end-effector transfer, where a model trained with suction constraints can guide parallel gripper grasp manipulation. The multi-step planning results further highlight the effectiveness of adaptive deepening and minimum-step search.

AI.tldr: Proposes differentiable, energy-based multi-step regrasp planning using an Energy-Based Model (EBM) to model grasp feasibility as a function of object pose, enabling gradient-based optimization of intermediate poses with adaptive step planning and demonstrating robust planning and cross-end-effector generalization.
AI.motivation: Regrasp planning with rigid objects often relies on brittle discrete search; a continuous, differentiable metric of pose-pair connectivity is needed to reason about intermediate poses and improve robustness.
AI.method: An implicit multi-step regrasp planner using an Energy-Based Model to capture grasp feasibility under object pose. Energy additivity constructs a continuous energy landscape for pose-pair connectivity, enabling gradient-based optimization of intermediate poses. Adaptive iterative deepening automatically determines the minimum number of intermediate steps.
AI.result: The energy-based formulation yields smooth, informative gradients that improve planning robustness over alternatives. It generalizes to unseen grasp poses and supports cross-end-effector transfer (e.g., suction-trained models guiding parallel gripper manipulation). The adaptive deepening and minimum-step search effectively determine the required number of intermediate steps.
AI.conclusion: The proposed differentiable, energy-based multi-step regrasp planner enhances robustness and generalization in regrasp planning, with automatic step determination and demonstrated cross-end-effector applicability.

title: Chain of Modality: From Static Fusion to Dynamic Orchestration in Omni-MLLMs
summary: Omni-modal Large Language Models (Omni-MLLMs) promise a unified integration of diverse sensory streams. However, recent evaluations reveal a critical performance paradox: unimodal baselines frequently outperform joint multimodal inference. We trace this perceptual fragility to the static fusion topologies universally employed by current models, identifying two structural pathologies: positional bias in sequential inputs and alignment traps in interleaved formats, which systematically distort attention regardless of task semantics. To resolve this functional rigidity, we propose Chain of Modality (CoM), an agentic framework that transitions multimodal fusion from passive concatenation to dynamic orchestration. CoM adaptively orchestrates input topologies, switching among parallel, sequential, and interleaved pathways to neutralize structural biases. Furthermore, CoM bifurcates cognitive execution into two task-aligned pathways: a streamlined ``Direct-Decide'' path for direct perception and a structured ``Reason-Decide'' path for analytical auditing. Operating in either a training-free or a data-efficient SFT setting, CoM achieves robust and consistent generalization across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: CoM presents a dynamic multimodal fusion framework with adaptive input topologies and two cognitive pathways to overcome static fusion biases in Omni-MLLMs, achieving robust generalization under training-free or data-efficient SFT settings.
AI.motivation: Static fusion topologies cause positional bias in sequential inputs and alignment traps in interleaved formats, systematically distorting attention and harming joint multimodal inference; an adaptive, orchestrated fusion strategy is needed.
AI.method: CoM adaptively switches among parallel, sequential, and interleaved input topologies; introduces two task-aligned cognitive pathways: Direct-Decide for direct perception and Reason-Decide for analytical auditing; supports training-free or data-efficient supervised fine-tuning (SFT).
AI.result: Claims robust and consistent generalization across diverse benchmarks in both training-free and data-efficient SFT regimes, indicating reduced sensitivity to input topology compared with static baselines.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, agentic multimodal fusion with dual cognitive pathways mitigates structural biases in Omni-MLLMs, enabling reliable multimodal reasoning and practical deployment across tasks.

title: Calibrate-Then-Delegate: Safety Monitoring with Risk and Budget Guarantees via Model Cascades
summary: Monitoring LLM safety at scale requires balancing cost and accuracy: a cheap latent-space probe can screen every input, but hard cases should be escalated to a more expensive expert. Existing cascades delegate based on probe uncertainty, but uncertainty is a poor proxy for delegation benefit, as it ignores whether the expert would actually correct the error. To address this problem, we introduce Calibrate-Then-Delegate (CTD), a model-cascade approach that provides probabilistic guarantees on the computation cost while enabling instance-level (streaming) decisions. CTD builds on a novel delegation value (DV) probe, a lightweight model operating on the same internal representations as the safety probe that directly predicts the benefit of escalation. To enforce budget constraints, CTD calibrates a threshold on the DV signal using held-out data via multiple hypothesis testing, yielding finite-sample guarantees on the delegation rate. Evaluated on four safety datasets, CTD consistently outperforms uncertainty-based delegation at every budget level, avoids harmful over-delegation, and adapts budget allocation to input difficulty without requiring group labels.

AI.tldr: Calibrate-Then-Delegate (CTD) uses a DV probe to predict the benefit of escalating to an expert in a model cascade, with finite-sample guarantees on delegation rate via calibrated thresholds, achieving cost-effective, instance-level safety evaluation without needing group labels.
AI.motivation: Balance cost and accuracy in scalable LLM safety monitoring; uncertainty-based delegation misaligns with actual correction benefits and may cause over-/under-delegation.
AI.method: Introduce a delegation value (DV) probe that operates on the same internal representations as the safety probe to predict escalation benefit; calibrate DV thresholds on held-out data using multiple hypothesis testing to enforce budget constraints and provide finite-sample guarantees; enables streaming, instance-level decisions across four safety datasets.
AI.result: CTD consistently outperforms uncertainty-based delegation across all budget levels, avoids harmful over-delegation, and adapts budget allocation to input difficulty without requiring group labels.
AI.conclusion: CTD provides probabilistic guarantees for delegation cost while improving safety coverage, offering a practical, label-free, cost-aware cascade for LLM safety evaluation.

title: Geometric Routing Enables Causal Expert Control in Mixture of Experts
summary:
Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale parameters while fixing active computation per token, but the specialization of individual experts remains opaque. In a companion paper we showed that routing topology is quality-neutral: five structurally different configurations converge to statistically equivalent language modeling quality. Here we show that expert identity is nonetheless causally meaningful: individual rank-1 experts are monosemantic by construction, and cosine-similarity routing in a low-dimensional metric space makes their specialization directly inspectable.
  We present four lines of evidence. First, projecting expert output vectors through the unembedding matrix yields a Semantic Dictionary: 15% of experts are monosemantic specialists spanning 10 categories (temporal, geographic, cardinal, discourse, emotional, financial, military, scientific). Second, routing exhibits a frequency-to-syntax gradient: early layers separate tokens by word frequency, deeper layers by syntactic class (Zipf-confound controls, all $p < 0.001$). Third, causal interventions confirm these labels: steering toward a temporal expert's centroid increases P(temporal) by +321% (median across 44 prompts); suppressing a geographic expert drops P(geographic) by -23%; rewriting an expert's output vector halves target-category probability, and effects compose additively across layers. Fourth, the interventions are not unique to cosine routing: linear routers support comparable steering, but only cosine routing provides geometric transparency -- expert specialization is readable directly from the centroid matrix.
  MoE expert-level specialization is a first-class interpretability primitive: architecturally monosemantic, causally validated, and controllable at inference with zero overhead.

AI.tldr: Sparse MoE experts are interpretable: individual rank-1 experts are monosemantic; cosine routing yields readable specialization and allows causal control at inference with no overhead.
AI.motivation: To determine whether individual experts in sparse MoE carry distinct semantic identities and whether routing geometry enables direct inspection of specialization.
AI.method: Four lines of evidence: (1) unembedding projection yields Semantic Dictionary identifying monosemantic categories; (2) routing gradient shows frequency-to-syntax progression across layers; (3) causal interventions steering/suppressing experts alter category probabilities; (4) cosine routing offers geometric transparency; compare to linear routers.
AI.result: 15% of experts are monosemantic across 10 categories; early layers separate by word frequency, deeper by syntactic class; steering toward a temporal centroid raises P(temporal) by median +321% (44 prompts); suppressing geographic expert reduces P(geographic) by -23%; editing an expert's vector halves its target probability; effects compose additively across layers; linear routers also perform steering, but cosine routing provides richer interpretability.
AI.conclusion: MoE expert-level specialization is an interpretable primitive: monosemantic by design, causally validated, and controllable at inference with zero overhead; cosine routing uniquely enables geometric readability of specialization.

title: Keep It CALM: Toward Calibration-Free Kilometer-Level SLAM with Visual Geometry Foundation Models via an Assistant Eye
comment: 19 pages, 8 figures, submitted to IEEE TPAMI
summary: Visual Geometry Foundation Models (VGFMs) demonstrate remarkable zero-shot capabilities in local reconstruction. However, deploying them for kilometer-level Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) remains challenging. In such scenarios, current approaches mainly rely on linear transforms (e.g., Sim3 and SL4) for sub-map alignment, while we argue that a single linear transform is fundamentally insufficient to model the complex, non-linear geometric distortions inherent in VGFM outputs. Forcing such rigid alignment leads to the rapid accumulation of uncorrected residuals, eventually resulting in significant trajectory drift and map divergence. To address these limitations, we present CAL2M (Calibration-free Assistant-eye based Large-scale Localization and Mapping), a plug-and-play framework compatible with arbitrary VGFMs. Distinct from traditional systems, CAL2M introduces an "assistant eye" solely to leverage the prior of constant physical spacing, effectively eliminating scale ambiguity without any temporal or spatial pre-calibration. Furthermore, leveraging the assumption of accurate feature matching, we propose an epipolar-guided intrinsic and pose correction model. Supported by an online intrinsic search module, it can effectively rectify rotation and translation errors caused by inaccurate intrinsics through fundamental matrix decomposition. Finally, to ensure accurate mapping, we introduce a globally consistent mapping strategy based on anchor propagation. By constructing and fusing anchors across the trajectory, we establish a direct local-to-global mapping relationship. This enables the application of nonlinear transformations to elastically align sub-maps, effectively eliminating geometric misalignments and ensuring a globally consistent reconstruction. The source code of CAL2M will be publicly available at https://github.com/IRMVLab/CALM.

AI.tldr: CAL2M introduces a calibration-free, plug-and-play framework to enable large-scale SLAM with Visual Geometry Foundation Models (VGFMs) by using an 'assistant eye' to fix scale, an epipolar-guided correction of intrinsics and pose, online intrinsic search, and a globally consistent anchor-propagation strategy to allow nonlinear elastic alignment of sub-maps.
AI.motivation: Current sub-map alignment for VGFMs at kilometer scales relies on single linear transforms (e.g., Sim3/SL4), which cannot capture complex non-linear geometric distortions and lead to drift. There is a need for a calibration-free, scalable approach that preserves global consistency without pre-calibration.
AI.method: 1) Introduce an 'assistant eye' enforcing a constant physical spacing to resolve scale ambiguity without pre-calibration. 2) Propose an epipolar-guided intrinsic and pose correction model with an online intrinsic-search module and fundamental matrix decomposition to rectify rotation/translation errors. 3) Develop a globally consistent mapping via anchor propagation, constructing/fusing anchors along the trajectory to enable nonlinear elastic alignment of sub-maps.
AI.result: Not specified in the abstract; the authors claim improved geometric consistency through nonlinear elastic alignment and anchor-based global mapping, with code to be released.
AI.conclusion: CAL2M is presented as a scalable, calibration-free framework that enables globally consistent large-scale SLAM with VGFMs by leveraging an assistant eye for scale, epipolar-based corrections for intrinsics/pose, and anchor propagation for nonlinear sub-map alignment.

title: FreqTrack: Frequency Learning based Vision Transformer for RGB-Event Object Tracking
summary: Existing single-modal RGB trackers often face performance bottlenecks in complex dynamic scenes, while the introduction of event sensors offers new potential for enhancing tracking capabilities. However, most current RGB-event fusion methods, primarily designed in the spatial domain using convolutional, Transformer, or Mamba architectures, fail to fully exploit the unique temporal response and high-frequency characteristics of event data. To address this, we1 propose FreqTrack, a frequency-aware RGBE tracking framework that establishes complementary inter-modal correlations through frequency-domain transformations for more robust feature fusion. We design a Spectral Enhancement Transformer (SET) layer that incorporates multi-head dynamic Fourier filtering to adaptively enhance and select frequency-domain features. Additionally, we develop a Wavelet Edge Refinement (WER) module, which leverages learnable wavelet transforms to explicitly extract multi-scale edge structures from event data, effectively improving modeling capability in high-speed and low-light scenarios. Extensive experiments on the COESOT and FE108 datasets demonstrate that FreqTrack achieves highly competitive performance, particularly attaining leading precision of 76.6\% on the COESOT benchmark, validating the effectiveness of frequency-domain modeling for RGBE tracking.

AI.tldr: FreqTrack introduces a frequency-aware RGB-E tracker that uses spectral domain operations to fuse RGB and event data. It achieves strong performance on RGB-E benchmarks, notably 76.6% precision on COESOT.
AI.motivation: RGB trackers struggle in dynamic scenes; event sensors offer rich temporal/high-frequency information, but existing RGB-event fusion methods operate primarily in the spatial domain and fail to exploit temporal characteristics. A frequency-domain approach can better exploit the temporal content and high-frequency information in event data to improve tracking robustness.
AI.method: Propose FreqTrack with a Spectral Enhancement Transformer (SET) that performs multi-head dynamic Fourier filtering to adaptively select/enhance frequency-domain features, and a Wavelet Edge Refinement (WER) module that uses learnable wavelet transforms to extract multi-scale edge structures from event data. The framework fuses RGB and event streams in the frequency domain to strengthen inter-modal correlations and improve tracking under challenging conditions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on COESOT and FE108 show competitive performance, with a leading precision of 76.6% on the COESOT benchmark, validating the efficacy of frequency-domain modeling for RGBE tracking.
AI.conclusion: Frequency-domain modeling—via SET and WER—effectively exploits temporal and high-frequency content in event data to enhance RGBE tracking, yielding strong results on standard RGB-E datasets.

title: GUI-Perturbed: Domain Randomization Reveals Systematic Brittleness in GUI Grounding Models
summary: GUI grounding models report over 85% accuracy on standard benchmarks, yet drop 27-56 percentage points when instructions require spatial reasoning rather than direct element naming. Current benchmarks miss this because they evaluate each screenshot once with a single fixed instruction. We introduce GUI-Perturbed, a controlled perturbation framework that independently varies visual scenes and instructions to measure grounding robustness. Evaluating three 7B models from the same architecture lineage, we find that relational instructions cause systematic accuracy collapse across all models, a 70% browser zoom produces statistically significant degradation, and rank-8 LoRA fine-tuning with augmented data degrades performance rather than improving it. By perturbing along independent axes, GUI-Perturbed isolates which specific capability axes are affected-spatial reasoning, visual robustness, reasoning calibration-providing diagnostic signal that aggregate benchmarks cannot. We release the dataset, augmentation pipeline, and a fine-tuned model.

AI.tldr: GUI-Perturbed introduces a controlled perturbation framework to test GUI grounding models by independently varying visuals and instructions, revealing robustness gaps in spatial reasoning and visual calibration; results show accuracy drops with relational instructions, zoom perturbation, and LoRA fine-tuning with augmented data; dataset and model released.
AI.motivation: Identify limitations of standard benchmarks that only evaluate a single screenshot-instruction pair, and quantify robustness along independent axes of variation to isolate specific capability gaps.
AI.method: Create GUI-Perturbed perturbations along two axes: visual scenes and textual instructions; evaluate three 7B models from same architecture lineage; apply relational instructions, browser zoom changes, rank-8 LoRA with augmented data; measure accuracy degradation relative to baselines.
AI.result: Relational instructions cause systematic accuracy collapse across all models; 70% browser zoom produces statistically significant degradation; rank-8 LoRA with augmented data degrades performance; perturbations isolate capability axes providing diagnostic signal; dataset and model released.
AI.conclusion: GUI-Perturbed is effective for isolating and diagnosing robustness issues in GUI grounding models, enabling targeted improvements beyond aggregate benchmarks.

title: On Tackling Complex Tasks with Reward Machines and Signal Temporal Logics
summary: We propose a Reinforcement Learning (RL) based control design framework for handling complex tasks. The approach extends the concept of Reward Machines (RM) with Signal Temporal Logic (STL) formulas that can be used for event generation. The use of STL allows not only a more efficient representation of rewards for complex tasks but also guiding the training process to converge towards behaviors satisfying specified requirements. We also propose an implementation of the framework that leverages the STL online monitoring algorithms. We illustrate the framework with three case studies (minigrid, cart-pole and high-way environments) with non-trivial tasks.

AI.tldr: An RL-based control framework that combines Reward Machines with Signal Temporal Logic for event generation and reward shaping; uses STL online monitoring; validated on miniGrid, cart-pole, and highway tasks.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of encoding complex temporal requirements in reinforcement learning and to mitigate brittle reward design by using formal temporal logic and structured event handling.
AI.method: Extend Reward Machines with STL formulas to generate events, and apply STL online monitoring to support real-time reward shaping within the RL loop. Implement and evaluate the framework in three non-trivial domains (minigrid, cart-pole, highway).
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility across three case studies, showing that STL-guided event generation and RM-based reward structuring can steer training toward behaviors that satisfy temporal specifications and improve reward representation efficiency.
AI.conclusion: STL-based RM integration with online monitoring provides a principled approach to enforce temporal requirements in RL, with demonstrated applicability to diverse control tasks; future work may include quantitative performance analysis, scalability, and broader domain adoption.

title: Switch: Learning Agile Skills Switching for Humanoid Robots
summary: Recent advancements in whole-body control through deep reinforcement learning have enabled humanoid robots to achieve remarkable progress in real-world chal lenging locomotion skills.
