However, existing approaches often struggle with flexible transitions between distinct skills, cre ating safety concerns and practical limitations. To address this challenge, we introduce a hierarchical multi-skill system, Switch, enabling seamless skill transitions at any moment. Our approach comprises three key components: (1) a Skill Graph (SG) that establishes potential cross-skill transitions based on kinematic similarity within multi-skill motion data, (2) a whole-body tracking policy trained on this skill graph through deep reinforcement learning, and (3) an online skill scheduler to drive the tracking policy for robust skill execution and smooth transitions. For skill switching or significant tracking deviations, the scheduler performs online graph search to find the optimal feasible path, which ensures efficient, stable, and real-time execution of diverse locomotion skills. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that Switch empowers humanoid to execute agile skill transitions with high success rates while maintaining strong motion imitation performance.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical multi-skill system Switch enables seamless cross-skill transitions for humanoid locomotion using a Skill Graph, whole-body tracking policy learned via DRL, and an online scheduler with graph search to ensure robust, real-time execution.
AI.motivation: To overcome brittle, unsafe, or inefficient transitions between distinct locomotion skills in deep reinforcement learning-based humanoid control.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Skill Graph (SG) builds potential cross-skill transitions by analyzing kinematic similarity from multi-skill motion data; (2) a whole-body tracking policy trained via deep reinforcement learning on the skill graph; (3) an online skill scheduler that drives the tracking policy and, upon switching or deviations, performs online graph search to select a feasible path for execution.
AI.result: Experiments show Switch enables agile skill transitions with high success rates while maintaining strong motion imitation performance.
AI.conclusion: Switch provides a robust framework for real-time, safe, and fluid transitions across multiple locomotion skills in humanoid robots, potentially enabling more versatile and resilient locomotion systems.

title: Design and Validation of a Low-Cost Smartphone Based Fluorescence Detection Platform Compared with Conventional Microplate Readers
summary: A low cost fluorescence-based optical system is developed for detecting the presence of certain microorganisms and molecules within a diluted sample. A specifically designed device setup compatible with conventional 96 well plates is chosen to create an ideal environment in which a smart phone camera can be used as the optical detector. In comparison with conventional microplate reading machines such as Perkin Elmer Victor Machine, the device presented in this paper is not equipped with expensive elements such as exciter filer, barrier filter and photomultiplier; instead, a phone camera is all needed to detect fluorescence within the sample. The strategy being involved is to determine the relationship between the image color of the sample in RGB color space and the molar concentration of the fluorescence specimen in that sample. This manuscript is a preprint version of work related to a publication in IEEE. The final version may differ from this manuscript.

AI.tldr: Low-cost smartphone-based fluorescence detection using 96-well plates; uses RGB image analysis to estimate fluorophore concentration; contrasts with expensive plate readers; presented as a preprint with potential changes in the final IEEE publication.
AI.motivation: Provide an affordable, accessible fluorescence readout by removing costly optical components and leveraging a smartphone camera, enabling detection in standard microplate format.
AI.method: Develop a 96-well-compatible setup where a smartphone camera serves as the detector; avoid exciter/barrier filters and photomultiplier tubes; establish a mapping between RGB image color and fluorescence molar concentration of the sample; calibration against known concentrations.
AI.result: Conceptual feasibility demonstrated; establishes a strategy to relate RGB color to concentration; the abstract does not report quantitative performance metrics; presented as a preprint with the final IEEE version possibly differing.
AI.conclusion: The work proposes a low-cost alternative to conventional plate readers using smartphone-based fluorescence readout, suitable for detecting microorganisms and molecules in diluted samples; further validation and final publication details expected in the IEEE version.

title: Reinforcement Learning via Value Gradient Flow
summary: We study behavior-regularized reinforcement learning (RL), where regularization toward a reference distribution (the dataset in offline RL or the base model in LLM RL finetuning) is essential to prevent value over-optimization caused by erroneous out-of-distribution extrapolation. Existing methods either rely on reparameterized policy gradient, which are difficult to scale to large generative models, or on reject sampling, which can be overly conservative when attempting to move beyond the behavior support. In this paper, we propose Value Gradient Flow (VGF), a scalable new paradigm for behavior-regularized RL. VGF casts behavior-regularized RL as an optimal transport problem that maps the reference distribution to the value-induced optimal policy distribution. We solve this transport problem via discrete gradient flow, where value gradients guide particles initialized from the reference distribution. Our analysis shows that VGF imposes regularization implicitly by controlling the transport budget. VGF eliminates explicit policy parameterization while remaining expressive and flexible, this enables adaptive test-time scaling by adjusting the transport budget. Extensive experiments demonstrate that VGF significantly outperforms prior methods, achieving state-of-the-art results on offline RL benchmarks (D4RL, OGBench) and LLM RL tasks. Code and runs can be found at https://ryanxhr.github.io/vgf.

AI.tldr: A scalable behavior-regularized RL framework that uses optimal transport and discrete gradient flow to map a reference distribution to a value-driven policy without explicit policy parameterization, achieving state-of-the-art in offline RL and LLM RL.
AI.motivation: To prevent value over-optimization due to out-of-distribution extrapolation in behavior-regularized RL (offline RL and LLM RL), where existing methods are brittle or conservative.
AI.method: Formulates as an optimal transport problem from the reference distribution to the value-induced policy distribution; solves via discrete gradient flow with value gradients guiding particles; eliminates explicit policy parameterization; transport budget acts as implicit regularization; enables test-time scaling.
AI.result: Empirical results show significant improvements over prior methods; state-of-the-art on D4RL, OGBench, and LLM RL tasks; code available at provided URL.
AI.conclusion: VGF provides a scalable, implicit regularization mechanism for behavior-regularized RL by controlling transport budget, offering a flexible, parameter-free policy representation while maintaining strong performance.

title: AIBuildAI: An AI Agent for Automatically Building AI Models
summary: AI models underpin modern intelligent systems, driving advances across science, medicine, finance, and technology. Yet developing high-performing AI models remains a labor-intensive process that requires expert practitioners to iteratively design architectures, engineer representations, implement training pipelines and refine approaches through empirical evaluation. Existing AutoML methods partially alleviate this burden but remain limited to narrow aspects such as hyperparameter optimization and model selection within predefined search spaces, leaving the full development lifecycle largely dependent on human expertise. To address this gap, we introduce AIBuildAI, an AI agent that automatically builds AI models from a task description and training data. AIBuildAI adopts a hierarchical agent architecture in which a manager agent coordinates three specialized sub-agents: a designer for modeling strategy, a coder for implementation and debugging, and a tuner for training and performance optimization. Each sub-agent is itself a large language model (LLM) based agent capable of multi-step reasoning and tool use, enabling end-to-end automation of the AI model development process that goes beyond the scope of existing AutoML approaches. We evaluate AIBuildAI on MLE-Bench, a benchmark of realistic Kaggle-style AI development tasks spanning visual, textual, time-series and tabular modalities. AIBuildAI ranks first on MLE-Bench with a medal rate of 63.1%, outperforming all existing baseline methods and matching the capability of highly experienced AI engineers. These results demonstrate that hierarchical agent systems can automate the full AI model development process from task specification to deployable model, suggesting a pathway toward broadly accessible AI development with minimal human intervention.

AI.tldr: AIBuildAI automates end-to-end AI model development using a hierarchical, LLM-based agent system, achieving top performance on MLE-Bench and matching expert engineers.
AI.motivation: To overcome AutoML limitations that cover only narrow aspects (e.g., hyperparameter optimization) and reduce reliance on human expertise across the full AI development lifecycle.
AI.method: A manager agent coordinates three specialized sub-agents (designer for modeling strategy, coder for implementation/debugging, tuner for training/optimization), each an LLM-based agent capable of multi-step reasoning and tool use, enabling end-to-end model development from task description and data.
AI.result: On MLE-Bench, AIBuildAI ranks first with a 63.1% medal rate, outperforming baselines and matching the capabilities of highly experienced AI engineers.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical AI agent systems can automate the full AI model development process, enabling broadly accessible AI development with minimal human intervention.

title: Graph Theoretical Outlier Rejection for 4D Radar Registration in Feature-Poor Environments
summary: Automotive 4D imaging radar is well suited for operation in dusty and low-visibility environments, but scan registration remains challenging due to scan sparsity and spurious detections caused by noise and multipath reflections. This difficulty is compounded in feature-poor open-pit mines, where the lack of distinctive landmarks reduces correspondence reliability. We integrate graph-based pairwise consistency maximization (PCM) as an outlier rejection step within the iterative closest points (ICP) loop. We propose a radar-adapted pairwise distance-invariant scoring function for graph-based (PCM) that incorporates anisotropic, per-detection uncertainty derived from a radar measurement model. The consistency maximization problem is approximated with a greedy heuristic that finds a large clique in the pairwise consistency graph. The refined correspondence set improves robustness when the initial association set is heavily contaminated. We evaluate a standard Euclidean distance residual and our uncertainty-aware residual on an open-pit mine dataset collected with a 4D imaging radar. Compared to the generalized ICP (GICP) baseline without PCM, our method reduces segment relative position error (RPE) by 29.6% on 1 m segments and by up to 55% on 100 m segments. The presented method is intended for integration into localization pipelines and is suitable for online use due to the greedy heuristic in graph-based (PCM).

AI.tldr: Radar-ICP with graph-based PCM and radar-uncertainty-aware scoring improves sparse 4D radar scan registration in feature-poor mines, achieving notable RPE reductions vs GICP, enabling online localization.
AI.motivation: Sparse, noisy radar data and lack of distinctive landmarks cause poor data association; integrating PCM as an outlier rejection within ICP and incorporating radar-specific uncertainty can improve robustness.
AI.method: Integrate graph-based pairwise consistency maximization (PCM) into ICP. Use a radar-adapted, distance-invariant score that accounts for anisotropic per-detection uncertainty from radar model. Greedy heuristic to extract a large clique in the pairwise consistency graph to refine correspondences. Compare standard Euclidean residual and uncertainty-aware residual on open-pit mine 4D radar data.
AI.result: Compared to GICP baseline without PCM, segment RPE reduced by 29.6% on 1 m segments and up to 55% on 100 m segments; the method improves robustness when initial associations are heavily contaminated; online feasible due to greedy clique search.
AI.conclusion: Radar-adapted PCM within ICP yields more robust scan registration for automotive 4D radar in challenging environments, suitable for integration into localization pipelines.

title: WILD-SAM: Phase-Aware Expert Adaptation of SAM for Landslide Detection in Wrapped InSAR Interferograms
summary: Detecting slow-moving landslides directly from wrapped Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) interferograms is crucial for efficient geohazard monitoring, yet it remains fundamentally challenged by severe phase ambiguity and complex coherence noise. While the Segment Anything Model (SAM) offers a powerful foundation for segmentation, its direct transfer to wrapped phase data is hindered by a profound spectral domain shift, which suppresses the high-frequency fringes essential for boundary delineation. To bridge this gap, we propose WILD-SAM, a novel parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework specifically designed to adapt SAM for high-precision landslide detection on wrapped interferograms. Specifically, the architecture integrates a Phase-Aware Mixture-of-Experts (PA-MoE) Adapter into the frozen encoder to align spectral distributions and introduces a Wavelet-Guided Subband Enhancement (WGSE) strategy to generate frequency-aware dense prompts. The PA-MoE Adapter exploits a dynamic routing mechanism across heterogeneous convolutional experts to adaptively aggregate multi-scale spectral-textural priors, effectively aligning the distribution discrepancy between natural images and interferometric phase data. Meanwhile, the WGSE strategy leverages discrete wavelet transforms to explicitly disentangle high-frequency subbands and refine directional phase textures, injecting these structural cues as dense prompts to ensure topological integrity along sharp landslide boundaries. Extensive experiments on the ISSLIDE and ISSLIDE+ benchmarks demonstrate that WILD-SAM achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing methods in both target completeness and contour fidelity.

AI.tldr: WILD-SAM adaptively tunes SAM for wrapped InSAR phase data via a Phase-Aware MoE adapter and Wavelet-Guided Subband Enhancement, achieving state-of-the-art landslide detection on ISSLIDE datasets with improved boundary fidelity.
AI.motivation: Detect slow-moving landslides directly from wrapped InSAR interferograms; challenge arises from severe phase ambiguity and coherence noise, plus a spectral-domain shift between natural images and interferometric phase data, hindering standard foundation-model transfer.
AI.method: A parameter-efficient fine-tuning framework that: (1) inserts a Phase-Aware Mixture-of-Experts (PA-MoE) adapter into a frozen encoder to align spectral distributions using dynamic routing across heterogeneous convolutional experts; (2) uses Wavelet-Guided Subband Enhancement (WGSE) with discrete wavelet transforms to disentangle high-frequency subbands and refine directional phase textures, injecting these cues as dense prompts to the model to preserve boundary topology.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ISSLIDE and ISSLIDE+ show state-of-the-art performance, with significant gains in target completeness and contour fidelity compared with existing methods.
AI.conclusion: WILD-SAM provides a parameter-efficient, phase-aware adaptation of SAM for high-precision landslide detection in wrapped InSAR data, bridging spectral-domain gaps and preserving sharp boundaries.

title: Enhancing LLM-based Search Agents via Contribution Weighted Group Relative Policy Optimization
comment: Accepted to the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026), Main Conference
summary: Search agents extend Large Language Models (LLMs) beyond static parametric knowledge by enabling access to up-to-date and long-tail information unavailable during pretraining. While reinforcement learning has been widely adopted for training such agents, existing approaches face key limitations: process supervision often suffers from unstable value estimation, whereas outcome supervision struggles with credit assignment due to sparse, trajectory-level rewards. To bridge this gap, we propose Contribution-Weighted GRPO (CW-GRPO), a framework that integrates process supervision into group relative policy optimization. Instead of directly optimizing process rewards, CW-GRPO employs an LLM judge to assess the retrieval utility and reasoning correctness at each search round, producing per-round contribution scores. These scores are used to rescale outcome-based advantages along the trajectory, enabling fine-grained credit assignment without sacrificing optimization stability. Experiments on multiple knowledge-intensive benchmarks show that CW-GRPO outperforms standard GRPO by 5.0\% on Qwen3-8B and 6.3\% on Qwen3-1.7B, leading to more effective search behaviors. Additional analysis reveals that successful trajectories exhibit concentrated contributions across rounds, providing empirical insight into search agent tasks.

AI.tldr: CW-GRPO introduces per-round contribution-weighted credit assignment in group relative policy optimization for LLM-based search agents, using an LLM judge to score round-level retrieval utility and reasoning, yielding better performance than standard GRPO on Qwen models (≈5–6% gains).
AI.motivation: To address two limitations in LLM-based search agents: unstable value estimation under process supervision and poor credit assignment under sparse, trajectory-level rewards in outcome supervision.
AI.method: Incorporate an LLM judge to assess per-round retrieval utility and reasoning correctness during search rounds, producing per-round contribution scores; use these scores to rescale outcome-based advantages along the trajectory within GRPO, enabling fine-grained credit assignment without destabilizing training.
AI.result: CW-GRPO outperforms standard GRPO by 5.0% on Qwen3-8B and 6.3% on Qwen3-1.7B, improving search behaviors; empirical analysis shows successful trajectories concentrate contributions across rounds.
AI.conclusion: Contribution-weighted credit assignment enhances stability and effectiveness of policy optimization for search agents, and offers empirical insight into how contributions distribute across search rounds, with implications for knowledge-intensive tasks.

title: Improving Human Performance with Value-Aware Interventions: A Case Study in Chess
summary: AI systems are increasingly used to assist humans in sequential decision-making tasks, yet determining when and how an AI assistant should intervene remains a fundamental challenge. A potential baseline is to recommend the optimal action according to a strong model. However, such actions assume optimal follow-up actions, which human decision makers may fail to execute, potentially reducing overall performance. In this work, we propose and study value-aware interventions, motivated by a basic principle in reinforcement learning: under the Bellman equation, the optimal policy selects actions that maximize the immediate reward plus the value function. When a decision maker follows a suboptimal policy, this policy-value consistency no longer holds, creating discrepancies between the actions taken by the policy and those that maximize the immediate reward plus the value of the next state. We show that these policy-value inconsistencies naturally identify opportunities for intervention. We formalize this problem in a Markov decision process where an AI assistant may override human actions under an intervention budget. In the single-intervention regime, we show that the optimal strategy is to recommend the action that maximizes the human value function. For settings with multiple interventions, we propose a tractable approximation that prioritizes interventions based on the magnitude of the policy-value discrepancy. We evaluate these ideas in the domain of chess by learning models of humans from large-scale gameplay data. In simulation, our approach consistently outperforms interventions based on the strongest chess engine (Stockfish) in a wide range of settings. A within-subject human study with 20 players and 600 games further shows that our interventions significantly improve performance for low- and mid-skill players while matching expert-engine interventions for high-skill players.

AI.tldr: Value-aware interventions use policy-value discrepancies to identify when an AI assistant should override human decisions under a finite intervention budget; single-intervention optimal action maximizes the human value function, while multi-intervention cases use a discrepancy-based priority; demonstrated in chess with simulations and a human study showing benefits for lower/mid-skill players and parity with expert interventions for high-skill players.
AI.motivation: To improve AI-assisted sequential decision-making by aligning interventions with the value function, accounting for human suboptimal follow-through and leveraging Bellman consistency to detect when human actions degrade expected value.
AI.method: Formulate the problem as a Markov decision process with an intervention budget. Derive the optimal single-intervention policy that maximizes the human value function. For multiple interventions, introduce a tractable approximation based on policy-value discrepancy magnitudes. Learn human behavior models from large-scale chess data and evaluate via simulation against Stockfish-based interventions and through a within-subject human study.
AI.result: In simulation, value-aware interventions consistently outperform interventions based on the strongest engine (Stockfish) across a wide range of settings. In a human study with 20 players and 600 games, interventions improved performance for low- and mid-skill players and matched expert-engine interventions for high-skill players.
AI.conclusion: Policy-value consistency provides a principled criterion for AI-assisted intervention, enabling value-aware support that compensates for suboptimal human execution and adapts to skill level; effective in chess and potentially transferable to other sequential decision-making domains.

title: 4D Radar Gaussian Modeling and Scan Matching with RCS
comment: This version is an Extended Abstract, sent to the Radar in Robotics: New Frontiers workshop of ICRA 2026
summary: 4D millimeter-wave (mmWave) radars are increasingly used in robotics, as they offer robustness against adverse environmental conditions. Besides the usual XYZ position, they provide Doppler velocity measurements as well as Radar Cross Section (RCS) information for every point. While Doppler is widely used to filter out dynamic points, RCS is often overlooked and not usually used in modeling and scan matching processes. Building on previous 3D Gaussian modeling and scan matching work, we propose incorporating the physical behavior of RCS in the model, in order to further enrich the summarized information about the scene, and improve the scan matching process.

AI.tldr: Integrates Radar Cross Section (RCS) into 3D Gaussian-based scan matching for 4D mmWave radar to leverage physical reflectivity cues alongside Doppler and position data.
AI.motivation: RCS provides informative, physics-based scene cues that are currently underutilized in radar-based mapping and scan matching; leveraging RCS could enrich scene representation and improve data association and robustness.
AI.method: Extend the existing 3D Gaussian modeling and scan matching framework to incorporate RCS as an additional feature. Formulate a joint likelihood over spatial coordinates and RCS (and potentially Doppler) and integrate this RCS-aware model into the scan matching optimization, building on prior 3D Gaussian modeling and scan matching work.
AI.result: Abstract does not report experimental results or quantitative outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating RCS into the Gaussian-based radar model is a promising approach to exploit reflectivity physics for improved scene understanding and scan matching; practical benefits will depend on handling RCS variability, measurement noise, and calibration, with potential improvements in robustness in adverse conditions.

title: Giving Faces Their Feelings Back: Explicit Emotion Control for Feedforward Single-Image 3D Head Avatars
summary: We present a framework for explicit emotion control in feed-forward, single-image 3D head avatar reconstruction. Unlike existing pipelines where emotion is implicitly entangled with geometry or appearance, we treat emotion as a first-class control signal that can be manipulated independently and consistently across identities. Our method injects emotion into existing feed-forward architectures via a dual-path modulation mechanism without modifying their core design. Geometry modulation performs emotion-conditioned normalization in the original parametric space, disentangling emotional state from speech-driven articulation, while appearance modulation captures identity-aware, emotion-dependent visual cues beyond geometry. To enable learning under this setting, we construct a time-synchronized, emotion-consistent multi-identity dataset by transferring aligned emotional dynamics across identities. Integrated into multiple state-of-the-art backbones, our framework preserves reconstruction and reenactment fidelity while enabling controllable emotion transfer, disentangled manipulation, and smooth emotion interpolation, advancing expressive and scalable 3D head avatars.

AI.tldr: A modular dual-path framework enabling explicit emotion control in feed-forward 3D head avatar reconstruction, with geometry-level emotion-conditioned normalization and appearance-level identity-aware cues, integrated into existing backbones and trained on a time-synchronized multi-identity dataset to enable controllable emotion transfer, disentangled manipulation, and smooth interpolation.
AI.motivation: Allow explicit, consistent control of emotional state across identities in 3D head avatars by decoupling emotion from geometry and speech-driven articulation, addressing entanglement in current pipelines and enabling scalable, expressive avatars.
AI.method: Inject emotion via a dual-path modulation mechanism into existing feed-forward architectures without altering core design: (1) geometry modulation applies emotion-conditioned normalization in the original parametric space to separate emotion from articulation; (2) appearance modulation captures identity-aware, emotion-dependent visual cues beyond geometry. Training relies on a time-synchronized, emotion-consistent multi-identity dataset created by transferring aligned emotional dynamics across identities, and the framework is integrated into multiple state-of-the-art backbones.
AI.result: Preserves reconstruction and reenactment fidelity while enabling controllable emotion transfer, disentangled manipulation, and smooth emotion interpolation across identities, advancing expressive and scalable 3D head avatars.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual-path, plug-in emotion control framework provides explicit, scalable control over emotional expression in 3D head avatars, compatible with existing backbones, and capable of robust emotion transfer and interpolation across identities.

title: Quantum-inspired tensor networks in machine learning models
comment: 28 pages, 11 figures, article class. The interactive version of the graph can be found at https://github.com/gvalverde21/research-graph-TensorNetworks-MachineLearning
summary: Tensor networks were developed in the context of many-body physics as compressed representations of multiparticle quantum states. These representations mitigate the exponential complexity of many-body systems by capturing only the most relevant dependencies. Due to the formal similarity between quantum entanglement and statistical correlations, tensor networks have recently been integrated in machine learning, operating both as alternative learning architectures and as decompositions of components of neural networks. The expectation is that the theoretical understanding of tensor networks developed within quantum many-body physics leads to novel methods that offer advantages in terms of computational efficiency, explainability, or privacy. Here we review the use of tensor networks in the context of machine learning, providing a critical assessment of the state of the art, the potential advantages, and the challenges that must be overcome.

AI.tldr: Tensor networks (TN) offer compressed, structured representations for ML by leveraging ideas from quantum many-body physics to model correlations efficiently. They are used as both alternative learning architectures and as decompositions within neural networks, aiming to improve efficiency, explainability, and privacy. This paper reviews the state of the art, the potential benefits, and the main challenges in applying TNs to ML.
AI.motivation: To assess how tensor networks can improve machine learning—through compact representations, structured factorization, and potential privacy/interpretability advantages—and to synthesize current methods, benefits, and obstacles.
AI.method: Critical review of existing literature on tensor networks in machine learning, categorized by use as (i) end-to-end TN architectures and (ii) TN-based decompositions of neural components; evaluation of theoretical foundations, practical implementations, performance metrics, and benchmarks; identification of advantages, limitations, and open research questions.
AI.result: TNs enable compact representations that capture relevant dependencies, offering potential gains in compression, structured interpretability, and privacy-preserving properties; they provide alternative architectures and factorization tools for neural networks. However, challenges remain in scalability, training stability, integration with standard ML pipelines, and a comprehensive theoretical understanding; the field faces gaps in benchmarks and guidelines for selecting TN architectures and decompositions.
AI.conclusion: Tensor networks hold promise for ML by delivering efficient and interpretable representations and decompositions, but realizing their full potential requires addressing scalability, training, and theoretical gaps. Future work should focus on standardized benchmarks, principled architecture design, and cross-pollination with quantum-information theory to unlock practical advantages.

title: Response-Aware User Memory Selection for LLM Personalization
summary: A common approach to personalization in large language models (LLMs) is to incorporate a subset of the user memory into the prompt at inference time to guide the model's generation. Existing methods select these subsets primarily using similarity between user memory items and input queries, ignoring how features actually affect the model's response distribution. We propose Response-Utility optimization for Memory Selection (RUMS), a novel method that selects user memory items by measuring the mutual information between a subset of memory and the model's outputs, identifying items that reduce response uncertainty and sharpen predictions beyond semantic similarity. We demonstrate that this information-theoretic foundation enables more principled user memory selection that aligns more closely with human selection compared to state-of-the-art methods, and models $400\times$ larger. Additionally, we show that memory items selected using RUMS result in better response quality compared to existing approaches, while having up to $95\%$ reduction in computational cost.

AI.tldr: RUMS selects memory items for LLM personalization by mutual information with model outputs, outperforming similarity-based methods and reducing computation while improving response quality.
AI.motivation: Improve personalization by aligning memory selection with their actual influence on the model's output distribution, not just semantic similarity; increase efficiency.
AI.method: Measure the mutual information between subsets of user memory and the model's output distribution, selecting items that reduce response uncertainty and sharpen predictions; compare to semantic-similarity baselines; assess human-alignment and scalability.
AI.result: RUMS yields memory selections more aligned with human preferences than state-of-the-art methods; scales to models ~400x larger; memory items chosen with RUMS improve response quality and cut computational cost by up to 95%.
AI.conclusion: An information-theoretic approach to memory selection provides principled, efficient personalization for LLMs, outperforming similarity-based methods and enabling scalable deployment.

title: An Intelligent Robotic and Bio-Digestor Framework for Smart Waste Management
comment: 8 pages, 10 figures, submitted to 7th International Conference on Smart Systems and Inventive Technology (ICSSIT 2026)
summary: Rapid urbanization and continuous population growth have made municipal solid waste management increasingly challenging. These challenges highlight the need for smarter and automated waste management solutions. This paper presents the design and evaluation of an integrated waste management framework that combines two connected systems, a robotic waste segregation module and an optimized bio-digestor. The robotic waste segregation system uses a MyCobot 280 Jetson Nano robotic arm along with YOLOv8 object detection and robot operating system (ROS)-based path planning to identify and sort waste in real time. It classifies waste into four different categories with high precision, reducing the need for manual intervention. After segregation, the biodegradable waste is transferred to a bio-digestor system equipped with multiple sensors. These sensors continuously monitor key parameters, including temperature, pH, pressure, and motor revolutions per minute. The Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) algorithm, combined with a regression model, is used to dynamically adjust system parameters. This intelligent optimization approach ensures stable operation and maximizes digestion efficiency under varying environmental conditions. System testing under dynamic conditions demonstrates a sorting accuracy of 98% along with highly efficient biological conversion. The proposed framework offers a scalable, intelligent, and practical solution for modern waste management, making it suitable for both residential and industrial applications.

AI.tldr: Integrated waste management framework combining robotic segregation (YOLOv8/ROS) and PSO-optimized bio-digestor, achieving 98% sorting accuracy and enhanced digestion efficiency.
AI.motivation: Rapid urbanization and population growth intensify municipal solid waste management challenges, necessitating automated, scalable, and intelligent solutions that reduce manual intervention while improving throughput and efficiency.
AI.method: Robotic segregation using a MyCobot 280 with Jetson Nano, YOLOv8 for four-category waste classification, and ROS-based path planning for real-time sorting. Biodegradable waste is sent to a sensor-equipped bio-digestor (temperature, pH, pressure, RPM). Particle Swarm Optimization combined with regression dynamically tunes system parameters to maximize digestion efficiency under varying conditions. Validation under dynamic conditions.
AI.result: Sorting accuracy of 98% and highly efficient biological conversion, indicating robust performance and potential for scalable deployment.
AI.conclusion: The integrated framework offers a scalable, intelligent solution for modern waste management, suitable for both residential and industrial applications, by uniting automated sorting with optimized digestion.

title: Controllable Video Object Insertion via Multiview Priors
summary: Video object insertion is a critical task for dynamically inserting new objects into existing environments. Previous video generation methods focus primarily on synthesizing entire scenes while struggling with ensuring consistent object appearance, spatial alignment, and temporal coherence when inserting objects into existing videos. In this paper, we propose a novel solution for Video Object Insertion, which integrates multi-view object priors to address the common challenges of appearance inconsistency and occlusion handling in dynamic environments. By lifting 2D reference images into multi-view representations and leveraging a dual-path view-consistent conditioning mechanism, our framework ensures stable identity guidance and robust integration across diverse viewpoints. A quality-aware weighting mechanism is also employed to adaptively handle noisy or imperfect inputs. Additionally, we introduce an Integration-Aware Consistency Module that guarantees spatial realism, effectively resolving occlusion and boundary artifacts while maintaining temporal continuity across frames. Experimental results show that our solution significantly improves the quality of video object insertion, providing stable and realistic integration.

AI.tldr: A multi-view priors-based video object insertion framework with dual-path conditioning and integration-aware consistency to ensure stable appearance, occlusion handling, and temporal coherence, achieving higher-quality video insertions.
AI.motivation: Address appearance inconsistency, occlusions, and temporal instability when inserting objects into existing videos; overcome limitations of full-scene synthesis methods by leveraging multi-view object priors and robust conditioning.
AI.method: Lift 2D reference images into multi-view representations; apply dual-path view-consistent conditioning; use quality-aware weighting to handle noisy inputs; integrate an Integration-Aware Consistency Module to ensure spatial realism and temporal continuity.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate significant improvements in insertion quality, with stable identity guidance, reduced occlusion/boundary artifacts, and better temporal coherence across frames.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a robust, view-consistent video object insertion framework leveraging multi-view priors, showing practical gains in realism and stability across diverse viewpoints.

title: Heat and Matérn Kernels on Matchings
summary: Applying kernel methods to matchings is challenging due to their discrete, non-Euclidean nature. In this paper, we develop a principled framework for constructing geometric kernels that respect the natural geometry of the space of matchings. To this end, we first provide a complete characterization of stationary kernels, i.e. kernels that respect the inherent symmetries of this space. Because the class of stationary kernels is too broad, we specifically focus on the heat and Matérn kernel families, adding an appropriate inductive bias of smoothness to stationarity. While these families successfully extend widely popular Euclidean kernels to matchings, evaluating them naively incurs a prohibitive super-exponential computational cost. To overcome this difficulty, we introduce and analyze a novel, sub-exponential algorithm leveraging zonal polynomials for efficient kernel evaluation. Finally, motivated by the known bijective correspondence between matchings and phylogenetic trees-a crucial data modality in biology-we explore whether our framework can be seamlessly transferred to the space of trees, establishing novel negative results and identifying a significant open problem.

AI.tldr: Principled geometry-aware kernels for matchings are developed, focusing on stationary kernels (heat and Matérn) with a smoothness bias, and a sub-exponential algorithm via zonal polynomials; the framework is tested for transfer to the space of phylogenetic trees, yielding negative results and highlighting an open problem.
AI.motivation: Matchings live in a discrete, non-Euclidean space, so kernels must respect its geometry. The bijection between matchings and trees motivates extending the approach to phylogenetic trees.
AI.method: Complete characterization of stationary kernels on the matching space; specialization to heat and Matérn kernels with added smoothness bias; development of a sub-exponential algorithm using zonal polynomials to evaluate kernels efficiently.
AI.result: The framework extends widely used Euclidean kernels to matchings and provides a practical sub-exponential evaluation technique; attempting to transfer the framework to trees yields negative results but reveals an important open problem.
AI.conclusion: Stationary, smoothness-biased kernels (heat and Matérn) can be adapted to matchings with a tractable evaluation strategy; transferring to tree spaces is nontrivial and warrants further investigation.

title: Evo-MedAgent: Beyond One-Shot Diagnosis with Agents That Remember, Reflect, and Improve
summary: Tool-augmented large language model (LLM) agents can orchestrate specialist classifiers, segmentation models, and visual question-answering modules to interpret chest X-rays. However, these agents still solve each case in isolation: they fail to accumulate experience across cases, correct recurrent reasoning mistakes, or adapt their tool-use behavior without expensive reinforcement learning. While a radiologist naturally improves with every case, current agents remain static. In this work, we propose Evo-MedAgent, a self-evolving memory module that equips a medical agent with the capacity for inter-case learning at test time. Our memory comprises three complementary stores: (1)~\emph{Retrospective Clinical Episodes} that retrieve problem-solving experiences from similar past cases, (2)~an \emph{Adaptive Procedural Heuristics} bank curating priority-tagged diagnostic rules that evolves via reflection, much like a physician refining their internal criteria, and (3)~a \emph{Tool Reliability Controller} that tracks per-tool trustworthiness. On ChestAgentBench, Evo-MedAgent raises multiple-choice question (MCQ) accuracy from 0.68 to 0.79 on GPT-5-mini, and from 0.76 to 0.87 on Gemini-3 Flash. With a strong base model, evolving memory improves performance more effectively than orchestrating external tools on qualitative diagnostic tasks. Because Evo-MedAgent requires no training, its per-case overhead is bounded by one additional retrieval pass and a single reflection call, making it deployable on top of any frozen model.

AI.tldr: Self-evolving memory module Evo-MedAgent enables inter-case learning for LLM-based medical agents without retraining, using retrospective episodes, adaptive heuristics, and a tool reliability controller; achieves notable MCQ accuracy gains on ChestAgentBench with strong base models; scalable and deployable on frozen models.
AI.motivation: Current tool-augmented agents solve cases in isolation and do not accumulate experience, correct recurrent reasoning errors, or adapt tool use without expensive RL; radiologists improve with each case, motivating test-time inter-case learning.
AI.method: Three memory stores: (1) Retrospective Clinical Episodes retrieving experiences from similar past cases; (2) Adaptive Procedural Heuristics bank with priority-tagged diagnostic rules updated via reflection; (3) Tool Reliability Controller tracking per-tool trustworthiness. Memory updates occur at test time via retrieval and a reflection call; no training required; compatible with frozen base models.
AI.result: On ChestAgentBench, MCQ accuracy improves from 0.68 to 0.79 for GPT-5-mini and from 0.76 to 0.87 for Gemini-3 Flash. With a strong base model, evolving memory outperforms pure external tool orchestration in qualitative tasks.
AI.conclusion: Evo-MedAgent enables inter-case learning without additional training, provides bounded per-case overhead (one retrieval pass and one reflection), and is deployable on top of frozen models; memory stores drive improved diagnostic performance.

title: HRDexDB: A Large-Scale Dataset of Dexterous Human and Robotic Hand Grasps
summary: We present HRDexDB, a large-scale, multi-modal dataset of high-fidelity dexterous grasping sequences featuring both human and diverse robotic hands. Unlike existing datasets, HRDexDB provides a comprehensive collection of grasping trajectories across human hands and multiple robot hand embodiments, spanning 100 diverse objects. Leveraging state-of-the-art vision methods and a new dedicated multi-camera system, our HRDexDB offers high-precision spatiotemporal 3D ground-truth motion for both the agent and the manipulated object. To facilitate the study of physical interaction, HRDexDB includes high-resolution tactile signals, synchronized multi-view video, and egocentric video streams. The dataset comprises 1.4K grasping trials, encompassing both successes and failures, each enriched with visual, kinematic, and tactile modalities. By providing closely aligned captures of human dexterity and robotic execution on the same target objects under comparable grasping motions, HRDexDB serves as a foundational benchmark for multi-modal policy learning and cross-domain dexterous manipulation.

AI.tldr: HRDexDB is a large-scale, multi-modal dexterous grasping dataset linking human and robotic hands across 100 objects with high-fidelity 3D ground-truth motion, tactile signals, and synchronized multi-view video to support cross-domain policy learning.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap in existing datasets by providing aligned dexterous manipulation data across humans and robots, with rich multi-modal signals and high-precision ground truth to facilitate cross-domain transfer and policy learning.
AI.method: Data collection using a dedicated multi-camera system; high-fidelity 3D motion capture for both agent and object; synchronized tactile sensing; egocentric and multi-view video; 1.4k grasping trials across 100 objects and multiple robotic hand embodiments; includes successes and failures.
AI.result: 1.4K trials with multi-modal annotations; high-precision spatiotemporal ground-truth; enables baseline benchmarks for multi-modal policy learning and cross-domain dexterous manipulation.
AI.conclusion: HRDexDB establishes a foundational benchmark for multi-modal, cross-domain dexterous manipulation research, enabling advanced policy learning and deeper study of physical interaction.

title: The Fourth Challenge on Image Super-Resolution ($\times$4) at NTIRE 2026: Benchmark Results and Method Overview
summary: This paper presents the NTIRE 2026 image super-resolution ($\times$4) challenge, one of the associated competitions of the NTIRE 2026 Workshop at CVPR 2026. The challenge aims to reconstruct high-resolution (HR) images from low-resolution (LR) inputs generated through bicubic downsampling with a $\times$4 scaling factor. The objective is to develop effective super-resolution solutions and analyze recent advances in the field. To reflect the evolving objectives of image super-resolution, the challenge includes two tracks: (1) a restoration track, which emphasizes pixel-wise fidelity and ranks submissions based on PSNR; and (2) a perceptual track, which focuses on visual realism and evaluates results using a perceptual score. A total of 194 participants registered for the challenge, with 31 teams submitting valid entries. This report summarizes the challenge design, datasets, evaluation protocol, main results, and methods of participating teams. The challenge provides a unified benchmark and offers insights into current progress and future directions in image super-resolution.

AI.tldr: NTIRE 2026 ×4 image SR challenge report with two tracks (restoration via PSNR; perceptual via perceptual score), 194 registered participants and 31 valid submissions; summarizes challenge design, datasets, evaluation protocol, main results, and methods, offering a unified benchmark and directions for future SR research.
AI.motivation: Advance image super-resolution research through a standardized, multi-track benchmark that balances pixel fidelity and perceptual realism, enabling analysis of current approaches and guiding future directions.
AI.method: Challenge design and evaluation: LR inputs created by bicubic downsampling with a ×4 factor; two tracks (restoration with PSNR-based ranking and perceptual with a perceptual score); description of datasets, evaluation protocol, and submission workflow; synthesis of participating teams' methods.
AI.result: Participation included 194 registered participants and 31 teams with valid entries; the report compiles main results, methodologies of entrants, and insights into current progress and limitations.
AI.conclusion: The NTIRE 2026 SR×4 challenge provides a unified benchmark for image super-resolution and yields actionable insights into prevailing approaches and future research directions.

title: Thermodynamic Diffusion Inference with Minimal Digital Conditioning
summary:
Diffusion-model inference and overdamped Langevin dynamics are formally identical. A physical substrate that encodes the score function therefore equilibrates to the correct output by thermodynamics alone, requiring no digital arithmetic during inference and potentially achieving a $10{,}000\times$ reduction in energy relative to a GPU. Two fundamental barriers have until now prevented this equivalence from being realized at production scale: non-local skip connections, which locally coupled analog substrates cannot represent, and input conditioning, in which the coupling constants carry roughly $2{,}600\times$ too little signal to anchor the system to a specific input.
  We resolve both obstacles. \emph{Hierarchical bilinear coupling} encodes U-Net skip connections as rank-$k$ inter-module interactions derived directly from the singular structure of the encoder and decoder Gram matrices, requiring only $O(Dk)$ physical connections instead of $O(D^2)$. A \emph{minimal digital interface} -- a 4-dimensional bottleneck encoder together with a 16-unit transfer network, totalling \textbf{2,560 parameters} -- overcomes the conditioning barrier. When evaluated on activations drawn from a trained denoising U-Net, the complete system attains a decoder cosine similarity of \textbf{0.9906} against an oracle upper bound of 1.0000, while preserving theoretical net energy savings of approximately $10^7\times$ over GPU inference. These results constitute the first demonstration of trained-weight, production-scale thermodynamic diffusion inference.

AI.tldr: Physical diffusion-inference substrate using hierarchical bilinear coupling and a minimal digital interface achieves near-ideal decoder performance with substantial energy savings, enabling production-scale thermodynamic diffusion inference.
AI.motivation: Overcome two barriers preventing diffusion-model equivalence with overdamped Langevin dynamics at scale: non-local skip connections and insufficient input conditioning; aim for energy-efficient, non-digital inference.
AI.method: Encode U-Net skip connections via rank-k inter-module interactions derived from encoder/decoder Gram matrices (hierarchical bilinear coupling). Add minimal digital interface: 4D bottleneck encoder + 16-unit transfer network, 2,560 parameters.
AI.result: On activations from a trained denoising U-Net, decoder cosine similarity 0.9906 vs oracle 1.0000; net energy savings ~ 1e7× over GPU; first demonstration of trained-weight, production-scale thermodynamic diffusion inference.
AI.conclusion: Shows feasibility of using physical substrates for scalable, energy-efficient diffusion inference and identifies a viable path to production deployment; potential limitations include integration complexity and hardware variability.

title: Seeing Through Circuits: Faithful Mechanistic Interpretability for Vision Transformers
summary: Transparency of neural networks' internal reasoning is at the heart of interpretability research, adding to trust, safety, and understanding of these models. The field of mechanistic interpretability has recently focused on studying task-specific computational graphs, defined by connections (edges) between model components. Such edge-based circuits have been defined in the context of large language models, yet vision-based approaches so far only consider neuron-based circuits. These tell which information is encoded, but not how it is routed through the complex wiring of a neural network. In this work, we investigate whether useful mechanistic circuits can be identified through computational graphs in vision transformers. We propose an effective method for Automatic Visual Circuit Discovery (Vi-CD) that recovers class-specific circuits for classification, identifies circuits underlying typographic attacks in CLIP, and discovers circuits that lend themselves for steering to correct harmful model behavior. Overall, we find that insightful and actionable edge-based circuits can be recovered from vision transformers, adding transparency to the internal computations of these models.

AI.tldr: Edge-based mechanistic circuits can be discovered in vision transformers using Vi-CD, enabling class-specific, attack-related, and steerable circuits that enhance transparency.
AI.motivation: Extend mechanistic interpretability from neuron-focused analyses to edge-based computations in vision models; improve transparency, safety against manipulation (e.g., typographic attacks), and controllability.
AI.method: Propose Automatic Visual Circuit Discovery (Vi-CD) to extract task-specific computational graphs from vision transformers, identify class-specific edge circuits, diagnose typographic-attack circuits in CLIP, and reveal circuits amenable to steering to correct harmful behavior.
AI.result: Vi-CD recovers actionable and insightful edge-based circuits in vision transformers, demonstrating their role in information routing and potential for intervention.
AI.conclusion: Edge-based circuits exist in vision transformers; Vi-CD provides a viable approach to expose and leverage them for improved interpretability, safety, and controllability of vision models.

title: POMDP-based Object Search with Growing State Space and Hybrid Action Domain
summary: Efficiently locating target objects in complex indoor environments with diverse furniture, such as shelves, tables, and beds, is a significant challenge for mobile robots. This difficulty arises from factors like localization errors, limited fields of view, and visual occlusion. We address this by framing the object-search task as a highdimensional Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) with a growing state space and hybrid (continuous and discrete) action spaces in 3D environments. Based on a meticulously designed perception module, a novel online POMDP solver named the growing neural process filtered k-center clustering tree (GNPF-kCT) is proposed to tackle this problem. Optimal actions are selected using Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with belief tree reuse for growing state space, a neural process network to filter useless primitive actions, and k-center clustering hypersphere discretization for efficient refinement of high-dimensional action spaces. A modified upper-confidence bound (UCB), informed by belief differences and action value functions within cells of estimated diameters, guides MCTS expansion. Theoretical analysis validates the convergence and performance potential of our method. To address scenarios with limited information or rewards, we also introduce a guessed target object with a grid-world model as a key strategy to enhance search efficiency. Extensive Gazebo simulations with Fetch and Stretch robots demonstrate faster and more reliable target localization than POMDP-based baselines and state-of-the-art (SOTA) non-POMDP-based solvers, especially large language model (LLM) based methods, in object search under the same computational constraints and perception systems. Real-world tests in office environments confirm the practical applicability of our approach. Project page: https://sites.google.com/view/gnpfkct.

AI.tldr: Efficient 3D indoor object search via a growing POMDP solver (GNPF-kCT) combining MCTS, neural process filtering, and k-center action discretization, with a guessed-target grid-world strategy; demonstrated in Gazebo with Fetch/Stretch and real-world offices, outperforming baselines and LLM-based methods under the same compute.
AI.motivation: Challenge of locating target objects under localization errors, limited FOV, and occlusion in cluttered indoor spaces; need scalable, high-dimension POMDP solutions with hybrid actions.
AI.method: Introduce growing neural process filtered k-center clustering tree (GNPF-kCT); use MCTS with belief-tree reuse for expanding state space; neural process network filters useless primitive actions; k-center clustering discretizes high-dimensional actions; a modified UCB guided by belief differences; theoretical convergence; add guessed-target grid-world strategy.
AI.result: Faster, more reliable localization than POMDP baselines and SOTA non-POMDP solvers (notably LLM-based) under same computation; Gazebo tests with Fetch/Stretch; real-world office tests confirm practicality.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of integrated POMDP-based search framework in 3D indoor environments; theoretical guarantees; plan for further validation and deployment via project page.

title: DVFace: Spatio-Temporal Dual-Prior Diffusion for Video Face Restoration
summary: Video face restoration aims to enhance degraded face videos into high-quality results with realistic facial details, stable identity, and temporal coherence. Recent diffusion-based methods have brought strong generative priors to restoration and enabled more realistic detail synthesis. However, existing approaches for face videos still rely heavily on generic diffusion priors and multi-step sampling, which limit both facial adaptation and inference efficiency. These limitations motivate the use of one-step diffusion for video face restoration, yet achieving faithful facial recovery alongside temporally stable outputs remains challenging. In this paper, we propose, DVFace, a one-step diffusion framework for real-world video face restoration. Specifically, we introduce a spatio-temporal dual-codebook design to extract complementary spatial and temporal facial priors from degraded videos. We further propose an asymmetric spatio-temporal fusion module to inject these priors into the diffusion backbone according to their distinct roles. Evaluation on various benchmarks shows that DVFace delivers superior restoration quality, temporal consistency, and identity preservation compared to recent methods. Code: https://github.com/zhengchen1999/DVFace.

AI.tldr: DVFace proposes a one-step diffusion framework for real-world video face restoration using a spatio-temporal dual-codebook and an asymmetric fusion module to inject priors into the diffusion process, achieving better quality, temporal coherence, and identity preservation.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based video face restoration relies on generic priors and multi-step sampling, which limit facial adaptation and efficiency. A one-step diffusion approach is desirable but challenging to maintain fidelity and temporal stability.
AI.method: Introduce a spatio-temporal dual-codebook to extract complementary spatial and temporal priors from degraded video frames; propose an asymmetric spatio-temporal fusion module to inject these priors into the diffusion backbone.
AI.result: Empirical benchmarks show DVFace achieves superior restoration quality, temporal consistency, and identity preservation compared to recent methods; code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: DVFace demonstrates effective one-step diffusion for video face restoration, delivering improved performance and efficiency with potential for real-world deployment; future work could further optimize priors and fusion strategies.

title: When Missing Becomes Structure: Intent-Preserving Policy Completion from Financial KOL Discourse
comment: Main paper with supplementary material included
summary: Key Opinion Leader (KOL) discourse on social media is widely consumed as investment guidance, yet turning it into executable trading strategies without injecting assumptions about unspecified execution decisions remains an open problem. We observe that the gaps in KOL statements are not random deficiencies but a structured separation: KOLs express directional intent (what to buy or sell and why) while leaving execution decisions (when, how much, how long) systematically unspecified. Building on this observation, we propose an intent-preserving policy completion framework that treats KOL discourse as a partial trading policy and uses offline reinforcement learning to complete the missing execution decisions around the KOL-expressed intent. Experiments on multimodal KOL discourse from YouTube and X (2022-2025) show that KICL achieves the best return and Sharpe ratio on both platforms while maintaining zero unsupported entries and zero directional reversals, and ablations confirm that the full framework yields an 18.9% return improvement over the KOL-aligned baseline.

AI.tldr: Proposes KICL, an offline RL framework to convert KOL discourse into executable trading policies by completing missing execution decisions; reports improved returns and risk-adjusted performance across multimodal data from YouTube and X, with zero policy violations.
AI.motivation: KOL statements convey directional intent but omit execution details; need a method to translate into executable policies without distorting stated intent; offline RL chosen to leverage historical data.
AI.method: Intent-preserving policy completion; treat KOL discourse as partial trading policy; offline RL to fill in 'when, how much, duration'; multimodal data (YouTube, X) from 2022-2025; evaluation metrics include return, Sharpe ratio; ablation tests.
AI.result: KICL achieves best return and Sharpe on both platforms; zero unsupported entries and zero directional reversals; ablation shows 18.9% return improvement over KOL-aligned baseline.
AI.conclusion: Intended to enable actionable trading strategies that respect KOL intent; demonstrates viability of policy-completion approach; potential for safer, higher-quality KOL-based trading systems; generalizable to other domains with partial intent.

title: Pushing the Limits of On-Device Streaming ASR: A Compact, High-Accuracy English Model for Low-Latency Inference
summary: Deploying high-quality automatic speech recognition (ASR) on edge devices requires models that jointly optimize accuracy, latency, and memory footprint while operating entirely on CPU without GPU acceleration. We conduct a systematic empirical study of state-of-the-art ASR architectures, encompassing encoder-decoder, transducer, and LLM-based paradigms, evaluated across batch, chunked, and streaming inference modes. Through a comprehensive benchmark of over 50 configurations spanning OpenAI Whisper, NVIDIA Nemotron, Parakeet TDT, Canary, Conformer Transducer, and Qwen3-ASR, we identify NVIDIA's Nemotron Speech Streaming as the strongest candidate for real-time English streaming on resource-constrained hardware. We then re-implement the complete streaming inference pipeline in ONNX Runtime and conduct a controlled evaluation of multiple post-training quantization strategies, including importance-weighted k-quant, mixed-precision schemes, and round-to-nearest quantization, combined with graph-level operator fusion. These optimizations reduce the model from 2.47 GB to as little as 0.67 GB while maintaining word error rate (WER) within 1% absolute of the full-precision PyTorch baseline. Our recommended configuration, the int4 k-quant variant, achieves 8.20% average streaming WER across eight standard benchmarks, running comfortably faster than real-time on CPU with 0.56 s algorithmic latency, establishing a new quality-efficiency Pareto point for on-device streaming ASR.

AI.tldr: Edge CPU-only streaming ASR can achieve near baseline WER with significant memory reduction using int4 k-quant in ONNX Runtime; Nemotron Speech Streaming identified as best; 0.56 s latency; 0.67 GB model; Pareto-optimal quality-efficiency.
AI.motivation: Deploy high-quality ASR on resource-limited edge devices (CPU only) requiring a balance of accuracy, latency, and memory; current solutions struggle to meet real-time constraints on such hardware.
AI.method: Systematic empirical study of encoder-decoder, transducer, and LLM-based ASR architectures across batch, chunked, and streaming inference modes. Benchmark over 50 configurations including Whisper, Nemotron, Parakeet TDT, Canary, Conformer Transducer, and Qwen3-ASR. Re-implemented streaming pipeline in ONNX Runtime and evaluated post-training quantization strategies (importance-weighted k-quant, mixed-precision, round-to-nearest) with graph-level operator fusion.
AI.result: NVIDIA Nemotron Speech Streaming emerges as the strongest candidate for real-time English streaming on constrained hardware. Quantization and graph fusion reduce model size from 2.47 GB to 0.67 GB while keeping WER within 1% absolute of full-precision PyTorch. The int4 k-quant variant achieves 8.20% average streaming WER across eight benchmarks, with 0.56 s algorithmic latency and real-time- Christmas? (note: latency means under real-time).
AI.conclusion: An ONNX Runtime-based streaming pipeline with int4 k-quant yields a new quality-efficiency Pareto point for on-device streaming ASR; practitioners should consider Nemotron Speech Streaming with IKQ quantization for edge deployments.

title: Momentum-constrained Hybrid Heuristic Trajectory Optimization Framework with Residual-enhanced DRL for Visually Impaired Scenarios
comment: 24 pages, 14 figures. arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2509.15582
summary: Safe and efficient assistive planning for visually impaired scenarios remains challenging, since existing methods struggle with multi-objective optimization, generalization, and interpretability. In response, this paper proposes a Momentum-Constrained Hybrid Heuristic Trajectory Optimization Framework (MHHTOF). To balance multiple objectives of comfort and safety, the framework designs a Heuristic Trajectory Sampling Cluster (HTSC) with a Momentum-Constrained Trajectory Optimization (MTO), which suppresses abrupt velocity and acceleration changes. In addition, a novel residual-enhanced deep reinforcement learning (DRL) module refines candidate trajectories, advancing temporal modeling and policy generalization. Finally, a dual-stage cost modeling mechanism (DCMM) is introduced to regulate optimization, where costs in the Frenet space ensure consistency, and reward-driven adaptive weights in the Cartesian space integrate user preferences for interpretability and user-centric decision-making. Experimental results show that the proposed framework converges in nearly half the iterations of baselines and achieves lower and more stable costs. In complex dynamic scenarios, MHHTOF further demonstrates stable velocity and acceleration curves with reduced risk, confirming its advantages in robustness, safety, and efficiency.

AI.tldr: MHHTOF introduces a momentum-constrained hybrid heuristic trajectory optimization framework for assistive planning, combining HTSC, MTO, residual-enhanced DRL, and DCMM to balance comfort, safety, and interpretability. It achieves faster convergence, lower/stabler costs, and robust trajectories in dynamic scenarios.
AI.motivation: Address safe and efficient assistive planning for visually impaired users, highlighting persistent challenges in multi-objective optimization, generalization, and interpretability.
AI.method: Propose MHHTOF: a Momentum-Constrained Hybrid Trajectory Optimization framework. Key components: (1) Heuristic Trajectory Sampling Cluster (HTSC); (2) Momentum-Constrained Trajectory Optimization (MTO) to damp abrupt velocity/acceleration changes; (3) residual-enhanced deep reinforcement learning (DRL) to refine candidate trajectories with better temporal modeling and generalization; (4) Dual-Stage Cost Modeling Mechanism (DCMM) to regulate optimization via Frenet-space costs for consistency and reward-driven adaptive weights in Cartesian space for interpretability and user-centric decision-making.
AI.result: Empirical results show MHHTOF converges in about half the iterations of baselines, achieves lower and more stable costs, and yields smoother velocity/acceleration with reduced risk in complex dynamics, indicating improved robustness, safety, and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: MHHTOF offers a robust, interpretable, and efficient trajectory optimization framework for assistive planning in visually impaired scenarios, integrating hybrid heuristic optimization, DRL refinement, and dual-stage cost modeling to address multi-objective trade-offs and generalization.

title: Revisiting Token Compression for Accelerating ViT-based Sparse Multi-View 3D Object Detectors
summary: Vision Transformer (ViT)-based sparse multi-view 3D object detectors have achieved remarkable accuracy but still suffer from high inference latency due to heavy token processing. To accelerate these models, token compression has been widely explored. However, our revisit of existing strategies, such as token pruning, merging, and patch size enlargement, reveals that they often discard informative background cues, disrupt contextual consistency, and lose fine-grained semantics, negatively affecting 3D detection. To overcome these limitations, we propose SEPatch3D, a novel framework that dynamically adjusts patch sizes while preserving critical semantic information within coarse patches. Specifically, we design Spatiotemporal-aware Patch Size Selection (SPSS) that assigns small patches to scenes containing nearby objects to preserve fine details and large patches to background-dominated scenes to reduce computation cost. To further mitigate potential detail loss, Informative Patch Selection (IPS) selects the informative patches for feature refinement, and Cross-Granularity Feature Enhancement (CGFE) injects fine-grained details into selected coarse patches, enriching semantic features. Experiments on the nuScenes and Argoverse 2 validation sets show that SEPatch3D achieves up to \textbf{57\%} faster inference than the StreamPETR baseline and \textbf{20\%} higher efficiency than the state-of-the-art ToC3D-faster, while preserving comparable detection accuracy. Code is available at https://github.com/Mingqj/SEPatch3D.

AI.tldr: SEPatch3D introduces dynamic patch sizing for ViT-based sparse multi-view 3D detectors, combining SPSS, IPS, and CGFE to preserve fine-grained details while reducing computation. It assigns small patches to scenes with nearby objects, uses informative patch selection for refinement, and injects fine-grained details into coarse patches. Evaluated on nuScenes and Argoverse 2, it achieves substantial latency reductions with comparable accuracy to baselines, outperforming StreamPETR and ToC3D-faster in efficiency.
AI.motivation: ViT-based sparse 3D detectors incur high inference latency due to heavy token processing. Existing token reduction strategies (pruning, merging, patch enlargement) can discard informative background cues, disrupt context, and degrade fine-grained semantics, harming 3D object detection. A method that reduces tokens while preserving semantic richness is needed.
AI.method: SEPatch3D comprises (1) SPSS: Spatiotemporal-aware Patch Size Selection that assigns small patches to scenes with nearby objects to retain details and large patches to background-dominated scenes to cut computation; (2) IPS: Informative Patch Selection that identifies patches most informative for feature refinement; (3) CGFE: Cross-Granularity Feature Enhancement that injects fine-grained details from selected coarse patches to richer semantic features.
AI.result: On nuScenes and Argoverse 2 validation sets, SEPatch3D achieves up to 57% faster inference than the StreamPETR baseline and ~20% higher efficiency than ToC3D-faster, while maintaining comparable detection accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic patch sizing with targeted refinement (SPSS, IPS, CGFE) can significantly accelerate ViT-based sparse 3D detectors without sacrificing accuracy, demonstrating a practical approach to balance computation and semantic fidelity in multi-view 3D detection. Code is available.

title: Path-Sampled Integrated Gradients
summary: We introduce path-sampled integrated gradients (PS-IG), a framework that generalizes feature attribution by computing the expected value over baselines sampled along the linear interpolation path. We prove that PS-IG is mathematically equivalent to path-weighted integrated gradients, provided the weighting function matches the cumulative distribution function of the sampling density. This equivalence allows the stochastic expectation to be evaluated via a deterministic Riemann sum, improving the error convergence rate from $O(m^{-1/2})$ to $O(m^{-1})$ for smooth models. Furthermore, we demonstrate analytically that PS-IG functions as a variance-reducing filter against gradient noise - strictly lowering attribution variance by a factor of 1/3 under uniform sampling - while preserving key axiomatic properties such as linearity and implementation invariance.

AI.tldr: PS-IG generalizes feature attribution by sampling baselines along the interpolation path; proves equivalence to path-weighted IG when weighting matches the sampling density CDF; enables a deterministic Riemann-sum evaluation to achieve O(m^{-1}) convergence; acts as a variance-reducing filter (factor 1/3 under uniform sampling) while preserving linearity and implementation invariance.
AI.motivation: Address instability and high variance in gradient-based attributions by introducing path-sampled baselines, and to improve convergence and efficiency of attribution estimates for smooth models, while preserving core axioms of attribution methods.
AI.method: Define PS-IG as the expectation of integrated gradients over baselines sampled along the linear interpolation path. Prove equivalence to path-weighted IG when the sampling-weighting function equals the CDF of the sampling density. Replace stochastic expectation with a deterministic Riemann sum to obtain improved error rate. Analyze variance reduction under uniform sampling and verify preservation of linearity and implementation invariance.
AI.result: PS-IG is mathematically equivalent to path-weighted integrated gradients under the appropriate weighting. The stochastic expectation can be computed via a deterministic Riemann sum, yielding an O(m^{-1}) convergence rate for smooth models, compared to the O(m^{-1/2}) rate of standard Monte Carlo. PS-IG also acts as a variance-reducing filter against gradient noise, strictly lowering attribution variance by a factor of 1/3 under uniform sampling, while maintaining linearity and implementation invariance of the attribution.
AI.conclusion: PS-IG provides a principled, more sample-efficient and stable framework for feature attribution, combining the benefits of integrated gradients with path-based sampling to reduce variance and improve convergence without compromising essential attribution properties.

title: Improving Machine Learning Performance with Synthetic Augmentation
summary:
Synthetic augmentation is increasingly used to mitigate data scarcity in financial machine learning, yet its statistical role remains poorly understood. We formalize synthetic augmentation as a modification of the effective training distribution and show that it induces a structural bias--variance trade-off: while additional samples may reduce estimation error, they may also shift the population objective whenever the synthetic distribution deviates from regions relevant under evaluation. To isolate informational gains from mechanical sample-size effects, we introduce a size-matched null augmentation and a finite-sample, non-parametric block permutation test that remains valid under weak temporal dependence.
  We evaluate this framework in both controlled Markov-switching environments and real financial datasets, including high-frequency option trade data and a daily equity panel. Across generators spanning bootstrap, copula-based models, variational autoencoders, diffusion models, and TimeGAN, we vary augmentation ratio, model capacity, task type, regime rarity, and signal-to-noise. We show that synthetic augmentation is beneficial only in variance-dominant regimes, such as persistent volatility forecasting-while it deteriorates performance in bias-dominant settings, including near-efficient directional prediction. Rare-regime targeting can improve domain-specific metrics but may conflict with unconditional permutation inference. Our results provide a structural perspective on when synthetic data improves financial learning performance and when it induces persistent distributional distortion.

AI.tldr: Synthetic augmentation in financial ML induces a bias-variance trade-off via distributional distortion: it lowers estimation error in variance-dominant settings but harms in bias-dominant regimes; a size-matched null augmentation and a block permutation test assess information gains under weak dependence; performance depends on regime rarity and task type.
AI.motivation: To understand the statistical role of synthetic data in finance where data scarcity exists, and to identify when augmentation improves learning versus when it distorts the population objective.
AI.method: Formalize augmentation as modification of the training distribution. Introduce a size-matched null augmentation and a finite-sample, non-parametric block permutation test valid under weak temporal dependence. Evaluate across controlled Markov-switching environments and real financial datasets using generators (bootstrap, copulas, VAEs, diffusion models, TimeGAN); vary augmentation ratio, model capacity, task type, regime rarity, and signal-to-noise.
AI.result: Synthetic augmentation helps primarily in variance-dominant regimes (e.g., persistent volatility forecasting) by reducing estimation error, but hurts in bias-dominant settings (e.g., near-efficient directional prediction). Rare-regime targeting can improve domain-specific metrics but may conflict with unconditional permutation inference. Across methods, augmented data introduces a structural distortion that may not align with the evaluation objective at times.
AI.conclusion: There is a structural bias-variance trade-off induced by synthetic data in financial learning; its utility depends on the dominant source of error and the alignment between synthetic and evaluation distributions. Practitioners should consider regime dynamics and evaluation objectives when employing synthetic augmentation.

title: DEX-Mouse: A Low-cost Portable and Universal Interface with Force Feedback for Data Collection of Dexterous Robotic Hands
summary: Data-driven dexterous hand manipulation requires large-scale, physically consistent demonstration data.
