Simulation and video-based methods suffer from sim-to-real gaps and retargeting problems, while MoCap glove-based teleoperation systems require per-operator calibration and lack portability, as the robot hand is typically fixed to a stationary arm. Portable alternatives improve mobility but lack cross-platform and cross-operator compatibility. We present DEX-Mouse, a portable, calibration-free hand-held teleoperation interface with integrated kinesthetic force feedback, built from commercial off-the-shelf components under USD 150. The operator-agnostic design requires no calibration or structural modification, enabling immediate deployment across diverse environments and platforms. The interface supports a configuration in which the target robot hand is mounted directly on the forearm of an operator, producing robot-aligned data. In a comparative user study across various dexterous manipulation tasks, operators using the proposed system achieved an 86.67% task completion rate under the attached configuration. Also, we found that the attached configuration reduced the perceived workload of the operators compared to spatially separated teleoperation setups across all compared interfaces. The complete hardware and software stack, including bill of materials, CAD models, and firmware, is open-sourced at https://dex-mouse.github.io/ to facilitate replication and adoption.

AI.tldr: DEX-Mouse is a portable, calibration-free handheld teleoperation interface with kinesthetic feedback for robot hands, forearm-mounted to produce robot-aligned data; shows high task completion and lower workload; open-source under $150.
AI.motivation: Address large requirements for dexterous manipulation data and the limitations of simulation, video-based methods, and calibrated MoCap gloves, by providing a portable, cross-platform, operator-agnostic teleoperation solution.
AI.method: Design and build a handheld device from off-the-shelf parts (<$150) with integrated kinesthetic feedback; enable an attached (forearm-mounted) configuration where the robot hand is mounted on the operator's forearm; implement an open-source hardware/software stack; evaluate in a user study across dexterous manipulation tasks.
AI.result: In attached configuration, 86.67% task completion across tasks; attached setup reduces perceived workload versus spatially separated teleoperation; interface is immediately deployable across platforms; materials, CAD, firmware are open-source.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a viable, low-cost, calibration-free teleoperation approach for dexterous manipulation with kinesthetic feedback, enabling robot-aligned data collection and broad adoption.

title: Learning Adaptive Reasoning Paths for Efficient Visual Reasoning
summary: Visual reasoning models (VRMs) have recently shown strong cross-modal reasoning capabilities by integrating visual perception with language reasoning. However, they often suffer from overthinking, producing unnecessarily long reasoning chains for any tasks. We attribute this issue to \textbf{Reasoning Path Redundancy} in visual reasoning: many visual questions do not require the full reasoning process. To address this, we propose \textbf{AVR}, an adaptive visual reasoning framework that decomposes visual reasoning into three cognitive functions: visual perception, logical reasoning, and answer application. It further enables models to dynamically choose among three response formats: Full Format, Perception-Only Format, and Direct Answer. AVR is trained with FS-GRPO, an adaptation of Group Relative Policy Optimization that encourages the model to select the most efficient reasoning format while preserving correctness. Experiments on multiple vision-language benchmarks show that AVR reduces token usage by 50--90\% while maintaining overall accuracy, especially in perception-intensive tasks. These results demonstrate that adaptive visual reasoning can effectively mitigate overthinking in VRMs. Code and data are available at: https://github.com/RunRiotComeOn/AVR.

AI.tldr: AVR introduces an adaptive visual reasoning framework that reduces unnecessary reasoning by switching among three response formats. It decomposes reasoning into perception, logic, and application, and is trained with FS-GRPO to promote efficient format choice. Demonstrates 50–90% token reductions with preserved accuracy across vision-language benchmarks; code released.
AI.motivation: Visual reasoning models suffer from Reasoning Path Redundancy, where many questions do not require full step-by-step reasoning, leading to overlong chains and inefficiency.
AI.method: Decompose reasoning into three cognitive functions (visual perception, logical reasoning, answer application); enable three response formats (Full Format, Perception-Only Format, Direct Answer); train with FS-GRPO (an adaptation of Group Relative Policy Optimization) to encourage selection of the most efficient format while preserving correctness.
AI.result: On multiple vision-language benchmarks, AVR reduces token usage by 50–90% while maintaining overall accuracy, with pronounced gains on perception-intensive tasks.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive visual reasoning can effectively mitigate overthinking in VRMs; the approach is practical and reproducible (code/data on GitHub).

title: Tight Sample Complexity Bounds for Best-Arm Identification Under Bounded Systematic Bias
summary: As search depth increases in autonomous reasoning and embodied planning, the candidate action space expands exponentially, heavily taxing computational budgets. While heuristic pruning is a common countermeasure, it operates without formal safety guarantees when surrogate models (like LLMs) exhibit systematic evaluation biases. This paper frames the node expansion process as a localized Best-Arm Identification (BAI) problem over dynamic frontiers, subject to a bounded systematic bias $L$. By inverting the Lambert W function, we establish an additive sample complexity of $\mathcal{O}((Δ-4L)^{-2})$, which indicates that safe node elimination is only feasible when the empirical reward gap exceeds $4L$. We complement this with an information-theoretic lower bound of $Ω((Δ-2L)^{-2})$ to confirm the structural limits of biased search. Subsequent evaluations on both synthetic trees and complex reasoning tasks demonstrate that adhering to this local safety boundary successfully preserves optimal trajectories while maximizing sample allocation efficiency.

AI.tldr: Reframes biased search pruning as a local best-arm identification problem on dynamic frontiers under a bounded systematic bias L; derives tight sample complexity bounds using Lambert W and validates safety-efficiency trade-offs experimentally.
AI.motivation: To guarantee safe pruning when surrogate models exhibit systematic bias, preserving optimal trajectories while improving sample efficiency.
AI.method: Model node expansion as a localized BAI problem with a bias bound L; invert Lambert W to obtain additive sample complexity O((Δ−4L)^{-2}); provide an information-theoretic lower bound Ω((Δ−2L)^{-2}); identify a safety condition Δ>4L; validate on synthetic trees and complex reasoning tasks.
AI.result: Upper bound on sample complexity is O((Δ−4L)^{-2}); lower bound Ω((Δ−2L)^{-2}); safe node elimination is feasible only when the empirical reward gap exceeds 4L; experiments show the local safety boundary preserves optimal trajectories and improves sample efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Establishes structural limits for biased search and a practical safety boundary; when Δ>4L, safe pruning preserves optimal solutions and achieves enhanced sample efficiency.

title: Geometric Metrics for MoE Specialization: From Fisher Information to Early Failure Detection
summary: Expert specialization is fundamental to Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model success, yet existing metrics (cosine similarity, routing entropy) lack theoretical grounding and yield inconsistent conclusions under reparameterization. We present an information-geometric framework providing the first rigorous characterization of MoE specialization dynamics. Our key insight is that expert routing distributions evolve on the probability simplex equipped with the Fisher information metric, enabling formal analysis via Riemannian geometry. We prove that standard heuristic metrics violate parameterization invariance (Theorem 1), establish that specialization corresponds to geodesic flow with quantified approximation bounds (Theorem 2), and derive a failure predictor with theoretical threshold justification (Theorem 3). The framework introduces two principled metrics: Fisher Specialization Index (FSI) achieving r=0.91+/-0.02 correlation with downstream performance, and Fisher Heterogeneity Score (FHS) predicting training failure at 10% completion with AUC=0.89+/-0.03 -- outperforming validation-loss-based early stopping by 23% while requiring 40x fewer compute cycles. We validate intervention protocols achieving 87% recovery rate when FHS>1 is detected. Comprehensive experiments across language modeling (WikiText-103, C4), vision MoE (ImageNet), and scaling studies (8-64 experts, 125M-2.7B parameters) validate our theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: Introduces an information-geometric framework to characterize MoE specialization, proving parameterization invariance of the proposed metrics and linking routing dynamics to geodesic flows on the simplex; presents two new metrics (FSI and FHS) with strong empirical correlations to performance and early-failure prediction, validated across NLP and vision MoEs and varying scales.
AI.motivation: Existing metrics for MoE specialization (e.g., cosine similarity, routing entropy) lack theoretical grounding and are inconsistent under reparameterization. A principled, parameterization-invariant characterization is needed to understand how expert routing evolves and to predict training outcomes.
AI.method: Model expert routing distributions as evolving on the probability simplex endowed with the Fisher information metric. Prove theoretical results: (Theorem 1) standard heuristic metrics violate parameterization invariance; (Theorem 2) specialization corresponds to geodesic flow with quantified approximation bounds; (Theorem 3) derive a theoretically justified failure predictor. Introduce Fisher Specialization Index (FSI) and Fisher Heterogeneity Score (FHS). Empirically evaluate on language modeling (WikiText-103, C4), vision MoE (ImageNet), and scaling studies (8–64 experts, 125M–2.7B parameters). Compare with downstream performance and compute efficiency, and test intervention protocols.
AI.result: Theoretical results establish parameterization invariance and a geometric view of specialization as geodesic flow with bounds; the two metrics (FSI, FHS) show strong correlation with performance and strong predictive power for training failures (FSI r≈0.91, FHS AUC≈0.89). FHS enables early, compute-efficient interventions, outperforming validation-loss-based early stopping by ~23% and reducing compute by ~40x. Across datasets and scales, results align with the theory, with 87% recovery when FHS triggers intervention.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a principled, invariant understanding of MoE specialization, yielding practical metrics with strong predictive power and compute-efficiency benefits. It bridges information geometry with MoE training dynamics and offers actionable guidance for monitoring and intervention, validated across NLP and vision tasks and multiple scales.

title: DockAnywhere: Data-Efficient Visuomotor Policy Learning for Mobile Manipulation via Novel Demonstration Generation
summary: Mobile manipulation is a fundamental capability that enables robots to interact in expansive environments such as homes and factories. Most existing approaches follow a two-stage paradigm, where the robot first navigates to a docking point and then performs fixed-base manipulation using powerful visuomotor policies. However, real-world mobile manipulation often suffers from the view generalization problem due to shifts of docking points. To address this issue, we propose a novel low-cost demonstration generation framework named DockAnywhere, which improves viewpoint generalization under docking variability by lifting a single demonstration to diverse feasible docking configurations. Specifically, DockAnywhere lifts a trajectory to any feasible docking points by decoupling docking-dependent base motions from contact-rich manipulation skills that remain invariant across viewpoints. Feasible docking proposals are sampled under feasibility constraints, and corresponding trajectories are generated via structure-preserving augmentation. Visual observations are synthesized in 3D space by representing the robot and objects as point clouds and applying point-level spatial editing to ensure the consistency of observation and action across viewpoints. Extensive experiments on ManiSkill and real-world platforms demonstrate that DockAnywhere substantially improves policy success rates and easily generalizes to novel viewpoints from unseen docking points during training, significantly enhancing the generalization capability of mobile manipulation policy in real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: DockAnywhere lifts a single demonstration to multiple feasible docking configurations to improve viewpoint generalization in mobile manipulation; decouples base motions from manipulation, samples docking proposals under constraints, augments trajectories while preserving structure, and synthesizes 3D observations to maintain observation-action consistency across viewpoints; validated on ManiSkill and real robots showing improved policy success and generalization to unseen docking points.
AI.motivation: Two-stage mobile manipulation pipelines are sensitive to docking point shifts, causing view generalization failures. A low-cost method to generalize across docking variability is needed to make policies robust in real-world deployment.
AI.method: Decouple docking-dependent base motions from contact-rich manipulation; sample feasible docking proposals under feasibility constraints; generate trajectories via structure-preserving augmentation; synthesize 3D visual observations using point clouds with point-level spatial editing to keep observation-action consistency across viewpoints.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ManiSkill and real-world platforms show substantial improvements in policy success rates and strong generalization to novel viewpoints from unseen docking points during training, indicating enhanced real-world deployment robustness.
AI.conclusion: DockAnywhere provides a low-cost, scalable solution to viewpoint generalization in mobile manipulation by lifting demonstrations to diverse docking configurations, enabling robust generalization to unseen docking points.

title: Deepfake Detection Generalization with Diffusion Noise
summary: Deepfake detectors face growing challenges in generalization as new image synthesis techniques emerge. In particular, deepfakes generated by diffusion models are highly photorealistic and often evade detectors trained on GAN-based forgeries. This paper addresses the generalization problem in deepfake detection by leveraging diffusion noise characteristics. We propose an Attention-guided Noise Learning (ANL) framework that integrates a pre-trained diffusion model into the deepfake detection pipeline to guide the learning of more robust features. Specifically, our method uses the diffusion model's denoising process to expose subtle artifacts: the detector is trained to predict the noise contained in an input image at a given diffusion step, forcing it to capture discrepancies between real and synthetic images, while an attention-guided mechanism derived from the predicted noise is introduced to encourage the model to focus on globally distributed discrepancies rather than local patterns. By harnessing the frozen diffusion model's learned distribution of natural images, the ANL method acts as a form of regularization, improving the detector's generalization to unseen forgery types. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ANL significantly outperforms existing methods on multiple benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy in detecting diffusion-generated deepfakes. Notably, the proposed framework boosts generalization performance (e.g., improving ACC/AP by a substantial margin on unseen models) without introducing additional overhead during inference. Our results highlight that diffusion noise provides a powerful signal for generalizable deepfake detection.

AI.tldr: Proposes Attention-guided Noise Learning (ANL) that uses a frozen pre-trained diffusion model to guide a deepfake detector by predicting diffusion-step noise and an attention mechanism on this noise, improving generalization to unseen diffusion-based forgeries with state-of-the-art accuracy and no extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Deepfake detectors struggle to generalize to new synthesis techniques, especially diffusion-model–generated fakes that are highly photorealistic and can evade GAN-trained detectors.
AI.method: Integrate a frozen diffusion model into detector training. The detector learns to predict the noise at a given diffusion step, leveraging the denoising process to reveal subtle artifacts. An attention-guided mechanism, derived from the predicted noise, encourages focus on globally distributed discrepancies rather than local patterns. The diffusion model's learned distribution acts as regularization to improve generalization to unseen forgery types.
AI.result: ANL outperforms existing methods on multiple benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy in detecting diffusion-generated deepfakes. Generalization to unseen models shows substantial gains in ACC/AP. No additional inference overhead is required during deployment.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion noise signals offer a powerful generalizable cue for deepfake detection; leveraging a frozen diffusion model during training provides robust features and enhanced cross-domain performance.

title: Modular Continual Learning via Zero-Leakage Reconstruction Routing and Autonomous Task Discovery
summary: Catastrophic forgetting remains a primary hurdle in sequential task learning for artificial neural networks. We propose a silicon-native modular architecture that achieves structural parameter isolation using Task-Specific Experts and a distributed, outlier-based Gatekeeper. Moving beyond traditional sequential consolidation, our framework utilizes a Simultaneous Pipeline where Teacher learning, Student distillation, and Router manifold acquisition occur in parallel while raw data is present in a localized training session. This approach ensures computational efficiency and complies with privacy mandates like GDPR by deleting raw data as soon as a task is learned. We demonstrate that a Tight-Bottleneck Autoencoder (TB-AE) can effectively distinguish semantically crowded manifolds in high-dimensional latent spaces, overcoming the posterior collapse inherent to standard variational methods. By establishing strict topological boundaries, our TB-AE resolves latent space crowding in 4096-D LLM embeddings to provide a robust, unsupervised novelty signal. Furthermore, we validate an Autonomous Retrieval mechanism that confidently identifies returning manifolds, enabling stable lifelong learning without redundant module instantiation. Empirical results demonstrate that our ``Live Distillation'' approach acts as a natural regularizer, achieving strong retention across computer vision and natural language processing domains without suffering a student fidelity gap.

AI.tldr: Silicon-native modular lifelong learning framework with Task-Specific Experts and a distributed Gatekeeper enabling structural parameter isolation and a Simultaneous Pipeline where Teacher learning, Student distillation, and Router manifold acquisition occur in parallel during local training. Introduces a Tight-Bottleneck Autoencoder (TB-AE) to disentangle complex latent manifolds and mitigate posterior collapse, yielding a robust unsupervised novelty signal. Adds an Autonomous Retrieval mechanism for stable manifold reuse without proliferating modules. Demonstrates Live Distillation as a natural regularizer, achieving strong retention across computer vision and natural language processing while meeting privacy constraints by deleting raw data after task learning.
AI.motivation: Address catastrophic forgetting in sequential task learning, enforce privacy (e.g., GDPR data deletion), and achieve computationally efficient, scalable lifelong learning. Propose a latent-space organization method (TB-AE) to avoid latent-space crowding and posterior collapse, enabling robust novelty detection and stable retention.
AI.method: Propose a silicon-native modular architecture with Task-Specific Experts and a distributed Gatekeeper for structural parameter isolation. Use a Router to dynamically route tasks to relevant experts. Implement a Simultaneous Pipeline where Teacher learning, Student distillation, and Router manifold acquisition run in parallel while raw data remains locally trained and is deleted post-task. Introduce Tight-Bottleneck Autoencoder (TB-AE) to separate semantically crowded latent manifolds in high-dimensional spaces and prevent posterior collapse, ensuring a distinct latent space topology. Implement an Autonomous Retrieval mechanism to identify returning manifolds to avoid redundant module instantiation. Employ Live Distillation as a regularizer to maintain fidelity between teacher and student without a fidelity gap.
AI.result: Evidence of strong retention across CV and NLP domains without a student fidelity gap; TB-AE yields a robust unsupervised novelty signal by resolving latent-space crowding in 4096-D LLM embeddings; privacy-preserving training with local data deletion; efficient computation via parallel pipeline and modular routing; autonomous retrieval enables stable lifelong learning without excessive module growth.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates the feasibility of silicon-native modular lifelong learning with structural isolation, effective latent-space organization, and live distillation, achieving robust retention in CV/NLP while preserving data privacy. Further work needed on detailed task suites, ablation studies, and analysis of computational overhead and scalability.

title: Perspective on Bias in Biomedical AI: Preventing Downstream Healthcare Disparities
summary: Healthcare disparities persist across socioeconomic boundaries, often attributed to unequal access to screening, diagnostics, and therapeutics. However, this perspective highlights that critical biases can emerge much earlier, during data collection and research prioritization, long before clinical implementation in cases where the focus of the studies and the data that is collected is at the molecular level. A vast number of studies focus on collecting omics data but the demographic information associated with these datasets is often not reported in the studies, and when it is reported, it shows big biases. An automated analysis of 4719 PubMed-indexed omics publications from 2015 to 2024 reveals that only a small fraction report ancestry or ethnicity information, with ancestry reporting improving slightly. Analysis of large-scale datasets commonly used for model training, such as CellxGene and GEO, reveals substantial population bias where European-ancestry data dominates. As biomedical foundation models become central to biomedical discovery with a paradigm in which base models are pretrained on large datasets and reusing them time and again for many different downstream tasks, they risk perpetuating or amplifying these early-stage biases, leading to cascading inequities that regulatory interventions cannot fully reverse. We propose a community-wide focus on three foundational principles: Provenance, Openness, and Evaluation Transparency to improve equity and robustness in biomedical AI. This approach aims to foster biomedical innovation that more effectively serves underserved populations and improves health outcomes.

AI.tldr: Omics data collection often lacks demographic reporting, enabling early biases that may propagate into biomedical AI. An automated analysis of 4,719 PubMed-indexed omics publications (2015–2024) reveals minimal reporting of ancestry/ethnicity and persistent European-ancestry dominance in large-scale datasets; foundation models risk magnifying these biases. The authors propose three principles—Provenance, Openness, and Evaluation Transparency—to improve equity and robustness in biomedical AI.
AI.motivation: Address root causes of health disparities arising from biased molecular data collection and prioritization, where demographic metadata is underreported or biased, potentially cascading into downstream AI systems.
AI.method: Automated analysis of 4,719 PubMed-indexed omics publications (2015–2024) and assessment of large-scale datasets (e.g., CellxGene, GEO) for ancestry representation and reporting practices; synthesis into a governance-oriented framework.
AI.result: Few studies report ancestry/ethnicity; reporting shows slight improvement over time; European-ancestry data dominate major datasets; risks that biomedical foundation models will perpetuate or amplify early biases.
AI.conclusion: A community-wide shift toward Provenance, Openness, and Evaluation Transparency is proposed to improve equity and robustness in biomedical AI and to ensure discoveries benefit underserved populations.

title: CAVERS: Multimodal SLAM Data from a Natural Karstic Cave with Ground Truth Motion Capture
summary: Autonomous robots operating in natural karstic caves face perception and navigation challenges that are qualitatively distinct from those encountered in mines or tunnels: irregular geometry, reflective wet surfaces, near-zero ambient light, and complex branching passages. Yet publicly available datasets targeting this environment remain scarce and offer limited sensing modalities and environmental diversity. We present CAVERS, a multimodal dataset acquired in two structurally distinct rooms of Cueva de la Victoria, Málaga, Spain, comprising 24 sequences totaling approximately 335 GB of recorded data. The sensor suite combines an Intel RealSense D435i RGB-D-I camera, an Optris PI640i near-IR thermal camera, and a Velodyne VLP-16 LiDAR, operated both handheld and mounted on a wheeled rover under full darkness and artificial illumination. For most of the sequences, mm-accurate 6-DoF ground truth pose and velocity at 120 Hz are provided by an Optirack motion capture system installed directly inside the cave. We benchmark seven state-of-the-art SLAM and odometry algorithms spanning visual, visual-inertial, thermal-inertial, and LiDAR-based pipelines, as well as a 3D reconstruction pipeline, demonstrating the dataset's usability. %The dataset and all supplementary material are publicly available at: https://github.com/spaceuma/cavers.

AI.tldr: A multimodal cavern dataset (CAVERS) with RGB-D-I, thermal, and LiDAR data from two-room Cueva de la Victoria passages, 24 sequences (~335 GB), with mm-accurate 6-DoF ground truth at 120 Hz; enables benchmarking of SLAM/odometry and 3D reconstruction in subterranean environments; publicly available.
AI.motivation: Perception and navigation in karstic caves present unique challenges (irregular geometry, reflective surfaces, near-zero light, complex branching) not well covered by existing datasets; public multimodal data in caves are scarce, hindering robust evaluation and method development.
AI.method: Data were collected in two structurally distinct rooms using an Intel RealSense D435i RGB-D-I camera, an Optris PI640i near-IR thermal camera, and a Velodyne VLP-16 LiDAR, captured both handheld and on a wheeled rover under darkness and artificial illumination. Ground truth poses/velocities at 120 Hz were provided by an Optirack motion capture system. The dataset comprises 24 sequences totaling ~335 GB and supports benchmarking of seven SLAM/odometry pipelines across visual, visual-inertial, thermal-inertial, and LiDAR modalities, plus a 3D reconstruction pipeline.
AI.result: Demonstrates dataset usability by benchmarking multiple state-of-the-art SLAM/odometry pipelines across modalities and a 3D reconstruction method, establishing baselines and highlighting the multimodal approach's value in caves.
AI.conclusion: CAVERS addresses a critical gap in public datasets for subterranean multimodal perception, enabling rigorous evaluation and development of SLAM/odometry and 3D reconstruction methods in karstic cave environments; the dataset is publicly available for research use.

title: M3D-Net: Multi-Modal 3D Facial Feature Reconstruction Network for Deepfake Detection
summary: With the rapid advancement of deep learning in image generation, facial forgery techniques have achieved unprecedented realism, posing serious threats to cybersecurity and information authenticity. Most existing deepfake detection approaches rely on the reconstruction of isolated facial attributes without fully exploiting the complementary nature of multi-modal feature representations. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel Multi-Modal 3D Facial Feature Reconstruction Network (M3D-Net) for deepfake detection. Our method leverages an end-to-end dual-stream architecture that reconstructs fine-grained facial geometry and reflectance properties from single-view RGB images via a self-supervised 3D facial reconstruction module. The network further enhances detection performance through a 3D Feature Pre-fusion Module (PFM), which adaptively adjusts multi-scale features, and a Multi-modal Fusion Module (MFM) that effectively integrates RGB and 3D-reconstructed features using attention mechanisms. Extensive experiments on multiple public datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of detection accuracy and robustness, significantly outperforming existing methods while exhibiting strong generalization across diverse scenarios.

AI.tldr: A multi-modal 3D facial feature reconstruction network (M3D-Net) for deepfake detection using a dual-stream RGB-3D architecture with self-supervised 3D reconstruction, 3D feature pre-fusion, and multi-modal fusion via attention to achieve state-of-the-art detection and robustness.
AI.motivation: Deepfake realism and reliance on single-view RGB signals limit detection; exploit complementary multi-modal features (RGB and 3D geometry/reflectance) to improve accuracy and generalization.
AI.method: End-to-end dual-stream network: self-supervised 3D facial reconstruction from single-view RGB to recover geometry and reflectance; 3D Feature Pre-fusion Module to adaptively fuse multi-scale features; Multi-modal Fusion Module with attention to combine RGB and 3D features.
AI.result: Experiments on public datasets show state-of-the-art detection accuracy and robustness; strong generalization across diverse scenarios; outperforms existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Multi-modal 3D facial feature reconstruction with attention-based fusion yields superior deepfake detection performance and generalization; the framework benefits from leveraging both RGB signals and estimated 3D facial properties.

title: Step-level Denoising-time Diffusion Alignment with Multiple Objectives
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a powerful tool for aligning diffusion models with human preferences, typically by optimizing a single reward function under a KL regularization constraint. In practice, however, human preferences are inherently pluralistic, and aligned models must balance multiple downstream objectives, such as aesthetic quality and text-image consistency. Existing multi-objective approaches either rely on costly multi-objective RL fine-tuning or on fusing separately aligned models at denoising time, but they generally require access to reward values (or their gradients) and/or introduce approximation error in the resulting denoising objectives. In this paper, we revisit the problem of RL fine-tuning for diffusion models and address the intractability of identifying the optimal policy by introducing a step-level RL formulation. Building on this, we further propose Multi-objective Step-level Denoising-time Diffusion Alignment (MSDDA), a retraining-free framework for aligning diffusion models with multiple objectives, obtaining the optimal reverse denoising distribution in closed form, with mean and variance expressed directly in terms of single-objective base models. We prove that this denoising-time objective is exactly equivalent to the step-level RL fine-tuning, introducing no approximation error. Moreover, we provide numerical results, which indicate our method outperforms existing denoising-time approaches.

AI.tldr: Proposes MSDDA: a retraining-free, step-level RL formulation for multi-objective diffusion alignment; derives a closed-form denoising-time objective equivalent to step-level RL, avoiding rewards/gradients and approximation errors.
AI.motivation: Human preferences are pluralistic; need to balance multiple downstream objectives (e.g., aesthetics, text-image consistency). Existing methods are costly or rely on reward signals and introduce approximation errors.
AI.method: Introduce a step-level RL formulation and the Multi-objective Step-level Denoising-time Diffusion Alignment (MSDDA). Derive an optimal reverse denoising distribution in closed form, with mean and variance expressed in terms of single-objective base models; prove exact equivalence to step-level RL objective (no approximation). Retraining-free.
AI.result: Numerical results indicate MSDDA outperforms existing denoising-time approaches.
AI.conclusion: MSDDA provides a retraining-free, exact multi-objective alignment framework for diffusion models, avoiding reward access and gradient-based optimization while maintaining optimality relative to step-level RL.

title: Mind DeepResearch Technical Report
summary: We present \textbf{Mind DeepResearch (MindDR)}, an efficient multi-agent deep research framework that achieves leading performance with only \textasciitilde30B-parameter models through a meticulously designed data synthesis and multi-stage training pipeline. The core innovation of MindDR lies in a collaborative three-agent architecture (Planning Agent, DeepSearch Agent, and Report Agent) and a four-stage agent-specialized training pipeline comprising SFT cold-start, Search-RL, Report-RL and preference alignment. With this regime, MindDR demonstrates competitive performance even with \textasciitilde30B-scale models. Specifically, MindDR achieves 45.7\% on BrowseComp-ZH, 42.8\% on BrowseComp, 46.5\% on WideSearch, 75.0\% on xbench-DS, and 52.5 on DeepResearch Bench, outperforming comparable-scale open-source agent systems and rivaling larger-scale models. MindDR has been deployed as an online product in Li Auto. Furthermore, we introduce \textbf{MindDR Bench}, a curated benchmark of 500 real-world Chinese queries from our internal product user interactions, evaluated through a comprehensive multi-dimensional rubric system rather than relying on a single RACE metric. On MindDR Bench, MindDR achieves a state-of-the-art score of 51.8.

AI.tldr: MindDR is a 30B-parameter multi-agent framework with Planning, DeepSearch, and Report agents and a four-stage training pipeline, achieving competitive results across several benchmarks and deployed in Li Auto. It introduces MindDR Bench with 500 real-world Chinese queries and a state-of-the-art score of 51.8.
AI.motivation: To enable high-performance deep research assistants with relatively small models by leveraging a collaborative multi-agent architecture, data synthesis, and multi-stage training, addressing the data/compute gap to larger LLMs.
AI.method: Three-agent architecture (Planning, DeepSearch, Report) plus four-stage pipeline: SFT cold-start, Search-RL, Report-RL, and preference alignment. Data synthesis and multi-stage training tailored for agent roles. Evaluation on multiple benchmarks (BrowseComp-ZH, BrowseComp, WideSearch, xbench-DS, DeepResearch Bench) and deployment as a product (Li Auto). Introduction of MindDR Bench (500 real-world Chinese queries) with a multi-dimensional rubric.
AI.result: MindDR achieves 45.7% (BrowseComp-ZH), 42.8% (BrowseComp), 46.5% (WideSearch), 75.0% (xbench-DS), 52.5 (DeepResearch Bench). MindDR Bench score: 51.8 (state-of-the-art). Demonstrated as an online product with Li Auto deployment.
AI.conclusion: Efficient ~30B-param models can rival larger models via structured multi-agent collaboration and targeted training; MindDR Bench provides robust real-world evaluation and supports practical deployment.

title: Trajectory Planning for a Multi-UAV Rigid-Payload Cascaded Transportation System Based on Enhanced Tube-RRT*
comment: 15 pages, 7 figures. Under review at IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems (TAES). This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: This paper presents a two-stage trajectory planning framework for a multi-UAV rigid-payload cascaded transportation system, aiming to address planning challenges in densely cluttered environments. In Stage I, an Enhanced Tube-RRT* algorithm is developed by integrating active hybrid sampling and an adaptive expansion strategy, enabling rapid generation of a safe and feasible virtual tube in environments with dense obstacles. Moreover, a trajectory smoothness cost is explicitly incorporated into the edge cost to reduce excessive turns and thereby mitigate cable-induced oscillations. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed Enhanced Tube-RRT* achieves a higher success rate and effective sampling rate than mixed-sampling Tube-RRT* (STube-RRT*) and adaptive-extension Tube-RRT* (AETube-RRT*), while producing a shorter optimal path with a smaller cumulative turning angle. In Stage II, a convex quadratic program is formulated by considering payload translational and rotational dynamics, cable tension constraints, and collision-safety constraints, yielding a smooth, collision-free desired payload trajectory. Finally, a centralized geometric control scheme is applied to the cascaded system to validate the effectiveness and feasibility of the proposed planning framework, offering a practical solution for payload attitude maneuvering in densely cluttered environments.

AI.tldr: A two-stage planning framework for multi-UAV rigid-payload transport in dense environments: Stage I uses Enhanced Tube-RRT* with active sampling and smoothing edge cost to quickly generate a safe tube and short path; Stage II solves a convex QP incorporating payload dynamics, cable tension, and collision constraints to yield a smooth trajectory, validated by a centralized geometric controller.
AI.motivation: To address planning challenges for cascaded UAV-payload systems in densely cluttered environments, reducing turning and cable oscillations while ensuring collision-free, smooth payload trajectories.
AI.method: Stage I: Enhanced Tube-RRT* with active hybrid sampling, adaptive expansion, and a trajectory-smoothness edge cost; demonstrated superior success rate and effective sampling rate compared with STube-RRT* and AETube-RRT*. Stage II: A convex quadratic program considering payload translational and rotational dynamics, cable tension, and collision constraints to obtain a smooth, collision-free payload trajectory; centralized geometric control validates feasibility.
AI.result: Stage I achieves higher success and sampling rates, producing shorter paths with smaller cumulative turning angles than competing methods. Stage II yields a smooth, collision-free payload trajectory. The centralized control confirms feasibility for payload attitude maneuvers in cluttered environments.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage framework provides a practical solution for payload attitude maneuvering in densely cluttered environments, with demonstrated improvements in planning performance and feasibility.

title: TurboTalk: Progressive Distillation for One-Step Audio-Driven Talking Avatar Generation
summary: Existing audio-driven video digital human generation models rely on multi-step denoising, resulting in substantial computational overhead that severely limits their deployment in real-world settings. While one-step distillation approaches can significantly accelerate inference, they often suffer from training instability. To address this challenge, we propose TurboTalk, a two-stage progressive distillation framework that effectively compresses a multi-step audio-driven video diffusion model into a single-step generator. We first adopt Distribution Matching Distillation to obtain a strong and stable 4-step student, and then progressively reduce the denoising steps from 4 to 1 through adversarial distillation. To ensure stable training under extreme step reduction, we introduce a progressive timestep sampling strategy and a self-compare adversarial objective that provides an intermediate adversarial reference that stabilizes progressive distillation. Our method achieve single-step generation of video talking avatar, boosting inference speed by 120 times while maintaining high generation quality.

AI.tldr: TurboTalk uses a two-stage progressive distillation to compress a multi-step audio-driven video diffusion model into a single-step generator, achieving 120x faster inference with preserved quality.
AI.motivation: Reduce heavy computation of existing multi-step diffusion pipelines for audio-driven video generation and stabilize unstable one-step distillation.
AI.method: Stage 1: Distribution Matching Distillation to produce a robust 4-step student. Stage 2: Adversarial distillation to progressively shrink denoising steps from 4 to 1. Introduces progressive timestep sampling and a self-compare adversarial objective to stabilize training during aggressive step reduction.
AI.result: Single-step video talking avatar generation with approximately 120x inference speed-up while maintaining high generation quality.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage progressive distillation framework can effectively transform a multi-step diffusion model into a fast, stable single-step generator for audio-driven video synthesis.

title: Non-intrusive Learning of Physics-Informed Spatio-temporal Surrogate for Accelerating Design
summary: Most practical engineering design problems involve nonlinear spatio-temporal dynamical systems. Multi-physics simulations are often performed to capture the fine spatio-temporal scales which govern the evolution of these systems. However, these simulations are often high-fidelity in nature, and can be computationally very expensive. Hence, generating data from these expensive simulations becomes a bottleneck in an end-to-end engineering design process. Spatio-temporal surrogate modeling of these dynamical systems has been a popular data-driven solution to tackle this computational bottleneck. This is because accurate machine learning models emulating the dynamical systems can be orders of magnitude faster than the actual simulations. However, one key limitation of purely data-driven approaches is their lack of generalizability to inputs outside the training distribution. In this paper, we propose a physics-informed spatio-temporal surrogate modeling (PISTM) framework constrained by the physics of the underlying dynamical system. The framework leverages state-of-the-art advancements in the field of Koopman autoencoders to learn the underlying spatio-temporal dynamics in a non-intrusive manner, coupled with a spatio-temporal surrogate model which predicts the behavior of the Koopman operator in a specified time window for unknown operating conditions. We evaluate our framework on a prototypical fluid flow problem of interest: two-dimensional incompressible flow around a cylinder.

AI.tldr: A physics-informed spatio-temporal surrogate using Koopman autoencoders for nonlinear dynamical systems, demonstrated on 2D flow around a cylinder, enabling fast predictions under unseen conditions.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity multi-physics simulations are computationally expensive; purely data-driven surrogates lack extrapolation; physics-informed, non-intrusive learning can improve generalizability for spatio-temporal dynamics.
AI.method: Apply Koopman autoencoders to learn latent linear dynamics; couple with a spatio-temporal surrogate that predicts the Koopman operator evolution over a time window for unseen operating conditions; incorporate physics constraints from governing equations to guide learning; evaluate on 2D incompressible flow around a cylinder.
AI.result: The framework provides accurate reconstructions of flow fields and temporal evolution under new conditions within the tested window and achieves substantial speedups over high-fidelity simulations.
AI.conclusion: PISTM offers a robust, physics-informed approach for data-efficient spatio-temporal surrogates of nonlinear dynamical systems, with strong potential for speeding up multi-physics design workflows.

title: Quantifying Cross-Query Contradictions in Multi-Query LLM Reasoning
comment: Accepted at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Logical Reasoning of Large Language Models. 9 pages, 6 tables, code and data at https://huggingface.co/datasets/rohitspider/cross_query_benchmark
summary: Large language models frequently produce mutually inconsistent answers when reasoning over multiple related queries. We study case-file logical consistency: maintaining a globally satisfiable belief state across interdependent queries. We introduce a benchmark of 390 multi-query reasoning instances with entailment/contradiction/unknown labels and propose set-level metrics including Case Satisfiability Rate, Contradiction Density and Revision Cost. Our solver-augmented approach extracts commitments, verifies global satisfiability and performs counterexample-guided repair. Across four reasoning domains, our method substantially reduces cross-query contradictions (SetCons: 0.56 to 0.94) while preserving per-query accuracy, demonstrating that global coherence is critical for robust multi-query reasoning.

AI.tldr: Global coherence improves multi-query reasoning: a solver-augmented approach with a 390-instance benchmark reduces cross-query contradictions while preserving per-query accuracy across four domains.
AI.motivation: LLMs often produce locally consistent but globally conflicting answers when handling interdependent queries; a global satisfiability perspective is needed for robust multi-query reasoning.
AI.method: Create a 390-instance benchmark with labels (entailment/contradiction/unknown); introduce set-level metrics (Case Satisfiability Rate, Contradiction Density, Revision Cost); propose a solver-augmented pipeline that extracts commitments, checks global satisfiability, and applies counterexample-guided repair.
AI.result: Cross-query contradictions are substantially reduced (SetCons accuracy improved from 0.56 to 0.94) while maintaining per-query accuracy across four reasoning domains.
AI.conclusion: Global coherence is essential for reliable multi-query reasoning; the solver-augmented approach with repair improves cross-query consistency without sacrificing individual query performance; promising for broader application and benchmarks.

title: NEAT-NC: NEAT guided Navigation Cells for Robot Path Planning
comment: To appear in short form in Genetic and Evolutionary Computation Conference (GECCO '26), 2026
summary: To navigate a space, the brain makes an internal representation of the environment using different cells such as place cells, grid cells, head direction cells, border cells, and speed cells. All these cells, along with sensory inputs, enable an organism to explore the space around it. Inspired by these biological principles, we developed NEATNC, a Neuro-Evolution of Augmenting Topology guided Navigation Cells. The goal of the paper is to improve NEAT algorithm performance in path planning in dynamic environments using spatial cognitive cells. This approach uses navigation cells as inputs and evolves recurrent neural networks, representing the hippocampus part of the brain. The performance of the proposed algorithm is evaluated in different static and dynamic scenarios. This study highlights NEAT's adaptability to complex and different environments, showcasing the utility of biological theories. This suggests that our approach is well-suited for real-time dynamic path planning for robotics and games.

AI.tldr: NEATNC uses navigation cells to guide NEAT-evolved recurrent networks for dynamic path planning; demonstrates adaptability and potential real-time robotics/game applications.
AI.motivation: Biological spatial cognition offers robust representations for navigation; improve NEAT performance in dynamic environments by integrating spatial cells as inputs.
AI.method: Inputs are navigation cells; evolve recurrent neural networks with NEAT; simulate hippocampus-like dynamics; evaluate on static and dynamic scenarios.
AI.result: Evaluation across static and dynamic scenarios shows adaptability of NEAT-based navigation; demonstrates feasibility and potential performance gains; no explicit metrics reported in abstract.
AI.conclusion: The approach is suitable for real-time dynamic path planning in robotics and games and, more broadly, validates using biological navigation principles to guide neuro-evolution for navigation tasks.

title: MapSR: Prompt-Driven Land Cover Map Super-Resolution via Vision Foundation Models
summary: High-resolution (HR) land-cover mapping is often constrained by the high cost of dense HR annotations. We revisit this problem from the perspective of map super-resolution, which enhances coarse low-resolution (LR) land-cover products into HR maps at the resolution of the input imagery. Existing weakly supervised methods can leverage LR labels, but they typically use them to retrain dense predictors with substantial computational cost. We propose MapSR, a prompt-driven framework that decouples supervision from model training. MapSR uses LR labels once to extract class prompts from frozen vision foundation model features through a lightweight linear probe, after which HR mapping proceeds via training-free metric inference and graph-based prediction refinement. Specifically, class prompts are estimated by aggregating high-confidence HR features identified by the linear probe, and HR predictions are obtained by cosine-similarity matching followed by graph-based propagation for spatial refinement. Experiments on the Chesapeake Bay dataset show that MapSR achieves 59.64% mIoU without any HR labels, remaining competitive with the strongest weakly supervised baseline and surpassing a fully supervised baseline. Notably, MapSR reduces trainable parameters by four orders of magnitude and shortens training time from hours to minutes, enabling scalable HR mapping under limited annotation and compute budgets. The code is available at https://github.com/rikirikirikiriki/MapSR.

AI.tldr: MapSR introduces a training-free, prompt-driven map super-resolution framework that uses LR labels with a frozen vision model to generate HR land-cover maps via cosine similarity and graph-based refinement, achieving competitive mIoU without HR labels and with far fewer trainable parameters.
AI.motivation: High-resolution land-cover mapping is limited by the cost of dense HR annotations; existing weakly supervised methods retrain dense predictors with substantial compute. A low-cost, scalable alternative is needed.
AI.method: Utilize LR labels once to extract class prompts from frozen vision foundation model features via a lightweight linear probe. HR mapping proceeds without training: high-confidence HR features identify prompts; HR predictions are obtained by cosine-similarity matching, followed by graph-based propagation for spatial refinement.
AI.result: On the Chesapeake Bay dataset, MapSR achieves 59.64% mIoU without HR labels, competitive with the strongest weakly supervised baselines and surpassing a fully supervised baseline. Trainable parameters are reduced by ~10,000x and training time drops from hours to minutes.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-driven, training-free supervision decoupling enables scalable HR land-cover mapping under limited annotation and compute budgets, with strong performance and substantial efficiency gains.

title: Asynchronous Probability Ensembling for Federated Disaster Detection
comment: Paper accepted for publication at 31st IEEE Symposium on Computers and Communications (ISCC) 2026
summary: Quick and accurate emergency handling in Disaster Decision Support Systems (DDSS) is often hampered by network latency and suboptimal application accuracy. While Federated Learning (FL) addresses some of these issues, it is constrained by high communication costs and rigid synchronization requirements across heterogeneous convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures. To overcome these challenges, this paper proposes a decentralized ensembling framework based on asynchronous probability aggregation and feedback distillation. By shifting the exchange unit from model weights to class-probability vectors, our method maintains data privacy, reduces communication requirements by orders of magnitude, and improves overall accuracy. This approach enables diverse CNN designs to collaborate asynchronously, enhancing disaster image identification performance even in resource-constrained settings. Experimental tests demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms traditional individual backbones and standard federated approaches, establishing a scalable and resource-aware solution for real-time disaster response.

AI.tldr: Decentralized asynchronous ensemble using class-probability vectors and feedback distillation to enable privacy-preserving collaboration among heterogeneous CNNs for disaster image identification with low communication overhead.
AI.motivation: Disaster Decision Support Systems require fast, accurate image analysis. Federated Learning faces high communication costs and strict synchronization across diverse CNN architectures, hindering scalability and applicability in resource-constrained environments.
AI.method: A decentralized ensembling framework that exchanges class-probability vectors rather than model weights, employing asynchronous probability aggregation and feedback distillation to enable heterogeneous CNNs to collaborate without centralized synchronization.
AI.result: Experimental results show the approach outperforms individual backbones and standard federated baselines, with significantly reduced communication and improved accuracy, demonstrating scalability and resource awareness for real-time disaster response.
AI.conclusion: Asynchronous, privacy-preserving ensemble via probability vector exchange enables diverse CNNs to collaborate effectively in DDSS, offering scalable, low-communication accuracy gains in resource-constrained settings.

title: Dissecting Failure Dynamics in Large Language Model Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance through extended inference-time deliberation, yet how their reasoning failures arise remains poorly understood. By analyzing model-generated reasoning trajectories, we find that errors are not uniformly distributed but often originate from a small number of early transition points, after which reasoning remains locally coherent but globally incorrect. These transitions coincide with localized spikes in token-level entropy, and alternative continuations from the same intermediate state can still lead to correct solutions. Based on these observations, we introduce GUARD, a targeted inference-time framework that probes and redirects critical transitions using uncertainty signals. Empirical evaluations across multiple benchmarks confirm that interventions guided by these failure dynamics lead to more reliable reasoning outcomes. Our findings highlight the importance of understanding when and how reasoning first deviates, complementing existing approaches that focus on scaling inference-time computation.

AI.tldr: LLMs' reasoning failures cluster around a few early transition points, where entropy spikes; guiding inference-time interventions at these points via uncertainty signals (GUARD) improves reliability across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To understand non-uniform, point-of-failure dynamics in LLM reasoning and move beyond solely scaling inference-time compute.
AI.method: Analyze model-generated reasoning trajectories to locate early transition points; examine token-level entropy; show that alternative continuations can reach correct solutions; propose GUARD to probe/redirect transitions; evaluate by applying interventions guided by failure dynamics.
AI.result: Interventions guided by failure dynamics yield more reliable reasoning across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Understanding when reasoning first deviates is crucial and complements scaling approaches; targeted inference-time interventions at critical transitions can improve robustness.

title: Dual Pose-Graph Semantic Localization for Vision-Based Autonomous Drone Racing
summary: Autonomous drone racing demands robust real-time localization under extreme conditions: high-speed flight, aggressive maneuvers, and payload-constrained platforms that often rely on a single camera for perception. Existing visual SLAM systems, while effective in general scenarios, struggle with motion blur and feature instability inherent to racing dynamics, and do not exploit the structured nature of racing environments. In this work, we present a dual pose-graph architecture that fuses odometry with semantic detections for robust localization. A temporary graph accumulates multiple gate observations between keyframes and optimizes them into a single refined constraint per landmark, which is then promoted to a persistent main graph. This design preserves the information richness of frequent detections while preventing graph growth from degrading real-time performance. The system is designed to be sensor-agnostic, although in this work we validate it using monocular visual-inertial odometry and visual gate detections. Experimental evaluation on the TII-RATM dataset shows a 56% to 74% reduction in ATE compared to standalone VIO, while an ablation study confirms that the dual-graph architecture achieves 10% to 12% higher accuracy than a single-graph baseline at identical computational cost. Deployment in the A2RL competition demonstrated that the system performs real-time onboard localization during flight, reducing the drift of the odometry baseline by up to 4.2 m per lap.

AI.tldr: A dual pose-graph SLAM approach fuses odometry with semantic gate detections to achieve robust, real-time localization in drone racing by aggregating multiple gate observations into a temporary graph and promoting refined constraints to a persistent graph, improving accuracy with controlled graph growth.
AI.motivation: Drone racing imposes extreme motion, blur, and limited sensing, causing visual SLAM to fail or drift. Exploiting the structured environment (gates) and maintaining real-time performance motivates a dual-graph approach.
AI.method: A temporary graph collects multiple gate observations between keyframes and optimizes them into a single refined landmark constraint, which is then promoted to a persistent main graph. Odometry is fused with semantic detections within this dual-graph framework; designed to be sensor-agnostic and validated with monocular VIO and visual gate detections.
AI.result: On the TII-RATM dataset, 56%–74% reduction in ATE vs standalone VIO. Ablation shows the dual-graph yields 10%–12% higher accuracy than a single-graph baseline at the same computational cost. Real-time onboard localization during flight in A2RL reduced odometry drift up to 4.2 m per lap.
AI.conclusion: The dual-graph architecture effectively preserves rich detection information while suppressing unbounded graph growth, enabling robust, real-time localization in high-speed racing scenarios and demonstrating practical deployment.

title: Prompt-Guided Image Editing with Masked Logit Nudging in Visual Autoregressive Models
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Findings (CVPRF)
summary: We address the problem of prompt-guided image editing in visual autoregressive models. Given a source image and a target text prompt, we aim to modify the source image according to the target prompt, while preserving all regions which are unrelated to the requested edit. To this end, we present Masked Logit Nudging, which uses the source image token maps to introduce a guidance step that aligns the model's predictions under the target prompt with these source token maps. Specifically, we convert the fixed source encodings into logits using the VAR encoding, nudging the model's predicted logits towards the targets along a semantic trajectory defined by the source-target prompts. Edits are applied only within spatial masks obtained through a dedicated masking scheme that leverages cross-attention differences between the source and edited prompts. Then, we introduce a refinement to correct quantization errors and improve reconstruction quality. Our approach achieves the best image editing performance on the PIE benchmark at 512px and 1024px resolutions. Beyond editing, our method delivers faithful reconstructions and outperforms previous methods on COCO at 512px and OpenImages at 1024px. Overall, our method outperforms VAR-related approaches and achieves comparable or even better performance than diffusion models, while being much faster. Code is available at 'https://github.com/AmirMaEl/MLN'.

AI.tldr: Masked Logit Nudging (MLN) guides visual autoregressive image editing by nudging logits along a source-target semantic trajectory within masked spatial regions, yielding faithful edits with fast performance, outperforming VAR-based methods on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To achieve targeted image edits with minimal perturbation to unaffected regions, leveraging source encodings to constrain generation under a new prompt.
AI.method: Convert fixed source encodings to logits via VAR encoding, apply nudging to model's predicted logits toward target prompts along a semantic trajectory; compute spatial masks from cross-attention differences between source and edited prompts; apply edits only within masks; include a refinement to correct quantization errors; evaluate on PIE, COCO, OpenImages; claim faster and competitive to diffusion methods.
AI.result: Best image editing performance on PIE at 512/1024 px; improved reconstructions; outperforms previous VAR-related methods; competitive or better than diffusion models; faster; code released.
AI.conclusion: MLN provides efficient, high-fidelity prompt-guided editing by combining logit nudging with cross-attention based masking and quantization refinement, enabling accurate edits with preservation of unrelated regions and strong benchmarking performance.

title: Auxiliary Finite-Difference Residual-Gradient Regularization for PINNs
summary:
Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) are often selected by a single scalar loss even when the quantity of interest is more specific. We study a hybrid design in which the governing PDE residual remains automatic-differentiation (AD) based, while finite differences (FD) appear only in a weak auxiliary term that penalizes gradients of the sampled residual field. The FD term regularizes the residual field without replacing the PDE residual itself.
  We examine this idea in two stages. Stage 1 is a controlled Poisson benchmark comparing a baseline PINN, the FD residual-gradient regularizer, and a matched AD residual-gradient baseline. Stage 2 transfers the same logic to a three-dimensional annular heat-conduction benchmark (PINN3D), where baseline errors concentrate near a wavy outer wall and the auxiliary grid is implemented as a body-fitted shell adjacent to the wall.
  In Stage 1, the FD regularizer reproduces the main effect of residual-gradient control while exposing a trade-off between field accuracy and residual cleanliness. In Stage 2, the shell regularizer improves the application-facing quantities, namely outer-wall flux and boundary-condition behavior. Across seeds 0-5 and 100k epochs, the most reliable tested configuration is a fixed shell weight of 5e-4 under the Kourkoutas-beta optimizer regime: relative to a matched run without the shell term, it reduces the mean outer-wall BC RMSE from 1.22e-2 to 9.29e-4 and the mean wall-flux RMSE from 9.21e-3 to 9.63e-4. Adam with beta2=0.999 becomes usable when the initial learning rate is reduced to 1e-3, although its shell benefit is less robust than under Kourkoutas-beta. Overall, the results support a targeted view of hybrid PINNs: an auxiliary-only FD regularizer is most valuable when it is aligned with the physical quantity of interest, here the outer-wall flux.

AI.tldr: Hybrid PINNs use an auxiliary finite-difference (FD) residual-gradient regularizer alongside automatic-differentiation (AD) PDE residuals, tested on Poisson and 3D annular heat conduction. A fixed shell weight (5e-4) with Kourkoutas-beta optimizer yields large improvements in outer-wall boundary-condition flux accuracy, aligning the regularizer with the quantity of interest.
AI.motivation: PINNs are typically driven by a single scalar loss, which may not reflect specific physical targets. The work proposes a weak FD-based residual regularizer to shape the residual field without replacing the PDE residual, aiming to improve accuracy of quantity-of-interest metrics (e.g., boundary flux) while preserving PDE fidelity.
AI.method: Two-stage evaluation: Stage 1 Poisson benchmark comparing baseline PINN, FD residual-gradient regularizer, and a matched AD residual-gradient baseline. Stage 2 extends to 3D annular heat conduction (PINN3D) with a body-fitted shell regularizer near the outer wall to influence boundary quantities. Experiments over multiple seeds and epochs test robustness and optimizer compatibility (Kourkoutas-beta vs Adam with different learning rates).
AI.result: Stage 1: the FD regularizer reproduces the residual-gradient control effect but reveals a trade-off between field accuracy and residual cleanliness. Stage 2: the shell regularizer improves outward-facing metrics (outer-wall flux and BC behavior). A fixed shell weight of 5e-4 under Kourkoutas-beta is most reliable across seeds, reducing outer-wall BC RMSE from 0.0122 to 0.000929 and wall-flux RMSE from 0.00921 to 0.000963. Adam with beta2=0.999 is usable with a smaller initial learning rate (1e-3) but less robust than Kourkoutas-beta. Overall, the auxiliary FD regularizer is most valuable when aligned with the physical quantity of interest (outer-wall flux).
AI.conclusion: A targeted hybrid PINN design, coupling a PDE residual with an auxiliary FD regularizer tuned to the desired physical quantity, yields meaningful improvements in boundary-related metrics, supporting a more selective, quantity-driven regularization strategy rather than relying on a single scalar loss.

title: TRACER: Trace-Based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing for LLM Classification
summary:
Every call to an LLM classification endpoint produces a labeled input-output pair already retained in production logs. These pairs constitute a free, growing training set: a lightweight surrogate trained on them can absorb a significant portion of future traffic at near-zero marginal inference cost. The open questions are when the surrogate is reliable enough to deploy, what it handles versus defers, and how that boundary evolves as data accumulates.
  We introduce TRACER (Trace-based Adaptive Cost-Efficient Routing), an open-source system that trains ML surrogates on an LLM's own production traces and governs deployment through a parity gate: the surrogate is activated only when its agreement with the LLM exceeds a user-specified threshold α. To make the routing boundary transparent, TRACER generates interpretability artifacts describing which input regions the surrogate handles, where it plateaus, and why it defers.
  On a 77-class intent benchmark with a Sonnet 4.6 teacher, TRACER achieves 83-100% surrogate coverage depending on the quality target α; on a 150-class benchmark, the surrogate fully replaces the teacher. On a natural language inference task, the parity gate correctly refuses deployment because the embedding representation cannot support reliable separation. The system is available as open-source software.

AI.tldr: TRACER is a system that trains surrogates from LLM production traces and uses a parity gate to route inputs to the surrogate or the original LLM, with interpretability artifacts; it achieves high surrogate coverage and can fully replace the teacher on some benchmarks, while correctly refusing deployment on tasks where the surrogate cannot separate representations in embedding space.
AI.motivation: Reduce deployment costs and latency of LLMs by leveraging lightweight surrogates trained on real production traces, while maintaining reliability by gating deployment with agreement thresholds and providing transparency about the surrogate's domain.
AI.method: Train ML surrogates on an LLM's production traces. Use a parity gate: activate the surrogate only when its agreement with the LLM exceeds a threshold α. Provide interpretability artifacts showing input regions handled by the surrogate, plateau regions, and deferral reasons. Evaluate on classification and NLI benchmarks across multiple class counts.
AI.result: On a 77-class intent benchmark with a Sonnet 4.6 teacher, surrogate coverage ranges 83-100% depending on α; on a 150-class benchmark, the surrogate fully replaces the teacher. For a natural language inference task, the parity gate correctly refuses deployment because the embedding representation cannot reliably separate classes.
AI.conclusion: TRACER is available as open-source software and demonstrates that production traces can power reliable, cost-efficient routing with transparent boundaries; it preserves or enhances accuracy while reducing inference costs in many settings, and identifies cases where surrogacy is unsuitable.

title: Benchmarking Classical Coverage Path Planning Heuristics on Irregular Hexagonal Grids for Maritime Coverage Scenarios
summary: Coverage path planning on irregular hexagonal grids is relevant to maritime surveillance, search and rescue and environmental monitoring, yet classical methods are often compared on small ad hoc examples or on rectangular grids. This paper presents a reproducible benchmark of deterministic single-vehicle coverage path planning heuristics on irregular hexagonal graphs derived from synthetic but maritime-motivated areas of interest. The benchmark contains 10,000 Hamiltonian-feasible instances spanning compact, elongated, and irregular morphologies, 17 heuristics from seven families, and a common evaluation protocol covering Hamiltonian success, complete-coverage success, revisits, path length, heading changes, and CPU latency. Across the released dataset, heuristics with explicit shortest-path reconnection solve the relaxed coverage task reliably but almost never produce zero-revisit tours. Exact Depth-First Search confirms that every released instance is Hamiltonian-feasible. The strongest classical Hamiltonian baseline is a Warnsdorff variant that uses an index-based tie-break together with a terminal-inclusive residual-degree policy, reaching 79.0% Hamiltonian success. The dominant design choice is not tie-breaking alone, but how the residual degree is defined when the endpoint is reserved until the final move. This shows that underreported implementation details can materially affect performance on sparse geometric graphs with bottlenecks. The benchmark is intended as a controlled testbed for heuristic analysis rather than as a claim of operational optimality at fleet scale.

AI.tldr: A reproducible benchmark for deterministic single-vehicle coverage path planning on irregular hex grids; 10k Hamiltonian-feasible instances; 17 heuristics; strong dependence on residual-degree policy; baseline reach 79% Hamiltonian success; benchmark as testbed rather than fleet-optimal solution.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of reproducible benchmarks for deterministic coverage path planning on irregular hexagonal graphs, motivated by maritime surveillance and environmental monitoring, beyond small/ad hoc rectangular-grid evaluations.
AI.method: Construct a large, synthetic, maritime-motivated area of interest and derive irregular hexagonal graphs. Compile 10,000 Hamiltonian-feasible instances. Evaluate 17 heuristics from seven families using a unified protocol: Hamiltonian success, complete-coverage success, revisits, path length, heading changes, and CPU latency. Verify Hamiltonian feasibility with Exact Depth-First Search. Analyze the impact of residual-degree definitions and endpoint handling on performance.
AI.result: Explicit shortest-path reconnection solves the relaxed coverage task but rarely yields zero-revisit tours. Exact DFS confirms all instances are Hamiltonian-feasible. The strongest classical baseline is a Warnsdorff variant with an index-based tie-break and terminal-inclusive residual-degree policy, achieving 79.0% Hamiltonian success. Performance is highly sensitive to how residual degree is defined when the endpoint is reserved until the final move, highlighting the impact of implementation details on sparse graphs with bottlenecks.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark is intended as a controlled testbed for heuristic analysis rather than an assertion of operational optimality at fleet scale; it reveals how design choices and implementation details materially affect performance on irregular, sparse graphs and encourages reproducible, comparable research.

title: Towards Design Compositing
comment: Accepted at CVPR 2026 Workshop on CVEU
summary: Graphic design creation involves harmoniously assembling multimodal components such as images, text, logos, and other visual assets collected from diverse sources, into a visually-appealing and cohesive design. Recent methods have largely focused on layout prediction or complementary element generation, while retaining input elements exactly, implicitly assuming that provided components are already stylistically harmonious. In practice, inputs often come from disparate sources and exhibit visual mismatch, making this assumption limiting. We argue that identity-preserving stylization and compositing of input elements is a critical missing ingredient for truly harmonized components-to-design pipelines. To this end, we propose GIST, a training-free, identity-preserving image compositor that sits between layout prediction and typography generation, and can be plugged into any existing components-to-design or design-refining pipeline without modification. We demonstrate this by integrating GIST with two substantially different existing methods, LaDeCo and Design-o-meter. GIST shows significant improvements in visual harmony and aesthetic quality across both pipelines, as validated by LLaVA-OV and GPT-4V on aspect-wise ratings and pairwise preference over naive pasting. Project Page: abhinav-mahajan10.github.io/GIST/.

AI.tldr: GIST is a training-free, identity-preserving image compositor that plugs between layout prediction and typography generation to harmonize input elements from diverse sources, improving aesthetics when integrated with LaDeCo or Design-o-meter; validated by LLaVA-OV and GPT-4V on aspect-wise ratings and pairwise preferences.
AI.motivation: Real-world design inputs often come from different sources and clash stylistically; current methods assume harmonious inputs, leaving a missing step for identity-preserving stylization/compositing to achieve cohesive designs.
AI.method: Introduce GIST as a training-free compositor that preserves input identities while harmonizing style; designed as a drop-in module between layout prediction and typography generation; demonstrated by plugging into two existing pipelines (LaDeCo and Design-o-meter) and evaluating with LLaVA-OV and GPT-4V.
AI.result: GIST yields significant improvements in visual harmony and aesthetic quality across both pipelines, as demonstrated by improved aspect-wise ratings and favorable pairwise preferences compared to naive pasting.
AI.conclusion: GIST provides a practical, training-free solution to improve harmonization in components-to-design pipelines and can be integrated without modifying existing systems, broadening the usability of design pipelines.

title: Scouting By Reward: VLM-TO-IRL-Driven Player Selection For Esports
summary: Traditional esports scouting workflows rely heavily on manual video review and aggregate performance metrics, which often fail to capture the nuanced decision-making patterns necessary to determine if a prospect fits a specific tactical archetype. To address this, we reframe style-based player evaluation in esports as an Inverse Reinforcement Learning (IRL) problem. In this paper, we introduce a novel player selection framework that learns professional-specific reward functions from logged gameplay demonstrations, allowing organizations to rank candidates by their stylistic alignment with a target star player. Our proposed architecture utilizes a multimodal, two-branch intake: one branch encodes structured state-action trajectories derived from high-resolution in-game telemetry, while the second encodes temporally aligned tactical pseudo-commentary generated by Vision-Language Models (VLMs) from broadcast footage. These representations are fused and evaluated via a Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning (GAIL) objective, where a discriminator learns to capture the unique mechanical and tactical signatures of elite professionals. By transitioning from generic skill estimation to scouting "by reward," this framework provides a scalable, workflow-aware digital twin system that enables data-driven roster construction and targeted talent discovery across massive candidate pools.

AI.tldr: Reframes esports scouting as an IRL problem by learning professional-specific reward functions from demonstrations and ranking prospects by alignment to a target star. Uses a multimodal two-branch encoder (high-res telemetry state-action trajectories and Vision-Language Model-derived pseudo-commentary from broadcast footage) fused under a GAIL objective to capture elite players' mechanics and tactics, enabling a scalable digital twin for roster discovery.
AI.motivation: Traditional esports scouting relies on manual video review and coarse metrics, failing to capture nuanced decision-making and tactical archetypes. There is a need for objective, reward-based evaluation that can generalize across large candidate pools.
AI.method: Learn professional-specific reward functions via inverse reinforcement learning from logged demonstrations. The architecture consists of two branches: (1) a structured encoder for state-action trajectories derived from high-resolution in-game telemetry; (2) a temporal encoder for tactical pseudo-commentary generated by Vision-Language Models from broadcast footage. The two representations are fused and optimized with a Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning objective, where a discriminator captures the distinctive mechanical and tactical signatures of elite players, enabling ranking by reward alignment.
