AI.result: Proposes a scalable, workflow-aware digital twin for esports scouting that ranks candidates by alignment with a target player’s reward structure, enabling data-driven roster construction and talent discovery across large candidate pools.
AI.conclusion: Shifts scouting focus from generic skill estimation to reward-based evaluation, enabling scalable, targeted talent discovery through a digital twin that captures elite players’ mechanical and tactical signatures.

title: MARS$^2$: Scaling Multi-Agent Tree Search via Reinforcement Learning for Code Generation
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) paradigms have demonstrated strong performance on reasoning-intensive tasks such as code generation. However, limited trajectory diversity often leads to diminishing returns, which constrains the achievable performance ceiling. Search-enhanced RL alleviates this issue by introducing structured exploration, which remains constrained by the single-agent policy priors. Meanwhile, leveraging multiple interacting policies can acquire more diverse exploratory signals, but existing approaches are typically decoupled from structured search. We propose \textbf{MARS$^2$} (Multi-Agent Reinforced Tree-Search Scaling), a unified RL framework in which multiple independently-optimized agents collaborate within a shared tree-structured search environment. MARS$^2$ models the search tree as a learnable multi-agent interaction environment, enabling heterogeneous agents to collaboratively generate and refine candidate solutions within a shared search topology. To support effective learning, we introduce a path-level group advantage formulation based on tree-consistent reward shaping, which facilitates effective credit assignment across complex search trajectories. Experiments on code generation benchmarks show that MARS$^2$ consistently improves performance across diverse model combinations and training settings, demonstrating the effectiveness of coupling multi-agent collaboration with tree search for enhancing reinforcement learning. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/TsinghuaC3I/MARTI.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent reinforcement learning framework (MARS^2) leverages a shared tree-structured search with collaborating agents to diversify exploration and boost code-generation performance; it introduces path-level group advantage with tree-consistent reward shaping and shows consistent gains across model variants; code is released.
AI.motivation: RL for reasoning-intensive tasks (e.g., code generation) suffers from limited trajectory diversity due to single-agent priors, and while search-based exploration helps, it remains constrained. Multi-agent collaboration can yield richer exploratory signals but is often not integrated with structured search.
AI.method: MARS^2 envisions multiple independently-optimized agents operating within a unified, tree-structured search environment. The search tree becomes a learnable multi-agent interaction platform where heterogeneous agents collaboratively generate and refine candidates. A path-level group advantage with tree-consistent reward shaping enables effective credit assignment across long search trajectories.
AI.result: Experiments on code generation benchmarks show consistent improvements across diverse model combinations and training settings, demonstrating the effectiveness of combining multi-agent collaboration with tree search for enhanced RL.
AI.conclusion: MARS^2 demonstrates that coupling multi-agent collaboration with tree search and a principled credit assignment strategy can elevate RL performance on reasoning tasks; the authors provide publicly available code.

title: A Hierarchical Spatiotemporal Action Tokenizer for In-Context Imitation Learning in Robotics
summary: We present a novel hierarchical spatiotemporal action tokenizer for in-context imitation learning. We first propose a hierarchical approach, which consists of two successive levels of vector quantization. In particular, the lower level assigns input actions to fine-grained subclusters, while the higher level further maps fine-grained subclusters to clusters. Our hierarchical approach outperforms the non-hierarchical counterpart, while mainly exploiting spatial information by reconstructing input actions. Furthermore, we extend our approach by utilizing both spatial and temporal cues, forming a hierarchical spatiotemporal action tokenizer, namely HiST-AT. Specifically, our hierarchical spatiotemporal approach conducts multi-level clustering, while simultaneously recovering input actions and their associated timestamps. Finally, extensive evaluations on multiple simulation and real robotic manipulation benchmarks show that our approach establishes a new state-of-the-art performance in in-context imitation learning.

AI.tldr: Introduces HiST-AT, a hierarchical two-level vector-quantization action tokenizer for in-context imitation learning that captures spatial and temporal structure, achieving state-of-the-art results on robotic benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Flat action encoders in in-context imitation learning often fail to capture hierarchical and temporal structure in action sequences; a multi-level, spatiotemporal tokenizer could improve representation, reconstruction, and downstream imitation performance.
AI.method: Two-level vector quantization: a lower level assigns actions to fine-grained subclusters, a higher level maps these subclusters to coarser clusters. Extends to HiST-AT by incorporating temporal cues and timestamps, enabling multi-level clustering that reconstructs actions and their times.
AI.result: Hierarchical approach outperforms non-hierarchical baselines; HiST-AT achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple simulation and real robotic manipulation benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical spatiotemporal action tokenization enhances in-context imitation learning by leveraging multi-level clustering and joint action-time reconstruction, yielding robust performance across benchmarks.

title: Multigrain-aware Semantic Prototype Scanning and Tri-Token Prompt Learning Embraced High-Order RWKV for Pan-Sharpening
summary: In this work, we propose a Multigrain-aware Semantic Prototype Scanning paradigm for pan-sharpening, built upon a high-order RWKV architecture and a tri-token prompting mechanism derived from semantic clustering. Specifically, our method contains three key components: 1) Multigrain-aware Semantic Prototype Scanning. Although RWKV offers a efficient linear-complexity alternative to Transformers, its conventional bidirectional raster scanning is still semantic-agnostic and prone to positional bias. To address this issue, we introduce a semantic-driven scanning strategy that leverages locality-sensitive hashing to group semantically related regions and construct multi-grain semantic prototypes, enabling context-aware token reordering and more coherent global interaction. 2) Tri-token Prompt Learning. We design a tri-token prompting mechanism consisting of a global token, cluster-derived prototype tokens, and a learnable register token. The global and prototype tokens provide complementary semantic priors for RWKV modeling, while the register token helps suppress noisy and artifact-prone intermediate representations. 3) Invertible Q-Shift. To counteract spatial details, we apply center difference convolution on the value pathway to inject high-frequency information, and introduce an invertible multi-scale Q-shift operation for efficient and lossless feature transformation without parameter-heavy receptive field expansion. Experimental results demonstrate the superiority of our method.

AI.tldr: Introduce a Multigrain-aware Semantic Prototype Scanning framework for pan-sharpening using a high-order RWKV model with semantic clustering, featuring a tri-token prompt design and an invertible Q-shift to preserve high-frequency details.
AI.motivation: RWKV's linear-complexity attention is still semantically agnostic with standard bidirectional raster scanning, causing positional bias and suboptimal context. The paper aims to inject semantic locality and multi-grain context into the scanning process for improved pan-sharpening results while mitigating noise and preserving details.
AI.method: 1) Multigrain-aware Semantic Prototype Scanning: uses locality-sensitive hashing to group semantically related regions and builds multi-grain semantic prototypes to inform context-aware token ordering and global interaction. 2) Tri-token Prompt Learning: introduces a global token, cluster-derived prototype tokens, and a learnable register token to provide semantic priors and suppress noise. 3) Invertible Q-Shift: applies center difference convolution on the value pathway to inject high-frequency information and uses an invertible multi-scale Q-shift for lossless feature transformation without heavy receptive-field changes.
AI.result: Experiments show the proposed method achieves superiority over baselines (specific metrics not provided in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: The proposed paradigm effectively combines semantic-aware multigrain scanning, tri-token prompting, and invertible Q-shift to enhance pan-sharpening performance, particularly in preserving detail while reducing artifacts.

title: Quantization of Spiking Neural Networks Beyond Accuracy
summary: Quantization is a natural complement to the sparse, event-driven computation of Spiking Neural Networks, reducing memory bandwidth and arithmetic cost for deployment on resource-constrained hardware. However, existing SNN quantization evaluation focuses almost exclusively on accuracy, overlooking whether a quantized network preserves the firing behavior of its full-precision counterpart. We demonstrate that quantization method, clipping range, and bit-width can produce substantially different firing distributions at equivalent accuracy, differences invisible to standard metrics but relevant to deployment, where firing activity governs effective sparsity, state storage, and event-processing load. To capture this gap, we propose Earth Mover's Distance as a diagnostic metric for firing distribution divergence, and apply it systematically across weight and membrane quantization on SEW-ResNet architectures trained on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. We find that uniform quantization induces distributional drift even when accuracy is preserved, while LQ-Net style learned quantization maintains firing behavior close to the full-precision baseline. Our results suggest that behavior preservation should be treated as an evaluation criterion alongside accuracy, and that EMD provides a principled tool for assessing it.

AI.tldr: Quantization of Spiking Neural Networks preserves accuracy but can alter firing distributions; Earth Mover's Distance is proposed to evaluate behavioral preservation, showing uniform quantization drifts firing distributions while learned quantization (LQ-Net style) maintains behavior similar to full-precision.
AI.motivation: Existing SNN quantization studies focus on accuracy; there is a need to evaluate whether quantization preserves firing behavior, which impacts sparsity, state storage, and event-processing load.
AI.method: Systematic assessment across weight and membrane quantization on SEW-ResNet architectures trained on CIFAR-10/100; use Earth Mover's Distance as the diagnostic metric to compare firing distributions; compare uniform quantization vs LQ-Net style learned quantization.
AI.result: EMD reveals distributional drift with uniform quantization even when accuracy is preserved; LQ-Net style learned quantization preserves firing behavior close to the full-precision baseline.
AI.conclusion: Behavior preservation should be treated as an evaluation criterion alongside accuracy; EMD provides a principled tool for assessing this aspect in SNN quantization designs.

title: Enhancing Mental Health Counseling Support in Bangladesh using Culturally-Grounded Knowledge
summary: Large language models (LLMs) show promise in generating supportive responses for mental health and counseling applications. However, their responses often lack cultural sensitivity, contextual grounding, and clinically appropriate guidance. This work addresses the gap of how to systematically incorporate domain-specific, clinically validated knowledge into LLMs to improve counseling quality. We utilize and compare two approaches, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and a knowledge graph (KG)-based method, designed to support para-counselors. Our KG is constructed manually and clinically validated, capturing causal relationships between stressors, interventions, and outcomes, with contributions from multidisciplinary people. We evaluated multiple LLMs in both settings using BERTScore F1 and SBERT cosine similarity, as well as human evaluation across five metrics, which is designed to directly measure the effectiveness of counseling beyond similarity at the surface level. The results show that KG-based approaches consistently improve contextual relevance, clinical appropriateness, and practical usability compared to RAG alone, demonstrating that structured, expert-validated knowledge plays a critical role in addressing LLMs limitations in counseling tasks.

AI.tldr: Knowledge-graph-based integration of clinically validated, causal-relations knowledge improves counseling quality from LLMs more than retrieval-augmented generation.
AI.motivation: LLMs in mental health counseling risk lack of cultural sensitivity, grounding, and clinically appropriate guidance; need systematic incorporation of domain-specific, validated knowledge.
AI.method: Compare two approaches (RAG and KG-based) using a manual, clinically validated KG capturing causal relations among stressors, interventions, outcomes; evaluate multiple LLMs with BERTScore F1, SBERT cosine similarity, and five-domain human evaluation.
AI.result: KG-based methods yield consistent improvements in contextual relevance, clinical appropriateness, and practical usability versus RAG.
AI.conclusion: Structured, expert-validated knowledge is crucial to mitigating LLM limitations in counseling tasks.

title: Vision-Based Safe Human-Robot Collaboration with Uncertainty Guarantees
summary: We propose a framework for vision-based human pose estimation and motion prediction that gives conformal prediction guarantees for certifiably safe human-robot collaboration. Our framework combines aleatoric uncertainty estimation with OOD detection for high probabilistic confidence. To integrate our pipeline in certifiable safety frameworks, we propose conformal prediction sets for human motion predictions with high, valid confidence. We evaluate our pipeline on recorded human motion data and a real-world human-robot collaboration setting.

AI.tldr: A conformal-prediction-based framework for vision-based human pose estimation and motion prediction to provide certifiable safety guarantees in human-robot interaction, combining aleatoric uncertainty with out-of-distribution detection to form valid prediction sets; evaluated on motion data and a real-world collaboration setting.
AI.motivation: Ensure provable safety in human–robot collaboration by accounting for uncertainty and distribution shift in vision-based pose estimation and motion forecasting, going beyond traditional point estimates.
AI.method: A unified pipeline that combines aleatoric uncertainty estimation for pose/motion, out-of-distribution detection, and conformal prediction to construct prediction sets with coverage guarantees. The conformal sets are designed for human motion predictions and are intended to be integrated into certifiable safety frameworks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on recorded human motion data and a real-world HRI scenario demonstrates that the pipeline yields high-confidence prediction sets with valid coverage, enabling safer human–robot interaction.
AI.conclusion: Conformal prediction-based uncertainty management can provide provable safety guarantees for vision-based human motion prediction in HRI and can be integrated into certifiable safety systems; future work includes scalability, tighter integration with control policies, and handling dynamic environments.

title: Switch-KD: Visual-Switch Knowledge Distillation for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown remarkable capabilities in joint vision-language understanding, but their large scale poses significant challenges for deployment in resource-constrained scenarios. Knowledge Distillation (KD) offers a viable way to improve model capabilities without increasing model size or data requirements, making deployment more efficient. However, applying KD to VLMs is challenged by modality-specific supervision: although multimodal knowledge in VLMs is fused within the language space, current methods supervise each modality separately without explicitly addressing multimodal alignment, leading to inconsistent multimodal knowledge transfer. To address this, we propose Switch-KD, a visual-switch distillation framework that unifies vision-language knowledge transfer within a shared text-probability space. Switch-KD comprises two key components: (1) Visual-Switch Distillation, which switches the student's visual outputs into the teacher's language pathway to construct cross-modal probabilistic references for implicit visual knowledge transfer; and (2) Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss, which adaptively aligns informative probability regions while preserving the distributional structures of teacher and student through bidirectional supervision. Guided by Switch-KD, a 0.5B TinyLLaVA effectively distills rich multimodal knowledge from its 3B teacher, yielding an average improvement of 3.6 points across 10 multimodal benchmarks without any architectural modification.

AI.tldr: Switch-KD unifies vision-language distillation by switching the student’s visual outputs into the teacher’s language pathway and using a dynamic bidirectional logits loss to align cross-modal knowledge in a shared text-probability space, yielding substantial gains for a small student without architectural changes.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models are powerful yet large; knowledge distillation can reduce size and maintain performance. However, existing KD methods apply modality-specific supervision and miss explicit multimodal alignment, leading to incomplete knowledge transfer. A unified cross-modal transfer in a shared space could improve efficiency and performance.
AI.method: Propose Visual-Switch Distillation to route the student’s visual predictions through the teacher’s language pathway, creating cross-modal probabilistic references; introduce Dynamic Bi-directional Logits Difference (DBiLD) loss to adaptively align informative probability regions while preserving teacher-student distributional structures via bidirectional supervision.
AI.result: With Switch-KD, a 0.5B TinyLLaVA student distills knowledge from a 3B teacher, achieving an average improvement of 3.6 points across 10 multimodal benchmarks without changing architecture.
AI.conclusion: Switch-KD demonstrates effective cross-modal knowledge transfer within a unified text-probability space, enabling efficient distillation for vision-language models and highlighting a promising direction for multimodal KD without architectural modifications.

title: Prompt Optimization Is a Coin Flip: Diagnosing When It Helps in Compound AI Systems
summary: Prompt optimization in compound AI systems is statistically indistinguishable from a coin flip: across 72 optimization runs on Claude Haiku (6 methods $\times$ 4 tasks $\times$ 3 repeats), 49% score below zero-shot; on Amazon Nova Lite, the failure rate is even higher. Yet on one task, all six methods improve over zero-shot by up to $+6.8$ points. What distinguishes success from failure? We investigate with 18,000 grid evaluations and 144 optimization runs, testing two assumptions behind end-to-end optimization tools like TextGrad and DSPy: (A) individual prompts are worth optimizing, and (B) agent prompts interact, requiring joint optimization. Interaction effects are never significant ($p > 0.52$, all $F < 1.0$), and optimization helps only when the task has exploitable output structure -- a format the model can produce but does not default to. We provide a two-stage diagnostic: an \$80 ANOVA pre-test for agent coupling, and a 10-minute headroom test that predicts whether optimization is worthwhile -- turning a coin flip into an informed decision.

AI.tldr: Prompt optimization in end-to-end AI systems often provides little gain; success is task-dependent and tied to exploitable output structure. A diagnostic workflow can predict when optimization is worthwhile.
AI.motivation: To understand why end-to-end prompt optimization methods (e.g., TextGrad, DSPy) frequently fail and to identify when optimization adds value. The study tests whether optimizing individual prompts and/or joint agent prompts yields benefits and aims to provide practical guidance for practitioners.
AI.method: Large-scale empirical study across Claude Haiku and Amazon Nova Lite with 6 prompt-optimization methods, 4 tasks, and 3 repeats (72 runs); plus 18,000 grid evaluations and 144 optimization runs to test two hypotheses: (A) whether individual prompts are worth optimizing, and (B) whether joint optimization (interaction effects) matters. Statistical diagnostics include an ANOVA pre-test for agent coupling and a 10-minute headroom test to predict optimization worth.
AI.result: Interaction effects are never statistically significant (p > 0.52; F < 1.0). Optimization improves performance only on tasks with exploitable output structure; across most tasks, performance is indistinguishable from zero-shot, with 49% of runs scoring below zero-shot. On one task, all six methods yield improvements up to +6.8 points. The diagnostics can turn optimization decisions from coin flips into informed choices.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end prompt optimization has limited universal value; its benefit hinges on the presence of exploitable output structure. When such structure is absent, optimization yields little to no gain and may be wasteful. The proposed two-stage diagnostic (ANOVA pre-test and headroom test) provides a practical, quick decision framework to determine whether optimization is worth pursuing.

title: Abstract Sim2Real through Approximate Information States
summary: In recent years, reinforcement learning (RL) has shown remarkable success in robotics when a fast and accurate simulator is available for a given task. When using RL and simulation, more simulator realism is generally beneficial but becomes harder to obtain as robots are deployed in increasingly complex and widescale domains. In such settings, simulators will likely fail to model all relevant details of a given target task and this observation motivates the study of sim2real with simulators that leave out key task details. In this paper, we formalize and study the abstract sim2real problem: given an abstract simulator that models a target task at a coarse level of abstraction, how can we train a policy with RL in the abstract simulator and successfully transfer it to the real-world? Our first contribution is to formalize this problem using the language of state abstraction from the RL literature. This framing shows that an abstract simulator can be grounded to match the target task if the grounded abstract dynamics take the history of states into account. Based on the formalism, we then introduce a method that uses real-world task data to correct the dynamics of the abstract simulator. We then show that this method enables successful policy transfer both in sim2sim and sim2real evaluation.

AI.tldr: Formalizes abstract sim2real using state abstraction; grounding requires history; uses real-world data to correct abstract dynamics; achieves sim2sim and sim2real transfer.
AI.motivation: When high-fidelity simulators are unavailable or expensive, abstract simulators with coarse task models are practical. Grounding these abstractions with history and real data enables RL policies to transfer to the real world.
AI.method: Formalize a state-abstraction-based RL framework. Ground abstract dynamics by incorporating the history of states. Propose a method to correct the abstract simulator dynamics using real-world task data. Train policies in the abstract simulator and transfer them to real-world and to other simulators (sim2sim).
AI.result: Policy transfers succeed in both sim2sim and sim2real evaluations, demonstrating that grounding abstract dynamics with history and real-data corrections can bridge the sim-to-real gap.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating history in state abstraction and correcting abstract dynamics with real data enables effective policy transfer from abstract simulators to real robots; the framework supports both sim2sim and sim2real transfer.

title: CMTM: Cross-Modal Token Modulation for Unsupervised Video Object Segmentation
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures. Accepted to IEEE ICIP 2025
summary: Recent advances in unsupervised video object segmentation have highlighted the potential of two-stream architectures that integrate appearance and motion cues. However, fully leveraging these complementary sources of information requires effectively modeling their interdependencies. In this paper, we introduce cross-modality token modulation, a novel approach designed to strengthen the interaction between appearance and motion cues. Our method establishes dense connections between tokens from each modality, enabling efficient intra-modal and inter-modal information propagation through relation transformer blocks. To improve learning efficiency, we incorporate a token masking strategy that addresses the limitations of relying solely on increased model complexity. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across all public benchmarks, outperforming existing methods.

AI.tldr: Cross-modality token modulation strengthens appearance-motion fusion in unsupervised VOS by densely connecting modality tokens and using relation transformers, with token masking to boost efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Two-stream architectures pair appearance and motion cues but often fail to model their interdependencies effectively; simply increasing model size is insufficient or inefficient.
AI.method: Proposes cross-modality token modulation with dense inter- and intra-modal token connections via relation transformer blocks; introduces a token masking strategy to improve learning efficiency without solely increasing parameters.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on all public benchmarks for unsupervised video object segmentation, outperforming existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective cross-modal interaction modeling and efficient learning; token masking complements increased capacity and enables robust performance; potential applicability to other cross-modal tasks.

title: CI-CBM: Class-Incremental Concept Bottleneck Model for Interpretable Continual Learning
comment: 31 pages, 6 figures. Published in Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR), 04/2026
summary: Catastrophic forgetting remains a fundamental challenge in continual learning, in which models often forget previous knowledge when fine-tuned on a new task. This issue is especially pronounced in class incremental learning (CIL), which is the most challenging setting in continual learning. Existing methods to address catastrophic forgetting often sacrifice either model interpretability or accuracy. To address this challenge, we introduce ClassIncremental Concept Bottleneck Model (CI-CBM), which leverage effective techniques, including concept regularization and pseudo-concept generation to maintain interpretable decision processes throughout incremental learning phases. Through extensive evaluation on seven datasets, CI-CBM achieves comparable performance to black-box models and outperforms previous interpretable approaches in CIL, with an average 36% accuracy gain. CICBM provides interpretable decisions on individual inputs and understandable global decision rules, as shown in our experiments, thereby demonstrating that human understandable concepts can be maintained during incremental learning without compromising model performance. Our approach is effective in both pretrained and non-pretrained scenarios; in the latter, the backbone is trained from scratch during the first learning phase. Code is publicly available at github.com/importAmir/CI-CBM.

AI.tldr: CI-CBM extends Concept Bottleneck Models to class-incremental continual learning with concept regularization and pseudo-concept generation, preserving interpretability while achieving competitive accuracy; outperforms prior interpretable methods (avg ~36% accuracy gain) and is effective in both pretrained and scratch settings; code released.
AI.motivation: Catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning undermines knowledge retention. There is a tension between accuracy and interpretability: many high-accuracy methods are black-box, while interpretable models lag in performance. This work aims to retain human-understandable concepts during incremental updates without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: Introduce ClassIncremental Concept Bottleneck Model (CI-CBM) that builds on concept bottleneck principles and adds two mechanisms: (1) concept regularization to stabilize concept predictions across incremental phases, and (2) pseudo-concept generation to handle unseen or evolving task concepts during incremental learning. The model maintains an interpretable decision process by mapping inputs to a set of human-understandable concepts and using those concepts for final predictions. The approach supports both pretrained backbones and training from scratch in the first phase.
AI.result: Evaluated on seven datasets. CI-CBM achieves accuracy comparable to black-box models and surpasses prior interpretable methods in class-incremental learning, with an average gain of 36% in accuracy. It provides interpretable decisions at the instance level and understandable global decision rules. The method is effective in both pretrained and non-pretrained scenarios.
AI.conclusion: CI-CBM demonstrates that human-understandable concepts can be maintained during incremental learning without compromising performance, offering a practical, interpretable alternative to black-box continual learners. The work includes public code (github.com/importAmir/CI-CBM).

title: GDPR Auto-Formalization with AI Agents and Human Verification
summary: We study the overall process of automatic formalization of GDPR provisions using large language models, within a human-in-the-loop verification framework. Rather than aiming for full autonomy, we adopt a role-specialized workflow in which LLM-based AI components, operating in a multi-agent setting with iterative feedback, generate legal scenarios, formal rules, and atomic facts. This is coupled with independent verification modules which include human reviewers' assessment of representational, logical, and legal correctness. Using this approach, we construct a high-quality dataset to be used for GDPR auto-formalization, and analyze both successful and problematic cases. Our results show that structured verification and targeted human oversight are essential for reliable legal formalization, especially in the presence of legal nuance and context-sensitive reasoning.

AI.tldr: An approach for GDPR auto-formalization via a role-specialized, multi-agent LLM workflow with human-in-the-loop verification to build a dataset and assess reliability.
AI.motivation: Legal NLP tasks require precision and context-sensitivity; full autonomous formalization risks errors; a structured, human-in-the-loop framework improves correctness and practical utility.
AI.method: A multi-agent LLM system generates legal scenarios, formal rules, and atomic facts; independent verification modules with human reviewers assess representational, logical, and legal correctness; a dataset for GDPR auto-formalization is constructed; analysis of successful and problematic cases.
AI.result: Creation of a high-quality dataset for GDPR auto-formalization; evidence that structured verification and targeted oversight improve reliability.
AI.conclusion: Structured verification and human oversight are essential for reliable legal formalization in nuanced, context-sensitive domains; the framework helps balance automation with accuracy.

title: Efficient closed-form approaches for pose estimation using Sylvester forms
summary: Solving non-linear least-squares problem for pose estimation (rotation and translation) is often a time consuming yet fundamental problem in several real-time computer vision applications. With an adequate rotation parametrization, the optimization problem can be reduced to the solution of a~system of polynomial equations and solved in closed form. Recent advances in efficient closed form solvers utilizing resultant matrices have shown a promising research direction to decrease the computation time while preserving the estimation accuracy. In this paper, we propose a new class of resultant-based solvers that exploit Sylvester forms to further reduce the complexity of the resolution. We demonstrate that our proposed methods are numerically as accurate as the state-of-the-art solvers, and outperform them in terms of computational time. We show that this approach can be applied for pose estimation in two different types of problems: estimating a pose from 3D to 3D correspondences, and estimating a pose from 3D points to 2D points correspondences.

AI.tldr: New class of resultant-based pose solvers using Sylvester forms to reduce complexity of solving non-linear least-squares for rotation and translation; achieves faster computation while maintaining accuracy, applicable to 3D-3D and 3D-2D pose estimation.
AI.motivation: Pose estimation via non-linear least squares is common but computationally expensive; real-time applications require fast, robust closed-form solvers. Existing resultant-based methods are promising but can be further optimized.
AI.method: Introduce Sylvester-form-based resultant solvers to solve polynomial systems arising from pose estimation. This reduces resolution complexity compared to prior resultant-based approaches, while maintaining numerical accuracy. Applicable to two problem types: 3D-3D pose estimation and 3D-2D pose estimation.
AI.result: The proposed solvers achieve numerical accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art methods but with significantly lower computational time.
AI.conclusion: Sylvester-form resultant solvers provide a more efficient closed-form solution for pose estimation without sacrificing accuracy, applicable to both 3D-3D and 3D-2D correspondence scenarios; suitable for real-time applications.

title: High-Speed Full-Color HDR Imaging via Unwrapping Modulo-Encoded Spike Streams
summary: Conventional RGB-based high dynamic range (HDR) imaging faces a fundamental trade-off between motion artifacts in multi-exposure captures and irreversible information loss in single-shot techniques. Modulo sensors offer a promising alternative by encoding theoretically unbounded dynamic range into wrapped measurements. However, existing modulo solutions remain bottlenecked by iterative unwrapping overhead and hardware constraints limiting them to low-speed, grayscale capture. In this work, we present a complete modulo-based HDR imaging system that enables high-speed, full-color HDR acquisition by synergistically advancing both the sensing formulation and the unwrapping algorithm. At the core of our approach is an exposure-decoupled formulation of modulo imaging that allows multiple measurements to be interleaved in time, preserving a clean, observation-wise measurement model. Building upon this, we introduce an iteration-free unwrapping algorithm that integrates diffusion-based generative priors with the physical least absolute remainder property of modulo images, supporting highly efficient, physics-consistent HDR reconstruction. Finally, to validate the practical viability of our system, we demonstrate a proof-of-concept hardware implementation based on modulo-encoded spike streams. This setup preserves the native high temporal resolution of spike cameras, achieving 1000 FPS full-color imaging while reducing output data bandwidth from approximately 20 Gbps to 6 Gbps. Extensive evaluations indicate that our coordinated approach successfully overcomes key systemic bottlenecks, demonstrating the feasibility of deploying modulo imaging in dynamic scenarios.

AI.tldr: A complete modulo-based HDR imaging pipeline enabling high-speed, full-color HDR via exposure-decoupled modulo sensing, an iteration-free unwrapping with diffusion priors, and a spike-stream hardware proof-of-concept achieving 1000 FPS color HDR and reduced bandwidth.
AI.motivation: To overcome the fundamental trade-offs in conventional RGB HDR (motion artifacts vs irreversible information loss) and to address bottlenecks of prior modulo sensors (slow, grayscale, iterative unwrap).
AI.method: Introduce an exposure-decoupled modulo imaging formulation enabling time-interleaved measurements with a clean observation model. Develop an iteration-free unwrapping algorithm that merges diffusion-based priors with the physics of modulo images via the least absolute remainder property. Validate with a proof-of-concept hardware implementation using modulo-encoded spike streams to preserve high temporal resolution while reducing output bandwidth.
AI.result: Demonstrates 1000 FPS full-color HDR in a proof-of-concept hardware setup; bandwidth reduced from ~20 Gbps to ~6 Gbps; extensive evaluations indicate the approach overcomes core bottlenecks and enables dynamic-scene modulo imaging.
AI.conclusion: The coordinated sensing, unwrapping, and hardware design make modulo imaging a feasible option for high-speed, dynamic HDR tasks, addressing previous speed and color limitations.

title: CSRA: Controlled Spectral Residual Augmentation for Robust Sepsis Prediction
summary: Accurate prediction of future risk and disease progression in sepsis is clinically important for early warning and timely intervention in intensive care. However, short-window sepsis prediction remains challenging, because shorter observation windows provide limited historical evidence, whereas longer prediction horizons reduce the number of patient trajectories with valid future supervision. To address this problem, we propose CSRA, a Controlled Spectral Residual Augmentation framework for short-window multi-system ICU time series. CSRA first groups variables by clinical systems and extracts system-level and global representations. It then performs input-adaptive residual perturbation in the spectral domain to generate structured and clinically plausible trajectory variations. To improve augmentation stability and controllability, CSRA is trained end-to-end with the downstream predictor under a unified objective, together with anchor consistency loss and controller regularization. Experiments on a MIMIC-IV sepsis cohort across multiple downstream models show that CSRA is consistently competitive and often superior, reducing regression error by 10.2\% in MSE and 3.7\% in MAE over the non-augmentation baseline, while also yielding consistent gains on classification. CSRA further maintains more favorable performance under shorter observation windows, longer prediction horizons, and smaller training data scales, while also remaining effective on an external clinical dataset~(ZiGongICUinfection), indicating stronger robustness and generalizability in clinically constrained settings.

AI.tldr: CSRA is a spectral augmentation framework for short-window multi-system ICU time series that improves sepsis prediction by generating plausible trajectory perturbations; it shows robust, consistent gains in MSE/MAE and classification, especially under limited data and shorter windows, and generalizes to external datasets.
AI.motivation: Short-window sepsis prediction is hard due to limited historical evidence and fewer valid future labels; a principled augmentation that preserves clinical plausibility and improves downstream performance is needed.
AI.method: CSRA groups variables by clinical systems to build system-level and global representations, then applies input-adaptive residual perturbations in the spectral domain to create structured trajectory variations. It is trained end-to-end with the downstream predictor using an objective that includes anchor consistency loss and controller regularization.
AI.result: In the MIMIC-IV sepsis cohort across multiple downstream models, CSRA reduced regression error by 10.2% in MSE and 3.7% in MAE versus a non-augmentation baseline and improved classification. It maintains performance gains under shorter observation windows, longer prediction horizons, and smaller training data, and generalizes to an external dataset (ZiGongICUinfection).
AI.conclusion: CSRA provides a robust, effective data augmentation approach for short-window ICU time series, improving predictive accuracy and generalizability in clinically constrained settings.

title: El Agente Forjador: Task-Driven Agent Generation for Quantum Simulation
summary: AI for science promises to accelerate the discovery process. The advent of large language models (LLMs) and agentic workflows enables the expediting of a growing range of scientific tasks. However, most of the current generation of agentic systems depend on static, hand-curated toolsets that hinder adaptation to new domains and evolving libraries. We present El Agente Forjador, a multi-agent framework in which universal coding agents autonomously forge, validate, and reuse computational tools through a four-stage workflow of tool analysis, tool generation, task execution, and iterative solution evaluation. Evaluated across 24 tasks spanning quantum chemistry and quantum dynamics on five coding agent setups, we compare three operating modes: zero-shot generation of tools per task, reuse of a curriculum-built toolset, and direct problem-solving with the coding agents as the baseline. We find that our tool generation and reuse framework consistently improves accuracy over the baseline. We also show that reusing a toolset built by a stronger coding agent can reduce API cost and substantially raises the solution quality for weaker coding agents. Case studies further demonstrate that tools forged for different domains can be combined to solve hybrid tasks. Taken together, these results show that LLM-based agents can use their scientific knowledge and coding capabilities to autonomously build reusable scientific tools, pointing toward a paradigm in which agent capabilities are defined by the tasks they are designed to solve rather than by explicitly engineered implementations.

AI.tldr: LLM-based multi-agent system autonomously forges, validates, and reuses computational tools, improving accuracy and efficiency over baselines by enabling tool generation/reuse across domains.
AI.motivation: Current agentic AI systems rely on static, hand-curated toolsets that do not adapt well to new domains or evolving libraries; there is a need for autonomous tool-building to expand scientific capabilities.
AI.method: Four-stage workflow (tool analysis → tool generation → task execution → iterative solution evaluation) applied to 24 tasks in quantum chemistry/dynamics across five coding agent setups; compare zero-shot tool generation, curriculum-based tool reuse, and direct problem-solving (baseline).
AI.result: Tool generation and reuse consistently improve accuracy over the baseline; reusing tools built by stronger agents reduces API costs and enhances solution quality for weaker agents; hybrid tasks solved by combining tools from multiple domains.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based agents can autonomously construct reusable scientific tools guided by task requirements, suggesting a shift from hand-engineered tool suites to task-defined agent capabilities.

title: ADAPT: Benchmarking Commonsense Planning under Unspecified Affordance Constraints
summary: Intelligent embodied agents should not simply follow instructions, as real-world environments often involve unexpected conditions and exceptions. However, existing methods usually focus on directly executing instructions, without considering whether the target objects can actually be manipulated, meaning they fail to assess available affordances. To address this limitation, we introduce DynAfford, a benchmark that evaluates embodied agents in dynamic environments where object affordances may change over time and are not specified in the instruction. DynAfford requires agents to perceive object states, infer implicit preconditions, and adapt their actions accordingly. To enable this capability, we introduce ADAPT, a plug-and-play module that augments existing planners with explicit affordance reasoning. Experiments demonstrate that incorporating ADAPT significantly improves robustness and task success across both seen and unseen environments. We also show that a domain-adapted, LoRA-finetuned vision-language model used as the affordance inference backend outperforms a commercial LLM (GPT-4o), highlighting the importance of task-aligned affordance grounding.

AI.tldr: DynAfford introduces a dynamic affordance benchmark and a plug-in ADAPT module to inject explicit affordance reasoning into planners, improving robustness and success, with a domain-adapted LoRA vision-language model outperforming GPT-4o as the inference backend.
AI.motivation: Embodied agents often execute instructions without validating whether target objects can be manipulated; real-world tasks involve changing affordances and implicit preconditions that are not specified in instructions, requiring perception, inference, and adaptation.
AI.method: Propose DynAfford as a benchmark to test agents in dynamic environments where affordances change; introduce ADAPT, a plug-and-play module that augments planners with explicit affordance reasoning; employ a domain-adapted LoRA-tuned vision-language model as the affordance inference backend, compared against GPT-4o; evaluate on seen and unseen environments.
AI.result: ADAPT improves robustness and task success across both seen and unseen environments; the LoRA-based affordance inference backend outperforms GPT-4o, underscoring the value of task-aligned grounding for affordances.
AI.conclusion: Explicit affordance reasoning via ADAPT enhances embodied agent robustness in dynamic settings; affordance grounding is critical, and domain-adapted models can surpass large commercial LLMs for this role.

title: Physically-Induced Atmospheric Adversarial Perturbations: Enhancing Transferability and Robustness in Remote Sensing Image Classification
summary: Adversarial attacks pose a severe threat to the reliability of deep learning models in remote sensing (RS) image classification. Most existing methods rely on direct pixel-wise perturbations, failing to exploit the inherent atmospheric characteristics of RS imagery or survive real-world image degradations. In this paper, we propose FogFool, a physically plausible adversarial framework that generates fog-based perturbations by iteratively optimizing atmospheric patterns based on Perlin noise. By modeling fog formations with natural, irregular structures, FogFool generates adversarial examples that are not only visually consistent with authentic RS scenes but also deceptive. By leveraging the spatial coherence and mid-to-low-frequency nature of atmospheric phenomena, FogFool embeds adversarial information into structural features shared across diverse architectures. Extensive experiments on two benchmark RS datasets demonstrate that FogFool achieves superior performance: not only does it exceed in white-box settings, but also exhibits exceptional black-box transferability (reaching 83.74% TASR) and robustness against common preprocessing-based defenses such as JPEG compression and filtering. Detailed analyses, including confusion matrices and Class Activation Map (CAM) visualizations, reveal that our atmospheric-driven perturbations induce a universal shift in model attention. These results indicate that FogFool represents a practical, stealthy, and highly persistent threat to RS classification systems, providing a robust benchmark for evaluating model reliability in complex environments.

AI.tldr: FogFool introduces a physically plausible fog-based adversarial framework for remote sensing image classification. It optimizes atmospheric patterns via Perlin noise to produce visually authentic, structurally embedded perturbations that transfer across models and resist common degradations, revealing universal attention shifts in CNNs.
AI.motivation: Existing pixel-wise attacks neglect atmospheric phenomena and fail under real-world degradations, risking overestimating model robustness. There is a need for adversarial perturbations that are physically plausible for RS imagery to accurately assess reliability and defense capabilities.
AI.method: Iteratively optimize atmospheric patterns using Perlin noise to simulate fog, exploiting the spatial coherence and mid-to-low-frequency components of atmospheric effects. Embed adversarial information into structural features common across architectures, improving both white-box strength and black-box transferability. Evaluate on two RS datasets with analyses including confusion matrices and CAM visualizations.
AI.result: FogFool achieves strong white-box performance and exceptional black-box transferability (reported TASR up to 83.74%). It remains robust against defenses like JPEG compression and filtering.
AI.conclusion: FogFool provides a practical, stealthy, and persistent threat to RS classification, offering a challenging benchmark for evaluating model reliability under atmospheric perturbations and complex degradations.

title: An unsupervised decision-support framework for multivariate biomarker analysis in athlete monitoring
comment: 15 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables, submitted to Springer Nature Scientific Reports
summary: Purpose. Athlete monitoring is constrained by small cohorts, heterogeneous biomarker scales, limited feasibility of repeated sampling, and the lack of reliable injury ground truth. These limitations reduce the interpretability and utility of traditional univariate and binary risk models. This study addresses these challenges by proposing an unsupervised multivariate framework to identify latent physiological states in athletes using real data. Methods. We propose a modular computational framework that operates in the joint biomarker space, integrating preprocessing, clinical safety screening, unsupervised clustering, and centroid-based physiological interpretation. Profiles are learned exclusively from amateur soccer players during a competitive microcycle. Synthetic data augmentation evaluates robustness and scalability. Ward hierarchical clustering supports monitoring and etiological differentiation, while Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) enable structural stability analysis in high-dimensional settings. Results. The framework identifies coherent profiles that distinguish mechanical damage from metabolic stress while preserving homeostatic states. Synthetic data augmentation demonstrates feasibility and detection of latent silent risk phenotypes typically missed by univariate monitoring. Structural analyses indicate robustness under augmentation and higher-dimensional settings. Conclusion. The framework enables interpretable identification of latent physiological states from multivariate biomarker data without injury labels. By distinguishing mechanisms and revealing silent risk patterns not captured by conventional monitoring, it provides actionable insights for individualized athlete monitoring and decision making.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised multivariate framework identifies latent physiological states in athletes from joint biomarker space, using Ward clustering and Gaussian Mixture Models; distinguishes mechanical damage from metabolic stress without injury labels; validated with synthetic data augmentation; provides interpretable, actionable insights for individual monitoring.
AI.motivation: Overcomes limitations of univariate/binary risk models caused by small, heterogeneous cohorts, limited repeated sampling, and poor injury ground truth by learning latent states from real, multivariate biomarker data.
AI.method: Modular pipeline in joint biomarker space: preprocessing, clinical safety screening, unsupervised clustering with Ward hierarchical method for profiling, centroid-based interpretation, and Gaussian Mixture Models for stability analysis. Includes synthetic data augmentation to test robustness and scalability. Profiles learned from amateur soccer players during a competitive microcycle.
AI.result: Identification of coherent physiological profiles that separate mechanical damage from metabolic stress while maintaining homeostasis. Synthetic augmentation demonstrates detection of latent silent risk phenotypes not captured by univariate monitoring. Structural analyses show robustness to augmentation and higher-dimensional settings.
AI.conclusion: The framework yields interpretable latent physiological states without injury labels, differentiates mechanisms, reveals silent risk patterns, and offers actionable insights for individualized athlete monitoring and decision making.

title: CoDaS: AI Co-Data-Scientist for Biomarker Discovery via Wearable Sensors
summary: Scientific discovery in digital health requires converting continuous physiological signals from wearable devices into clinically actionable biomarkers. We introduce CoDaS (AI Co-Data-Scientist), a multi-agent system that structures biomarker discovery as an iterative process combining hypothesis generation, statistical analysis, adversarial validation, and literature-grounded reasoning with human oversight using large-scale wearable datasets. Across three cohorts totaling 9,279 participant-observations, CoDaS identified 41 candidate digital biomarkers for mental health and 25 for metabolic outcomes, each subjected to an internal validation battery spanning replication, stability, robustness, and discriminative power. Across two independent depression cohorts, CoDaS surfaced circadian instability-related features in both datasets, reflected in sleep duration variability (DWB, ρ= 0.252, p < 0.001) and sleep onset variability (GLOBEM, ρ= 0.126, p < 0.001). In a metabolic cohort, CoDaS derived a cardiovascular fitness index (steps/resting heart rate; ρ= -0.374, p < 0.001), and recovered established clinical associations, including the hepatic function ratio (AST/ALT; ρ= -0.375, p < 0.001), a known correlate of insulin resistance. Incorporating CoDaS-derived features alongside demographic variables led to modest but consistent improvements in predictive performance, with cross-validated ΔR^2 increases of 0.040 for depression and 0.021 for insulin resistance. These findings suggest that CoDaS enables systematic and traceable hypothesis generation and prioritization for biomarker discovery from large-scale wearable data.

AI.tldr: CoDaS is a multi-agent AI framework for discovering digital biomarkers from large-scale wearable data, achieving several candidate biomarkers and modest predictive gains across mental health and metabolic outcomes with robust internal validation and literature-grounded reasoning.
AI.motivation: Transform continuous wearable signals into clinically actionable, traceable biomarkers with human oversight, addressing replication, robustness, and generalization in digital health discovery.
AI.method: An iterative, multi-agent pipeline combining hypothesis generation, statistical analysis, adversarial validation, and literature-grounded reasoning. Evaluated on 3 cohorts (9,279 observations) for biomarker discovery, and across two independent depression cohorts and one metabolic cohort for validation. Reports ρ and p-values for associations; cross-validated ΔR^2 improvements when combining CoDaS features with demographics.
AI.result: Identified 41 candidate digital biomarkers for mental health and 25 for metabolic outcomes. Detected circadian-instability features (sleep duration variability; sleep onset variability) in depression cohorts; derived a cardiovascular fitness index; recovered hepatic function ratio (AST/ALT). Internal validation across replication, stability, robustness, and discriminative power. Modest predictive gains: ΔR^2 of 0.040 for depression and 0.021 for insulin resistance.
AI.conclusion: CoDaS enables systematic, traceable hypothesis generation and prioritization for biomarker discovery from large-scale wearable data, with robust associations and modest improvements in predictive performance.

title: R3D: Revisiting 3D Policy Learning
summary: 3D policy learning promises superior generalization and cross-embodiment transfer, but progress has been hindered by training instabilities and severe overfitting, precluding the adoption of powerful 3D perception models. In this work, we systematically diagnose these failures, identifying the omission of 3D data augmentation and the adverse effects of Batch Normalization as primary causes. We propose a new architecture coupling a scalable transformer-based 3D encoder with a diffusion decoder, engineered specifically for stability at scale and designed to leverage large-scale pre-training. Our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art 3D baselines on challenging manipulation benchmarks, establishing a new and robust foundation for scalable 3D imitation learning. Project Page: https://r3d-policy.github.io/

AI.tldr: A scalable 3D imitation learning framework using a transformer-based 3D encoder and diffusion decoder, addressing 3D data augmentation and BN-related instabilities to achieve improved generalization and cross-embodiment transfer on manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: 3D policy learning offers generalization and cross-embodiment transfer but suffers from training instabilities and overfitting. The paper diagnoses primary causes as lack of 3D data augmentation and adverse effects of Batch Normalization, and seeks a stable, scalable solution.
AI.method: A scalable transformer-based 3D encoder paired with a diffusion decoder, designed for stability at scale and capable of leveraging large-scale pre-training, with explicit handling of 3D data augmentation and BN-related issues.
AI.result: Significant improvement over state-of-the-art 3D baselines on challenging manipulation benchmarks, demonstrating a robust foundation for scalable 3D imitation learning.
AI.conclusion: Introduces a robust, scalable architecture that enables stable, large-scale 3D policy learning and improves generalization, establishing a new foundation for scalable 3D imitation learning.

title: Chaotic CNN for Limited Data Image Classification
summary: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) often exhibit poor generalisation in limited training data scenarios due to overfitting and insufficient feature diversity. In this work, a simple and effective chaos-based feature transformation is proposed to enhance CNN performance without increasing model complexity. The method applies nonlinear transformations using logistic, skew tent, and sine maps to normalised feature vectors before the classification layer, thereby reshaping the feature space and improving class separability. The approach is evaluated on greyscale datasets (MNIST and Fashion-MNIST) and an RGB dataset (CIFAR-10) using CNN architectures of varying depth under limited data conditions. The results show consistent improvement over the standalone (SA) CNN across all datasets. Notably, a maximum performance gain of 5.43% is achieved on MNIST using the skew tent map with a 3-layer CNN at 40 samples per class. A higher gain of 9.11% is observed on Fashion-MNIST using the sine map with a 3-layer CNN at 50 samples per class. Additionally, a strong gain of 7.47% is obtained on CIFAR-10 using the skew tent map at 200 samples per class. The consistent improvements across different chaotic maps indicate that the performance gain is driven by the shared nonlinear and dynamical properties of chaotic systems. The proposed method is computationally efficient, requires no additional trainable parameters, and can be easily integrated into existing CNN architectures, making it a practical solution for data-scarce image classification tasks.

AI.tldr: Chaos-based nonlinear feature transforms are applied to CNN features before the classifier to improve generalization in data-scarce image classification without adding trainable parameters. They yield consistent accuracy gains on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10, with max improvements up to about 9% in few-shot settings, suggesting the benefit comes from nonlinear dynamical properties of chaotic maps.
AI.motivation: In limited training data regimes, CNNs suffer from overfitting and lack of feature diversity. A simple, parameter-free transformation that reshapes the feature space could enhance class separability without increasing model complexity.
AI.method: Normalize features, then apply nonlinear chaotic maps (logistic, skew tent, and sine maps) to the feature vectors prior to the classification layer. The transformation is parameter-free in terms of trainable weights and is compatible with CNNs of varying depth.
AI.result: Experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10 show consistent improvements over standalone CNNs across depths. Notable gains include 5.43% (MNIST, skew tent, 3-layer CNN at 40 samples/class), 9.11% (Fashion-MNIST, sine map, 3-layer CNN at 50 samples/class), and 7.47% (CIFAR-10, skew tent, 200 samples/class). The gains appear robust across different chaotic maps, indicating a shared benefit from nonlinear dynamical properties rather than map-specific effects.
AI.conclusion: Chaos-based feature transformation is an effective, computationally efficient, plug-in method to enhance CNN generalization under data scarcity without adding parameters. Its compatibility with various architectures and consistency across maps suggests broad applicability to data-limited image classification tasks.

title: Predicting Post-Traumatic Epilepsy from Clinical Records using Large Language Model Embeddings
summary: Objective: Post-traumatic epilepsy (PTE) is a debilitating neurological disorder that develops after traumatic brain injury (TBI). Early prediction of PTE remains challenging due to heterogeneous clinical data, limited positive cases, and reliance on resource-intensive neuroimaging data. We investigate whether routinely collected acute clinical records alone can support early PTE prediction using language model-based approaches. Methods: Using a curated subset of the TRACK-TBI cohort, we developed an automated PTE prediction framework that implements pretrained large language models (LLMs) as fixed feature extractors to encode clinical records. Tabular features, LLM-generated embeddings, and hybrid feature representations were evaluated using gradient-boosted tree classifiers under stratified cross-validation. Results: LLM embeddings achieved performance improvements by capturing contextual clinical information compared to using tabular features alone. The best performance was achieved by a modality-aware feature fusion strategy combining tabular features and LLM embeddings, achieving an AUC-ROC of 0.892 and AUPRC of 0.798. Acute post-traumatic seizures, injury severity, neurosurgical intervention, and ICU stay are key contributors to the predictive performance. Significance: These findings demonstrate that routine acute clinical records contain information suitable for early PTE risk prediction using LLM embeddings in conjunction with gradient-boosted tree classifiers. This approach represents a promising complement to imaging-based prediction.

AI.tldr: Routinely collected acute clinical records can predict post-traumatic epilepsy (PTE) early when encoded with pretrained large language model (LLM) embeddings and fused with tabular data, achieving AUC-ROC 0.892 and AUPRC 0.798.
AI.motivation: PTE prediction is hindered by heterogeneous data, limited positive cases, and reliance on resource-intensive neuroimaging. The study tests whether non-imaging, routinely collected acute records suffice for early PTE risk prediction using LLM-derived embeddings.
AI.method: Using a curated TRACK-TBI subset, an automated prediction framework encodes clinical records with fixed-feature-extractor LLM embeddings. Combines tabular features, LLM embeddings, and hybrids via modality-aware feature fusion. Gradient-boosted trees are trained under stratified cross-validation.
AI.result: LLM embeddings improved predictive power over tabular features alone. The best results came from modality-aware fusion of tabular features and LLM embeddings: AUC-ROC 0.892, AUPRC 0.798. Key contributing factors include acute post-traumatic seizures, injury severity, neurosurgical intervention, and ICU stay.
AI.conclusion: Routine acute clinical records, when paired with LLM-generated embeddings and a gradient-boosted classifier, provide a promising early-risk prediction framework for PTE and can complement imaging-based approaches.

title: A Parallel Approach to Counting Exact Covers Based on Decomposability Property
summary: The exact cover problem is a classical NP-hard problem with broad applications in the area of AI. Algorithm DXZ is a method to count exact covers representing by zero-suppressed binary decision diagrams (ZBDDs). In this paper, we propose a zero-suppressed variant of decision decomposable negation normal form (in short, decision-ZDNNF), which is strictly more succinct than ZBDDs. We then design a novel parallel algorithm, namely DXD, which constructs a decision-ZDNNF representing the set of all exact covers. Furthermore, we improve DXD by dynamically updating connected components. The experimental results demonstrate that the improved DXD algorithm outperforms all of state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: Introduces a succinct zero-suppressed decision-ZDNNF representation and a parallel DXD algorithm to enumerate all exact covers, achieving better performance than state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: The exact cover problem is NP-hard and widely used in AI; existing representations like ZBDDs may not be optimally compact for counting/enumerating all exact covers, motivating a more succinct formalism and scalable parallel construction.
AI.method: Define a zero-suppressed variant of decision decomposable negation normal form (decision-ZDNNF); prove it is strictly more succinct than ZBDDs; design a parallel algorithm DXD to build a decision-ZDNNF representing all exact covers; further optimize DXD via dynamic updates of connected components.
AI.result: Experimental results indicate that the improved DXD algorithm outperforms state-of-the-art methods on tasks involving exact covers.
AI.conclusion: Decision-ZDNNF is strictly more succinct than ZBDDs for representing exact covers, and the DXD approach with dynamic component updates provides a scalable, high-performance solution for counting/enumerating all exact covers.

title: Seen-to-Scene: Keep the Seen, Generate the Unseen for Video Outpainting
comment: 8 pages, 8 figures (main paper); 9 pages, 10 figures (supplementary). Accepted to the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) 2026, Findings
summary: Video outpainting aims to expand the visible content of a video beyond the original frame boundaries while preserving spatial fidelity and temporal coherence across frames. Existing methods primarily rely on large-scale generative models, such as diffusion models. However, generationbased approaches suffer from implicit temporal modeling and limited spatial context. These limitations lead to intraframe and inter-frame inconsistencies, which become particularly pronounced in dynamic scenes and large outpainting scenarios. To overcome these challenges, we propose Seen-to-Scene, a novel framework that unifies propagationbased and generation-based paradigms for video outpainting. Specifically, Seen-to-Scene leverages flow-based propagation with a flow completion network pre-trained for video inpainting, which is fine-tuned in an end-to-end manner to bridge the domain gap and reconstruct coherent motion fields. To further improve the efficiency and reliability of propagation, we introduce a reference-guided latent propagation that effectively propagates source content across frames. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior temporal coherence and visual realism with efficient inference, surpassing even prior state-of-the-art methods that require input-specific adaptation.

AI.tldr: Seen-to-Scene unifies propagation-based and generation-based video outpainting by integrating a flow-completion network with end-to-end fine-tuning and reference-guided latent propagation, achieving superior temporal coherence and realism with efficient inference, without input-specific adaptation.
AI.motivation: Existing generation- and diffusion-model-based video outpainting methods struggle with temporal coherence due to implicit temporal modeling and limited spatial context. Large outpaintings exacerbate intra-frame and inter-frame inconsistencies, particularly in dynamic scenes. A hybrid approach that couples accurate motion propagation with generation promises better coherence and efficiency.
AI.method: 1) Use flow-based propagation with a flow-completion network pre-trained for video inpainting, which is fine-tuned end-to-end to bridge the domain gap and reconstruct coherent motion fields. 2) Introduce reference-guided latent propagation to reliably propagate source content across frames and improve efficiency. 3) Combine propagation and generation within Seen-to-Scene to expand frames while preserving spatial fidelity and temporal coherence.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show superior temporal coherence and visual realism with efficient inference, surpassing prior state-of-the-art methods that require input-specific adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Seen-to-Scene delivers a unified, efficient framework that overcomes the limitations of purely generative methods by leveraging flow-based propagation and latent guidance for robust, scalable video outpainting.

title: Material-Agnostic Zero-Shot Thermal Inference for Metal Additive Manufacturing via a Parametric PINN Framework
summary: Accurate thermal modeling in metal additive manufacturing (AM) is essential for understanding the process-structure-performance relationship. While prior studies have explored generalization across unseen process conditions, they often require extensive datasets, costly retraining, or pre-training. Generalization across different materials also remains relatively unexplored due to the challenges posed by distinct material-dependent thermal behaviors. This paper introduces a parametric physics-informed neural network (PINN) framework for zero-shot generalization across arbitrary materials without labeled data, retraining, or pre-training. The framework adopts a decoupled parametric PINN architecture that separately encodes material properties and spatiotemporal coordinates, fusing them through conditional modulation to better align with the multiplicative role of material parameters in the governing equation and boundary conditions. Physics-guided output scaling derived from Rosenthal's analytical solution and a hybrid optimization strategy are further incorporated to enhance physical consistency, training stability, and convergence. Experiments on bare plate laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) across diverse metal alloys, including both in-distribution and out-of-distribution cases, demonstrate effective zero-shot generalizability along with superior training efficiency. Specifically, the proposed framework achieved up to a 64.2% reduction in relative L2 error compared to the non-parametric baseline while surpassing its performance within only 4.4% of the baseline training epochs. Ablation studies confirm that the proposed framework's components are broadly applicable to other PINN-based approaches. Overall, the proposed framework provides an efficient and scalable material-agnostic solution for zero-shot thermal modeling, contributing to more flexible and practical deployment in metal AM.

AI.tldr: Proposes a decoupled parametric PINN enabling zero-shot, material-agnostic thermal modeling for metal AM; uses material-property conditioning, Rosenthal-guided scaling, and a hybrid optimizer to generalize across unseen alloys without labeled data or retraining, with improved accuracy and training efficiency.
AI.motivation: Generalization across unseen materials and process conditions is challenging in AM thermal modeling; existing approaches require extensive data, retraining, or pre-training, hindering practical deployment. A material-agnostic, data-free zero-shot method would enable scalable, flexible modeling.
AI.method: A decoupled parametric PINN that encodes material properties and spatiotemporal coordinates separately and fuses them via conditional modulation. Incorporates physics-guided output scaling based on Rosenthal’s solution and a hybrid optimization strategy. Trained/evaluated on bare plate LPBF across diverse metal alloys, including in- and out-of-distribution cases.
AI.result: Achieves up to 64.2% reduction in relative L2 error vs a non-parametric baseline; reaches comparable performance within only 4.4% of the baseline training epochs. Ablation studies show the components improve generalization and training stability, and are broadly applicable to other PINN-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers an efficient, scalable, material-agnostic solution for zero-shot thermal modeling in metal AM, enabling practical deployment and potentially extending to other PINN-based approaches.

title: Learning to Draw ASCII Improves Spatial Reasoning in Language Models
summary: When faced with complex spatial problems, humans naturally sketch layouts to organize their thinking, and the act of drawing further sharpens their understanding. In this work, we ask whether a similar principle holds for Large Language Models (LLMs): can learning to construct explicit visual layouts from spatial descriptions instill genuine spatial understanding? We introduce Text2Space, a dataset that pairs natural language descriptions with ground-truth ASCII grid layouts and spatial QA pairs, enabling us to separate failures in constructing spatial representations from failures in reasoning over them. We adopt ASCII because it is human-readable, operates entirely within the token space of language models, and encodes spatial relations in a structurally verifiable form. Our evaluation reveals a pronounced "Read-Write Asymmetry": LLMs interpret ASCII representations effectively but struggle to produce them from text, and these construction errors propagate to incorrect answers downstream. To address this limitation, we train models on layout construction (Text$\rightarrow$ASCII) and find that it significantly improves spatial reasoning from text alone, even without producing any ASCII at inference time. Combining construction with comprehension training further amplifies these gains. Crucially, these improvements transfer to three external spatial reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating that, much as sketching sharpens human spatial thinking, learning to construct explicit layouts instills spatial understanding that generalizes beyond the training format.

AI.tldr: Text2Space introduces a dataset pairing natural language spatial descriptions with ground-truth ASCII grid layouts to diagnose and improve LLM spatial understanding. It reveals a Read-Write Asymmetry (LLMs read ASCII well but struggle to generate it from text) and shows that training on layout construction (Text→ASCII) substantially improves downstream spatial reasoning from text, with further gains when combined with comprehension training; these benefits transfer to external spatial benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To determine whether enabling explicit spatial layout construction can induce genuine spatial understanding in LLMs, addressing the gap between reading and producing spatial representations and the impact on reasoning.
AI.method: Create Text2Space dataset with natural language descriptions, ground-truth ASCII layouts, and spatial QA. Diagnose read-write asymmetry. Train models on layout construction (Text→ASCII) and on joint construction+comprehension. Evaluate spatial reasoning across internal tests and three external benchmarks to assess generalization.
AI.result: Training on layout construction improves spatial reasoning from text even when ASCII is not used at inference. Joint construction+comprehension training yields stronger gains, and improvements transfer to three external spatial reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly learning to construct layouts cultivates spatial understanding that generalizes beyond the training format, akin to sketching sharpening human spatial thinking.

title: DETR-ViP: Detection Transformer with Robust Discriminative Visual Prompts
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026
summary: Visual prompted object detection enables interactive and flexible definition of target categories, thereby facilitating open-vocabulary detection. Since visual prompts are derived directly from image features, they often outperform text prompts in recognizing rare categories. Nevertheless, research on visual prompted detection has been largely overlooked, and it is typically treated as a byproduct of training text prompted detectors, which hinders its development. To fully unlock the potential of visual-prompted detection, we investigate the reasons why its performance is suboptimal and reveal that the underlying issue lies in the absence of global discriminability in visual prompts. Motivated by these observations, we propose DETR-ViP, a robust object detection framework that yields class-distinguishable visual prompts. On top of basic image-text contrastive learning, DETR-ViP incorporates global prompt integration and visual-textual prompt relation distillation to learn more discriminative prompt representations. In addition, DETR-ViP employs a selective fusion strategy that ensures stable and robust detection. Extensive experiments on COCO, LVIS, ODinW, and Roboflow100 demonstrate that DETR-ViP achieves substantially higher performance in visual prompt detection compared to other state-of-the-art counterparts. A series of ablation studies and analyses further validate the effectiveness of the proposed improvements and shed light on the underlying reasons for the enhanced detection capability of visual prompts.

AI.tldr: DETR-ViP improves visual-prompted object detection by generating discriminative visual prompts through global prompt integration and visual–textual relation distillation, plus a selective fusion strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results on COCO, LVIS, ODinW, and Roboflow100.
AI.motivation: Visual prompts can outperform text prompts but suffer from a lack of global discriminability, limiting open-vocabulary detection. The study identifies global discriminability as the key bottleneck and seeks to unlock the full potential of visual-prompted detection.
