The model is trained in a DL framework via a composite loss that jointly optimizes prediction accuracy and PI quality. Results on benchmark SysID datasets show that xFODE+ matches FODE in PI quality and achieves comparable accuracy, while providing interpretability.

AI.tldr: An interpretable, uncertainty-aware system identification model xFODE+ that provides prediction intervals while preserving physically meaningful incremental states by using Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Logic Systems with constrained membership and integrating into additive ODEs; trained with a joint loss to balance accuracy and interval quality; achieves PI quality comparable to FODE and similar accuracy on benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is essential for data-driven system identification (SysID) but existing UQ-capable models (e.g., FODE) lack interpretability. The work aims to combine UQ with interpretability in a DL framework.
AI.method: Develop xFODE+ where each fuzzy additive model uses Interval Type-2 FLSs with membership functions constrained to two neighboring rules to limit overlap; type-reduced sets are aggregated to form state updates and PIs; train via a composite loss that jointly optimizes prediction accuracy and PI quality.
AI.result: On benchmark SysID datasets, xFODE+ achieves PI quality comparable to FODE and comparable predictive accuracy, while providing interpretability.
AI.conclusion: xFODE+ offers an interpretable, uncertainty-aware SysID framework that retains predictive performance and produces reliable prediction intervals, enhancing transparency in data-driven modeling.

title: The LLM Fallacy: Misattribution in AI-Assisted Cognitive Workflows
summary: The rapid integration of large language models (LLMs) into everyday workflows has transformed how individuals perform cognitive tasks such as writing, programming, analysis, and multilingual communication. While prior research has focused on model reliability, hallucination, and user trust calibration, less attention has been given to how LLM usage reshapes users' perceptions of their own capabilities. This paper introduces the LLM fallacy, a cognitive attribution error in which individuals misinterpret LLM-assisted outputs as evidence of their own independent competence, producing a systematic divergence between perceived and actual capability. We argue that the opacity, fluency, and low-friction interaction patterns of LLMs obscure the boundary between human and machine contribution, leading users to infer competence from outputs rather than from the processes that generate them. We situate the LLM fallacy within existing literature on automation bias, cognitive offloading, and human--AI collaboration, while distinguishing it as a form of attributional distortion specific to AI-mediated workflows. We propose a conceptual framework of its underlying mechanisms and a typology of manifestations across computational, linguistic, analytical, and creative domains. Finally, we examine implications for education, hiring, and AI literacy, and outline directions for empirical validation. We also provide a transparent account of human--AI collaborative methodology. This work establishes a foundation for understanding how generative AI systems not only augment cognitive performance but also reshape self-perception and perceived expertise.

AI.tldr: New cognitive attribution bias: LLM fallacy—users misattribute LLM-assisted outputs to their own competence, causing a gap between perceived and actual ability; framework, typology, implications, and directions for empirical validation.
AI.motivation: As LLMs become pervasive in writing, coding, analysis, and multilingual tasks, there is a need to understand how their outputs alter users' self-perception and perceived expertise.
AI.method: Conceptual synthesis of literature on automation bias, cognitive offloading, and human–AI collaboration; development of a conceptual framework and a typology of LLM fallacy manifestations; discussion of implications and plan for empirical validation; transparent methodology.
AI.result: Introduces the LLM fallacy, a distinct attributional distortion in AI-mediated workflows; provides a framework and typology across computational, linguistic, analytical, and creative domains; outlines educational, hiring, and AI literacy implications; suggests empirical validation pathways.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI reshapes self-perception and perceived expertise beyond performance gain; highlights need for education and policy adjustments and lays groundwork for future empirical work.

title: Open-Set Vein Biometric Recognition with Deep Metric Learning
comment: This preprint has not undergone peer review (when applicable) or any post-submission improvements or corrections. The Version of Record of this contribution is published in International Conference on Computational Science (ICCS 2026), and is available online at https://doi.org/[pending]
summary: Most state-of-the-art vein recognition methods rely on closed-set classification, which inherently limits their scalability and prevents the adaptive enrollment of new users without complete model retraining. We rigorously evaluate the computational boundaries of Deep Metric Learning (DML) under strict open-set constraints. Unlike standard closed-set approaches, we analyze the impact of data scarcity and domain shift on recognition performance. Our approach learns discriminative L2-normalised embeddings and employs prototype-based matching with a calibrated similarity threshold to effectively distinguish between enrolled users and unseen impostors. We evaluate the framework under a strict subject-disjoint protocol across four diverse datasets covering finger, wrist, and dorsal hand veins (MMCBNU 6000, UTFVP, FYO, and a dorsal hand-vein dataset). On the large-scale MMCBNU 6000 benchmark, our best model (ResNet50-CBAM) achieves an OSCR of 0.9945, AUROC of 0.9974, and EER of 1.57%, maintaining high identification accuracy (99.6% Rank-1) while robustly rejecting unknown subjects. Cross-dataset experiments evaluate the framework's generalisation across different acquisition setups, confirming that while the model handles large-scale data robustly, performance remains sensitive to domain shifts in low-data regimes. Ablation studies demonstrate that triplet-based objectives combined with a simple 1-NN classifier offer an optimal trade-off between accuracy and efficiency, enabling real-time deployment on commodity hardware.

AI.tldr: Open-set vein recognition using deep metric learning with L2-normalised embeddings and prototype-based matching; achieves near-perfect open-set metrics on MMCBNU 6000 and shows strong cross-dataset performance, with triplet loss and 1-NN offering efficient real-time deployment.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable vein recognition that supports adaptive enrollment without full model retraining, addressing data scarcity and domain shift in open-set settings.
AI.method: Learn discriminative L2-normalised embeddings; prototype-based matching with calibrated similarity threshold; strict subject-disjoint protocol; four vein datasets; ablations show triplet-based objectives + 1-NN; evaluations include OSCR, AUROC, EER, Rank-1; ResNet50-CBAM as backbone.
AI.result: On MMCBNU 6000, best model ResNet50-CBAM achieves OSCR 0.9945, AUROC 0.9974, EER 1.57%, Rank-1 99.6%. Cross-dataset experiments indicate robustness to scale but sensitivity to domain shifts in low-data regimes.
AI.conclusion: Open-set DML with prototype-based matching is effective for vein recognition, offering high accuracy and efficient deployment; triplet loss + 1-NN provides a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off; domain shift remains a challenge, especially under data scarcity.

title: xFODE: An Explainable Fuzzy Additive ODE Framework for System Identification
comment: in IEEE Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 2026
summary: Recent advances in Deep Learning (DL) have strengthened data-driven System Identification (SysID), with Neural and Fuzzy Ordinary Differential Equation (NODE/FODE) models achieving high accuracy in nonlinear dynamic modeling. Yet, system states in these frameworks are often reconstructed without clear physical meaning, and input contributions to the state derivatives remain difficult to interpret. To address these limitations, we propose Explainable FODE (xFODE), an interpretable SysID framework with integrated DL-based training. In xFODE, we define states in an incremental form to provide them with physical meanings. We employ fuzzy additive models to approximate the state derivative, thereby enhancing interpretability per input. To provide further interpretability, Partitioning Strategies (PSs) are developed, enabling the training of fuzzy additive models with explainability. By structuring the antecedent space during training so that only two consecutive rules are activated for any given input, PSs not only yield lower complexity for local inference but also enhance the interpretability of the antecedent space. To train xFODE, we present a DL framework with parameterized membership function learning that supports end-to-end optimization. Across benchmark SysID datasets, xFODE matches the accuracy of NODE, FODE, and NLARX models while providing interpretable insights.

AI.tldr: xFODE is an interpretable neural/fuzzy ODE-based system identification framework that uses incrementally defined states and fuzzy additive models with partitioning strategies to obtain explainable state derivatives without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current DL-based SysID methods (e.g., NODE/FODE/NLARX) often learn states with limited physical interpretation and opaque input contributions to state derivatives. There is a need for models that provide interpretable, physically meaningful state representations and interpretable derivatives.
AI.method: Define states in an incremental form to impart physical meaning; approximate state derivatives with fuzzy additive models. Introduce Partitioning Strategies (PSs) so that training activates only two consecutive rules for any input, reducing local inference complexity and enhancing interpretability of the antecedent space. Train end-to-end using a DL framework with parameterized membership functions for end-to-end optimization.
AI.result: On benchmark SysID datasets, xFODE matches the accuracy of NODE, FODE, and NLARX models while delivering interpretable insights into state dynamics and input contributions.
AI.conclusion: xFODE provides an interpretable yet accurate SysID framework by combining incremental state representations, fuzzy derivative modelling, and partitioning-based explainability, with end-to-end trainability.

title: Beyond Literal Summarization: Redefining Hallucination for Medical SOAP Note Evaluation
summary:
Evaluating large language models (LLMs) for clinical documentation tasks such as SOAP note generation remains challenging. Unlike standard summarization, these tasks require clinical abstraction, normalization of colloquial language, and medically grounded inference. However, prevailing evaluation methods including automated metrics and LLM as judge frameworks rely on lexical faithfulness, often labeling any information not explicitly present in the transcript as hallucination.
  We show that such approaches systematically misclassify clinically valid outputs as errors, inflating hallucination rates and distorting model assessment. Our analysis reveals that many flagged hallucinations correspond to legitimate clinical transformations, including synonym mapping, abstraction of examination findings, diagnostic inference, and guideline consistent care planning.
  By aligning evaluation criteria with clinical reasoning through calibrated prompting and retrieval grounded in medical ontologies we observe a significant shift in outcomes. Under a lexical evaluation regime, the mean hallucination rate is 35%, heavily penalizing valid reasoning. With inference aware evaluation, this drops to 9%, with remaining cases reflecting genuine safety concerns. These findings suggest that current evaluation practices over penalize valid clinical reasoning and may measure artifacts of evaluation design rather than true errors, underscoring the need for clinically informed evaluation in high context domains like medicine.

AI.tldr: Calibrated, inference-aware evaluation markedly reduces the estimated hallucinations in clinical notes, revealing that lexical metrics overestimate errors.
AI.motivation: Clinical documentation tasks require abstraction, normalization of colloquial language, and medically grounded inference. Lexical-only evaluation misclassifies legitimate clinical transformations as hallucinations, biasing model assessment in high-stakes medicine.
AI.method: Compare lexical faithfulness with inference-aware evaluation using calibrated prompting and retrieval grounded in medical ontologies. Analyze SOAP note generation for transformations such as synonym mapping, abstraction of findings, diagnostic inference, and guideline-based care planning.
AI.result: Lexical regime reports a mean hallucination rate of 35%; inference-aware evaluation reduces this to 9%; remaining cases are true safety concerns.
AI.conclusion: Evaluation practices should align with clinical reasoning and ontology-grounded grounding to avoid penalizing valid reasoning; clinically informed evaluation yields more accurate assessments of LLM performance in medicine.

title: FSDETR: Frequency-Spatial Feature Enhancement for Small Object Detection
summary: Small object detection remains a significant challenge due to feature degradation from downsampling, mutual occlusion in dense clusters, and complex background interference. To address these issues, this paper proposes FSDETR, a frequency-spatial feature enhancement framework built upon the RT-DETR baseline. By establishing a collaborative modeling mechanism, the method effectively leverages complementary structural information. Specifically, a Spatial Hierarchical Attention Block (SHAB) captures both local details and global dependencies to strengthen semantic representation. Furthermore, to mitigate occlusion in dense scenes, the Deformable Attention-based Intra-scale Feature Interaction (DA-AIFI) focuses on informative regions via dynamic sampling. Finally, the Frequency-Spatial Feature Pyramid Network (FSFPN) integrates frequency filtering with spatial edge extraction via the Cross-domain Frequency-Spatial Block (CFSB) to preserve fine-grained details. Experimental results show that with only 14.7M parameters, FSDETR achieves 13.9% APS on VisDrone 2019 and 48.95% AP50 tiny on TinyPerson, showing strong performance on small-object benchmarks. The code and models are available at https://github.com/YT3DVision/FSDETR.

AI.tldr: FSDETR is a lightweight frequency-spatial feature enhancement framework based on RT-DETR that improves small-object detection by introducing SHAB, DA-AIFI, and CFSB-based FSFPN; achieves strong APS/AP50 tiny on VisDrone 2019 and TinyPerson with 14.7M params.
AI.motivation: Small-object detection suffers from feature degradation due to downsampling, occlusion in dense scenes, and background clutter; a collaborative frequency-spatial strategy is proposed to preserve fine details and strengthen semantic representation.
AI.method: Proposes SHAB (Spatial Hierarchical Attention Block) for local-global attention; DA-AIFI (Deformable Attention-based Intra-scale Feature Interaction) for dynamic, informative-region sampling; FSFPN (Frequency-Spatial Feature Pyramid Network) with CFSB (Cross-domain Frequency-Spatial Block) combining frequency filtering with spatial edge extraction.
AI.result: With 14.7M parameters, FSDETR achieves 13.9% APS on VisDrone 2019 and 48.95% AP50 tiny on TinyPerson, demonstrating strong small-object performance.
AI.conclusion: FSDETR delivers effective small-object detection with a lightweight model by leveraging collaborative frequency-spatial modeling; code available at the provided GitHub link.

title: Can LLMs Score Medical Diagnoses and Clinical Reasoning as well as Expert Panels?
summary: Evaluating medical AI systems using expert clinician panels is costly and slow, motivating the use of large language models (LLMs) as alternative adjudicators. Here, we evaluate an LLM jury composed of three frontier AI models scoring 3333 diagnoses on 300 real-world middle-income country (MIC) hospital cases. Model performance was benchmarked against expert clinician panel and independent human re-scoring panel evaluations. Both LLM and clinician-generated diagnoses are scored across four dimensions: diagnosis, differential diagnosis, clinical reasoning and negative treatment risk. For each of these, we assess scoring difference, inter-rater agreement, scoring stability, severe safety errors and the effect of post-hoc calibration. We find that: (i) the uncalibrated LLM jury scores are systematically lower than clinician panels scores; (ii) the LLM Jury preserves ordinal agreement and exhibits better concordance with the primary expert panels than the human expert re-score panels do; (iii) the probability of severe errors is lower in \lj models compared to the human expert re-score panels; (iv) the LLM Jury shows excellent agreement with primary expert panels' rankings. We find that the LLM jury combined with AI model diagnoses can be used to identify ward diagnoses at high risk of error, enabling targeted expert review and improved panel efficiency; (v) LLM jury models show no self-preference bias. They did not score diagnoses generated by their own underlying model or models from the same vendor more (or less) favourably than those generated by other models. Finally, we demonstrate that LLM jury calibration using isotonic regression improves alignment with human expert panel evaluations. Together, these results provide compelling evidence that a calibrated, multi-model LLM jury can serve as a trustworthy and reliable proxy for expert clinician evaluation in medical AI benchmarking.

AI.tldr: A calibrated, multi-model LLM jury can serve as a trustworthy proxy for expert clinician evaluation in medical AI benchmarking, improving efficiency and enabling targeted expert review.
AI.motivation: Medical AI evaluation with expert clinician panels is costly and slow. This study investigates whether a multi-model LLM jury can reliably replace or supplement expert panels for benchmarking medical AI systems.
AI.method: A three-model frontier LLM jury scored 3333 diagnoses across 300 real-world MIC hospital cases. Comparisons were made against primary expert clinician panels and independent human re-scoring panels across four dimensions (diagnosis, differential diagnosis, clinical reasoning, negative treatment risk). Metrics included scoring differences, inter-rater agreement, scoring stability, severe safety errors, and post-hoc calibration effects via isotonic regression.
AI.result: Key findings: (i) Uncalibrated LLM jury scores are systematically lower than clinician panels. (ii) LLM jury preserves ordinal agreement and aligns better with primary expert panels than re-scoring panels do. (iii) Lower probability of severe errors in LLM models versus human re-scores. (iv) LLM jury rankings show excellent agreement with primary expert panels. (v) LLM jury + AI diagnoses can flag ward diagnoses with high error risk for targeted expert review, improving efficiency. (vi) No self-preference bias; calibration via isotonic regression improves alignment with human evaluations.
AI.conclusion: A calibrated, multi-model LLM jury is a trustworthy and reliable proxy for expert clinician evaluation in medical AI benchmarking, enabling efficient evaluation, targeted review, and reduced risk of severe errors.

title: Intermediate Layers Encode Optimal Biological Representations in Single-Cell Foundation Models
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures, 4 tables. Accepted at the LMRL (Learning Meaningful Representations of Life) Workshop at ICLR 2026
summary: Current single-cell foundation model benchmarks universally extract final layer embeddings, assuming these represent optimal feature spaces. We systematically evaluate layer-wise representations from scFoundation (100M parameters) and Tahoe-X1 (1.3B parameters) across trajectory inference and perturbation response prediction. Our analysis reveals that optimal layers are task-dependent (trajectory peaks at 60% depth, 31% above final layers) and context-dependent (perturbation optima shift 0-96% across T cell activation states). Notably, first-layer embeddings outperform all deeper layers in quiescent cells, challenging assumptions about hierarchical feature abstraction. These findings demonstrate that "where" to extract features matters as much as "what" the model learns, necessitating systematic layer evaluation tailored to biological task and cellular context rather than defaulting to final-layer embeddings.

AI.tldr: Layer selection in scFoundation and Tahoe-X1 is task- and context-dependent; early layers can outperform final embeddings; a systematic per-task layer evaluation is needed.
AI.motivation: Challenging the common assumption that final-layer embeddings are optimal; to improve performance on trajectory and perturbation tasks in single-cell models.
AI.method: Evaluate representations across layers (from two models: scFoundation 100M parameters and Tahoe-X1 1.3B parameters) for trajectory inference and perturbation response prediction; analyze optimal depth and layer-wise performance across cellular contexts.
AI.result: Optimal layers depend on task and context; trajectory peaks at ~60% depth, and is ~31% beyond the final layer; perturbation optima vary 0–96% across T cell activation states; first-layer embeddings outperform deeper layers in quiescent cells.
AI.conclusion: Feature extraction should consider layer choice; tailor layer selection to the specific biological task and cellular context rather than defaulting to final-layer embeddings.

title: Reward-Aware Trajectory Shaping for Few-step Visual Generation
summary: Achieving high-fidelity generation in extremely few sampling steps has long been a central goal of generative modeling. Existing approaches largely rely on distillation-based frameworks to compress the original multi-step denoising process into a few-step generator. However, such methods inherently constrain the student to imitate a stronger multi-step teacher, imposing the teacher as an upper bound on student performance. We argue that introducing \textbf{preference alignment awareness} enables the student to optimize toward reward-preferred generation quality, potentially surpassing the teacher instead of being restricted to rigid teacher imitation. To this end, we propose \textbf{Reward-Aware Trajectory Shaping (RATS)}, a lightweight framework for preference-aligned few-step generation. Specifically, teacher and student latent trajectories are aligned at key denoising stages through horizon matching, while a \textbf{reward-aware gate} is introduced to adaptively regulate teacher guidance based on their relative reward performance. Trajectory shaping is strengthened when the teacher achieves higher rewards, and relaxed when the student matches or surpasses the teacher, thereby enabling continued reward-driven improvement. By seamlessly integrating trajectory distillation, reward-aware gating, and preference alignment, RATS effectively transfers preference-relevant knowledge from high-step generators without incurring additional test-time computational overhead. Experimental results demonstrate that RATS substantially improves the efficiency--quality trade-off in few-step visual generation, significantly narrowing the gap between few-step students and stronger multi-step generators.

AI.tldr: RATS enables efficient few-step generation by aligning teacher and student trajectories and using a reward-aware gate, letting the student exceed the teacher in reward-based quality without extra test-time cost.
AI.motivation: Current distillation-based approaches constrain the student to imitate a stronger multi-step teacher, potentially capping performance. A preference-aware framework can optimize for reward-aligned generation quality and allow surpassing the teacher.
AI.method: Align teacher and student latent trajectories at key denoising stages via horizon matching. Introduce a reward-aware gate to adaptively regulate teacher guidance based on relative rewards. Strengthen trajectory shaping when the teacher has higher rewards; relax when the student matches or surpasses the teacher. Combine trajectory distillation, reward gating, and preference alignment for efficient few-step generation with no added test-time cost.
AI.result: Empirically improves the efficiency–quality trade-off in few-step visual generation, substantially narrowing the gap between few-step students and stronger multi-step generators.
AI.conclusion: Preference-aligned trajectory shaping with horizon matching and reward-aware gating enables reward-driven improvements and can surpass the teacher, achieving efficient high-quality generation in few steps.

title: Beyond Importance Sampling: Rejection-Gated Policy Optimization
comment: 27 pages, includes theoretical analysis and experiments
summary: We propose a new perspective on policy optimization: rather than reweighting all samples by their importance ratios, an optimizer should select which samples are trustworthy enough to drive a policy update. Building on this view, we introduce Rejection-Gated Policy Optimization (RGPO), which replaces the importance sampling ratio r_theta = pi_theta / pi_old with a smooth, differentiable acceptance gate alpha_theta(s, a) = g(r_theta(s, a)) in the range [0, 1]. Unlike prior work that applies rejection sampling as a data-level heuristic before training, RGPO elevates rejection to an optimization principle: the gate participates directly in gradient computation and is implicitly updated alongside the policy. RGPO provides a unified framework: the policy gradients of TRPO, PPO, and REINFORCE all correspond to specific choices of the effective gradient weight w(r) = g'(r) * r. We prove that RGPO guarantees finite, bounded gradient variance even when importance sampling ratios are heavy-tailed (where IS variance diverges). We further show that RGPO incurs only a bounded, controllable bias and provides an approximate monotonic policy improvement guarantee analogous to TRPO. RGPO matches PPO in computational cost, requires no second-order optimization, and extends naturally to RLHF-style preference alignment. In online preference fine-tuning of Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct on Anthropic HH-RLHF (n = 3 seeds), RGPO uses a dual-ratio gate that anchors learning to both the previous policy and the reference model, achieving a Pareto-dominant outcome: the highest reward among online RL methods (+14.8% vs. PPO-RLHF) and the lowest KL divergence to the reference model (-16.0% vs. PPO-RLHF, -53.1% vs. GRPO).

AI.tldr: RGPO introduces a differentiable acceptance gate on importance ratios to guide policy updates, unifying TRPO/PPO/REINFORCE as special cases, and achieving finite gradient variance with heavy-tailed IS while maintaining bounded bias and approximate monotonic improvement; empirically competitive in online RLHF with Pareto-dominant outcomes.
AI.motivation: Standard importance-sampling based reweighting in policy optimization yields high variance and unstable learning. RGPO proposes to gate samples by trustworthiness and integrate this gate into gradients, offering a unified perspective across policy-gradient methods and enabling RLHF-style preference alignment.
AI.method: Define r_theta(s,a)=pi_theta(a|s)/pi_old(a|s) and a differentiable gate alpha_theta(s,a)=g(r_theta(s,a)) in [0,1]. Use w(r)=g'(r)·r as the effective gradient weight; show TRPO/PPO/REINFORCE correspond to specific w choices. Prove finite gradient variance with heavy-tailed IS, bound bias, and approximate monotonic improvement. Match PPO in compute cost; no second-order optimization; extend to RLHF. In online RLHF on Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct (HH-RLHF, n=3), use a dual-ratio gate anchored to both previous policy and reference model to achieve Pareto-dominant results.
AI.result: RGPO guarantees finite, bounded gradient variance under heavy-tailed importance ratios, with a controlled bias and an approximate monotonic policy improvement guarantee. It matches PPO in computational cost, avoids second-order optimization, and naturally extends to RLHF. In online RLHF experiments on Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct, RGPO achieved a Pareto-dominant outcome: highest reward (+14.8% vs PPO-RLHF) and the lowest KL divergence to the reference model (-16.0% vs PPO-RLHF, -53.1% vs GRPO).
AI.conclusion: RGPO provides a unified, efficient, and robust framework for policy optimization that integrates sample rejection directly into gradient updates, enabling stable learning with heavy-tailed IS and extending naturally to RLHF-style alignment. Further work may explore gate design choices, robustness across domains, and empirical scalability.

title: Beyond Prompts: Unconditional 3D Inversion for Out-of-Distribution Shapes
summary: Text-driven inversion of generative models is a core paradigm for manipulating 2D or 3D content, unlocking numerous applications such as text-based editing, style transfer, or inverse problems. However, it relies on the assumption that generative models remain sensitive to natural language prompts. We demonstrate that for state-of-the-art native text-to-3D generative models, this assumption often collapses. We identify a critical failure mode where generation trajectories are drawn into latent ``sink traps'': regions where the model becomes insensitive to prompt modifications. In these regimes, changes to the input text fail to alter internal representations in a way that alters the output geometry. Crucially, we observe that this is not a limitation of the model's \textit{geometric} expressivity; the same generative models possess the ability to produce a vast diversity of shapes but, as we demonstrate, become insensitive to out-of-distribution \textit{text} guidance. We investigate this behavior by analyzing the sampling trajectories of the generative model, and find that complex geometries can still be represented and produced by leveraging the model's unconditional generative prior. This leads to a more robust framework for text-based 3D shape editing that bypasses latent sinks by decoupling a model's geometric representation power from its linguistic sensitivity. Our approach addresses the limitations of current 3D pipelines and enables high-fidelity semantic manipulation of out-of-distribution 3D shapes. Project webpage: https://daidedou.sorpi.fr/publication/beyondprompts

AI.tldr: The paper reveals latent sink traps in text-guided 3D generation where prompt changes fail to affect outputs, and proposes a decoupled framework that leverages the unconditional prior to achieve robust text-based 3D shape editing.
AI.motivation: To overcome prompts insensitivity in native text-to-3D models, enabling reliable semantic editing of 3D shapes, including out-of-distribution shapes.
AI.method: Analyze sampling trajectories to identify sink traps; demonstrate that geometric expressivity is intact; show that unconditional prior can realize diverse geometries; propose a decoupled approach that separates geometric representation from linguistic sensitivity to bypass sinks.
AI.result: Evidence that complex geometries can be produced via unconditional prior; new editing framework achieves higher fidelity semantic manipulation of out-of-distribution 3D shapes; addresses limitations of current 3D pipelines; project page.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling geometry from language sensitivity provides robust text-based 3D editing; results in improved manipulation across out-of-distribution shapes; sets direction for future 3D generation pipelines.

title: Multi-User mmWave Beam and Rate Adaptation via Combinatorial Satisficing Bandits
summary: We study downlink beam and rate adaptation in a multi-user mmWave MISO system where multiple base stations (BSs), each using analog beamforming from finite codebooks, serve multiple single-antenna user equipments (UEs) with a unique beam per UE and discrete data transmission rates. BSs learn about transmission success based on ACK/NACK feedback. To encode service goals, we introduce a satisficing throughput threshold $τ_r$ and cast joint beam and rate adaptation as a combinatorial semi-bandit over beam-rate tuples. Within this framework, we propose SAT-CTS, a lightweight, threshold-aware policy that blends conservative confidence estimates with posterior sampling, steering learning toward meeting $τ_r$ rather than merely maximizing. Our main theoretical contribution provides the first finite-time regret bounds for combinatorial semi-bandits with satisficing objective: when $τ_r$ is realizable, we upper bound the cumulative satisficing regret to the target with a time-independent constant, and when $τ_r$ is non-realizable, we show that SAT-CTS incurs only a finite expected transient outside committed CTS rounds, after which its regret is governed by the sum of the regret contributions of restarted CTS rounds, yielding an $O((\log T)^2)$ standard regret bound. On the practical side, we evaluate the performance via cumulative satisficing regret to $τ_r$ alongside standard regret and fairness. Experiments with time-varying sparse multipath channels show that SAT-CTS consistently reduces satisficing regret and maintains competitive standard regret, while achieving favorable average throughput and fairness across users, indicating that feedback-efficient learning can equitably allocate beams and rates to meet QoS targets without channel state knowledge.

AI.tldr: Proposes SAT-CTS, a threshold-aware combinatorial semi-bandit policy for joint beam and rate adaptation in multi-user mmWave MISO with finite codebooks; provides finite-time regret guarantees for a satisficing objective and shows empirical benefits in satisficing regret, throughput, and fairness.
AI.motivation: To meet QoS targets in mmWave multi-user downlink with limited feedback (ACK/NACK) and no full CSI, using a finite set of beams and discrete data rates, while ensuring fairness.
AI.method: Model the problem as a combinatorial semi-bandit over beam-rate tuples with a satisficing threshold τ_r. Introduce SAT-CTS, blending conservative confidence estimates with posterior sampling to favor achieving τ_r over pure reward maximization. Derive finite-time regret bounds under realizable and non-realizable τ_r, and validate via experiments on time-varying sparse multipath channels with ACK/NACK feedback.
AI.result: Theoretical: finite-time regret bounds for satisficing combinatorial semi-bandits: if τ_r realizable, cumulative satisficing regret to τ_r is upper-bounded by a time-independent constant; if τ_r non-realizable, only finite transient outside committed CTS rounds, then standard regret grows as O((log T)^2). Empirical: SAT-CTS reduces satisficing regret while maintaining competitive standard regret, achieving higher average throughput and fairness across users.
AI.conclusion: SAT-CTS provides a feedback-efficient policy that reliably meets QoS targets without full CSIT, backed by rigorous regret guarantees and favorable empirical performance in realistic mmWave multi-user settings.

title: Benchmarks for Trajectory Safety Evaluation and Diagnosis in OpenClaw and Codex: ATBench-Claw and ATBench-CodeX
summary: As agent systems move into increasingly diverse execution settings, trajectory-level safety evaluation and diagnosis require benchmarks that evolve with them. ATBench is a diverse and realistic agent trajectory benchmark for safety evaluation and diagnosis. This report presents ATBench-Claw and ATBench-CodeX, two domain-customized extensions that carry ATBench into the OpenClaw and OpenAI Codex / Codex-runtime settings. The key adaptation mechanism is to analyze each new setting, customize the three-dimensional Safety Taxonomy over risk source, failure mode, and real-world harm, and then use that customized taxonomy to define the benchmark specification consumed by the shared ATBench construction pipeline. This extensibility matters because agent frameworks remain relatively stable at the architectural level even as their concrete execution settings, tool ecosystems, and product capabilities evolve quickly. Concretely, ATBench-Claw targets OpenClaw-sensitive execution chains over tools, skills, sessions, and external actions, while ATBench-CodeX targets trajectories in the OpenAI Codex / Codex-runtime setting over repositories, shells, patches, dependencies, approvals, and runtime policy boundaries. Our emphasis therefore falls on taxonomy customization, domain-specific risk coverage, and benchmark design under a shared ATBench generation framework.

AI.tldr: ATBench extends a general trajectory-safety benchmark into domain-specific extensions by customizing a 3D Safety Taxonomy (risk source, failure mode, real-world harm) for OpenClaw (ATBench-Claw) and Codex/Codex-runtime (ATBench-CodeX) within a shared generation framework; enabling domain-focused safety evaluation while preserving architectural stability.
AI.motivation: Trajectory-level safety benchmarks must evolve with diverse execution settings and tool ecosystems. A stable architectural framework (ATBench) can be specialized via taxonomy-driven customization to cover domain-specific risks and harms.
AI.method: Define and customize a 3D Safety Taxonomy for each target domain, translate taxonomy into concrete benchmark specifications, and integrate these specs into the ATBench generation pipeline. Implement two domain-specific extensions: ATBench-Claw (tools, skills, sessions, external actions) and ATBench-CodeX (repositories, shells, patches, dependencies, approvals, runtime policies).
AI.result: Two domain-customized benchmarks (ATBench-Claw and ATBench-CodeX) that demonstrate taxonomy-driven adaptation within the shared ATBench pipeline, enabling domain-specific risk coverage and benchmark design in OpenClaw and Codex contexts.
AI.conclusion: Taxonomy-driven customization within a common ATBench framework yields extensible, domain-tailored trajectory benchmarks while preserving architectural stability, supporting safer evaluation as agent execution settings evolve.

title: Hybrid Latents -- Geometry-Appearance-Aware Surfel Splatting
summary: We introduce a hybrid Gaussian-hash-grid radiance representation for reconstructing 2D Gaussian scene models from multi-view images. Similar to NeST splatting, our approach reduces the entanglement between geometry and appearance common in NeRF-based models, but adds per-Gaussian latent features alongside hash-grid features to bias the optimizer toward a separation of low- and high-frequency scene components. This explicit frequency-based decomposition reduces the tendency of high-frequency texture to compensate for geometric errors. Encouraging Gaussians with hard opacity falloffs further strengthens the separation between geometry and appearance, improving both geometry reconstruction and rendering efficiency. Finally, probabilistic pruning combined with a sparsity-inducing BCE opacity loss allows redundant Gaussians to be turned off, yielding a minimal set of Gaussians sufficient to represent the scene. Using both synthetic and real-world datasets, we compare against the state of the art in Gaussian-based novel-view synthesis and demonstrate superior reconstruction fidelity with an order of magnitude fewer primitives.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Gaussian-hash-grid radiance representation for 2D Gaussian scene models; improves geometry-appearance separation, uses per-Gaussian latent features, hard opacity falloffs, probabilistic pruning; achieves superior fidelity with far fewer primitives.
AI.motivation: Address entanglement between geometry and appearance in NeRF-based models and reduce representation complexity. Enable efficient, sparse scene representation without sacrificing fidelity.
AI.method: Combine Gaussian primitives with a hash-grid radiance representation. Introduce per-Gaussian latent features to bias optimization toward separating low- and high-frequency components. Use hard opacity falloffs to reinforce geometry-appearance separation. Apply probabilistic pruning with a sparsity-inducing BCE opacity loss to deactivate redundant Gaussians.
AI.result: Evaluated on synthetic and real-world datasets; achieves superior reconstruction fidelity compared to state-of-the-art Gaussian-based novel-view synthesis, using an order of magnitude fewer primitives.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach improves geometry reconstruction and rendering efficiency by enforcing frequency-based separation, enabling a compact, sparse representation with high fidelity.

title: LongAct: Harnessing Intrinsic Activation Patterns for Long-Context Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has emerged as a critical driver for enhancing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). While recent advancements have focused on reward engineering or data synthesis, few studies exploit the model's intrinsic representation characteristics to guide the training process. In this paper, we first observe the presence of high-magnitude activations within the query and key vectors when processing long contexts. Drawing inspiration from model quantization -- which establishes the criticality of such high-magnitude activations -- and the insight that long-context reasoning inherently exhibits a sparse structure, we hypothesize that these weights serve as the pivotal drivers for effective model optimization. Based on this insight, we propose LongAct, a strategy that shifts from uniform to saliency-guided sparse updates. By selectively updating only the weights associated with these significant activations, LongAct achieves an approximate 8% improvement on LongBench v2 and enhances generalization on the RULER benchmark. Furthermore, our method exhibits remarkable universality, consistently boosting performance across diverse RL algorithms such as GRPO and DAPO. Extensive ablation studies suggest that focusing on these salient features is key to unlocking long-context potential.

AI.tldr: LongAct uses saliency-guided sparse updates focusing on high-magnitude activations in queries and keys during long-context processing, achieving ~8% gains on LongBench v2 and better RULER generalization, with universal improvements across RL algorithms such as GRPO and DAPO.
AI.motivation: Long-context reasoning shows sparse structure; existing RL-LLM work emphasizes reward engineering/data, but neglects intrinsic representation signals. Inspired by quantization and sparsity, leverage high-magnitude activations as key drivers for optimization.
AI.method: Identify high-magnitude activations in query/key vectors during long contexts; replace uniform updates with saliency-guided sparse updates, updating only weights linked to salient activations; demonstrated across RL settings and benchmarks.
AI.result: ≈8% improvement on LongBench v2; improved generalization on RULER; consistent gains across GRPO and DAPO; ablation studies highlight the importance of salient-feature focus.
AI.conclusion: Targeting salient, high-magnitude activations in long-context processing yields better optimization and generalization; LongAct provides a universal, sparse-update strategy that enhances RL with LLMs.

title: The Missing Knowledge Layer in AI: A Framework for Stable Human-AI Reasoning
summary:
Large language models are increasingly integrated into decision-making in areas such as healthcare, law, finance, engineering, and government. Yet they share a critical limitation: they produce fluent outputs even when their internal reasoning has drifted. A confident answer can conceal uncertainty, speculation, or inconsistency, and small changes in phrasing can lead to different conclusions. This makes LLMs useful assistants but unreliable partners in high-stakes contexts.
  Humans exhibit a similar weakness, often mistaking fluency for reliability. When a model responds smoothly, users tend to trust it, even when both model and user are drifting together. This paper is the first in a five-paper research series on stabilising human-AI reasoning. The series proposes a two-layer approach: Parts II-IV introduce human-side mechanisms such as uncertainty cues, conflict surfacing, and auditable reasoning traces, while Part V develops a model-side Epistemic Control Loop (ECL) that detects instability and modulates generation accordingly.
  Together, these layers form a missing operational substrate for governance by increasing signal-to-noise at the point of use. Stabilising interaction makes uncertainty and drift visible before enforcement is applied, enabling more precise capability governance. This aligns with emerging compliance expectations, including the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001, by making reasoning processes traceable under real conditions of use.
  The central claim is that fluency is not reliability. Without structures that stabilise both human and model reasoning, AI cannot be trusted or governed where it matters most.

AI.tldr: Proposes a two-layer stabilization framework for human–AI reasoning: Part II–IV introduce human-side mechanisms (uncertainty cues, conflict surfacing, auditable traces) and Part V adds a model-side Epistemic Control Loop to detect instability and modulate generation; aims to improve governance and reliability, arguing that fluency does not equal reliability.
AI.motivation: Address the drift and overconfidence seen in LLMs and users, which undermines reliability in high-stakes settings. Provide traceability and governance compatibility (e.g., EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001) by surfacing uncertainty and instability before enforcement.
AI.method: Conceptual design across a five-paper series: a two-layer architecture combining human-side interfaces for uncertainty signaling, conflict surfacing, and auditable reasoning traces with a model-side Epistemic Control Loop (ECL) that detects instability and modulates generation. The current abstract sets up the framework and governance rationale, with Parts II–IV detailing human-side mechanisms and Part V detailing the model-side controller.
AI.result: The paper lays out a theoretical framework and motivates a research program. It argues that stabilizing reasoning and surfacing uncertainty can raise the signal-to-noise ratio at the point of use, enabling governance and compliance with emerging standards.
AI.conclusion: Fluency is not reliability. Without stabilizing both human and model reasoning and providing auditable traces, AI cannot be trusted in high-stakes contexts. The proposed two-layer architecture is presented as a path toward trustworthy, governable AI.

title: Generative Data Augmentation for Skeleton Action Recognition
summary: Skeleton-based human action recognition is a powerful approach for understanding human behaviour from pose data, but collecting large-scale, diverse, and well-annotated 3D skeleton datasets is both expensive and labor-intensive. To address this challenge, we propose a conditional generative pipeline for data augmentation in skeleton action recognition. Our method learns the distribution of real skeleton sequences under the constraint of action labels, enabling the synthesis of diverse and high-fidelity data. Even with limited training samples, it can effectively generate skeleton sequences and achieve competitive recognition performance in low-data scenarios, demonstrating strong generalisation in downstream tasks. Specifically, we introduce a Transformer-based encoder-decoder architecture, combined with a generative refinement module and a dropout mechanism, to balance fidelity and diversity during sampling. Experiments on HumanAct12 and the refined NTU-RGBD (NTU-VIBE) dataset show that our approach consistently improves the accuracy of multiple skeleton-based action recognition models, validating its effectiveness in both few-shot and full-data settings. The source code can be found at here.

AI.tldr: A conditional generative pipeline using a Transformer-based encoder-decoder for skeleton sequence augmentation improves action recognition, especially in low-data regimes.
AI.motivation: Skeleton action recognition suffers from limited, diverse 3D skeleton data; data augmentation via conditional generation can enhance generalization and reduce data collection costs.
AI.method: A Transformer-based encoder-decoder models the distribution of real skeleton sequences conditioned on action labels. It includes a generative refinement module and a dropout mechanism to balance fidelity and diversity, enabling synthesis of diverse, high-fidelity sequences. Training leverages labeled skeleton data to produce augmented samples that improve downstream models.
AI.result: The approach yields consistent accuracy gains across multiple skeleton-based action recognition models on the HumanAct12 and NTU-VIBE datasets, demonstrating effectiveness in both few-shot and full-data settings and showing strong generalization.
AI.conclusion: Conditional, label-guided generative augmentation for skeleton sequences is effective for improving action recognition, particularly under data scarcity; the method is validated on standard benchmarks and code is made available.

title: Improving Sparse Autoencoder with Dynamic Attention
summary: Recently, sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a promising technique for interpreting activations in foundation models by disentangling features into a sparse set of concepts. However, identifying the optimal level of sparsity for each neuron remains challenging in practice: excessive sparsity can lead to poor reconstruction, whereas insufficient sparsity may harm interpretability. While existing activation functions such as ReLU and TopK provide certain sparsity guarantees, they typically require additional sparsity regularization or cherry-picked hyperparameters. We show in this paper that dynamically sparse attention mechanisms using sparsemax can bridge this trade-off, due to their ability to determine the activation numbers in a data-dependent manner. Specifically, we first explore a new class of SAEs based on the cross-attention architecture with the latent features as queries and the learnable dictionary as the key and value matrices. To encourage sparse pattern learning, we employ a sparsemax-based attention strategy that automatically infers a sparse set of elements according to the complexity of each neuron, resulting in a more flexible and general activation function. Through comprehensive evaluation and visualization, we show that our approach successfully achieves lower reconstruction loss while producing high-quality concepts, particularly in top-n classification tasks.

AI.tldr: Dynamic sparse attention with sparsemax in cross-attention SAEs enables adaptive sparsity per neuron, improving reconstruction while maintaining interpretable concepts.
AI.motivation: Identify per-neuron sparsity without manual tuning; balance reconstruction quality and interpretability in SAEs for foundation-model activations.
AI.method: Propose a cross-attention SAE where latent features query a learnable dictionary as keys/values; use sparsemax-based attention to infer sparse activations in a data-dependent manner.
AI.result: Achieves lower reconstruction loss and produces high-quality concepts; particularly effective for top-n classification tasks.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, data-driven sparsity via sparsemax offers flexible activation functions that enhance interpretability without sacrificing performance.

title: Cooperate to Compete: Strategic Data Generation and Incentivization Framework for Coopetitive Cross-Silo Federated Learning
comment: Competition-aware Federated Learning, Strategic data generation approaches, A payoff-redistribution based incentive mechanism, Potential game, Social welfare
summary: In data-sensitive domains such as healthcare, cross-silo federated learning (CFL) allows organizations to collaboratively train AI models without sharing raw data. However, practical CFL deployments are inherently coopetitive, in which organizations cooperate during model training while competing in downstream markets. In such settings, training contributions, including data volume, quality, and diversity, can improve the global model yet inadvertently strengthen rivals. This dilemma is amplified by non-IID data, which leads to asymmetric learning gains and undermines sustained participation. While existing competition-aware CFL and incentive-design approaches reward organizations based on marginal training contributions, they fail to account for the costs of strengthening competitors. In this paper, we introduce CoCoGen+, a coopetition-compatible data generation and incentivization framework that jointly models non-IID data and inter-organizational competition while endogenizing GenAI-based synthetic data generation as a strategic decision. Specifically, CoCoGen+ formulates each training round as a weighted potential game, where organizations strategically decide how much synthetic data to generate by balancing learning performance gains against computational costs and competition-caused utility losses. We then provide a tractable equilibrium characterization and derive implementable generation strategies to maximize social welfare. To promote long-term collaboration, we integrate a payoff redistribution-based incentive mechanism to compensate organizations for their contributions and competition-caused utility degradation. Experiments on varying learning tasks validate the feasibility of CoCoGen+. The results show how non-IID data, competition intensity, and incentives shape organizational strategies and social welfare, while CoCoGen+ outperforms baselines in efficiency.

AI.tldr: CoCoGen+ models coopetitive cross-silo FL with non-IID data, endogenizing synthetic data generation as strategic action; uses a weighted potential game and payoff-redistribution incentives to maximize social welfare and promote long-term collaboration.
AI.motivation: In coopetitive CFL, data contributions can unintentionally strengthen rivals; non-IID data leads to asymmetric learning gains and poorer sustained participation; existing incentive schemes overlook costs of aiding competitors.
AI.method: Formulate per-round learning as a weighted potential game; treat GenAI-based synthetic data generation as a strategic variable; derive tractable equilibrium; design implementable data-generation strategies and a payoff-redistribution incentive mechanism; validate across tasks.
AI.result: Equilibria are characterized; optimal generation strategies depend on data heterogeneity, competition intensity, and incentives; CoCoGen+ yields higher social welfare and efficiency than baselines; empirical experiments verify feasibility.
AI.conclusion: CoCoGen+ offers a tractable framework that balances learning gains, data-generation costs, and competition-induced degradation, promoting long-term collaboration through redistribution; adaptable to various learning tasks.

title: RaTA-Tool: Retrieval-based Tool Selection with Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Tool learning with foundation models aims to endow AI systems with the ability to invoke external resources -- such as APIs, computational utilities, and specialized models -- to solve complex tasks beyond the reach of standalone language generation. While recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have expanded their reasoning and perception capabilities, existing tool-use methods are predominantly limited to text-only inputs and closed-world settings. Consequently, they struggle to interpret multimodal user instructions and cannot generalize to tools unseen during training. In this work, we introduce RaTA-Tool, a novel framework for open-world multimodal tool selection. Rather than learning direct mappings from user queries to fixed tool identifiers, our approach enables an MLLM to convert a multimodal query into a structured task description and subsequently retrieve the most appropriate tool by matching this representation against semantically rich, machine-readable tool descriptions. This retrieval-based formulation naturally supports extensibility to new tools without retraining. To further improve alignment between task descriptions and tool selection, we incorporate a preference-based optimization stage using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). To support research in this setting, we also introduce the first dataset for open-world multimodal tool use, featuring standardized tool descriptions derived from Hugging Face model cards. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly improves tool-selection performance, particularly in open-world, multimodal scenarios.

AI.tldr: RaTA-Tool presents a retrieval-based framework for open-world multimodal tool use. It converts multimodal queries into structured task descriptions and matches them to semantically rich tool descriptions to select tools, enabling easy extension to unseen tools. It adds Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for better alignment and introduces a new multimodal tool-use dataset derived from Hugging Face model cards. Experiments show notable improvements in tool selection in open-world multimodal settings.
AI.motivation: Current tool-use methods are largely text-only and operate in closed worlds, hindering interpretation of multimodal instructions and generalization to unseen tools. There is a need for scalable, open-world tool selection that can handle multimodal queries.
AI.method: Convert multimodal queries into structured task descriptions using an LLM, then retrieve the best-matching tool by comparing this description to machine-readable tool descriptions. Employ Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to better align task representations with tool choices. Provide a new dataset of open-world multimodal tool use with standardized tool descriptions derived from Hugging Face model cards.
AI.result: Retrieval-based tool selection improves performance, particularly in open-world multimodal scenarios, indicating better generalization to unseen tools and modalities.
AI.conclusion: The RaTA-Tool framework enables scalable, open-world multimodal tool use by decoupling query understanding from tool selection through retrieval, augmented by DPO for alignment; the accompanying dataset supports research in this area.

title: Calibration-Gated LLM Pseudo-Observations for Online Contextual Bandits
summary: Contextual bandit algorithms suffer from high regret during cold-start, when the learner has insufficient data to distinguish good arms from bad. We propose augmenting Disjoint LinUCB with LLM pseudo-observations: after each round, a large language model predicts counterfactual rewards for the unplayed arms, and these predictions are injected into the learner as weighted pseudo-observations. The injection weight is controlled by a calibration-gated decay schedule that tracks the LLM's prediction accuracy on played arms via an exponential moving average; high calibration error suppresses the LLM's influence, while accurate predictions receive higher weight during the critical early rounds. We evaluate on two contextual bandit environments - UCI Mushroom (2-arm, asymmetric rewards) and MIND-small (5-arm news recommendation) - and find that when equipped with a task-specific prompt, LLM pseudo-observations reduce cumulative regret by 19% on MIND relative to pure LinUCB. However, generic counterfactual prompt framing increases regret on both environments, demonstrating that prompt design is the dominant factor, more important than the choice of decay schedule or calibration gating parameters. We analyze the failure modes of calibration gating on domains with small prediction errors and provide a theoretical motivation for the bias-variance trade-off governing pseudo-observation weight.

AI.tldr: LLM pseudo-observations with calibration gate improved cold-start regret in contextual bandits; task-specific prompts outperform generic prompts, which degrade performance. Prompt design dominates beyond decay schedule or gating.
AI.motivation: Mitigate cold-start regret in contextual bandits by injecting LLM-predicted pseudo-rewards for unplayed arms to augment learning when data is scarce.
AI.method: Augment Disjoint LinUCB with LLM-based pseudo-observations: after each round, an LLM predicts counterfactual rewards for unplayed arms; predictions are injected with weights controlled by a calibration-gated decay schedule using EMA of LLM accuracy on played arms.
AI.result: On Mushroom and MIND-small, task-specific prompts reduce MIND regret by 19% vs pure LinUCB; generic prompts increase regret in both domains; decay/gating parameters less influential than prompt design.
AI.conclusion: Prompt design is the dominant factor governing performance; calibration gating has nuanced effects, with failure modes in low-variance domains; theoretical motivation provided for the bias-variance trade-off in pseudo-observation weighting.

title: MemoSight: Unifying Context Compression and Multi Token Prediction for Reasoning Acceleration
summary: While Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning enables LLMs to solve challenging reasoning problems, as KV cache grows linearly with the number of generated tokens, CoT reasoning faces scaling issues in terms of speed and memory usage. In this work, we propose MemoSight (Memory-Foresight-based reasoning), a unified framework that integrates both context compression and multi-token prediction to mitigate the efficiency issues while maintaining CoT reasoning performance. Our framework adopts the same minimalist design for both context compression and multi-token prediction via special tokens and their corresponding position layout tailored to each token type. Comprehensive experiments on four reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that MemoSight reduces the KV cache footprint by up to 66% and accelerates inference by 1.56x, while outperforming existing CoT compression methods.

AI.tldr: MemoSight integrates context compression and multi-token prediction to curb KV-cache growth in chain-of-thought reasoning, achieving up to 66% cache reduction and 1.56x speedup on four benchmarks while preserving reasoning performance and surpassing existing CoT compression methods.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-thought reasoning scales poorly due to linear KV-cache growth with generated tokens, causing slowdown and high memory usage; there is a need for a unified approach that preserves reasoning quality while improving efficiency.
AI.method: A unified framework that uses minimalist design with special tokens and tailored position layouts for different token types to enable both context compression and multi-token prediction; includes a memory-foresight-based strategy.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four reasoning benchmarks shows up to 66% KV-cache reduction and 1.56x inference speedup, outperforming existing CoT compression methods.
AI.conclusion: MemoSight effectively mitigates efficiency bottlenecks in CoT reasoning without sacrificing performance, offering practical gains for large-scale reasoning tasks.

title: Prompt-to-Gesture: Measuring the Capabilities of Image-to-Video Deictic Gesture Generation
comment: Accepted at 2026 International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (FG)
summary: Gesture recognition research, unlike NLP, continues to face acute data scarcity, with progress constrained by the need for costly human recordings or image processing approaches that cannot generate authentic variability in the gestures themselves. Recent advancements in image-to-video foundation models have enabled the generation of photorealistic, semantically rich videos guided by natural language. These capabilities open up new possibilities for creating effort-free synthetic data, raising the critical question of whether video Generative AI models can augment and complement traditional human-generated gesture data. In this paper, we introduce and analyze prompt-based video generation to construct a realistic deictic gestures dataset and rigorously evaluate its effectiveness for downstream tasks. We propose a data generation pipeline that produces deictic gestures from a small number of reference samples collected from human participants, providing an accessible approach that can be leveraged both within and beyond the machine learning community. Our results demonstrate that the synthetic gestures not only align closely with real ones in terms of visual fidelity but also introduce meaningful variability and novelty that enrich the original data, further supported by superior performance of various deep models using a mixed dataset. These findings highlight that image-to-video techniques, even in their early stages, offer a powerful zero-shot approach to gesture synthesis with clear benefits for downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: Prompt-based video synthesis can generate deictic gesture data from few references, improving downstream gesture recognition by augmenting real data with synthetic, varied videos; zero-shot video generation shows promise.
AI.motivation: Gesture recognition suffers from data scarcity and reliance on costly human recordings. Image-to-video foundation models offer a scalable way to synthesize semantically rich gestures with authentic variability, enabling augmented datasets for better model training.
AI.method: Develop a data generation pipeline that uses prompts to guide video generation. Start from a small set of human-provided reference samples to synthesize deictic gestures. Create a mixed dataset combining real and synthetic videos, and evaluate downstream tasks with deep models to assess fidelity, variability, and performance.
AI.result: Synthetic deictic gestures achieve visual fidelity comparable to real gestures and introduce meaningful variability and novelty. Models trained on the mixed dataset show improved performance on downstream gesture recognition tasks compared to using only real data.
AI.conclusion: Early image-to-video techniques enable zero-shot gesture synthesis that can augment traditional data, offering practical benefits for downstream tasks and broad accessibility for researchers.

title: Blazing the trails before beating the path: Sample-efficient Monte-Carlo planning
comment: Published in Neural Information Processing Systems 2016
summary: You are a robot and you live in a Markov decision process (MDP) with a finite or an infinite number of transitions from state-action to next states. You got brains and so you plan before you act. Luckily, your roboparents equipped you with a generative model to do some Monte-Carlo planning. The world is waiting for you and you have no time to waste. You want your planning to be efficient. Sample-efficient. Indeed, you want to exploit the possible structure of the MDP by exploring only a subset of states reachable by following near-optimal policies. You want guarantees on sample complexity that depend on a measure of the quantity of near-optimal states. You want something, that is an extension of Monte-Carlo sampling (for estimating an expectation) to problems that alternate maximization (over actions) and expectation (over next states). But you do not want to StOP with exponential running time, you want something simple to implement and computationally efficient. You want it all and you want it now. You want TrailBlazer.

AI.tldr: TrailBlazer blends Monte-Carlo planning with a structured, sample-efficient search over near-optimal states in MDPs, addressing alternating max-over-actions and expectation-over-next-states problems while remaining computationally tractable.
AI.motivation: To achieve sample-efficient planning by exploiting the structure of the MDP, enabling exploration only of states reachable under near-optimal policies and providing guarantees that scale with the amount of near-optimal state mass.
AI.method: Develop a practical Monte-Carlo planning framework that extends standard sampling to problems with alternating maximization over actions and expectation over next states, emphasizing simplicity and computational efficiency; introduce TrailBlazer as the algorithmic solution.
AI.result: TrailBlazer is proposed as a simple, implementable algorithm with theoretical guarantees on sample complexity that depend on the quantity of near-optimal states, and it emphasizes computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: TrailBlazer offers a scalable, sample-efficient planning approach for MDPs by leveraging near-optimal structure and extending Monte-Carlo planning to alternating max/expectation problems.

title: Toward Agentic RAG for Ukrainian
comment: This paper is a research report based on our participation in the UNLP 2026 Shared Task
summary: We present an initial investigation into Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for Ukrainian, conducted within the UNLP 2026 Shared Task on Multi-Domain Document Understanding. Our system combines two-stage retrieval (BGE-M3 with BGE reranking) with a lightweight agentic layer performing query rephrasing and answer-retry loops on top of Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct. Our analysis reveals that retrieval quality is the primary bottleneck: agentic retry mechanisms improve answer accuracy but the overall score remains constrained by document and page identification. We discuss practical limitations of offline agentic pipelines and outline directions for combining stronger retrieval with more advanced agentic reasoning for Ukrainian.

AI.tldr: Agentic RAG for Ukrainian shows that retrieval quality is the main bottleneck; agentic retries help accuracy but cannot overcome document/page identification limits; future work should couple stronger retrieval with advanced agentic reasoning.
AI.motivation: Improve multi-domain document understanding for Ukrainian via Retrieval-Augmented Generation, addressing bottlenecks in retrieval and agentic components, and exploring offline agentic pipelines.
AI.method: Two-stage retrieval (BGE-M3 with BGE reranking) combined with a lightweight agentic layer for query reformulation and answer-retry loops, all built on Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct; evaluated in UNLP 2026 Shared Task.
AI.result: Retrieval quality identified as the primary bottleneck; agentic retry mechanisms improve accuracy but overall performance is constrained by document and page identification; offline agentic pipelines present practical limitations.
AI.conclusion: Stronger retrieval must be paired with more advanced agentic reasoning for Ukrainian; future work should explore integrated approaches to mitigate identification bottlenecks and improve end-to-end performance.

title: Frequency-Enhanced Dual-Subspace Networks for Few-Shot Fine-Grained Image Classification
summary: Few-shot fine-grained image classification aims to recognize subcategories with high visual similarity using only a limited number of annotated samples. Existing metric learning-based methods typically rely solely on spatial domain features. Confined to this single perspective, models inevitably suffer from inherent texture biases, entangling essential structural details with high-frequency background noise. Furthermore, lacking cross-view geometric constraints, single-view metrics tend to overfit this noise, resulting in structural instability under few-shot conditions. To address these issues, this paper proposes the Frequency-Enhanced Dual-Subspace Network (FEDSNet). Specifically, FEDSNet utilizes the Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) and a low-pass filtering mechanism to explicitly isolate low-frequency global structural components from spatial features, thereby suppressing background interference. Truncated Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) is employed to construct independent, low-rank linear subspaces for both spatial texture and frequency structural features. An adaptive gating mechanism is designed to dynamically fuse the projection distances from these dual views. This strategy leverages the structural stability of the frequency subspace to prevent the spatial subspace from overfitting to background features. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets - CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, Stanford Dogs, and FGVC-Aircraft - demonstrate that FEDSNet exhibits excellent classification performance and robustness, achieving highly competitive results compared to existing metric learning algorithms. Complexity analysis further confirms that the proposed network achieves a favorable balance between high accuracy and computational efficiency, providing an effective new paradigm for few-shot fine-grained visual recognition.

AI.tldr: FEDSNet introduces a dual-view (spatial texture and frequency structural) framework for few-shot fine-grained classification using DCT-based low-pass filtering and truncated SVD to create independent subspaces, with adaptive gating to fuse distances, yielding improved robustness to background noise and competitive accuracy with efficient computation.
AI.motivation: Single-view metric learning for few-shot FGVC suffers from texture bias and overfitting to high-frequency background noise due to lack of cross-view structural constraints. A dual-subspace approach aims to stabilize structure by incorporating frequency-domain information.
AI.method: Apply DCT and low-pass filtering to extract low-frequency global structure from spatial features. Use truncated SVD to form two low-rank linear subspaces: one for spatial texture and one for frequency structural features. Use an adaptive gating mechanism to fuse projection distances from both views for classification in few-shot settings.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on CUB-200-2011, Stanford Cars, Stanford Dogs, and FGVC-Aircraft show that FEDSNet achieves competitive or superior accuracy and robustness compared with existing metric-learning methods; complexity analysis indicates a favorable accuracy-efficiency balance.
AI.conclusion: A dual-view, frequency-enhanced subspace framework provides cross-view geometric constraints that reduce overfitting to background noise and improve stability in few-shot FGVC, offering a new paradigm with strong empirical performance and efficiency.

title: What Is the Minimum Architecture for Prolepsis? Early Irrevocable Commitment Across Tasks in Small Transformers
comment: 24 pages, 3 figures. Under review at COLM 2026. Independent replication of the rhyme-planning finding from Lindsey et al. (2025) on open-weights models; extended to factual recall
summary: When do transformers commit to a decision, and what prevents them from correcting it? We introduce \textbf{prolepsis}: a transformer commits early, task-specific attention heads sustain the commitment, and no layer corrects it. Replicating \citeauthor{lindsey2025biology}'s (\citeyear{lindsey2025biology}) planning-site finding on open models (Gemma~2 2B, Llama~3.2 1B), we ask five questions. (Q1)~Planning is invisible to six residual-stream methods; CLTs are necessary. (Q2)~The planning-site spike replicates with identical geometry. (Q3)~Specific attention heads route the decision to the output, filling a gap flagged as invisible to attribution graphs. (Q4)~Search requires ${\leq}16$ layers; commitment requires more. (Q5)~Factual recall shows the same motif at a different network depth, with zero overlap between recurring planning heads and the factual top-10. Prolepsis is architectural: the template is shared, the routing substrates differ. All experiments run on a single consumer GPU (16\,GB VRAM).

AI.tldr: Transformers exhibit prolepsis: they commit to a decision early, with task-specific heads sustaining the commitment and no layer correcting it; planning-site signals and routing are architectural and differ across models.
AI.motivation: To diagnose when and how transformer decisions become fixed, and to test whether planning-like signals persist across architectures, inspired by planning-site findings.
AI.method: Replicates planning-site experiments on open models (Gemma 2 2B, Llama 3.2 1B). Analyzes six residual-stream methods; CLTs; examines planning-site spikes with identical geometry; identifies how specific attention heads route decisions; investigates depth requirements for search versus commitment; compares planning heads to factual recall across depths.
AI.result: Evidence of prolepsis: early commitment; planning-site spikes replicable with identical geometry; particular attention heads route the decision to outputs; planning is invisible to several residual-stream analyses; CLTs are necessary; searches require ≤16 layers while commitment often needs more; a similar motif appears in factual recall at other depths but with zero overlap with recurring planning heads; prolepsis is architectural (shared template, different routing substrates); experiments run on a single consumer GPU (16 GB).
AI.conclusion: Prolepsis is an architectural routing phenomenon, not simply residual-stream dynamics. Detecting it requires analyzing routing substrates and head-level decisions. The findings generalize to open models, with practical constraints due to hardware and the need for targeted interpretability tools.

title: Governing Reflective Human-AI Collaboration: A Framework for Epistemic Scaffolding and Traceable Reasoning
summary:
Large language models have advanced rapidly, from pattern recognition to emerging forms of reasoning, yet they remain confined to linguistic simulation rather than grounded understanding. They can produce fluent outputs that resemble reflection, but lack temporal continuity, causal feedback, and anchoring in real-world interaction. This paper proposes a complementary approach in which reasoning is treated as a relational process distributed between human and model rather than an internal capability of either.
  Building on recent work on "System-2" learning, we relocate reflective reasoning to the interaction layer. Instead of engineering reasoning solely within models, we frame it as a cognitive protocol that can be structured, measured, and governed using existing systems. This perspective emphasizes collaborative intelligence, combining human judgment and contextual understanding with machine speed, memory, and associative capacity.
  We introduce "The Architect's Pen" as a practical method. Like an architect who thinks through drawing, the human uses the model as an external medium for structured reflection. By embedding phases of articulation, critique, and revision into human-AI interaction, the dialogue itself becomes a reasoning loop: human abstraction -> model articulation -> human reflection.
  This reframes the question from whether the model can think to whether the human-AI system can reason. The framework enables auditable reasoning traces and supports alignment with emerging governance standards, including the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001. It provides a practical path toward more transparent, controllable, and accountable AI use without requiring new model architectures.

AI.tldr: Proposes a human-in-the-loop reasoning framework where reflection is distributed across human–AI interaction via 'The Architect's Pen', enabling auditable traces and governance-aligned accountability without new model architectures.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of current LLMs (fluent language but lacking temporal continuity, causal feedback, and grounded real-world interaction) and the need for auditable, governed reasoning; promote collaborative intelligence between humans and models.
