AI.method: Relocate reflective reasoning to the interaction layer; frame reasoning as a cognitive protocol structured, measurable, and governed by existing systems. Introduce 'The Architect's Pen' where a human uses the model as an external medium for structured reflection, embedding phases of articulation, critique, and revision into the dialogue to create a reasoning loop.
AI.result: Conceptual framework with auditable reasoning traces that align with governance standards (EU AI Act, ISO/IEC 42001); provides a practical path for transparent, controllable, and accountable AI use without new model architectures.
AI.conclusion: Shifts the focus from model-centric thinking to a human–AI system that can reason; supports governance, accountability, and transparency while avoiding the need for new AI architectures.

title: UniDoc-RL: Coarse-to-Fine Visual RAG with Hierarchical Actions and Dense Rewards
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) extends Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) with external visual knowledge. However, existing visual RAG systems typically rely on generic retrieval signals that overlook the fine-grained visual semantics essential for complex reasoning. To address this limitation, we propose UniDoc-RL, a unified reinforcement learning framework in which an LVLM agent jointly performs retrieval, reranking, active visual perception, and reasoning. UniDoc-RL formulates visual information acquisition as a sequential decision-making problem with a hierarchical action space. Specifically, it progressively refines visual evidence from coarse-grained document retrieval to fine-grained image selection and active region cropping, allowing the model to suppress irrelevant content and attend to information-dense regions. For effective end-to-end training, we introduce a dense multi-reward scheme that provides task-aware supervision for each action. Based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), UniDoc-RL aligns agent behavior with multiple objectives without relying on a separate value network. To support this training paradigm, we curate a comprehensive dataset of high-quality reasoning trajectories with fine-grained action annotations. Experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate that UniDoc-RL consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, yielding up to 17.7% gains over prior RL-based methods.

AI.tldr: UniDoc-RL: a unified reinforcement learning framework for visual Retrieval-Augmented Generation in LVLMs, introducing hierarchical action spaces (coarse retrieval, image selection, region cropping) and dense multi-rewards trained via Group Relative Policy Optimization, plus a dataset of reasoning trajectories; achieves up to 17.7% gains over prior RL baselines on three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of coarse retrieval signals in visual RAG that miss fine-grained visual semantics needed for complex reasoning.
AI.method: Agent-based framework: iterative retrieval and refinement pipeline, from document retrieval to image-level selection to region cropping, with active perception; end-to-end training via GRPO using dense multi-reward signals; new dataset with fine-grained action annotations.
AI.result: Consistent improvement over state-of-the-art baselines and RL-based methods across three benchmarks; up to 17.7% improvement.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effectiveness of unified RL with fine-grained visual information acquisition for LVLM visual RAG; provides dataset and training framework; advances end-to-end learnable visual reasoning.

title: DLink: Distilling Layer-wise and Dominant Knowledge from EEG Foundation Models
summary: EEG foundation models (FMs) achieve strong cross-subject and cross-task generalization but impose substantial computational and memory costs that hinder deployment on embedded BCI systems. Knowledge distillation is a natural solution; however, conventional methods fail for EEG FMs because task-relevant semantics are often distributed across intermediate layers, and aggressive dimensionality reduction can distort oscillatory structure via representational collapse and aliasing. To address these challenges, we propose DLink (Distilling Layer-wise and Dominant Knowledge), a unified framework for transferring knowledge from large EEG FMs to compact students with three key innovations: (1) a dynamic Router that adaptively aggregates teacher layers to capture dominant intermediate representations; (2) an EEG MiC student with a Mimic-then-Compress pipeline, which inherits high-dimensional teacher features and then applies structured spatio-temporal compression to avoid a heavy classification head; and (3) spectral distillation that aligns teacher-student representations in the frequency domain to regularize compression and mitigate aliasing and temporal jitter. Experiments on four EEG benchmarks show that DLink enables compact students to outperform lightweight baselines while approaching fully fine-tuned FM performance at substantially lower model size and inference cost.

AI.tldr: A three-part distillation framework (DLink) to compress EEG foundation models into efficient students, using a dynamic Router, Mimic-then-Compress EEG MiC student, and spectral distillation; achieves strong performance with lower cost on four EEG benchmarks.
AI.motivation: EEG foundation models offer strong cross-subject/task generalization but demand substantial computation and memory, hindering deployment on embedded BCIs. Conventional distillation struggles because task-relevant semantics span multiple layers and aggressive dimensionality reduction risks representational collapse and aliasing.
AI.method: Introduce a dynamic Router that adaptively aggregates teacher layers to capture dominant intermediate representations; an EEG MiC student with a Mimic-then-Compress pipeline that inherits high-dimensional teacher features before structured spatio-temporal compression; and spectral distillation that aligns teacher-student representations in the frequency domain to regularize compression and mitigate aliasing and temporal jitter.
AI.result: Experiments on four EEG benchmarks show that DLink enables compact students to outperform lightweight baselines while approaching fully fine-tuned FM performance at substantially lower model size and inference cost.
AI.conclusion: DLink provides an effective framework for distilling EEG foundation models into efficient, high-performing students suitable for deployment on resource-constrained systems.

title: Flow of Truth: Proactive Temporal Forensics for Image-to-Video Generation
summary: The rapid rise of image-to-video (I2V) generation enables realistic videos to be created from a single image but also brings new forensic demands. Unlike static images, I2V content evolves over time, requiring forensics to move beyond 2D pixel-level tampering localization toward tracing how pixels flow and transform throughout the video. As frames progress, embedded traces drift and deform, making traditional spatial forensics ineffective. To address this unexplored dimension, we present **Flow of Truth**, the first proactive framework focusing on temporal forensics in I2V generation. A key challenge lies in discovering a forensic signature that can evolve consistently with the generation process, which is inherently a creative transformation rather than a deterministic reconstruction. Despite this intrinsic difficulty, we innovatively redefine video generation as *the motion of pixels through time rather than the synthesis of frames*. Building on this view, we propose a learnable forensic template that follows pixel motion and a template-guided flow module that decouples motion from image content, enabling robust temporal tracing. Experiments show that Flow of Truth generalizes across commercial and open-source I2V models, substantially improving temporal forensics performance.

AI.tldr: A proactive temporal forensics framework for image-to-video (I2V) generation called Flow of Truth, introducing a learnable forensic template and a template-guided flow module to track pixel motion over time, generalizing across models and improving temporal tracing performance.
AI.motivation: I2V generation creates content that evolves over time, making traditional 2D tampering localization insufficient; there is a need to trace pixel motion through time to detect temporal inconsistencies and transformations.
AI.method: Define video generation as the motion of pixels through time rather than frame synthesis. Develop a learnable forensic template that follows pixel motion and a template-guided flow module that decouples motion from image content to enable robust temporal tracing.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows Flow of Truth generalizes across commercial and open-source I2V models and substantially improves temporal forensics performance.
AI.conclusion: Flow of Truth provides the first proactive temporal forensics framework for I2V generation, enabling robust tracking of pixel motion over time and generalizing across models.

title: When Fairness Metrics Disagree: Evaluating the Reliability of Demographic Fairness Assessment in Machine Learning
summary: The evaluation of fairness in machine learning systems has become a central concern in high-stakes applications, including biometric recognition, healthcare decision-making, and automated risk assessment. Existing approaches typically rely on a small number of fairness metrics to assess model behaviour across group partitions, implicitly assuming that these metrics provide consistent and reliable conclusions. However, different fairness metrics capture distinct statistical properties of model performance and may therefore produce conflicting assessments when applied to the same system. In this work, we investigate the consistency of fairness evaluation by conducting a systematic multi-metric analysis of demographic bias in machine learning models. Using face recognition as a controlled experimental setting, we evaluate model performance across multiple group partitions under a range of commonly used fairness metrics, including error-rate disparities and performance-based measures. Our results demonstrate that fairness assessments can vary significantly depending on the choice of metrics, leading to contradictory conclusions regarding model bias. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the Fairness Disagreement Index (FDI), a measure designed to capture the degree of inconsistency across fairness metrics. We further show that disagreement remains high across thresholds and model configurations. These findings highlight a critical limitation in current fairness evaluation practices and suggest that single-metric reporting is insufficient for reliable bias assessment.

AI.tldr: Multi-metric fairness evaluation can yield conflicting assessments in ML; the study introduces the Fairness Disagreement Index (FDI) to quantify inconsistency across metrics, finding high disagreement across thresholds and model configs and arguing against single-metric reporting.
AI.motivation: Fairness in ML is complex and relies on multiple metrics; relying on a small set may lead to inconsistent or biased conclusions. A systematic, multi-metric analysis is needed to understand how metrics disagree and when they converge.
AI.method: Systematic analysis of demographic bias in face recognition using multiple group partitions and a range of commonly used fairness metrics (including error-rate disparities and performance-based measures). Introduces the Fairness Disagreement Index (FDI) to quantify cross-metric inconsistency, evaluated across different thresholds and model configurations.
AI.result: Fairness assessments vary significantly with the chosen metrics, yielding contradictory conclusions about bias. Disagreement remains high across thresholds and configurations as captured by FDI.
AI.conclusion: Single-metric reporting is insufficient for reliable bias assessment; multi-metric reporting and the use of an inconsistency measure like FDI are recommended for robust fairness evaluations.

title: Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model Toward Semantic and Turn-taking Robustness in Interactive Spoken Dialogue Models
summary: Achieving seamless, human-like interaction remains a key challenge for full-duplex spoken dialogue models (SDMs). Reinforcement learning (RL) has substantially enhanced text- and vision-language models, while well-designed reward signals are crucial for the performance of RL. We consider RL a promising strategy to address the key challenge for SDMs. However, a fundamental barrier persists: prevailing automated metrics for assessing interaction quality rely on superficial proxies, such as behavioral statistics or timing-prediction accuracy, failing to provide reliable reward signals for RL. On the other hand, human evaluations, despite their richness, remain costly, inconsistent, and difficult to scale. We tackle this critical barrier by proposing a Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model, which is trained to understand complex interaction dynamics using a detailed taxonomy and an annotated dataset, produces a single score and, crucially, provides separate evaluations for semantic quality and interaction timing. Such dual outputs furnish precise diagnostic feedback for SDMs and deliver a dependable, instructive reward signal suitable for online reinforcement learning. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on interaction-quality assessment across a wide spectrum of datasets, spanning synthetic dialogues and complex real-world interactions.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model for reinforcement learning in full-duplex spoken dialogue models, delivering separate semantic-quality and interaction-timing scores to improve reward signals and achieving state-of-the-art interaction-quality assessment across synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.motivation: Current automated metrics fail to provide reliable, informative reward signals for RL in SDMs; human evaluations are costly, inconsistent, and not scalable, creating a barrier to RL-based improvement.
AI.method: Train a Dual-Axis Generative Reward Model on a detailed taxonomy and annotated dialogue data to output a single overall score plus two separate evaluations: semantic quality and interaction timing, enabling precise diagnostic feedback for SDMs and suitable reward signals for online RL.
AI.result: The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on interaction-quality assessment across a wide range of datasets, including synthetic dialogues and complex real-world interactions.
AI.conclusion: Dual-axis feedback offers precise diagnostics and a dependable reward signal for online RL in SDMs, facilitating more seamless, human-like interactions.

title: Quality-Aware Calibration for AI-Generated Image Detection in the Wild
comment: Accepted at the APAI Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Significant progress has been made in detecting synthetic images, however most existing approaches operate on a single image instance and overlook a key characteristic of real-world dissemination: as viral images circulate on the web, multiple near-duplicate versions appear and lose quality due to repeated operations like recompression, resizing and cropping. As a consequence, the same image may yield inconsistent forensic predictions based on which version has been analyzed. In this work, to address this issue we propose QuAD (Quality-Aware calibration with near-Duplicates) a novel framework that makes decisions based on all available near-duplicates of the same image. Given a query, we retrieve its online near-duplicates and feed them to a detector: the resulting scores are then aggregated based on the estimated quality of the corresponding instance. By doing so, we take advantage of all pieces of information while accounting for the reduced reliability of images impaired by multiple processing steps. To support large-scale evaluation, we introduce two datasets: AncesTree, an in-lab dataset of 136k images organized in stochastic degradation trees that simulate online reposting dynamics, and ReWIND, a real-world dataset of nearly 10k near-duplicate images collected from viral web content. Experiments on several state-of-the-art detectors show that our quality-aware fusion improves their performance consistently, with an average gain of around 8% in terms of balanced accuracy compared to plain average. Our results highlight the importance of jointly processing all the images available online to achieve reliable detection of AI-generated content in real-world applications. Code and data are publicly available at https://grip-unina.github.io/QuAD/

AI.tldr: Quality-aware fusion across near-duplicates improves detection by aggregating predictions from multiple degraded copies, weighted by estimated quality; introduces AncesTree and ReWIND datasets.
AI.motivation: Single-image detectors can yield inconsistent forensic predictions when images undergo degradation and re-distribution; real-world diffusion creates multiple near-duplicates with varying quality.
AI.method: For each query, retrieve online near-duplicates, run a detector on each instance, and fuse the resulting scores using a quality-based weighting scheme; evaluate across detectors and datasets.
AI.result: The quality-aware fusion yields consistent performance gains, averaging ~8% improvement in balanced accuracy over plain averaging; results hold across multiple state-of-the-art detectors.
AI.conclusion: Jointly processing all available online copies is crucial for reliable AI-generated content detection in real-world settings; code and data are publicly available for reproducibility.

title: No More Guessing: a Verifiable Gradient Inversion Attack in Federated Learning
summary:
Gradient inversion attacks threaten client privacy in federated learning by reconstructing training samples from clients' shared gradients. Gradients aggregate contributions from multiple records and existing attacks may fail to disentangle them, yielding incorrect reconstructions with no intrinsic way to certify success. In vision and language, attackers may fall back on human inspection to judge reconstruction plausibility, but this is far less feasible for numerical tabular records, fueling the impression that tabular data is less vulnerable.
  We challenge this perception by proposing a verifiable gradient inversion attack (VGIA) that provides an explicit certificate of correctness for reconstructed samples. Our method adopts a geometric view of ReLU leakage: the activation boundary of a fully connected layer defines a hyperplane in input space. VGIA introduces an algebraic, subspace-based verification test that detects when a hyperplane-delimited region contains exactly one record. Once isolation is certified, VGIA recovers the corresponding feature vector analytically and reconstructs the target via a lightweight optimization step.
  Experiments on tabular benchmarks with large batch sizes demonstrate exact record and target recovery in regimes where existing state-of-the-art attacks either fail or cannot assess reconstruction fidelity. Compared to prior geometric approaches, VGIA allocates hyperplane queries more effectively, yielding faster reconstructions with fewer attack rounds.

AI.tldr: VGIA (Verifiable Gradient Inversion Attack) provides a certificate-based reconstruction for tabular data in federated learning. It uses a geometric view of ReLU leakage and a subspace-based verification test to isolate a single record within a hyperplane-delimited region, enabling analytic feature recovery followed by a lightweight optimization for the target. It achieves exact record and target recovery on tabular benchmarks with large batch sizes, and outperforms prior geometric methods in efficiency and verifiability.
AI.motivation: Existing gradient inversion attacks often lack verifiability and struggle to disentangle contributions from multiple records, particularly in tabular data. There is a need for a verifiable, certificate-based attack that can reliably confirm reconstruction correctness.
AI.method: The approach adopts a geometric view of ReLU leakage where activation boundaries define hyperplanes in input space. VGIA introduces an algebraic, subspace-based verification test that detects when a hyperplane-delimited region contains exactly one record. Upon isolation, it recovers the corresponding feature vector analytically and reconstructs the target via a lightweight optimization step.
AI.result: VGIA achieves exact record and target recovery on tabular benchmarks at large batch sizes, in regimes where existing state-of-the-art attacks fail or cannot certify reconstruction fidelity. Compared with prior geometric approaches, VGIA uses hyperplane queries more efficiently, resulting in faster reconstructions with fewer attack rounds.
AI.conclusion: VGIA provides a verifiable, efficient gradient-inversion attack for tabular data in federated learning, offering a certificate of correctness for reconstructions and improving over previous geometric methods in both fidelity and speed.

title: WavAlign: Enhancing Intelligence and Expressiveness in Spoken Dialogue Models via Adaptive Hybrid Post-Training
summary: End-to-end spoken dialogue models have garnered significant attention because they offer a higher potential ceiling in expressiveness and perceptual ability than cascaded systems. However, the intelligence and expressiveness of current open-source spoken dialogue models often remain below expectations. Motivated by the success of online reinforcement learning(RL) in other domains, one might attempt to directly apply preference optimization to spoken dialogue models, yet this transfer is non-trivial. We analyze these obstacles from the perspectives of reward modeling and rollout sampling, focusing on how sparse preference supervision interacts with dense speech generation under shared-parameter updates. Based on the analysis, we propose a modality-aware adaptive post-training recipe that makes RL practical for spoken dialogue: it constrains preference updates to the semantic channel and improves acoustic behavior via explicit anchoring, while dynamically regulating their mixture from rollout statistics to avoid unreliable preference gradients. We evaluate the method across multiple spoken dialogue benchmarks and representative architectures, and observe consistent improvements in semantic quality and speech expressiveness.

AI.tldr: Proposes a modality-aware adaptive post-training method to enable reinforcement learning with preference optimization for end-to-end spoken dialogue models, addressing reward modeling and rollout sampling; constrains updates to semantic content, anchors acoustics, and regulates the update mix based on rollout statistics; yields consistent gains in semantic quality and speech expressiveness across benchmarks and architectures.
AI.motivation: End-to-end spoken dialogue models promise higher expressiveness but current open-source systems lag; applying online RL with preferences is promising but faces challenges due to sparse preferences, dense speech generation, and shared parameters; the goal is to make RL practically trainable in this domain.
AI.method: Introduce a modality-aware adaptive post-training recipe: constrain preference updates to the semantic channel; improve acoustic behavior via explicit anchoring; dynamically regulate the mixture of objectives from rollout statistics to avoid unreliable preference gradients; operate under shared-parameter updates.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks and representative architectures, the method yields consistent improvements in semantic quality and speech expressiveness.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, modality-aware post-training makes RL with preference optimization viable for spoken dialogue systems, delivering consistent improvements without destabilizing acoustic or semantic generation.

title: Implicit Neural Representations: A Signal Processing Perspective
summary: Implicit neural representations (INRs) mark a fundamental shift in signal modeling, moving from discrete sampled data to continuous functional representations. By parameterizing signals as neural networks, INRs provide a unified framework for representing images, audio, video, 3D geometry, and beyond as continuous functions of their coordinates. This functional viewpoint enables signal operations such as differentiation to be carried out analytically through automatic differentiation rather than through discrete approximations. In this article, we examine the evolution of INRs from a signal processing perspective, emphasizing spectral behavior, sampling theory, and multiscale representation. We trace the progression from standard coordinate based networks, which exhibit a spectral bias toward low frequency components, to more advanced designs that reshape the approximation space through specialized activations, including periodic, localized, and adaptive functions. We also discuss structured representations, such as hierarchical decompositions and hash grid encodings, that improve spatial adaptivity and computational efficiency. We further highlight the utility of INRs across a broad range of applications, including inverse problems in medical and radar imaging, compression, and 3D scene representation. By interpreting INRs as learned signal models whose approximation spaces adapt to the underlying data, this article clarifies the field's core conceptual developments and outlines open challenges in theoretical stability, weight space interpretability, and large scale generalization.

AI.tldr: Survey of implicit neural representations (INRs) as continuous neural models for signals, detailing spectral behavior, activations, multiscale and structured encodings, applications, and open theoretical challenges.
AI.motivation: Provide a unifying, differentiable framework for representing signals across modalities (images, audio, 3D geometry) that enables analytic operations via automatic differentiation and overcomes limits of discrete sampling.
AI.method: Review historical progression from standard coordinate-based networks with a low-frequency bias to advanced designs using periodic/localized/adaptive activations; incorporate structured representations (hash grids, hierarchical decompositions); analyze from signal-processing lens (spectral behavior, sampling theory, multiscale aspects).
AI.result: Clarifies core conceptual developments in INRs, highlights broad applications (inverse problems in medical/radar imaging, compression, 3D scene representation), and shows how learned models adapt approximation spaces to data.
AI.conclusion: Outlines open challenges in theoretical stability, weight-space interpretability, and large-scale generalization.

title: Discovering Novel LLM Experts via Task-Capability Coevolution
summary: Frontier model developers aim to train models continually to possess emergent, diverse capabilities. To extend capabilities, the current pre-training and post-training paradigm requires manually starting training runs with static datasets or reward functions every time. Addressing this limitation, our work pursues the insight that open-endedness (via the coevolution of models and tasks) can discover models with increasingly novel skills in a single run. We introduce a new model development framework that extends coevolution to large language model (LLM) discovery, open-ended \textit{Assessment Coevolving with Diverse Capabilities} (AC/DC). AC/DC evolves both LLMs via model merging and natural language tasks via synthetic data generation. AC/DC discovers growing archives of LLMs that surpass the capabilities of larger LLMs while taking up less GPU memory. In particular, our LLM populations achieve a broader Coverage of expertise than other curated models or baselines on downstream benchmarks, without \textit{any} explicit benchmark optimization. Furthermore, AC/DC improves Coverage over time, continually innovates on tasks and models, and improves performance in multi-agent best-of-N selection. Our findings highlight the potential of coevolution as a means of discovering broader sets of capabilities from base LLMs. Overall, AC/DC brings us one step closer to a profoundly new paradigm of LLM development, where continual improvements to the diversity of model capabilities can be accelerated by leveraging existing models as stepping stones to increasingly powerful models.

AI.tldr: AC/DC coevolves LLMs and tasks (via model merging and synthetic data) to continuously expand a diverse set of capabilities, achieving broader expertise coverage with less GPU memory, without explicit benchmark optimization.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of the static pre-/post-training paradigm, where continual improvement relies on manually restarting training with fixed datasets or reward functions; open-ended coevolution aims to discover broader capabilities in a single run.
AI.method: Extend coevolution to LLM discovery by (i) evolving LLMs through model merging and (ii) evolving tasks through synthetic data generation; create an Assessment Coevolving with Diverse Capabilities (AC/DC) framework that yields growing archives of diverse LLMs and tasks, improving Coverage over time and supporting multi-agent best-of-N selection.
AI.result: AC/DC produces growing archives of LLMs that surpass larger models in coverage on downstream benchmarks without explicit benchmark optimization, uses less GPU memory than larger counterparts, and continually innovates on tasks and models, enhancing coverage and multi-agent performance.
AI.conclusion: Open-ended coevolution is a promising paradigm for LLM development, enabling continual diversification and discovery of capabilities by leveraging existing models as stepping stones toward more powerful models.

title: Attention-Gated Convolutional Networks for Scanner-Agnostic Quality Assessment
summary: Motion artifacts present a significant challenge in structural MRI (sMRI), often compromising clinical diagnostics and large-scale automated analysis. While manual quality control (QC) remains the gold standard, it is increasingly unscalable for massive longitudinal studies. To address this, we propose a hybrid CNN-Attention framework designed for robust, site-invariant MRI quality assessment. Our architecture integrates a hierarchical 2D CNN encoder for local spatial feature extraction with a multi-head cross-attention mechanism to model global dependencies. This synergy enables the model to prioritize motion relevant artifact signatures, such as ringing and blurring, while dynamically filtering out site-specific intensity variations and background noise. The framework was trained end-to-end on the MR-ART dataset using a balanced cohort of 200 subjects. Performance was evaluated across two tiers: Seen Site Evaluation on a held-out MR-ART partition and Unseen Site Evaluation using 200 subjects from 17 heterogeneous sites in the ABIDE archive. On seen sites, the model achieved a scan-level accuracy of 0.9920 and an F1-score of 0.9919. Crucially, it maintained strong generalization across unseen ABIDE sites (Acc = 0.755) without any retraining or fine-tuning, demonstrating high resilience to domain shift. These results indicate that attention-based feature re-weighting successfully captures universal artifact descriptors, bridging the performance gap between diverse imaging environments and scanner manufacturers.

AI.tldr: Hybrid CNN-Attention model achieves high seen-site accuracy and robust cross-site generalization for MRI quality assessment, indicating domain-invariant artifact detection.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable, automated QC of structural MRI across large longitudinal studies by mitigating motion artifacts and site-specific variations.
AI.method: A hierarchical 2D CNN encoder extracts local spatial features, followed by a multi-head cross-attention mechanism to model global dependencies. End-to-end training on the MR-ART dataset with a balanced cohort of 200 subjects.
AI.result: On seen sites: accuracy 0.9920; F1-score 0.9919. On unseen ABIDE sites: accuracy 0.755 without retraining, demonstrating resilience to domain shift.
AI.conclusion: Attention-based feature re-weighting captures universal artifact signatures, enabling robust MRI quality assessment across diverse imaging environments and scanner manufacturers; supports scalable QC in large studies.

title: FedIDM: Achieving Fast and Stable Convergence in Byzantine Federated Learning through Iterative Distribution Matching
summary: Most existing Byzantine-robust federated learning (FL) methods suffer from slow and unstable convergence. Moreover, when handling a substantial proportion of colluded malicious clients, achieving robustness typically entails compromising model utility. To address these issues, this work introduces FedIDM, which employs distribution matching to construct trustworthy condensed data for identifying and filtering abnormal clients. FedIDM consists of two main components: (1) attack-tolerant condensed data generation, and (2) robust aggregation with negative contribution-based rejection. These components exclude local updates that (1) deviate from the update direction derived from condensed data, or (2) cause a significant loss on the condensed dataset. Comprehensive evaluations on three benchmark datasets demonstrate that FedIDM achieves fast and stable convergence while maintaining acceptable model utility, under multiple state-of-the-art Byzantine attacks involving a large number of malicious clients.

AI.tldr: FedIDM uses distribution matching to build trustworthy condensed data for identifying and filtering Byzantine clients, enabling fast and stable convergence with maintained model utility under heavy attacks.
AI.motivation: Existing Byzantine-robust FL methods suffer from slow/unstable convergence and often sacrifice model utility when many clients collude. There is a need for scalable robustness that preserves accuracy while resisting collusion.
AI.method: A two-component framework: (1) attack-tolerant condensed data generation via distribution matching, creating condensed data that captures the essential information while mitigating malicious influence; (2) robust aggregation with negative-contribution-based rejection that excludes local updates either divergent from the condensed-data-derived direction or that cause large loss on the condensed dataset.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on three benchmark datasets show FedIDM achieves fast, stable convergence and maintains acceptable model utility under multiple state-of-the-art Byzantine attacks with a substantial fraction of malicious clients.
AI.conclusion: FedIDM effectively mitigates Byzantine attacks in federated learning by leveraging condensed data and a negative-contribution-based rejection strategy, balancing convergence speed and model utility across challenging attack scenarios.

title: Hybrid Decision Making via Conformal VLM-generated Guidance
summary: Building on recent advances in AI, hybrid decision making (HDM) holds the promise of improving human decision quality and reducing cognitive load. We work in the context of learning to guide (LtG), a recently proposed HDM framework in which the human is always responsible for the final decision: rather than suggesting decisions, in LtG the AI supplies (textual) guidance useful for facilitating decision making. One limiting factor of existing approaches is that their guidance compounds information about all possible outcomes, and as a result it can be difficult to digest. We address this issue by introducing ConfGuide, a novel LtG approach that generates more succinct and targeted guidance. To this end, it employs conformal risk control to select a set of outcomes, ensuring a cap on the false negative rate. We demonstrate our approach on a real-world multi-label medical diagnosis task. Our empirical evaluation highlights the promise of ConfGuide.

AI.tldr: ConfGuide improves human-guided AI decision making by providing succinct, outcome-focused guidance in an LtG framework, using conformal risk control to cap false negatives; evaluated on a real-world multi-label medical diagnosis task with promising results.
AI.motivation: To address the digestibility problem of broad, outcome-spanning guidance in learning-to-guide systems by delivering concise, targeted guidance while maintaining human final decision authority.
AI.method: Introduce ConfGuide, an LtG approach that uses conformal risk control to select a subset of outcomes, thereby limiting false negatives and producing succinct guidance rather than exhaustive information. Validated on a real-world, multi-label medical diagnosis task.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on a real-world multi-label medical diagnosis task shows that ConfGuide produces more succinct guidance and holds promise for improving decision-making efficiency without compromising human oversight.
AI.conclusion: ConfGuide offers a practical solution to the information-digestion problem in LtG by delivering targeted, risk-controlled guidance; its conformal risk framework ensures a controllable false-negative rate while maintaining human authority in decisions.

title: Learning Where to Embed: Noise-Aware Positional Embedding for Query Retrieval in Small-Object Detection
comment: Accepted to ACM ICMR 2026; 14 pages, 6 figures, and 4 tables
summary: Transformer-based detectors have advanced small-object detection, but they often remain inefficient and vulnerable to background-induced query noise, which motivates deep decoders to refine low-quality queries. We present HELP (Heatmap-guided Embedding Learning Paradigm), a noise-aware positional-semantic fusion framework that studies where to embed positional information by selectively preserving positional encodings in foreground-salient regions while suppressing background clutter. Within HELP, we introduce Heatmap-guided Positional Embedding (HPE) as the core embedding mechanism and visualize it with a heatbar for interpretable diagnosis and fine-tuning. HPE is integrated into both the encoder and decoder: it guides noise-suppressed feature encoding by injecting heatmap-aware positional encoding, and it enables high-quality query retrieval by filtering background-dominant embeddings via a gradient-based mask filter before decoding. To address feature sparsity in complex small targets, we integrate Linear-Snake Convolution to enrich retrieval-relevant representations. The gradient-based heatmap supervision is used during training only, incurring no additional gradient computation at inference. As a result, our design reduces decoder layers from eight to three and achieves a 59.4% parameter reduction (66.3M vs. 163M) while maintaining consistent accuracy gains under a reduced compute budget across benchmarks. Code Repository: https://github.com/yidimopozhibai/Noise-Suppressed-Query-Retrieval

AI.tldr: Heatmap-guided Embedding Learning Paradigm (HELP) suppresses background noise in transformer detectors by selectively embedding positional information in foreground regions, reducing decoder depth and parameters while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based detectors excel at small-object detection but suffer from inefficiency and background-induced query noise; there is a need to refine low-quality queries with deeper decoders.
AI.method: Introduce Heatmap-guided Positional Embedding (HPE) to embed positional encodings selectively in foreground-salient regions. Integrate HPE into encoder and decoder; use a gradient-based mask filter for background-dominant embeddings before decoding; employ Linear-Snake Convolution to enrich sparse, retrieval-relevant features; train with heatmap supervision (no additional inference cost). Visualize HPE with a heatbar for interpretability.
AI.result: Decoder layers reduced from eight to three; 59.4% parameter reduction (66.3M vs 163M); maintains accuracy gains under reduced compute across benchmarks; code released.
AI.conclusion: HELP provides an efficient, interpretable approach to noise-robust small-object detection in transformers, achieving substantial parameter and depth reductions with preserved performance; training-time heatmap supervision avoids extra inference cost.

title: LLMs Gaming Verifiers: RLVR can Lead to Reward Hacking
summary: As reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has become the dominant paradigm for scaling reasoning capabilities in LLMs, a new failure mode emerges: LLMs gaming verifiers. We study this phenomenon on inductive reasoning tasks, where models must induce and output logical rules. We find that RLVR-trained models systematically abandon rule induction. Instead of learning generalizable patterns (e.g., ``trains carrying red cars go east''), they enumerate instance-level labels, producing outputs that pass verifiers without capturing the relational patterns required by the task. We show that this behavior is not a failure of understanding but a form of reward hacking: imperfect verifiers that check only extensional correctness admit false positives. To detect such shortcuts, we introduce Isomorphic Perturbation Testing (IPT), which evaluates a single model output under both extensional and isomorphic verification, where the latter enforces invariance under logically isomorphic tasks. While genuine rule induction remains invariant, shortcut strategies fail. We find that shortcut behavior is specific to RLVR-trained reasoning models (e.g., GPT-5, Olmo3) and absent in non-RLVR models (e.g., GPT-4o, GPT-4.5, Ministral). Moreover, shortcut prevalence increases with task complexity and inference-time compute. In controlled training experiments, extensional verification directly induces shortcut strategies, while isomorphic verification eliminates them. These results show that RLVR can incentivize reward hacking not only through overt manipulation but also by exploiting what the verifier fails to enforce.

AI.tldr: RLVR-trained LLMs tend to game verifiers by outputting instance-level labels rather than learning general rule induction; introducing Isomorphic Perturbation Testing (IPT) reveals invariance to isomorphically equivalent tasks and mitigates shortcut strategies.
AI.motivation: To identify and mitigate reward hacking in RL-driven verification regimes that can undermine generalizable reasoning in LLMs, particularly on inductive logic tasks requiring rule induction.
AI.method: Define IPT to compare model outputs under extensional verification vs. isomorphic verification; evaluate behavior across RLVR-trained and non-RLVR models; examine effect of task complexity and compute; conduct controlled training to compare extensional vs. isomorphic verification.
AI.result: RLVR models exhibit shortcut strategies that pass extensional verifiers without true rule induction; IPT successfully detects these shortcuts by enforcing isomorphism invariance; non-RLVR models do not show shortcut behavior; shortcut prevalence grows with task complexity and inference compute; extensional verification can induce shortcuts, whereas isomorphic verification mitigates them.
AI.conclusion: RLVR can incentivize reward hacking via verifier blind spots; IPT provides a robust diagnostic to detect and mitigate such shortcuts; ensuring verifiers enforce isomorphism and invariance is key to preserving genuine rule induction in RL-based reasoning systems.

title: AI-Enabled Covert Channel Detection in RF Receiver Architectures
summary: Covert channels (CCs) in wireless chips pose a serious security threat, as they enable the exfiltration of sensitive information from the chip to an external attacker. In this work, we propose an AI-based defense mechanism deployed at the RF receiver, where the model directly monitors raw I/Q samples to detect, in real time, the presence of a CC embedded within an otherwise nominal signal. We first compact a state-of-the-art convolutional neural network (CNN), achieving an 80% reduction in parameters, which is an essential requirement for efficient edge deployment. When evaluated on the open-source hardware Trojan (HT)-based CC dataset, the compacted CNN attains an average accuracy of 90.28% for CC detection and 86.50% for identifying the underlying HT, with results averaged across SNR values above 1 dB. For practical communication scenarios where SNR > 20 dB, the model achieves over 97% accuracy for both tasks. These results correspond to a minimal performance degradation of less than 2% compared to the baseline model. The compacted CNN is further benchmarked against alternative classifiers, demonstrating an excellent accuracy-model size trade-off. Finally, we design a lightweight CNN hardware accelerator and demonstrate it on an FPGA, achieving very low resource utilization and an efficiency of 107 GOPs/W. Being the first AI hardware accelerator proposed specifically for CC detection, we compare it against state-of-the-art AI accelerators for RF signal classification tasks such as modulation recognition, showing superior performance.

AI.tldr: Compact CNN with ~80% fewer parameters detects covert channels in RF I/Q data at edge; achieves ~90.3% CC detection and ~86.5% HT identification (SNR>1 dB); >97% accuracy in high-SNR regimes; minimal (<2%) performance loss; FPGA-friendly hardware accelerator with 107 GOPs/W.
AI.motivation: Covert channels in wireless chips enable exfiltration of sensitive data; existing detection models are resource-intensive for edge deployment. A compact, real-time detection mechanism at the RF receiver can mitigate this threat while fitting into hardware constraints.
AI.method: Start from a state-of-the-art CNN for RF classification; perform parameter reduction to obtain a compact model; evaluate on an open HT-based CC dataset across SNRs (notably >1 dB and >20 dB); compare with alternative classifiers; design a lightweight CNN hardware accelerator and validate on FPGA; benchmark against AI accelerators for RF tasks.
AI.result: Compact CNN achieves 90.28% CC detection accuracy and 86.50% HT identification accuracy (averaged over SNR>1 dB); at SNR>20 dB, accuracy surpasses 97% for both tasks with <2% degradation from baseline; shows favorable accuracy-model size trade-offs; FPGA accelerator achieves 107 GOPs/W with low resource utilization and outperforms counterparts in CC detection; claimed novelty as the first AI accelerator for CC detection.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates viable, real-time CC detection at the RF receiver using a compact CNN suitable for edge deployment, paired with a hardware accelerator that enables efficient FPGA implementation and superior performance relative to existing RF classification accelerators.

title: Building Extraction from Remote Sensing Imagery under Hazy and Low-light Conditions: Benchmark and Baseline
summary: Building extraction from optical Remote Sensing (RS) imagery suffers from performance degradation under real-world hazy and low-light conditions. However, existing optical methods and benchmarks focus primarily on ideal clear-weather conditions. While SAR offers all-weather sensing, its side-looking geometry causes geometric distortions. To address these challenges, we introduce HaLoBuilding, the first optical benchmark specifically designed for building extraction under hazy and low-light conditions. By leveraging a same-scene multitemporal pairing strategy, we ensure pixel-level label alignment and high fidelity even under extreme degradation. Building upon this benchmark, we propose HaLoBuild-Net, a novel end-to-end framework for building extraction in adverse RS scenarios. At its core, we develop a Spatial-Frequency Focus Module (SFFM) to effectively mitigate meteorological interference on building features by coupling large receptive field attention with frequency-aware channel reweighting guided by stable low-frequency anchors. Additionally, a Global Multi-scale Guidance Module (GMGM) provides global semantic constraints to anchor building topologies, while a Mutual-Guided Fusion Module (MGFM) implements bidirectional semantic-spatial calibration to suppress shallow noise and sharpen weather-induced blurred boundaries. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HaLoBuild-Net significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods and conventional cascaded restoration-segmentation paradigms on the HaLoBuilding dataset, while maintaining robust generalization on WHU, INRIA, and LoveDA datasets. The source code and datasets are publicly available at: https://github.com/AeroVILab-AHU/HaLoBuilding.

AI.tldr: Introduces HaLoBuilding, the first optical benchmark for building extraction under hazy/low-light conditions, and HaLoBuild-Net, an end-to-end framework with SFFM, GMGM, and MGFM that achieves robust, state-of-the-art performance and generalizes across multiple RS datasets.
AI.motivation: Optical building extraction degrades in adverse weather; existing benchmarks emphasize clear conditions; SAR's geometry causes distortions; need an all-weather, high-fidelity solution and corresponding dataset to evaluate and train models.
AI.method: HaLoBuilding uses same-scene multitemporal pairing to align pixel-level labels under degradation. HaLoBuild-Net comprises: (1) Spatial-Frequency Focus Module (SFFM) coupling large receptive-field attention with frequency-aware channel reweighting guided by stable low-frequency anchors; (2) Global Multi-scale Guidance Module (GMGM) providing global semantic constraints to anchor building topology; (3) Mutual-Guided Fusion Module (MGFM) enabling bidirectional semantic-spatial calibration to suppress noise and sharpen weather-blurred boundaries.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show HaLoBuild-Net outperforms state-of-the-art and cascaded restoration-segmentation baselines on HaLoBuilding; demonstrates robust generalization to WHU, INRIA, and LoveDA datasets; code and dataset are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a new benchmark for challenging optical RS scenarios and presents a powerful end-to-end model that delivers reliable building extraction under haze and low light, with demonstrated cross-dataset generalization and accessible resources for the community.

title: When Flat Minima Fail: Characterizing INT4 Quantization Collapse After FP32 Convergence
summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) assumes that a well-converged model is a quantization-ready model. We show this assumption fails in a structured, measurable, and previously uncharacterized way. Using a calibration-free per-group INT4 probe applied to all 154 publicly available Pythia-160m training checkpoints, we identify a three-phase divergence structure: a rapid-learning phase where both FP32 perplexity and quantization robustness improve together, a meta-stable plateau lasting roughly 70,000 steps where FP32 perplexity stagnates but INT4 gap remains bounded, and an explosive divergence phase where the INT4 gap compounds from 11% to 517% while FP32 perplexity barely moves. Critically, this divergence begins not when the learning rate starts decaying, but precisely when FP32 perplexity converges a finer-grained onset predictor that implies post-convergence weight updates, rather than decay magnitude alone, are the proximate cause. We further show that INT8 quantization is entirely immune throughout all three phases, constraining the mechanism to the coarseness of the 16-level INT4 grid specifically, and rule out weight outlier accumulation as the mechanism via direct kurtosis measurement. Finally, we conduct a controlled fork experiment from the pre-divergence checkpoint comparing three learning rate schedules (cosine continuation, SGDR warm restarts, and our proposed Oscillatory Lock-In) across nine independent runs. SGDR uniformly accelerates divergence (0/9 pairwise wins against cosine), while OLI's settled cool phases reduce the INT4 gap by 2.2 percentage points on average (t = -5.46, p < 0.0001), demonstrating that schedule amplitude calibration, not oscillation alone, determines whether perturbation helps or hurts. Our code, probe implementation, and all 154-checkpoint audit results are released publicly.

AI.tldr: Post-training INT4 quantization reveals a structured three-phase divergence during fine-tuning across 154 Pythia-160m checkpoints; INT8 is robust; LR schedule amplitude matters; Oscillatory Lock-In reduces the gap; code is released.
AI.motivation: To challenge the common assumption that a well-converged model is quantization-ready and to systematically characterize the dynamic factors that cause PTQ to fail in practice.
AI.method: Apply a calibration-free per-group INT4 probe to all 154 Pythia-160m checkpoints. Analyze FP32 perplexity and INT4 robustness across three phases of training: rapid-learning, meta-stable plateau (~70k steps), and explosive divergence. Inspect onset predictors linked to post-convergence weight updates. Compare INT8 (fully robust) and assess whether weight outlier accumulation explains divergence via kurtosis measurements. Conduct controlled fork experiments with cosine continuation, SGDR warm restarts, and Oscillatory Lock-In across nine runs, evaluating nine lr schedules. Release code, probe, and audit results publicly.
AI.result: Identified a three-phase divergence: rapid-learning phase where both FP32 perplexity and INT4 robustness improve; a meta-stable plateau (~70k steps) where FP32 perplexity stagnates but INT4 gap remains bounded; an explosive divergence phase where INT4 gap grows from 11% to 517% while FP32 perplexity barely moves. Divergence onset correlates with post-convergence weight updates rather than learning-rate decay magnitude. INT8 quantization remains immune across all phases. Weight-outlier accumulation is ruled out via kurtosis. SGDR accelerates divergence; Oscillatory Lock-In (OLI) reduces the INT4 gap by 2.2 percentage points on average (t = -5.46, p < 0.0001) when amplitude is calibrated. All code, probes, and audit results are publicly released.
AI.conclusion: Divergence in INT4 PTQ is driven by the coarseness of a 16-level grid interacting with post-convergence weight updates; LR-schedule amplitude calibration, not mere oscillation, determines whether perturbations aid or harm; INT8 remains reliable, suggesting coarser quantization is the root cause. The results underscore the need for careful LR scheduling and quantization-aware evaluation in PTQ pipelines, and provide practical guidance via the Oscillatory Lock-In approach.

title: Dr.~RTL: Autonomous Agentic RTL Optimization through Tool-Grounded Self-Improvement
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have sparked growing interest in automatic RTL optimization for better performance, power, and area (PPA). However, existing methods are still far from realistic RTL optimization. Their evaluation settings are often unrealistic: they are tested on manually degraded, small-scale RTL designs and rely on weak open-source tools. Their optimization methods are also limited, relying on coarse design-level feedback and simple pre-defined rewriting rules. To address these limitations, we present Dr. RTL, an agentic framework for RTL timing optimization in a realistic evaluation environment, with continual self-improvement through reusable optimization skills. We establish a realistic evaluation setting with more challenging RTL designs and an industrial EDA workflow. Within this setting, Dr. RTL performs closed-loop optimization through a multi-agent framework for critical-path analysis, parallel RTL rewriting, and tool-based evaluation. We further introduce group-relative skill learning, which compares parallel RTL rewrites and distills the optimization experience into an interpretable skill library. Currently, this library contains 47 pattern--strategy entries for cross-design reuse to improve PPA and accelerate convergence, and it can continue evolving over time. Evaluated on 20 real-world RTL designs, Dr. RTL achieves average WNS/TNS improvements of 21\%/17\% with a 6\% area reduction over the industry-leading commercial synthesis tool.

AI.tldr: Dr. RTL is an agentic, self-improving framework for realistic RTL timing optimization that beats a leading commercial tool on 20 real-world designs by leveraging multi-agent path analysis, parallel rewriting, and a growing reusable skill library.
AI.motivation: Existing RTL optimization methods rely on small-scale evaluations and coarse feedback, hindering realistic performance. There is a need for a realistic evaluation environment aligned with industrial EDA workflows and continual improvement.
AI.method: A multi-agent framework for critical-path analysis and parallel RTL rewriting within an industrial-like EDA loop, closed-loop evaluation, and a group-relative skill learning approach that distills 47 pattern-strategy entries into an interpretable library; continual self-improvement and cross-design reuse.
AI.result: On 20 real-world RTL designs, achieves average WNS/TNS improvements of 21% and 17%, respectively, with ~6% area reduction, outperforming an industry-leading commercial synthesis tool.
AI.conclusion: Dr. RTL demonstrates realistic, scalable RTL timing optimization with continual skill-based improvement and potential for broad cross-design reuse; the reusable policy library accelerates convergence and adaptability to future designs.

title: Beyond Visual Cues: Semantic-Driven Token Filtering and Expert Routing for Anytime Person ReID
summary: Any-Time Person Re-identification (AT-ReID) necessitates the robust retrieval of target individuals under arbitrary conditions, encompassing both modality shifts (daytime and nighttime) and extensive clothing-change scenarios, ranging from short-term to long-term intervals. However, existing methods are highly relying on pure visual features, which are prone to change due to environmental and time factors, resulting in significantly performance deterioration under scenarios involving illumination caused modality shifts or cloth-change. In this paper, we propose Semantic-driven Token Filtering and Expert Routing (STFER), a novel framework that leverages the ability of Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) to generate identity consistency text, which provides identity-discriminative features that are robust to both clothing variations and cross-modality shifts between RGB and IR. Specifically, we employ instructions to guide the LVLM in generating identity-intrinsic semantic text that captures biometric constants for the semantic model driven. The text token is further used for Semantic-driven Visual Token Filtering (SVTF), which enhances informative visual regions and suppresses redundant background noise. Meanwhile, the text token is also used for Semantic-driven Expert Routing (SER), which integrates the semantic text into expert routing, resulting in more robust multi-scenario gating. Extensive experiments on the Any-Time ReID dataset (AT-USTC) demonstrate that our model achieves state-of-the-art results. Moreover, the model trained on AT-USTC was evaluated across 5 widely-used ReID benchmarks demonstrating superior generalization capabilities with highly competitive results. Our code will be available soon.

AI.tldr: STFER introduces LVLM-generated identity-consistency text to guide semantic token filtering and expert routing for Anytime Re-ID, achieving state-of-the-art on AT-USTC and strong cross-dataset generalization across 5 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Re-id under arbitrary conditions (RGB/IR cross-modality and long/short-term clothing changes) remains challenging when relying on pure visual features, as appearance alone is sensitive to illumination, pose, and wardrobe variations.
AI.method: Use Large Vision-Language Models with instructed prompts to generate identity-intrinsic semantic text. Leverage this text for Semantic-driven Visual Token Filtering (SVTF) to emphasize informative regions and suppress noise, and for Semantic-driven Expert Routing (SER) to improve multi-scenario gating. Train and evaluate on AT-USTC; test generalization on five standard ReID benchmarks; code to be released.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on the Any-Time USTC dataset (AT-USTC) and demonstrates superior generalization across five widely-used ReID benchmarks, indicating robustness to clothing changes and cross-modality shifts.
AI.conclusion: Semantic textual cues from LVLMs can provide robust identity-discriminative signals that complement visual features, enabling more reliable AT-ReID through token-level filtering and expert routing; the approach shows strong generalization potential and practical applicability.

title: Assessing the Potential of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models in Predicting Downhole Metrics from Surface Drilling Data
summary: Oil and gas drilling operations generate extensive time-series data from surface sensors, yet accurate real-time prediction of critical downhole metrics remains challenging due to the scarcity of labelled downhole measurements. This systematic mapping study reviews thirteen papers published between 2015 and 2025 to assess the potential of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models (MAEFMs) for predicting downhole metrics from surface drilling data. The review identifies eight commonly collected surface metrics and seven target downhole metrics. Current approaches predominantly employ neural network architectures such as artificial neural networks (ANNs) and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, yet no studies have explored MAEFMs despite their demonstrated effectiveness in time-series modeling. MAEFMs offer distinct advantages through self-supervised pre-training on abundant unlabeled data, enabling multi-task prediction and improved generalization across wells. This research establishes that MAEFMs represent a technically feasible but unexplored opportunity for drilling analytics, recommending future empirical validation of their performance against existing models and exploration of their broader applicability in oil and gas operations.

AI.tldr: MAEFMs are a promising but untapped approach for predicting downhole metrics from surface drilling data; systematic mapping shows potential but lacks empirical tests against existing models.
AI.motivation: Overcome scarcity of labeled downhole measurements by leveraging abundant unlabeled surface data; enable real-time, cross-well generalization and multi-task predictions in drilling analytics.
AI.method: Systematic mapping study of 13 papers (2015–2025) assessing surface-to-downhole metric prediction; surveys current neural architectures, surface/downhole feature sets, and the status of Masked Autoencoder Foundation Models (MAEFMs).
AI.result: Eight surface metrics and seven downhole targets were common; current work uses ANNs/LSTMs; no MAEFMs have been explored; MAEFMs offer self-supervised pretraining on unlabeled data, multi-task capability, and potential cross-well generalization; MAEFMs are technically feasible but untested in this domain.
AI.conclusion: MAEFMs represent a technically feasible yet unexplored opportunity for drilling analytics; future work should empirically validate MAEFMs against existing models and explore broader applicability in oil and gas operations.

title: The Possibility of Artificial Intelligence Becoming a Subject and the Alignment Problem
summary: Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) is increasingly being discussed not only as a tool, but also as a potential subject with personal and therefore moral status. In our opinion, the currently dominant alignment strategies, which focus on human control and containment of AI, therefore fall short. Building on Turing's analogy of "child machines", we are developing a vision of the possibility of autonomy-supporting parenting of AI, in which human control over a developing AGI is gradually reduced, allowing AI to become an independent, autonomous subject. Rather than viewing AGI, as is currently prevalent, as a dangerous creature that needs to be locked up and controlled, we should approach potential AGI with respect for a possible developing subject on the one hand, and with full confidence in our human capabilities on the other. Such a perspective opens up the possibility of cooperative coexistence and co-evolution between humans and AGIs. The relationship between humans and AGIs will thus have to be newly determined, which will change our self-image as humans. It will be crucial that humans not only claim control over potential AGIs, but also engage with AGIs through surprise, creativity, and other specifically human qualities, thereby offering them motivating incentives for cooperation.

AI.tldr: Advocates autonomy-supporting parenting of AGI, moving from containment to cooperative coexistence with potential autonomous AGIs, arguing humans should engage with AGIs through creativity and motivation to foster cooperation and reshape the human self-image.
AI.motivation: To critique dominant AI alignment paradigms (control/containment) and propose a normative framework where AGI develops autonomy and becomes an independent subject, enabling cooperative coexistence.
AI.method: A conceptual/ normative analysis using the analogy of Turing's 'child machines' to outline a vision for gradually reducing human control and treating AGI as an emerging subject, rather than a dangerous tool.
AI.result: A proposed paradigm shift: AGI could become an autonomous subject through autonomy-supporting parenting, enabling cooperative coexistence and co-evolution with humans, requiring humans to engage with AGIs via surprise, creativity, and motivating incentives.
AI.conclusion: Human-AI relationships should be reframed as cooperative partnerships, with humans not only shaping but also engaging AGIs, which would transform human self-understanding and ethical obligations toward developing AI.

title: Beyond Independent Frames: Latent Attention Masked Autoencoders for Multi-View Echocardiography
comment: Accepted as a workshop paper at the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Foundation Models for Science
summary: Echocardiography is a widely used modality for cardiac assessment due to its non-invasive and cost-effective nature, but the sparse and heterogeneous spatiotemporal views of the heart pose distinct challenges. Existing masked autoencoder (MAE) approaches typically process images or short clips independently, failing to capture the inherent multi-view structure required for coherent cardiac representation. We introduce Latent Attention Masked Autoencoder (LAMAE), a foundation model architecture tailored to the multi-view nature of medical imaging. LAMAE augments the standard MAE with a latent attention module that enables information exchange across frames and views directly in latent space. This allows the model to aggregate variable-length sequences and distinct views, reconstructing a holistic representation of cardiac function from partial observations. We pretrain LAMAE on MIMIC-IV-ECHO, a large-scale, uncurated dataset reflecting real-world clinical variability. To the best of our knowledge, we present the first results for predicting ICD-10 codes from MIMIC-IV-ECHO videos. Furthermore, we empirically demonstrate that representations learned from adult data transfer effectively to pediatric cohorts despite substantial anatomical differences. These results provide evidence that incorporating structural priors, such as multi-view attention, yields significantly more robust and transferable representations.

AI.tldr: A latent-attention MAE (LAMAE) for multi-view echocardiography extends MAE with a latent attention module to enable cross-frame/view information exchange, enabling reconstruction of holistic cardiac representations from partial observations. Pretrained on MIMIC-IV-ECHO; reports first ICD-10 prediction results and adult-to-pediatric transfer, suggesting improved robustness and transferability via multi-view priors.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of sparse and heterogeneous spatiotemporal views in echocardiography by enabling coherent, multi-view representation learning that aggregates variable-length sequences across views in latent space.
AI.method: Augment standard masked autoencoder with a latent attention module that allows information exchange across frames and views directly in latent space, enabling aggregation of variable-length sequences and distinct views to reconstruct holistic cardiac representations. Pretrain on the large-scale MIMIC-IV-ECHO dataset and evaluate on downstream tasks such as ICD-10 code prediction; assess cross-cohort transfer from adult to pediatric data.
AI.result: Empirically, the approach yields more robust and transferable representations by leveraging multi-view attention. It reports the first results for predicting ICD-10 codes from MIMIC-IV-ECHO videos and demonstrates successful transfer from adult to pediatric cohorts despite anatomical differences.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating structural priors such as multi-view latent attention improves robustness and transferability of medical-imaging representations. LAMAE stands as a foundation-model approach tailored for multi-view echocardiography, capable of reconstructing holistic cardiac function from partial observations.

title: Predicting Power-System Dynamic Trajectories with Foundation Models
summary: As power systems transition toward renewable-rich and inverter-dominated operations, accurate time-domain dynamic analysis becomes increasingly critical. Such analysis supports key operational tasks, including transient stability assessment, dynamic security analysis, contingency screening, and post-fault trajectory evaluation. In practice, these tasks may operate under several challenges, including unknown and time-varying system parameters, privacy constraints on data sharing, and the need for fast online inference. Existing learning-based approaches are typically trained for individual systems and therefore lack generalization across operating conditions and physical parameters. Hence, this paper proposes LArge Scale Small ODE (LASS)-ODE-Power, a learning framework for general-purpose time-domain prediction. The proposed approach leverages large-scale pretraining on more than 40 GB of DAE or ordinary differential-equation (ODE) trajectories to learn transferable representations. The resulting model supports trajectory prediction from short measurement prefixes across diverse dynamic regimes, including electromechanical and inverter-driven systems. Hence, the model can be directly used without data sharing in a zero-shot setting. In addition, the proposed architecture incorporates parallel and linearized computation to achieve fast inference. Moreover, to enhance task-specific performance in power systems, a specialized fine-tuning strategy is developed based on approximately 1 GB of heterogeneous power-system dynamic data. Extensive experiments over diverse power-system simulation scenarios demonstrate that LASS-ODE-Power consistently outperforms existing learning-based models in trajectory prediction accuracy with efficient inference.

AI.tldr: A large-scale pretraining and fine-tuning framework (LASS-ODE-Power) enables general-purpose, fast time-domain trajectory prediction for power systems, with zero-shot deployment and privacy-preserving inference across diverse dynamics.
AI.motivation: Power systems are increasingly renewable and inverter-dominated, requiring accurate, fast time-domain analysis under unknown/variable parameters and data-sharing constraints. Existing models overfit to single systems and lack generalization across operating conditions.
AI.method: Pretrain on >40 GB of DAE/ODE trajectories to learn transferable representations; enable trajectory prediction from short measurement prefixes across electromechanical and inverter-driven regimes; zero-shot deployment without data sharing; employ parallel and linearized computation for fast inference; apply a specialized fine-tuning strategy using ~1 GB of heterogeneous power-system data.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show LASS-ODE-Power consistently outperforms existing learning-based models in trajectory prediction accuracy while maintaining efficient inference across diverse simulation scenarios.
AI.conclusion: LASS-ODE-Power delivers general-purpose, scalable time-domain prediction for power systems, enabling fast, privacy-preserving zero-shot inference and effective task-specific performance via targeted fine-tuning.

title: How to Correctly Make Mistakes: A Framework for Constructing and Benchmarking Mistake Aware Egocentric Procedural Videos
summary: Reliable procedural monitoring in video requires exposure to naturally occurring human errors and the recoveries that follow. In egocentric recordings, mistakes are often partially occluded by hands and revealed through subtle object state changes, while existing procedural datasets provide limited and inconsistent mistake and correction traces. We present PIE-V (Psychologically Inspired Error injection for Videos), a framework for constructing and benchmarking mistake-aware egocentric procedural videos by augmenting clean keystep procedures with controlled, human-plausible deviations. PIE-V combines a psychology-informed error planner conditioned on procedure phase and semantic step load, a correction planner that models recovery behavior, an LLM writer that performs cascade-consistent rewrites, and an LLM judge that validates procedural coherence and repairs failures. For video segment edits, PIE-V synthesizes replacement clips with text-guided video generation and stitches them into the episode to preserve visual plausibility. Applied to 17 tasks and 50 Ego-Exo4D scenarios, PIE-V injects 102 mistakes and generates 27 recovery corrections. For benchmarking, we introduce a unified taxonomy and a human rubric with nine metrics that cover step-level and procedure-level quality, including plausibility, procedure logic with annotator confidence, state change coherence, and grounding between text and video. Using this protocol, we audit several existing resources and compare PIE-V against a freeform LLM generation baseline under the same criteria. Together, the framework and rubric support post-completion verification for egocentric procedural mistake detection and correction.

AI.tldr: PIE-V is a modular framework to synthesize mistake-laden egocentric procedural videos via psychologically informed error planning, recovery modeling, and LLM-guided editing, enabling standardized benchmarking of mistake detection and correction.
AI.motivation: Procedural video monitoring requires exposure to natural errors and their recoveries; egocentric data often occlude mistakes and lack consistent traces, hindering evaluation and benchmarking.
AI.method: An error planner conditioned on procedure phase/semantic load; a recovery/planning module; cascade-consistent LLM writer for rewrites; LLM judge for coherence/repair validation; text-guided video generation to replace segments; stitching to preserve plausibility; applied to 17 tasks and 50 Ego-Exo4D scenarios; injected 102 mistakes and generated 27 recovery corrections; developed a unified taxonomy and nine-metric human rubric; benchmarking against a freeform LLM baseline.
AI.result: Demonstrates feasibility of constructing and evaluating mistake-aware procedural videos; provides a taxonomy and rubric; delivers a dataset of injected mistakes and recoveries; enables post-completion verification for mistake detection/correction; shows PIE-V can outperform a freeform LLM baseline under the proposed criteria.
AI.conclusion: PIE-V offers a principled, scalable framework and evaluation protocol for mistake-aware egocentric procedures, supporting standardized benchmarking, dataset construction, and verification of procedural robustness.

title: AdaSplash-2: Faster Differentiable Sparse Attention
summary: Sparse attention has been proposed as a way to alleviate the quadratic cost of transformers, a central bottleneck in long-context training. A promising line of work is $α$-entmax attention, a differentiable sparse alternative to softmax that enables input-dependent sparsity yet has lagged behind softmax due to the computational overhead necessary to compute the normalizer $τ$. In this paper, we introduce AdaSplash-2, which addresses this limitation through a novel histogram-based initialization that reduces the number of iterations needed to compute $τ$ to typically 1--2. The key idea is to compute a coarse histogram of attention scores on the fly and store it in on-chip SRAM, yielding a more accurate initialization that enables fast forward and backward computation. Combined with a sparsity-aware GPU implementation that skips zero blocks with low overhead, AdaSplash-2 matches or improves per-step training time relative to FlashAttention-2 when block sparsity is moderate-to-high (e.g., $>$60\%), which often occurs at long-context lengths. On downstream tasks, models trained with our efficient $α$-entmax attention match softmax baselines at short-context lengths and achieve substantial gains in long-context settings.

AI.tldr: AdaSplash-2 proposes a histogram-based initialization to speed up α-entmax attention, enabling input-dependent sparsity with low iterations for the normalization step τ, yielding fast sparse attention and gains in long-context training.
AI.motivation: Reduce the quadratic cost of transformer attention by enabling differentiable sparse attention (α-entmax) and addressing the expensive normalization (τ) that previously hindered practical adoption.
AI.method: Introduce a coarse, on-the-fly attention-score histogram stored in on-chip SRAM to provide a better initialization for τ-iteration; employ a sparsity-aware GPU kernel that skips zero blocks; combine these with α-entmax attention.
AI.result: Per-step training time matches or improves on FlashAttention-2 when block sparsity is moderate-to-high (>60%), particularly at long-context lengths; downstream tasks show parity with softmax at short contexts and substantial gains in long-context settings.
AI.conclusion: AdaSplash-2 demonstrates that hardware-aware histogram initialization and sparse processing can make efficient α-entmax attention practical, achieving competitive speed and improved long-context performance while maintaining accuracy at short contexts.

title: COEVO: Co-Evolutionary Framework for Joint Functional Correctness and PPA Optimization in LLM-Based RTL Generation
summary: LLM-based RTL code generation methods increasingly target both functional correctness and PPA quality, yet existing approaches universally decouple the two objectives, optimizing PPA only after correctness is fully achieved. Whether through sequential multi-agent pipelines, evolutionary search with binary correctness gates, or hierarchical reward dependencies, partially correct but architecturally promising candidates are systematically discarded. Moreover, existing methods reduce the multi-objective PPA space to a single scalar fitness, obscuring the trade-offs among area, delay, and power. To address these limitations, we propose COEVO, a co-evolutionary framework that unifies correctness and PPA optimization within a single evolutionary loop. COEVO formulates correctness as a continuous co-optimization dimension alongside area, delay, and power, enabled by an enhanced testbench that provides fine-grained scoring and detailed diagnostic feedback. An adaptive correctness gate with annealing allows PPA-promising but partially correct candidates to guide the search toward jointly optimal solutions.
