To preserve the full PPA trade-off structure, COEVO employs four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting with configurable intra-level sorting, replacing scalar fitness without manual weight tuning. Evaluated on VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0, COEVO achieves 97.5\% and 94.5\% Pass@1 with GPT-5.4-mini, surpassing all agentic baselines across four LLM backbones, while attaining the best PPA on 43 out of 49 synthesizable RTLLM designs.

AI.tldr: COEVO introduces a co-evolutionary framework that unifies correctness and PPA optimization in LLM-guided RTL code generation, using a four-dimensional Pareto (area, delay, power, correctness) sorting and an adaptive correctness gate to preserve partially correct but promising candidates within a single evolutionary loop.
AI.motivation: Existing RTL generation methods decouple correctness from PPA optimization, discarding partially correct architectures and reducing multi-objective PPA trade-offs to a single scalar fitness, which hinders exploration of optimal correctness/PPA trade-offs.
AI.method: COEVO embeds correctness as a continuous optimization dimension in the evolutionary loop, augmented by an enhanced testbench for fine-grained scoring and diagnostic feedback. It employs an adaptive annealing-based correctness gate to allow PPA-promising though partially correct candidates to influence search, and uses four-dimensional Pareto-based non-dominated sorting with configurable intra-level sorting to preserve the full PPA trade-off structure without manual weight tunings.
AI.result: On VerilogEval 2.0 and RTLLM 2.0, COEVO achieves Pass@1 of 97.5% and 94.5% using GPT-5.4-mini, surpassing all agentic baselines across four LLM backbones, and obtains the best PPA on 43 of 49 synthesizable RTLLM designs.
AI.conclusion: COEVO demonstrates that unifying correctness and PPA within a single evolutionary loop with continuous optimization and 4D Pareto sorting yields superior correctness and PPA outcomes, suggesting stronger capabilities for scalable RTL synthesis with LLMs and improved exploration of the full objective space.

title: KVNN: Learnable Multi-Kernel Volterra Neural Networks
summary: Higher-order learning is fundamentally rooted in exploiting compositional features. It clearly hinges on enriching the representation by more elaborate interactions of the data which, in turn, tends to increase the model complexity of conventional large-scale deep learning models. In this paper, a kernelized Volterra Neural Network (kVNN) is proposed. The key to the achieved efficiency lies in using a learnable multi-kernel representation, where different interaction orders are modeled by distinct polynomial-kernel components with compact, learnable centers, yielding an order-adaptive parameterization. Features are learned by the composition of layers, each of which consists of parallel branches of different polynomial orders, enabling kVNN filters to directly replace standard convolutional kernels within existing architectures. The theoretical results are substantiated by experiments on two representative tasks: video action recognition and image denoising. The results demonstrate favorable performance-efficiency trade-offs: kVNN consistently yields reduced model (parameters) and computational (GFLOPs) complexity with competitive and often improved performance. These results are maintained even when trained from scratch without large-scale pretraining. In summary, we substantiate that structured kernelized higher-order layers offer a practical path to balancing expressivity and computational cost in modern deep networks.

AI.tldr: Kernelized Volterra Neural Network (kVNN) introduces a learnable multi-kernel, order-aware architecture with parallel branches per polynomial order, enabling higher-order interactions with fewer parameters and FLOPs; it can replace standard conv layers and shows favorable accuracy-efficiency on video action recognition and image denoising, even from scratch.
AI.motivation: Address the expressivity-cost trade-off of higher-order representations in deep networks by providing a compact, adaptable, kernelized higher-order layer that can plug into standard architectures.
AI.method: Proposes kVNN using multiple polynomial-kernel components (one per interaction order) with learnable centers to form an order-adaptive parameterization. Each layer has parallel branches corresponding to different polynomial orders; these kVNN branches can substitute standard convolutional kernels in existing architectures.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on video action recognition and image denoising shows reduced model parameters and GFLOPs while maintaining or improving performance; these benefits persist even when trained from scratch without large-scale pretraining.
AI.conclusion: Structured kernelized higher-order layers offer a practical approach to balance expressivity and computational cost in modern deep networks and can be integrated into existing architectures to achieve better efficiency without sacrificing performance.

title: One-shot learning for the complex dynamical behaviors of weakly nonlinear forced oscillators
summary: Extrapolative prediction of complex nonlinear dynamics remains a central challenge in engineering. This study proposes a one-shot learning method to identify global frequency-response curves from a single excitation time history by learning governing equations. We introduce MEv-SINDy (Multi-frequency Evolutionary Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics) to infer the governing equations of non-autonomous and multi-frequency systems. The methodology leverages the Generalized Harmonic Balance (GHB) method to decompose complex forced responses into a set of slow-varying evolution equations. We validated the capabilities of MEv-SINDy on two critical Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS). These applications include a nonlinear beam resonator and a MEMS micromirror. Our results show that the model trained on a single point accurately predicts softening/hardening effects and jump phenomena across a wide range of excitation levels. This approach significantly reduces the data acquisition burden for the characterization and design of nonlinear microsystems.

AI.tldr: MEv-SINDy is a one-shot learning framework that identifies global frequency-response curves from a single excitation time history by learning governing equations for non-autonomous, multi-frequency systems. It combines Generalized Harmonic Balance with sparse identification to predict nonlinear MEMS dynamics from limited data.
AI.motivation: Extrapolative prediction of nonlinear dynamics with minimal data is challenging, especially for non-autonomous, multi-frequency forcing. A method that can infer governing equations from a single measurement would reduce data acquisition burden and enable reliable extrapolation of frequency-response curves.
AI.method: MEv-SINDy (Multi-frequency Evolutionary Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics) uses Generalized Harmonic Balance to decompose forced responses into slow-varying evolution equations and then applies sparse identification to recover governing equations governing the slow dynamics across multiple frequencies.
AI.result: A model trained from a single excitation point accurately predicts softening/hardening, jump phenomena, and other nonlinear effects across a broad range of excitation levels. Validation on two MEMS devices (nonlinear beam resonator and MEMS micromirror) demonstrates the approach’s predictive capability and data-efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The approach substantially reduces data acquisition requirements for characterizing and designing nonlinear microsystems and offers a generalizable framework for extrapolating global frequency-response curves from limited time-history data.

title: Towards Faster Language Model Inference Using Mixture-of-Experts Flow Matching
summary: Flow matching retains the generation quality of diffusion models while enabling substantially faster inference, making it a compelling paradigm for generative modeling. However, when applied to language modeling, it exhibits fundamental limitations in representing complex latent distributions with irregular geometries, such as anisotropy and multimodality. To address these challenges, we propose a mixture-of-experts flow matching (MoE-FM) framework, which captures complex global transport geometries in latent space by decomposing them into locally specialized vector fields. Building on MoE-FM, we develop a non-autoregressive (NAR) language modeling approach, named YAN, instantiated with both Transformer and Mamba architectures. Across multiple downstream tasks, YAN achieves generation quality on par with both autoregressive (AR) and diffusion-based NAR language models, while requiring as few as three sampling steps. This yields a $40\times$ speedup over AR baselines and up to a $10^3\times$ speedup over diffusion language models, demonstrating substantial efficiency advantages for language modeling.

AI.tldr: Mixture-of-experts flow matching (MoE-FM) decomposes latent transport into locally specialized vector fields to capture complex geometries, enabling a non-autoregressive language model (YAN) with Transformer and Mamba backends that matches autoregressive and diffusion-quality generation with only a few sampling steps and substantial speedups (~3 steps; ~40x faster than AR; up to 1000x faster than diffusion).
AI.motivation: Flow matching preserves diffusion-quality generation but struggles to represent complex, irregular latent distributions in language modeling (e.g., anisotropy, multimodality). There is a need for a framework that captures global transport geometry in latent space with high expressivity and efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce a mixture-of-experts flow matching (MoE-FM) framework to model latent-space transport via locally specialized vector fields. Build a non-autoregressive language model (YAN) using MoE-FM, instantiated with Transformer and Mamba architectures. Evaluate across multiple downstream tasks, focusing on generation quality and sampling efficiency with a small number of steps.
AI.result: YAN achieves generation quality on par with autoregressive and diffusion-based non-autoregressive LMs while requiring as few as three sampling steps, yielding ~40× speedup over AR baselines and up to ~1000× speedup over diffusion LMs.
AI.conclusion: MoE-FM effectively captures complex latent geometries in language modeling and enables highly efficient non-autoregressive generation. The YAN model demonstrates that combining MoE-FM with both Transformer and Mamba backends can match traditional AR/diffusion-quality performance with a fraction of the sampling steps, offering substantial practical efficiency gains.

title: Class Unlearning via Depth-Aware Removal of Forget-Specific Directions
comment: Accepted to the CVPR 2026 Workshop on Machine Unlearning for Vision (MUV)
summary: Machine unlearning aims to remove targeted knowledge from a trained model without the cost of retraining from scratch. In class unlearning, however, reducing accuracy on forget classes does not necessarily imply true forgetting: forgotten information can remain encoded in internal representations, and apparent forgetting may arise from classifier-head suppression rather than representational removal. We show that existing class-unlearning methods often exhibit weak or negative selectivity, preserve forget-class structure in deep representations, or rely heavily on final-layer bias shifts. We then introduce DAMP (Depth-Aware Modulation by Projection), a one-shot, closed-form weight-surgery method that removes forget-specific directions from a pretrained network without gradient-based optimization. At each stage, DAMP computes class prototypes in the input space of the next learnable operator, extracts forget directions as residuals relative to retain-class prototypes, and applies a projection-based update to reduce downstream sensitivity to those directions. To preserve utility, DAMP uses a parameter-free depth-aware scaling rule derived from probe separability, applying smaller edits in early layers and larger edits in deeper layers. The method naturally extends to multi-class forgetting through low-rank subspace removal. Across MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and Tiny ImageNet, and across convolutional and transformer architectures, DAMP more closely resembles the retraining gold standard than some of the prior methods, improving selective forgetting while better preserving retain-class performance and reducing residual forget-class structure in deep layers.

AI.tldr: Introduces DAMP, a one-shot, projection-based method for class forgetting that removes forget-directions without retraining, with depth-aware scaling, achieving better selective forgetting while preserving retain-class performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To overcome shortcomings of existing class-unlearning methods (weak selectivity, residual forget-class structure in deep representations, and overreliance on final-layer bias shifts) and to enable robust forgetting of targeted information with minimal cost.
AI.method: Compute class prototypes in the input space of the next learnable operator; extract forget directions as residuals relative to retain-class prototypes; apply a projection-based update to reduce downstream sensitivity to forget directions; parameter-free depth-aware scaling derived from probe separability (smaller edits in early layers, larger in deeper layers); one-shot, closed-form (no gradient-based optimization); extends to multi-class forgetting via low-rank subspace removal.
AI.result: Empirically, DAMP more closely resembles the retraining gold standard than prior methods across MNIST, CIFAR-10/100, and Tiny ImageNet; effective across CNNs and Transformers; improves selective forgetting, better preserves retain-class performance, and reduces residual forget-class structure in deep layers.
AI.conclusion: DAMP provides a practical, generalizable one-shot forgetting mechanism that avoids gradient-based retraining, preserves utility, and scales to multi-class forgetting across diverse architectures and datasets.

title: RL-STPA: Adapting System-Theoretic Hazard Analysis for Safety-Critical Reinforcement Learning
summary: As reinforcement learning (RL) deployments expand into safety-critical domains, existing evaluation methods fail to systematically identify hazards arising from the black-box nature of neural network enabled policies and distributional shift between training and deployment. This paper introduces Reinforcement Learning System-Theoretic Process Analysis (RL-STPA), a framework that adapts conventional STPA's systematic hazard analysis to address RL's unique challenges through three key contributions: hierarchical subtask decomposition using both temporal phase analysis and domain expertise to capture emergent behaviors, coverage-guided perturbation testing that explores the sensitivity of state-action spaces, and iterative checkpoints that feed identified hazards back into training through reward shaping and curriculum design. We demonstrate RL-STPA in the safety-critical test case of autonomous drone navigation and landing, revealing potential loss scenarios that can be missed by standard RL evaluations. The proposed framework provides practitioners with a toolkit for systematic hazard analysis, quantitative metrics for safety coverage assessment, and actionable guidelines for establishing operational safety bounds. While RL-STPA cannot provide formal guarantees for arbitrary neural policies, it offers a practical methodology for systematically evaluating and improving RL safety and robustness in safety-critical applications where exhaustive verification methods remain intractable.

AI.tldr: RL-STPA introduces a safety-analysis framework for RL that adapts STPA to RL challenges, combining hierarchical subtask decomposition, coverage-guided perturbation testing, and iterative training feedback. Applied to autonomous drone navigation/landing, it reveals hazards missed by standard evaluations and offers metrics and guidelines for safety bounds, while not providing formal guarantees.
AI.motivation: To systematically identify hazards arising from the black-box nature of neural policies and distributional shift in RL, addressing gaps left by conventional RL evaluations in safety-critical domains.
AI.method: Three core contributions: (i) hierarchical subtask decomposition using temporal phase analysis and domain expertise to capture emergent behaviors; (ii) coverage-guided perturbation testing exploring sensitivities in the state-action space; (iii) iterative checkpoints feeding hazards back into training via reward shaping and curriculum design.
AI.result: Demonstrated on autonomous drone navigation and landing, revealing potential loss scenarios that standard RL evaluations miss; provides practitioners with a hazard-analysis toolkit, quantitative safety-coverage metrics, and actionable guidelines for safety bounds.
AI.conclusion: RL-STPA offers a practical methodology for systematically evaluating and improving RL safety and robustness in safety-critical applications where exhaustive verification is intractable, though it does not guarantee formal safety for arbitrary neural policies.

title: Autogenesis: A Self-Evolving Agent Protocol
summary: Recent advances in LLM based agent systems have shown promise in tackling complex, long horizon tasks. However, existing agent protocols (e.g., A2A and MCP) under specify cross entity lifecycle and context management, version tracking, and evolution safe update interfaces, which encourages monolithic compositions and brittle glue code. We introduce \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis Protocol (AGP)}}, a self evolution protocol that decouples what evolves from how evolution occurs. Its Resource Substrate Protocol Layer (RSPL) models prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memory as protocol registered resources\footnote{Unless otherwise specified, resources refer to instances of the five RSPL entity types: \emph{prompt}, \emph{agent}, \emph{tool}, \emph{environment}, \emph{memory} with agent \emph{outputs}.} with explicit state, lifecycle, and versioned interfaces. Its Self Evolution Protocol Layer (SEPL) specifies a closed loop operator interface for proposing, assessing, and committing improvements with auditable lineage and rollback. Building on \textbf{\textsc{AGP}}, we present \textbf{\textsc{Autogenesis System (AGS)}}, a self-evolving multi-agent system that dynamically instantiates, retrieves, and refines protocol-registered resources during execution. We evaluate \textbf{\textsc{AGS}} on multiple challenging benchmarks that require long horizon planning and tool use across heterogeneous resources. The results demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines, supporting the effectiveness of agent resource management and closed loop self evolution.

AI.tldr: Introduces Autogenesis Protocol (AGP) and Autogenesis System (AGS)—a resource-centric, self-evolving framework for LLM-based agents that decouples evolution from implementation, using RSPL to register and version resources and SEPL to govern auditable improvements; evaluated on long-horizon tasks with heterogeneous tools showing improved performance.
AI.motivation: Current agent protocols (e.g., A2A, MCP) under-specify cross-entity lifecycle, context management, versioning, leading to monolithic glue code and brittle systems; a modular, auditable, evolvable architecture for agents is needed.
AI.method: Define RSPL to model prompts, agents, tools, environments, and memory as protocol-registered resources with explicit state, lifecycle, and versioned interfaces; SEPL provides a closed-loop operator for proposing, assessing, and committing improvements with auditable lineage and rollback; AGS dynamically instantiates, retrieves, and refines protocol-registered resources during execution.
AI.result: AGS achieves consistent improvements over strong baselines on benchmarks requiring long-horizon planning and tool use across heterogeneous resources, demonstrating effective resource management and closed-loop self-evolution.
AI.conclusion: The framework shows that resource-driven, self-evolving agent systems can achieve robust adaptability and maintainable evolution by separating what evolves from how evolution occurs, enabling scalable, auditable agent architectures.

title: OmniLight: One Model to Rule All Lighting Conditions
comment: CVPRW 2026; NTIRE 2026 Image Shadow Removal & Ambient Lighting Normalization Challenges (1st Perceptual Rank for White Lighting, 2nd Fidelity Rank & 4th Perceptual Rank for Color Lighting)
summary: Adverse lighting conditions, such as cast shadows and irregular illumination, pose significant challenges to computer vision systems by degrading visibility and color fidelity. Consequently, effective shadow removal and ALN are critical for restoring underlying image content, improving perceptual quality, and facilitating robust performance in downstream tasks. However, while achieving state-of-the-art results on specific benchmarks is a primary goal in image restoration challenges, real-world applications often demand robust models capable of handling diverse domains. To address this, we present a comprehensive study on lighting-related image restoration by exploring two contrasting strategies. We leverage a robust framework for ALN, DINOLight, as a specialized baseline to exploit the characteristics of each individual dataset, and extend it to OmniLight, a generalized alternative incorporating our proposed Wavelet Domain Mixture-of-Experts (WD-MoE) that is trained across all provided datasets. Through a comparative analysis of these two methods, we discuss the impact of data distribution on the performance of specialized and unified architectures in lighting-related image restoration. Notably, both approaches secured top-tier rankings across all three lighting-related tracks in the NTIRE 2026 Challenge, demonstrating their outstanding perceptual quality and generalization capabilities. Our codes are available at https://github.com/OBAKSA/Lighting-Restoration.

AI.tldr: A comparative study of specialized vs generalized lighting restoration approaches, showing that per-dataset DINOLight and cross-domain OmniLight with WD-MoE both achieve top results on NTIRE 2026 tracks; data distribution affects performance; code released.
AI.motivation: Address robustness and generalization of lighting-related image restoration across diverse domains; real-world applications require models that generalize beyond single datasets.
AI.method: Two strategies: (1) DINOLight, a dataset-specific baseline exploiting per-dataset characteristics; (2) OmniLight, a generalized model built with Wavelet Domain MoE (WD-MoE) trained across all datasets.
AI.result: Both approaches secured top-tier rankings across all three lighting-related NTIRE 2026 tracks, indicating strong perceptual quality and cross-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: Specialized and generalized architectures can achieve state-of-the-art results; data distribution critically shapes performance; WD-MoE offers robust cross-domain restoration; code available.

title: Optimal last-iterate convergence in matrix games with bandit feedback using the log-barrier
summary: We study the problem of learning minimax policies in zero-sum matrix games. Fiegel et al. (2025) recently showed that achieving last-iterate convergence in this setting is harder when the players are uncoupled, by proving a lower bound on the exploitability gap of Omega(t^{-1/4}). Some online mirror descent algorithms were proposed in the literature for this problem, but none have truly attained this rate yet. We show that the use of a log-barrier regularization, along with a dual-focused analysis, allows this O-tilde(t^{-1/4}) convergence with high-probability. We additionally extend our idea to the setting of extensive-form games, proving a bound with the same rate.

AI.tldr: Achieves the optimalish last-iterate rate for minimax learning in zero-sum games using log-barrier regularization and dual-focused analysis, matching the known lower bound up to log factors; extends to extensive-form games.
AI.motivation: To close the gap between lower bounds (Omega(t^{-1/4})) for last-iterate convergence with uncoupled players and existing online mirror descent algorithms, by designing a method that attains this rate with high probability.
AI.method: Integrate log-barrier regularization into online mirror descent and perform a dual-focused analysis to analyze the exploitability gap, deriving high-probability convergence at O~(t^{-1/4}); extend the approach to extensive-form games.
AI.result: Proves O~(t^{-1/4}) last-iterate convergence in zero-sum matrix games under uncoupled players with high probability; extends the same rate to extensive-form games.
AI.conclusion: Log-barrier regularization combined with a dual-focused analysis achieves the conjectured rate, closing the gap to the Omega(t^{-1/4}) lower bound; the technique generalizes to extensive-form games, suggesting near-optimal performance under these conditions.

title: From Reactive to Proactive: Assessing the Proactivity of Voice Agents via ProVoice-Bench
summary: Recent advancements in LLM agents are gradually shifting from reactive, text-based paradigms toward proactive, multimodal interaction. However, existing benchmarks primarily focus on reactive responses, overlooking the complexities of proactive intervention and monitoring. To bridge this gap, we introduce ProVoice-Bench, the first evaluation framework specifically designed for proactive voice agents, featuring four novel tasks. By leveraging a multi-stage data synthesis pipeline, we curate 1,182 high-quality samples for rigorous testing. Our evaluation of state-of-the-art Multimodal LLMs reveals a significant performance gap, particularly regarding over-triggering and reasoning capabilities. These findings highlight the limitations of current models and offer a roadmap for developing more natural, context-aware proactive agents.

AI.tldr: ProVoice-Bench introduces the first evaluation framework for proactive voice agents, with four tasks and a data synthesis pipeline yielding 1,182 samples; it exposes a notable gap in current Multimodal LLMs, especially in over-triggering and reasoning, and charts future directions for more natural, context-aware proactive agents.
AI.motivation: There is a need to move beyond reactive, text-only benchmarks to evaluate proactive, multimodal agents that monitor and intervene autonomously. Existing benchmarks inadequately test proactive behaviors, limiting progress resolution.
AI.method: A multi-stage data synthesis pipeline creates 1,182 high-quality samples across four novel tasks. The framework evaluates state-of-the-art Multimodal LLMs on these tasks to assess proactive capabilities.
AI.result: The evaluation reveals a significant performance gap in proactive capabilities, with prevalent issues of over-triggering and insufficient reasoning. Findings diagnose model limitations and suggest a roadmap for improvement.
AI.conclusion: ProVoice-Bench provides a baseline and roadmap for developing more natural, context-aware proactive voice agents and highlights key challenges to address in future research.

title: Stability and Generalization in Looped Transformers
summary: Looped transformers promise test-time compute scaling by spending more iterations on harder problems, but it remains unclear which architectural choices let them extrapolate to harder problems at test time rather than memorize training-specific solutions. We introduce a fixed-point based framework for analyzing looped architectures along three axes of stability -- reachability, input-dependence, and geometry -- and use it to characterize when fixed-point iteration yields meaningful predictions. Theoretically, we prove that looped networks without recall have countable fixed points and cannot achieve strong input-dependence at any spectral regime, while recall combined with outer normalization reliably produces a regime in which fixed points are simultaneously reachable, locally smooth in the input, and supported by stable backpropagation. Empirically, we train single-layer looped transformers on chess, sudoku, and prefix-sums and find that downstream performance tracks the framework's predictions across tasks and architectural configurations. We additionally introduce internal recall, a novel recall placement variant, and show that it becomes competitive with -- and on sudoku, substantially better than -- standard recall placement once outer normalization is applied.

AI.tldr: Presents a fixed-point framework to analyze looped transformers along three stability axes (reachability, input-dependence, geometry). Theoretical results show that without recall, fixed points are countable and cannot yield strong input-dependence; with recall and outer normalization, fixed points become reachable, locally input-smooth, and support stable backpropagation. Empirically, single-layer looped transformers trained on chess, sudoku, and prefix-sums align with predictions; introduces internal recall as a competitive recall-placement variant when outer normalization is used.
AI.motivation: To understand when looped architectures can extrapolate to harder problems at test time (rather than memorize training cases) and to identify architectural ingredients that enable stable, generalizable fixed-point behavior.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of looped networks via a fixed-point framework along three axes: reachability, input-dependence, and geometry. Proves properties under recall vs no-recall and with/without outer normalization. Empirical validation on small-scale tasks (chess, sudoku, prefix sums) with single-layer looped transformers and a novel internal recall placement variant.
AI.result: Theorically, looped nets without recall have countable fixed points and lack strong input-dependence across spectral regimes. Recall plus outer normalization yields fixed points that are reachable, locally input-smooth, and backpropagation-stable. Empirically, downstream performance tracks framework predictions across tasks and configurations; internal recall competes with standard recall, and on sudoku, surpasses it when outer normalization is applied.
AI.conclusion: Architectural choices—specifically incorporating recall and applying outer normalization—are crucial for enabling meaningful fixed-point extrapolation in looped transformers. The proposed fixed-point lens explains when extrapolation is feasible and guides design; internal recall offers a competitive alternative to standard recall placement.

title: Where are the Humans? A Scoping Review of Fairness in Multi-agent AI Systems
comment: In proceedings of European Conference on Information Systems (ECIS) 2026
summary: Rapid advances in Generative AI are giving rise to increasingly sophisticated Multi-Agent AI (MAAI) systems. While AI fairness has been extensively studied in traditional predictive scenarios, its examination in MAAI remains nascent and fragmented. This scoping review critically synthesizes existing research on fairness in MAAI systems. Through a qualitative content analysis of 23 selected studies, we identify five archetypal approaches. Our findings reveal that fairness in MAAI systems is often addressed superficially, lacks robust normative foundations, and frequently overlooks the complex dynamics introduced by agent autonomy and system-level interactions. We argue that fairness must be embedded structurally throughout the development lifecycle of MAAI, rather than appended as a post-hoc consideration. Meaningful evaluation requires explicit human oversight, normative clarity, and a precise articulation of fairness objectives and beneficiaries. This review provides a foundation for advancing fairness research in MAAI systems by highlighting critical gaps, exposing prevailing limitations, and suggesting pathways.

AI.tldr: A scoping review of fairness in multi-agent AI (MAAI) identifies five archetypal fairness approaches, reveals superficial treatment and weak normative underpinnings, and argues for structural integration of fairness across MAAI development, with explicit human oversight and clear fairness objectives and beneficiaries; the work outlines gaps, limitations, and pathways for future research.
AI.motivation: Fairness in multi-agent AI systems is underexplored relative to single-agent fairness, yet the autonomy and complex interactions in MAAI can amplify inequities and bias propagation. A rigorous synthesis is needed to guide principled, lifecycle-spanning fairness integration.
AI.method: Qualitative content analysis of 23 studies in the domain, conducted as a scoping review. The authors classify existing approaches into five archetypes and critique their normative and practical foundations, with an emphasis on system-level dynamics and agent autonomy.
AI.result: Identification of five archetypal approaches to fairness in MAAI. The review finds that current work often addresses fairness superficially, lacks robust normative grounding, and neglects how agent autonomy and system-level interactions shape fairness. It argues for structural embedding of fairness across development, explicit human oversight, and clear articulation of fairness objectives and beneficiaries. The review highlights critical gaps, prevailing limitations, and directions for future work.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a foundational synthesis for fairness research in MAAI, illuminating gaps and limitations and proposing pathways to embed fairness within the full lifecycle of MAAI development, rather than treating it as an afterthought.

title: Boundary-Centric Active Learning for Temporal Action Segmentation
summary: Temporal action segmentation (TAS) demands dense temporal supervision, yet most of the annotation cost in untrimmed videos is spent identifying and refining action transitions, where segmentation errors concentrate and small temporal shifts disproportionately degrade segmental metrics. We introduce B-ACT, a clip-budgeted active learning framework that explicitly allocates supervision to these high-leverage boundary regions. B-ACT operates in a hierarchical two-stage loop: (i) it ranks and queries unlabeled videos using predictive uncertainty, and (ii) within each selected video, it detects candidate transitions from the current model predictions and selects the top-$K$ boundaries via a novel boundary score that fuses neighborhood uncertainty, class ambiguity, and temporal predictive dynamics. Importantly, our annotation protocol requests labels for only the boundary frames while still training on boundary-centered clips to exploit temporal context through the model's receptive field. Extensive experiments on GTEA, 50Salads, and Breakfast demonstrate that boundary-centric supervision delivers strong label efficiency and consistently surpasses representative TAS active learning baselines and prior state of the art under sparse budgets, with the largest gains on datasets where boundary placement dominates edit and overlap-based F1 scores.

AI.tldr: B-ACT is a boundary-centric active learning framework for temporal action segmentation that budgets annotations to action boundary frames using a two-stage selection loop and a boundary score, achieving high label efficiency across several TAS datasets.
AI.motivation: TAS requires dense labels, but annotation effort is concentrated at action transitions where errors harm metrics; a cost-effective strategy should prioritize boundary regions.
AI.method: A hierarchical two-stage loop: (i) query unlabeled videos using predictive uncertainty; (ii) in selected videos, detect candidate transitions from current predictions and select top-K boundaries via a boundary score that fuses neighborhood uncertainty, class ambiguity, and temporal predictive dynamics. An annotation protocol labels only boundary frames while training on boundary-centered clips to leverage temporal context through the model's receptive field.
AI.result: Experiments on GTEA, 50Salads, and Breakfast show that boundary-centric supervision yields strong label efficiency and consistently outperforms representative TAS active learning baselines and prior SOTA under sparse budgets, with the largest gains on datasets where boundary placement dominates edit and overlap-based F1 scores.
AI.conclusion: Boundary-focused active learning is effective for TAS, enabling efficient use of labeling budgets and improving boundary-sensitive metrics; the approach generalizes across multiple TAS datasets.

title: How Embeddings Shape Graph Neural Networks: Classical vs Quantum-Oriented Node Representations
summary: Node embeddings act as the information interface for graph neural networks, yet their empirical impact is often reported under mismatched backbones, splits, and training budgets. This paper provides a controlled benchmark of embedding choices for graph classification, comparing classical baselines with quantum-oriented node representations under a unified pipeline. We evaluate two classical baselines alongside quantum-oriented alternatives, including a circuit-defined variational embedding and quantum-inspired embeddings computed via graph operators and linear-algebraic constructions. All variants are trained and tested with the same backbone, stratified splits, identical optimization and early stopping, and consistent metrics. Experiments on five different TU datasets and on QM9 converted to classification via target binning show clear dataset dependence: quantum-oriented embeddings yield the most consistent gains on structure-driven benchmarks, while social graphs with limited node attributes remain well served by classical baselines. The study highlights practical trade-offs between inductive bias, trainability, and stability under a fixed training budget, and offers a reproducible reference point for selecting quantum-oriented embeddings in graph learning.

AI.tldr: Under a unified pipeline, quantum-oriented node embeddings offer more consistent gains on structure-driven graph classification tasks, while classical baselines remain competitive for social graphs with limited node attributes; a dataset-dependent benchmark across five TU datasets plus QM9 demonstrates trade-offs and provides a reproducible reference for selecting quantum-oriented embeddings.
AI.motivation: To address inconsistent reporting of embedding impact due to mismatched backbones, splits, and budgets by conducting a controlled benchmark of embedding choices for graph classification, including quantum-oriented representations.
AI.method: Compare two classical baselines and multiple quantum-oriented embeddings (circuit-defined variational embedding; quantum-inspired embeddings from graph operators and linear-algebraic constructions) within a fixed pipeline: same backbone, stratified splits, identical optimization/early stopping, consistent metrics. Datasets: five TU datasets and QM9 (binned for classification).
AI.result: Quantum-oriented embeddings yield the most consistent gains on structure-driven benchmarks; social graphs with limited node attributes are well served by classical baselines. Clear dataset dependence; practical trade-offs between inductive bias, trainability, and stability under a fixed training budget; provides a reproducible reference point for embedding selection.
AI.conclusion: The study offers guidance for choosing quantum-oriented embeddings in graph learning, illustrating dataset-dependent benefits and the importance of controlled benchmarks to balance inductive bias, trainability, and stability.

title: OpenMobile: Building Open Mobile Agents with Task and Trajectory Synthesis
summary: Mobile agents powered by vision-language models have demonstrated impressive capabilities in automating mobile tasks, with recent leading models achieving a marked performance leap, e.g., nearly 70% success on AndroidWorld. However, these systems keep their training data closed and remain opaque about their task and trajectory synthesis recipes. We present OpenMobile, an open-source framework that synthesizes high-quality task instructions and agent trajectories, with two key components: (1) The first is a scalable task synthesis pipeline that constructs a global environment memory from exploration, then leverages it to generate diverse and grounded instructions. and (2) a policy-switching strategy for trajectory rollout. By alternating between learner and expert models, it captures essential error-recovery data often missing in standard imitation learning. Agents trained on our data achieve competitive results across three dynamic mobile agent benchmarks: notably, our fine-tuned Qwen2.5-VL and Qwen3-VL reach 51.7% and 64.7% on AndroidWorld, far surpassing existing open-data approaches. Furthermore, we conduct transparent analyses on the overlap between our synthetic instructions and benchmark test sets, and verify that performance gains stem from broad functionality coverage rather than benchmark overfitting. We release data and code at https://njucckevin.github.io/openmobile/ to bridge the data gap and facilitate broader mobile agent research.

AI.tldr: OpenMobile is an open-source framework that synthesizes task instructions and agent trajectories for mobile-vision agents, using a scalable environment-memory task synthesis pipeline and policy-switching trajectory rollout. It achieves strong results on AndroidWorld with Qwen2.5-VL and Qwen3-VL, and releases data and code to enable broader mobile-agent research.
AI.motivation: Address opacity and closed data in vision-language mobile agents; provide an open, scalable data-generation pipeline to democratize research and enable robust evaluation beyond closed models.
AI.method: Two key components: (1) a scalable task synthesis pipeline that builds a global environment memory from exploration to generate diverse, grounded instructions; (2) a policy-switching trajectory rollout that alternates between learner and expert models to collect error-recovery data typically missing in standard imitation learning.
AI.result: Agents trained on the synthesized data achieve 51.7% (Qwen2.5-VL) and 64.7% (Qwen3-VL) on AndroidWorld, surpassing existing open-data approaches. Analyses show substantial overlap control with test sets and indicate performance gains stem from broad functionality coverage rather than overfitting. Data and code are released at the project page.
AI.conclusion: OpenMobile bridges the data gap for mobile-agent research by providing an open, scalable data-generation framework that yields competitive performance and enables transparent evaluation and reproduction.

title: VisPCO: Visual Token Pruning Configuration Optimization via Budget-Aware Pareto-Frontier Learning for Vision-Language Models
summary: Visual token pruning methods effectively mitigate the quadratic computational growth caused by processing high-resolution images and video frames in vision-language models (VLMs). However, existing approaches rely on predefined pruning configurations without determining whether they achieve computation-performance optimality. In this work, we introduce , a novel framework that formulates visual token pruning as a Pareto configuration optimization problem to automatically identify optimal configurations. Our approach employs continuous relaxation and straight-through estimators to enable gradient-based search, solved via the Augmented Lagrangian method. Extensive experiments across 8 visual benchmarks demonstrate that effectively approximates the empirical Pareto frontier obtained through grid search and generalizes well across various pruning methods and VLM architectures. Furthermore, through learnable kernel functions, we investigate layer-wise pruning patterns and reveal that multi-step progressive pruning captures VLMs' hierarchical compression structure, achieving superior accuracy-efficiency trade-offs compared to single-layer approaches.

AI.tldr: We present Pareto-optimal visual token pruning for vision-language models via a continuous-relaxation, gradient-based search guided by an Augmented Lagrangian, achieving near-Pareto-frontier configurations across benchmarks and revealing multi-step progressive pruning that leverages hierarchical compression.
AI.motivation: Mitigate quadratic cost of high-resolution inputs in vision-language models by pruning visual tokens; current methods rely on fixed pruning configurations and may not guarantee optimal compute-performance trade-offs.
AI.method: Formulate visual token pruning as a Pareto configuration optimization problem. Employ continuous relaxation and straight-through estimators to enable gradient-based search; solve with the Augmented Lagrangian method. Introduce learnable kernel functions to examine layer-wise pruning and identify multi-step progressive pruning patterns.
AI.result: Empirically approximate the empirical Pareto frontier comparable to grid search across 8 visual benchmarks; generalizes across pruning methods and VLM architectures; reveals that multi-step progressive pruning captures hierarchical compression and yields superior accuracy–efficiency trade-offs versus single-layer pruning.
AI.conclusion: The framework automatically identifies Pareto-optimal pruning configurations, uncovers hierarchical compression structure through progressive layer-wise pruning, and generalizes across methods and models, enabling optimized compute–performance trade-offs in VLMs.

title: Benchmarking Optimizers for MLPs in Tabular Deep Learning
summary: MLP is a heavily used backbone in modern deep learning (DL) architectures for supervised learning on tabular data, and AdamW is the go-to optimizer used to train tabular DL models. Unlike architecture design, however, the choice of optimizer for tabular DL has not been examined systematically, despite new optimizers showing promise in other domains. To fill this gap, we benchmark \Noptimizers optimizers on \Ndatasets tabular datasets for training MLP-based models in the standard supervised learning setting under a shared experiment protocol. Our main finding is that the Muon optimizer consistently outperforms AdamW, and thus should be considered a strong and practical choice for practitioners and researchers, if the associated training efficiency overhead is affordable. Additionally, we find exponential moving average of model weights to be a simple yet effective technique that improves AdamW on vanilla MLPs, though its effect is less consistent across model variants.

AI.tldr: Muon optimizer outperforms AdamW for training MLP-based tabular DL models across multiple datasets; EMA of weights boosts AdamW but with inconsistent gains; Muon is recommended if training overhead is acceptable.
AI.motivation: Despite Muon-like optimizers showing promise in other domains, optimizer choices for tabular DL have not been systematically studied. This paper benchmarks optimizers on tabular MLPs to guide practitioners.
AI.method: Benchmarking \\Noptimizers on \\Ndatasets tabular datasets using MLP backbones in supervised learning under a shared protocol; measure accuracy and training efficiency; analyze EMA of weights on AdamW.
AI.result: Muon consistently outperforms AdamW; weight EMA improves AdamW on vanilla MLPs; gains vary across model variants; efficiency overhead is a consideration.
AI.conclusion: Muon is a strong practical choice for tabular DL under realistic compute budgets; EMA is a simple tactic to boost AdamW, though effects vary.

title: HyperSpace: A Generalized Framework for Spatial Encoding in Hyperdimensional Representations
summary: Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) provide a well-defined algebraic framework for compositional representations in hyperdimensional spaces. We introduce HyperSpace, an open-source framework that decomposes VSA systems into modular operators for encoding, binding, bundling, similarity, cleanup, and regression. Using HyperSpace, we analyze and benchmark two representative VSA backends: Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR) and Fourier Holographic Reduced Representations (FHRR). Although FHRR provides lower theoretical complexity for individual operations, HyperSpaces modularity reveals that similarity and cleanup dominate runtime in spatial domains. As a result, HRR and FHRR exhibit comparable end-to-end performance. Differences in memory footprint introduce additional deployment trade-offs where HRR requires approximately half the memory of FHRR vectors. By enabling modular, system-level evaluation, HyperSpace reveals practical trade-offs in VSA pipelines that are not apparent from theoretical or operator-level comparisons alone.

AI.tldr: HyperSpace modularizes VSA workflows into encoding/binding/bundling/similarity/cleanup/regression, enabling system-level benchmarking of HRR and FHRR. Despite FHRR's lower per-operation complexity, runtime is dominated by similarity/cleanup, yielding similar end-to-end performance; HRR uses about half the memory of FHRR, highlighting memory as a key deployment trade-off.
AI.motivation: To understand real-world performance and deployment trade-offs in vector symbolic architectures by decomposing pipelines into modular operations, and to compare two representative backends (HRR and FHRR) under a unified framework.
AI.method: Introduce HyperSpace, an open-source framework that decomposes VSA systems into modular operators (encoding, binding, bundling, similarity, cleanup, regression). Implement benchmarks for HRR and FHRR within this framework, evaluating end-to-end performance and memory usage in spatial domains.
AI.result: Similarity and cleanup steps dominate runtime in spatial VSA pipelines. HRR and FHRR achieve comparable end-to-end performance despite differing per-operation complexities. HRR uses approximately half the memory of FHRR vectors, introducing memory-based deployment trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: System-level, modular evaluation with HyperSpace uncovers practical trade-offs in VSA pipelines that operator-level or theoretical analyses miss. The framework enables fair, apples-to-apples comparisons and highlights when memory, not just computation, constrains deployment.

title: Unsupervised Skeleton-Based Action Segmentation via Hierarchical Spatiotemporal Vector Quantization
summary: We propose a novel hierarchical spatiotemporal vector quantization framework for unsupervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation. We first introduce a hierarchical approach, which includes two consecutive levels of vector quantization. Specifically, the lower level associates skeletons with fine-grained subactions, while the higher level further aggregates subactions into action-level representations. Our hierarchical approach outperforms the non-hierarchical baseline, while primarily exploiting spatial cues by reconstructing input skeletons. Next, we extend our approach by leveraging both spatial and temporal information, yielding a hierarchical spatiotemporal vector quantization scheme. In particular, our hierarchical spatiotemporal approach performs multi-level clustering, while simultaneously recovering input skeletons and their corresponding timestamps. Lastly, extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks, including HuGaDB, LARa, and BABEL, demonstrate that our approach establishes a new state-of-the-art performance and reduces segment length bias in unsupervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical spatiotemporal vector quantization (VQ) for unsupervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation, with a two-level VQ (subactions → actions) and an extension to spatiotemporal VQ that also recovers timestamps, achieving state-of-the-art on HuGaDB, LARa, and BABEL and reducing segment-length bias.
AI.motivation: Address unsupervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation by capturing hierarchical structure (subactions and actions) and incorporating both spatial and temporal cues, while mitigating segment length bias and leveraging reconstruction objectives.
AI.method: 1) A two-level hierarchical vector quantization: lower level maps skeletons to fine-grained subactions; higher level aggregates subactions into action-level representations; reconstruction of input skeletons to emphasize spatial cues. 2) Extension to hierarchical spatiotemporal VQ: multi-level clustering that also recovers input skeletons and their timestamps, combining spatial and temporal information.
AI.result: The hierarchical approach outperforms a non-hierarchical baseline and, when extended to spatiotemporal VQ, achieves state-of-the-art performance on HuGaDB, LARa, and BABEL, while reducing segment length bias in unsupervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical (and hierarchical spatiotemporal) VQ is effective for unsupervised skeleton-based temporal action segmentation, offering improved accuracy and reduced segment length bias, validated on multiple benchmarks.

title: SRMU: Relevance-Gated Updates for Streaming Hyperdimensional Memories
summary: Sequential associative memories (SAMs) are difficult to build and maintain in real-world streaming environments, where observations arrive incrementally over time, have imbalanced sampling, and non-stationary temporal dynamics. Vector Symbolic Architectures (VSAs) provide a biologically-inspired framework for building SAMs. Entities and attributes are encoded as quasi-orthogonal hyperdimensional vectors and processed with well defined algebraic operations. Despite this rich framework, most VSA systems rely on simple additive updates, where repeated observations reinforce existing information even when no new information is introduced. In non-stationary environments, this leads to the persistence of stale information after the underlying system changes. In this work, we introduce the Sequential Relevance Memory Unit (SRMU), a domain- and cleanup-agnostic update rule for VSA-based SAMs. The SRMU combines temporal decay with a relevance gating mechanism. Unlike prior approaches that solely rely on cleanup, the SRMU regulates memory formation by filtering redundant, conflicting, and stale information before storage. We evaluate the SRMU on streaming state-tracking tasks that isolate non-uniform sampling and non-stationary temporal dynamics. Our results show that the SRMU increases memory similarity by $12.6\%$ and reduces cumulative memory magnitude by $53.5\%$. This shows that the SRMU produces more stable memory growth and stronger alignment with the ground-truth state.

AI.tldr: SRMU is a decay- and relevance-guided update rule for VSA-based sequential associative memories to handle non-stationary streaming data.
AI.motivation: Address memory drift and stale information in SAMs under non-stationary, imbalanced streaming inputs; improve stability and alignment with ground truth.
AI.method: Introduce Sequential Relevance Memory Unit (SRMU) integrating temporal decay and relevance gating. Filters redundant/conflicting/stale info before storage instead of pure additive updates; combines decay with gating; used for VSA-based SAMs.
AI.result: In streaming state-tracking tasks isolating non-uniform sampling and non-stationary dynamics, SRMU increased memory similarity by 12.6% and reduced cumulative memory magnitude by 53.5%, yielding more stable memory growth and better ground-truth alignment.
AI.conclusion: SRMU provides a domain- and cleanup-agnostic memory update rule that improves robustness of VSA-based SAMs in non-stationary environments; potential for more durable representations in streaming inference.

title: StreamCacheVGGT: Streaming Visual Geometry Transformers with Robust Scoring and Hybrid Cache Compression
summary: Reconstructing dense 3D geometry from continuous video streams requires stable inference under a constant memory budget. Existing $O(1)$ frameworks primarily rely on a ``pure eviction'' paradigm, which suffers from significant information destruction due to binary token deletion and evaluation noise from localized, single-layer scoring. To address these bottlenecks, we propose StreamCacheVGGT, a training-free framework that reimagines cache management through two synergistic modules: Cross-Layer Consistency-Enhanced Scoring (CLCES) and Hybrid Cache Compression (HCC). CLCES mitigates activation noise by tracking token importance trajectories across the Transformer hierarchy, employing order-statistical analysis to identify sustained geometric salience. Leveraging these robust scores, HCC transcends simple eviction by introducing a three-tier triage strategy that merges moderately important tokens into retained anchors via nearest-neighbor assignment on the key-vector manifold. This approach preserves essential geometric context that would otherwise be lost. Extensive evaluations on five benchmarks (7-Scenes, NRGBD, ETH3D, Bonn, and KITTI) demonstrate that StreamCacheVGGT sets a new state-of-the-art, delivering superior reconstruction accuracy and long-term stability while strictly adhering to constant-cost constraints.

AI.tldr: Training-free, constant-memory streaming 3D reconstruction via CLCES + HCC; improves beyond pure eviction by preserving geometry.
AI.motivation: Existing constant-memory O(1) methods suffer from information destruction due to binary token eviction and noise from localized, single-layer scoring; a stable, memory-budget-compliant streaming approach is needed.
AI.method: Cross-Layer Consistency-Enhanced Scoring (CLCES) tracks token importance across Transformer layers with order-statistics to identify sustained salience; Hybrid Cache Compression (HCC) uses a three-tier triage to merge moderately important tokens into retained anchors via nearest-neighbor assignment on the key-vector manifold, preserving geometric context.
AI.result: Evaluations on five benchmarks (7-Scenes, NRGBD, ETH3D, Bonn, KITTI) show state-of-the-art reconstruction accuracy and long-term stability under constant-cost constraints.
AI.conclusion: StreamCacheVGGT provides a training-free, robust caching framework for dense 3D reconstruction in streaming settings, balancing memory efficiency with geometric fidelity.

title: An Axiomatic Benchmark for Evaluation of Scientific Novelty Metrics
summary: The rigorous evaluation of the novelty of a scientific paper is, even for human scientists, a challenging task. With the increasing interest in AI scientists and AI involvement in scientific idea generation and paper writing, it also becomes increasingly important that this task be automatable and reliable, lest both human attention and compute tokens be wasted on ideas that have already been explored. Due to the challenge of quantifying ground-truth novelty, however, existing novelty metrics for scientific papers generally validate their results against noisy, confounded signals such as citation counts or peer review scores. These proxies can conflate novelty with impact, quality, or reviewer preference, which in turn makes it harder to assess how well a given metric actually evaluates novelty. We therefore propose an axiomatic benchmark for scientific novelty metrics. We first define a set of axioms that a well-behaved novelty metric should satisfy, grounded in human scientific norms and practice, then evaluate existing metrics across ten tasks spanning three domains of AI research. Our results reveal that no existing metric satisfies all axioms consistently, and that metrics fail on systematically different axioms, reflecting their underlying architectures. Additionally, we show that combining metrics of complementary architectures leads to consistent improvements on the benchmark, with per-axiom weighting achieving 90.1% versus 71.5% for the best individual metric, suggesting that developing architecturally diverse metrics is a promising direction for future work. We release the benchmark code as supplementary material to encourage the development of more robust scientific literature novelty metrics.

AI.tldr: An axiomatic benchmark is proposed for scientific novelty metrics, revealing no single metric meets all axioms; architecturally diverse metric ensembles outperform single metrics, with per-axiom weighting achieving substantial gains; benchmark code released.
AI.motivation: Ground truth novelty is hard to quantify and existing proxies (citations, peer review) confound novelty with impact or quality. An axiomatic, human-aligned benchmark is needed to reliably evaluate and guide the development of novelty metrics for AI-assisted science.
AI.method: Define a set of human-aligned axioms for novelty. Evaluate existing novelty metrics across ten tasks in three AI domains. Analyze which axioms are violated by each metric. Explore whether combining metrics with complementary architectures and per-axiom weighting improves alignment with the axioms. Release benchmark code as supplementary material.
AI.result: No existing metric satisfies all axioms consistently; metrics fail on different axioms due to their underlying architectures. Ensembles of metrics with complementary architectures yield consistent improvements on the benchmark. Per-axiom weighting achieves 90.1% versus 71.5% for the best individual metric. Benchmark code released to foster further development.
AI.conclusion: Architecturally diverse novelty metrics are a promising direction for robust evaluation of scientific novelty. An axiomatic benchmark can drive the development of more reliable metrics, reducing dependence on noisy proxies.

title: TokenGS: Decoupling 3D Gaussian Prediction from Pixels with Learnable Tokens
summary: In this work, we revisit several key design choices of modern Transformer-based approaches for feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) prediction. We argue that the common practice of regressing Gaussian means as depths along camera rays is suboptimal, and instead propose to directly regress 3D mean coordinates using only a self-supervised rendering loss. This formulation allows us to move from the standard encoder-only design to an encoder-decoder architecture with learnable Gaussian tokens, thereby unbinding the number of predicted primitives from input image resolution and number of views. Our resulting method, TokenGS, demonstrates improved robustness to pose noise and multiview inconsistencies, while naturally supporting efficient test-time optimization in token space without degrading learned priors. TokenGS achieves state-of-the-art feed-forward reconstruction performance on both static and dynamic scenes, producing more regularized geometry and more balanced 3DGS distribution, while seamlessly recovering emergent scene attributes such as static-dynamic decomposition and scene flow.

AI.tldr: TokenGS shifts from depth regression along rays to direct 3D mean regression using a self-supervised rendering loss, enabling an encoder–decoder with learnable Gaussian tokens. This decouples the number of predicted primitives from image resolution and view count, yielding robust, state-of-the-art feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting for both static and dynamic scenes with emergent attributes and efficient token-space test-time optimization.
AI.motivation: Regressing Gaussian means as depths along camera rays is suboptimal for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS); a flexible, compact representation is desired that is robust to pose noise and multiview inconsistencies and that decouples primitive count from input resolution/views.
AI.method: Replace depth regression with direct 3D mean coordinate regression using a self-supervised rendering loss. Adopt an encoder–decoder architecture with learnable Gaussian tokens, enabling the number of predicted primitives to be independent of image resolution and views. Enable test-time optimization in token space.
AI.result: TokenGS achieves state-of-the-art feed-forward reconstruction on static and dynamic scenes, with more regularized geometry and a balanced 3DGS distribution. It also reveals emergent scene attributes such as static–dynamic decomposition and scene flow, and shows robustness to pose noise and multiview inconsistencies.
AI.conclusion: The Gaussian-token architecture and direct 3D mean regression improve robustness, priors, and scalability for 3DGS, enabling efficient token-space inference and strong performance across diverse scenes.

title: IG-Search: Step-Level Information Gain Rewards for Search-Augmented Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning has emerged as an effective paradigm for training large language models to perform search-augmented reasoning. However, existing approaches rely on trajectory-level rewards that cannot distinguish precise search queries from vague or redundant ones within a rollout group, and collapse to a near-zero gradient signal whenever every sampled trajectory fails. In this paper, we propose IG-Search, a reinforcement learning framework that introduces a step-level reward based on Information Gain (IG). For each search step, IG measures how much the retrieved documents improve the model's confidence in the gold answer relative to a counterfactual baseline of random documents, thereby reflecting the effectiveness of the underlying search query. This signal is fed back to the corresponding search-query tokens via per-token advantage modulation in GRPO, enabling fine-grained, step-level credit assignment within a rollout. Unlike prior step-level methods that require either externally annotated intermediate supervision or shared environment states across trajectories, IG-Search derives its signals from the policy's own generation probabilities, requiring no intermediate annotations beyond standard question-answer pairs. Experiments on seven single-hop and multi-hop QA benchmarks demonstrate that IG-Search achieves an average EM of 0.430 with Qwen2.5-3B, outperforming the strongest trajectory-level baseline (MR-Search) by 1.6 points and the step-level method GiGPO by 0.9 points on average across benchmarks, with particularly pronounced gains on multi-hop reasoning tasks. Despite introducing a dense step-level signal, IG-Search adds only ~6.4% to per-step training wall-clock time over the trajectory-level baseline and leaves inference latency unchanged, while still providing a meaningful gradient signal even when every sampled trajectory answers incorrectly.

AI.tldr: IG-Search introduces a step-level Information Gain reward for search-augmented RL in LLM QA, enabling per-token credit and robust gradients even when all trajectories fail; shows gains over trajectory-level baselines with modest overhead and unchanged inference latency.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of trajectory-level rewards which cannot distinguish precise vs vague search queries and collapse to near-zero gradients when all samples fail; enable fine-grained credit assignment for search queries to improve search effectiveness and QA performance.
AI.method: Propose IG-Search which uses a step-level reward based on Information Gain: at each search step, measure how retrieved documents increase confidence in the gold answer relative to a counterfactual baseline of random documents. Propagate this signal to the corresponding search-query tokens via per-token advantage modulation in GRPO (a policy-gradient with rollouts) for fine-grained credit assignment. No external intermediate supervision; relies on standard QA pairs.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on seven single-hop and multi-hop QA benchmarks shows average EM of 0.430 on Qwen2.5-3B, surpassing the trajectory-level MR-Search baseline by 1.6 points and the step-level GiGPO baseline by 0.9 points, with larger gains on multi-hop tasks. Training adds ~6.4% per-step time over trajectory-level baseline, and inference latency remains unchanged; the approach provides meaningful gradient signals even when all trajectories fail.
AI.conclusion: Step-level Information Gain rewards enable precise credit assignment for search queries in reinforcement learning for LLM QA, yielding consistent accuracy improvements with modest training-time overhead and no need for intermediate supervision, especially beneficial for multi-hop reasoning.

title: SegWithU: Uncertainty as Perturbation Energy for Single-Forward-Pass Risk-Aware Medical Image Segmentation
summary:
Reliable uncertainty estimation is critical for medical image segmentation, where automated contours feed downstream quantification and clinical decision support. Many strong uncertainty methods require repeated inference, while efficient single-forward-pass alternatives often provide weaker failure ranking or rely on restrictive feature-space assumptions. We present $\textbf{SegWithU}$, a post-hoc framework that augments a frozen pretrained segmentation backbone with a lightweight uncertainty head. SegWithU taps intermediate backbone features and models uncertainty as perturbation energy in a compact probe space using rank-1 posterior probes. It produces two voxel-wise uncertainty maps: a calibration-oriented map for probability tempering and a ranking-oriented map for error detection and selective prediction. Across ACDC, BraTS2024, and LiTS, SegWithU is the strongest and most consistent single-forward-pass baseline, achieving AUROC/AURC of $0.9838/2.4885$, $0.9946/0.2660$, and $0.9925/0.8193$, respectively, while preserving segmentation quality. These results suggest that perturbation-based uncertainty modeling is an effective and practical route to reliability-aware medical segmentation.
  Source code is available at https://github.com/ProjectNeura/SegWithU.

AI.tldr: Post-hoc, perturbation-energy based uncertainty head (SegWithU) augments a frozen segmentation backbone to provide two voxel-wise uncertainty maps (calibration and ranking) with strong single-pass performance across ACDC, BraTS2024, LiTS.
AI.motivation: Need reliable uncertainty estimation for medical image segmentation with single-pass efficiency; existing methods trade-off speed vs reliability or rely on restrictive feature-space assumptions.
AI.method: Freeze pretrained segmentation backbone; add lightweight uncertainty head that taps intermediate backbone features; model uncertainty as perturbation energy in a rank-1 posterior probe space; outputs two voxel-wise maps: calibration-oriented probability tempering and ranking-oriented error detection.
AI.result: Strongest single-forward-pass baseline across ACDC, BraTS2024, LiTS with AUROC/AURC values: 0.9838/2.4885 (ACDC), 0.9946/0.2660 (BraTS2024), 0.9925/0.8193 (LiTS); preserves segmentation quality.
AI.conclusion: Perturbation-based uncertainty modeling is effective and practical for reliability-aware medical segmentation; SegWithU provides consistent performance and two-purpose uncertainty maps; code available at GitHub.

title: Agent-Aided Design for Dynamic CAD Models
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, to be published in CAIS'26
summary: In the past year, researchers have started to create agentic systems that can design real-world CAD-style objects in a training-free setting, a new variety of system that we call Agent-Aided Design. Generally speaking, these systems place an agent in a feedback loop in which it can write code, compile that code to an assembly of CAD model(s), visualize the model, and then iteratively refine its code based on visual and other feedback. Despite rapid progress, a key problem remains: none of these systems can build complex 3D assemblies with moving parts. For example, no existing system can build a piston, a pendulum, or even a pair of scissors. In order for Agent-Aided Design to make a real impact in industrial manufacturing, we need a system that is capable of generating such 3D assemblies. In this paper we present a prototype of AADvark, an agentic system designed for this task. Unlike previous state-of-the-art systems, AADvark captures the dynamic part interactions with one or more degrees-of-freedom. This design decision allows AADvark to reason directly about assemblies with moving parts and can thereby achieve cross-cutting goals, including but not limited to mechanical movements. Unfortunately, current LLMs are imperfect spatial reasoners, a problem that AADvark addresses by incorporating external constraint solver tools with a specialized visual feedback mechanism. We demonstrate that, by modifying the agent's tools (FreeCAD and the assembly solver), we are able to create a strong verification signal which enables our system to build 3D assemblies with movable parts.

AI.tldr: AADvark is a prototype agentic CAD system capable of generating 3D assemblies with movable parts by integrating code generation, external constraint solving, and visual feedback to overcome LLM spatial reasoning limits.
AI.motivation: To enable autonomous design of complex 3D assemblies (with moving parts) for industrial manufacturing, addressing the limitation that existing agentic CAD systems cannot build moving-component devices.
AI.method: Architectural design: represent dynamic interactions with one or more degrees of freedom; use code to generate CAD models, visualize, and refine. Incorporate external constraint solver tools and a specialized visual feedback mechanism; modify FreeCAD and the assembly solver to provide a strong verification signal.
AI.result: The system can build 3D assemblies with movable parts, demonstrated by the use of updated tools yielding a strong verification signal that enables moving-part assemblies.
AI.conclusion: AADvark extends agent-aided design to dynamic assemblies, addressing LLM spatial reasoning gaps via external solvers and feedback, enabling mechanical movement goals and offering potential industrial impact.

title: Why Do Vision Language Models Struggle To Recognize Human Emotions?
summary: Understanding emotions is a fundamental ability for intelligent systems to be able to interact with humans. Vision-language models (VLMs) have made tremendous progress in the last few years for many visual tasks, potentially offering a promising solution for understanding emotions. However, it is surprising that even the most sophisticated contemporary VLMs struggle to recognize human emotions or to outperform even specialized vision-only classifiers. In this paper we ask the question "Why do VLMs struggle to recognize human emotions?", and observe that the inherently continuous and dynamic task of facial expression recognition (DFER) exposes two critical VLM vulnerabilities. First, emotion datasets are naturally long-tailed, and the web-scale data used to pre-train VLMs exacerbates this head-class bias, causing them to systematically collapse rare, under-represented emotions into common categories. We propose alternative sampling strategies that prevent favoring common concepts. Second, temporal information is critical for understanding emotions. However, VLMs are unable to represent temporal information over dense frame sequences, as they are limited by context size and the number of tokens that can fit in memory, which poses a clear challenge for emotion recognition. We demonstrate that the sparse temporal sampling strategy used in VLMs is inherently misaligned with the fleeting nature of micro-expressions (0.25-0.5 seconds), which are often the most critical affective signal. As a diagnostic probe, we propose a multi-stage context enrichment strategy that utilizes the information from "in-between" frames by first converting them into natural language summaries. This enriched textual context is provided as input to the VLM alongside sparse keyframes, preventing attentional dilution from excessive visual data while preserving the emotional trajectory.

AI.tldr: VLMs struggle with emotion recognition due to two vulnerabilities: (1) long-tailed emotion data causing head-class bias that collapses rare emotions into common ones, and (2) inability to model dense temporal information, misaligning with fleeting micro-expressions. The paper proposes (i) sampling strategies to reduce bias and (ii) a multi-stage context enrichment that summarizes in-between frames into text to augment sparse keyframes, preserving emotional trajectories.
AI.motivation: Understand why vision-language models underperform on human emotion recognition in dynamic facial expressions and diagnose core weaknesses related to data distribution and temporal modeling.
AI.method: Identify two vulnerabilities in VLM-based DFER: (a) long-tailed data and head-class bias; (b) limited temporal context and inefficient handling of micro-expressions. Propose alternative sampling strategies to mitigate bias and a diagnostic multi-stage context enrichment that converts intermediate frames into natural language summaries and uses this textual context alongside sparse keyframes to guide the VLM.
AI.result: Diagnosis of two critical vulnerabilities: data distribution bias toward common emotions and limited temporal modeling causing failure to capture micro-expressions. Proposes concrete remedies: re-sampling strategies to balance concepts; a context-enrichment pipeline that summarizes in-between frames into text and feeds it with sparse keyframes to the VLM, aiming to preserve emotional trajectory without excessive visual input.
AI.conclusion: VLMs’ weakness in DFER stems from data frequency biases and constrained temporal representation. Addressing head-class bias and leveraging textual summaries of intermediate frames can enhance emotion recognition by aligning temporal dynamics with VLM input constraints.

title: Meituan Merchant Business Diagnosis via Policy-Guided Dual-Process User Simulation
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, 2 tables, accepted at SIGIR 2026 Industry Track
summary: Simulating group-level user behavior enables scalable counterfactual evaluation of merchant strategies without costly online experiments. However, building a trustworthy simulator faces two structural challenges. First, information incompleteness causes reasoning-based simulators to over-rationalize when unobserved factors such as offline context and implicit habits are missing. Second, mechanism duality requires capturing both interpretable preferences and implicit statistical regularities, which no single paradigm achieves alone. We propose Policy-Guided Hybrid Simulation (PGHS), a dual-process framework that mines transferable decision policies from behavioral trajectories and uses them as a shared alignment layer. This layer anchors an LLM-based reasoning branch that prevents over-rationalization and an ML-based fitting branch that absorbs implicit regularities. Group-level predictions from both branches are fused for complementary correction. We deploy PGHS on Meituan with 101 merchants and over 26,000 trajectories. PGHS achieves a group simulation error of 8.80%, improving over the best reasoning-based and fitting-based baselines by 45.8% and 40.9% respectively.

AI.tldr: Policy-Guided Hybrid Simulation (PGHS) combines policy extraction from historical trajectories with dual reasoning and fitting branches, fused for group-level predictions, achieving 8.80% group error and large gains over baselines.
AI.motivation: Enable scalable, trustworthy counterfactual evaluation of merchant strategies by simulating group-level user behavior. Two core challenges: (1) information incompleteness leads to over-rationalization when unobserved factors are missing, (2) mechanism duality requires capturing both interpretable preferences and implicit statistical regularities, which no single paradigm handles well.
